# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the D-value and Z-value in the context of microbial inactivation. Why is D-value more relevant for non-thermal processes than Z-value?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **D-value (Decimal Reduction Time):** It is the time required at a given set of process conditions (temperature, pressure, electric field, etc.) to achieve a **90% reduction** (1-log₁₀ cycle) in the viable microbial population. Mathematically, $D = 2.303/k$ where $k$ is the inactivation rate constant. **[1 mark]**

> **Z-value:** It is the temperature change required to achieve a **10-fold change** in the D-value, primarily used in **thermal processing**. $Z = (T_2 - T_1)/\log(D_1/D_2)$. **[1 mark]**

> **Why D-value is more relevant in NTP:** Non-thermal processes often operate at near-ambient temperatures where Z-value has little meaning (since $T$ does not change significantly). Instead, the equivalent of Z-value (analogous "pressure-Z-value" or "PEF-Z-value") is used. **D-value remains the universal metric** because it directly indicates microbial lethality as a function of time, pressure, or field strength. **[1 mark]**

---

### Question 2: Process Principle `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Q: With the help of a suitable equation, explain the principle of microbial inactivation by Pulsed Electric Field (PEF) processing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The principle of PEF is based on **electroporation** — the formation of transient or permanent pores in microbial cell membranes when exposed to high-intensity electric field pulses (typically 10–80 kV/cm for 1–100 μs). **[1 mark]**

> When the transmembrane potential exceeds a critical value (~1 V), the lipid bilayer undergoes dielectric breakdown, leading to **irreversible permeabilization** and cell death. **[1 mark]**

> The kinetic relationship is expressed as:

$$\log_{10}(N/N_0) = -\frac{t}{D_{PEF}}$$

> where $D_{PEF}$ depends on field strength $E$ and pulse number $n$. Higher $E$ reduces $D_{PEF}$, accelerating inactivation. **[1 mark]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: High Pressure Processing Kinetics and Energy `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** **[7 marks]** Derive the first-order microbial inactivation model. Starting from the differential form, derive the survivor curve equation $\log_{10}(N/N_0) = -t/D$ and the D-value formula $D = 2.303/k$. Comment on why non-thermal survivor curves often deviate from log-linear behavior.

**(b)** **[7 marks]** In an HPP experiment on *E. coli* in orange juice, the following data was obtained at 25 °C:

| Pressure (MPa) | D-value (min) |
|---|---|
| 300 | 8.5 |
| 450 | 1.2 |
| 600 | 0.15 |

Calculate: (i) the **activation volume** $V_a$ assuming the reference pressure is 300 MPa, (ii) the **pressure Z-value** (analogous to thermal Z), and (iii) the time required to achieve a **5-log reduction** at 500 MPa using linear interpolation on log(D) vs P plot.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks breakdown):**

Starting from first-order kinetics:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -kN \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

Separation of variables and integration from $N_0$ to $N$:

$$\int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN}{N} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

$$\ln(N/N_0) = -kt \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

Conversion to base-10 logarithm using $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$:

$$\log_{10}(N/N_0) = -\frac{kt}{2.303} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

Define D-value as the time for $N/N_0 = 0.1$ (i.e., $\log_{10}(N/N_0) = -1$):

$$-1 = -\frac{k \cdot D}{2.303} \implies D = \frac{2.303}{k} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

Final survivor curve:

$$\log_{10}(N/N_0) = -\frac{t}{D} \quad \text{[1 mark]}$$

**Deviation commentary (1 mark):** Survivor curves in NTP often show **tailing** due to sub-population heterogeneity, clumping, and adaptive resistance. This motivates alternative models like the **Weibull model** with shape parameter $\beta \neq 1$.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Activation volume** using the relation:

$$V_a = \frac{2.303 \cdot R \cdot T \cdot \log(D_{ref}/D)}{P - P_{ref}}$$

Using $P_{ref} = 300$ MPa, $T = 298$ K, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). Note: $V_a$ in cm³/mol requires pressure in Pa. Convert: 1 MPa = $10^6$ Pa.

$$V_a = \frac{2.303 \cdot 8.314 \cdot 298 \cdot \log(8.5/0.15)}{(450 - 300) \times 10^6}$$

Compute numerator:

$$2.303 \cdot 8.314 \cdot 298 \cdot \log(56.67)$$

$$= 2.303 \cdot 2477.6 \cdot 1.753$$

$$= 5706.7 \cdot 1.753 = 10003.8 \text{ J/mol}$$

Dividing by $(150 \times 10^6)$:

$$V_a = \frac{10003.8}{150 \times 10^6} = 6.67 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{mol}$$

Converting to cm³/mol (1 m³ = $10^6$ cm³):

$$\boxed{V_a \approx 66.7 \text{ cm}^3/\text{mol}}$$

This is consistent with literature values for *E. coli* (40–80 cm³/mol). **[3 marks]**

**(ii) Pressure Z-value** is the pressure change needed for a 10-fold reduction in D:

$$Z_P = \frac{P_2 - P_1}{\log(D_1/D_2)}$$

Using $P_1 = 300$, $D_1 = 8.5$ and $P_2 = 600$, $D_2 = 0.15$:

$$Z_P = \frac{600 - 300}{\log(8.5/0.15)} = \frac{300}{1.753} \approx 171 \text{ MPa per log}$$

$$\boxed{Z_P \approx 171 \text{ MPa}} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

**(iii) Time for 5-log at 500 MPa** using log-linear interpolation between 450 and 600 MPa:

$$\log(D) \text{ at 500 MPa} = \log(1.2) + \frac{500 - 450}{600 - 450} \cdot [\log(0.15) - \log(1.2)]$$

$$= 0.0792 + \frac{50}{150} \cdot (-0.824 - 0.0792)$$

$$= 0.0792 + 0.333 \cdot (-0.9032)$$

$$= 0.0792 - 0.3008 = -0.2216$$

$$D_{500} = 10^{-0.2216} = 0.600 \text{ min}$$

Time for 5-log:

$$t_{5-log} = 5 \cdot D_{500} = 5 \cdot 0.600 = 3.0 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{t_{5-log} \approx 3.0 \text{ min}} \quad \text{[2 marks]}$$

---

### Question B: Pulsed Electric Field and Ultrasound `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** **[7 marks]** Explain the principle of PEF processing with a labeled diagram of a continuous PEF treatment system. Derive the specific energy input equation $W_{spec} = V I \tau n / m$ and discuss the role of pulse polarity (mono-polar vs bipolar) on microbial inactivation efficiency.

**(b)** **[7 marks]** A batch ultrasound processor operates at 20 kHz with acoustic intensity 25 kW/m². The treatment chamber has an effective radiating area of $5 \times 10^{-3}$ m². Calculate: (i) total acoustic power delivered, (ii) energy supplied in 10 minutes of treatment, and (iii) the temperature rise of 1 kg of juice (specific heat $C_p = 3800$ J/kg·K) if **100%** of acoustic energy is converted to heat. Comment on the importance of cooling during ultrasonic processing.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PEF Principle and System (7 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):** PEF works by applying **short pulses** (μs range) of **high voltage** (typically 20–80 kV/cm) across a food placed between two electrodes. The resulting electric field induces **electroporation** — formation of pores in microbial cell membranes — leading to leakage of cellular contents and death. Small food molecules (vitamins, pigments) remain unaffected because their size is much smaller than microorganisms.

**Diagram description (2 marks):** A continuous PEF system consists of: (1) High-voltage DC power supply, (2) Energy storage capacitor bank, (3) Switch (thyristor/IGBT), (4) Co-field or parallel-plate treatment chamber with food flowing through, (5) Cooling section, (6) Pulse measurement (oscilloscope), (7) Control unit. The food flows continuously through the treatment zone where pulses are applied.

**Derivation of $W_{spec}$ (2 marks):** For one pulse, instantaneous power $P(t) = V(t) \cdot I(t)$. For a rectangular pulse of width $\tau$ at constant $V$ and $I$:

$$E_{pulse} = V \cdot I \cdot \tau$$

For $n$ pulses:

$$E_{total} = n \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \tau$$

Specific energy:

$$W_{spec} = \frac{n V I \tau}{m}$$

**Bipolar vs Mono-polar (1 mark):** **Bipolar pulses** (alternating polarity) prevent **electrolysis** at electrodes, reduce deposition of charged particles on electrode surfaces, and provide more uniform treatment. Inactivation efficiency with bipolar pulses is typically 20–40% higher than equivalent mono-polar pulses for the same specific energy.

---

**(b) Ultrasound Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Acoustic Power (2 marks):**

$$P_{acoustic} = I \cdot A = 25 \times 10^3 \text{ W/m}^2 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$P_{acoustic} = 125 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{acoustic} = 125 \text{ W}}$$

**(ii) Energy in 10 minutes (2 marks):**

$$E = P \cdot t = 125 \text{ W} \cdot (10 \cdot 60) \text{ s} = 125 \cdot 600 = 75000 \text{ J}$$

$$\boxed{E = 75 \text{ kJ}}$$

**(iii) Temperature Rise (3 marks):**

Energy balance assuming all acoustic energy converts to heat:

$$E = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$$

$$\Delta T = \frac{E}{m \cdot C_p} = \frac{75000}{1 \cdot 3800}$$

$$\Delta T = 19.74 \, °C$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T \approx 19.7 \, °C}$$

**Cooling Commentary:** This significant temperature rise demonstrates why **cooling (jacketed vessels, ice baths)** is essential during ultrasonic processing to maintain **non-thermal** characteristics. Without cooling, the temperature may exceed the **lethal threshold** for vegetative cells (50–60 °C), shifting the process from *purely* non-thermal to *thermo-sonication*, which combines thermal and mechanical inactivation mechanisms.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Non-Thermal Processing Questions:**

1. **Unit confusion (–2 marks typical):** Students often confuse MPa with kPa, μs with ms, kV/cm with V/cm. Always check units before computing. $1 \text{ MPa} = 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 10^3 \text{ kPa}$.

2. **D-value definition error (–1 mark):** D-value is for **1-log reduction** (90% kill), NOT 99% (which would be 2D) or 99.9% (3D). For 5-log reduction, time required is **5D**.

3. **Activation volume units (–2 marks):** Pressure in Eyring equation must be in **Pa (SI)** to get $V_a$ in m³/mol. Convert to cm³/mol only at the end by multiplying by $10^6$.

4. **First-order assumption not stated (–1 mark):** When applying $\log_{10}(N/N_0) = -t/D$, **explicitly state** "assuming first-order kinetics."

5. **Energy units (–1 mark):** PEF energy is typically expressed in **J/kg** or **kJ/kg**, NOT J alone. Always divide by mass.

6. **Weibull vs first-order confusion:** If the question mentions "tail" or "shoulder" in the survivor curve, **do NOT use** $D = 2.303/k$ formula blindly. Use $\alpha$ and $\beta$ from Weibull model.

7. **Bipolar vs mono-polar PEF:** Examiners frequently ask why bipolar is preferred — answer must mention **electrolysis prevention** AND **enhanced microbial inactivation**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2: Process**

### Core Concepts

- Non-thermal processing (NTP) preserves food without high heat, targeting microbes and enzymes selectively.
- Major NTP technologies: **HPP, PEF, Ultrasound, UV/PL, Cold Plasma, Irradiation, DPCD, Ozone**.
- Module 2 emphasizes **process kinetics**, **equipment design**, and **microbial inactivation**.

### Kinetic Models (Most Important!)

- **First-order:** $\log_{10}(N/N_0) = -t/D$, where $D = 2.303/k$.
- **Weibull:** $\log_{10}(N/N_0) = -(t/\alpha)^{\beta}$.
- **Pressure-death (Eyring):** $\ln(N/N_0) = -V_a(P-P_c)/RT$.
- **Z-value (analogous):** $Z_P = \Delta P / \log(D_1/D_2)$.

### Energy Equations

- **PEF:** $W_{spec} = nV I \tau / m = nCV^2/(2m)$.
- **Ultrasound:** $I = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho c \omega^2 \xi^2$.
- **HPP:** $W = P \cdot \Delta V$; typically 400–800 kJ/kg.

### Typical Operating Conditions

- **HPP:** 400–600 MPa, 5–15 min, 20–25 °C.
- **PEF:** 20–35 kV/cm, 1–100 μs pulses, 5–50 pulses total.
- **Ultrasound:** 20–100 kHz, 100–600 W.
- **UV-C:** 254 nm, 100–400 J/m².
- **Cold Plasma:** 30–80 kV, 1–5 min exposure.

### KTU Exam Hot Topics

- Derivation of first-order kinetics from $dN/dt = -kN$.
- D-value and Z-value calculations.
- Activation volume from two-point pressure data.
- PEF specific energy calculations.
- Survivor curve interpretation (log-linear, tail, shoulder).
- Comparison between processes (energy efficiency, log reduction, applicability).

### Quick Numbers to Memorize

- $1 \text{ MPa} = 10^3 \text{ kPa} = 10^6 \text{ Pa}$.
- $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$.
- Speed of sound in water: **$c \approx 1480$ m/s**.
- Germicidal UV wavelength: **254 nm**.
- Standard reference temperature: **25 °C = 298 K**.
- Decimal reduction time → **D-value** for 90% kill.

### Application Triggers

- **Liquid clear** → PEF or UV.
- **Liquid particulate** → PEF.
- **Solid food** → HPP.
- **Surface decontamination** → Cold Plasma, Pulsed Light, UV.
- **Dry products/spices** → Gamma Irradiation.
- **Combined hurdle** → Ultrasound + heat, PEF + mild heat.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Strategy:** For a 14-mark question, always include: (1) definition, (2) principle with equation, (3) process parameters, (4) numerical solution with units, (5) advantages/limitations, (6) industrial example. This 6-part structure guarantees full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equipment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT631 - NON-THERMAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Non-Thermal Processing Equipment

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Non-Thermal Processing Equipment** refers to the specialized industrial machinery and apparatus designed to preserve, sterilize, pasteurize, or modify food products by applying physical, chemical, or electrical agents at ambient or sub-pasteurization temperatures, thereby retaining the native nutritional, sensory, and functional qualities of the food matrix.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, equipment classification is tied directly to the principle of microbial inactivation, enzyme suppression, or structural modification without phase change of water or bulk heating.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:**
> Equipment in Non-Thermal Processing is characterized by its **energy vector** — High Pressure (mechanical), Electric Field (electrical), Acoustic Wave (mechanical-oscillatory), Electromagnetic Radiation (photonic), or Plasma (ionized gas). Each vector demands a distinct engineering architecture.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a glass jar full of fresh orange juice. You want to kill the bacteria inside, but you also want to keep the vitamin C, color, and fresh taste intact.

- **Traditional thermal pasteurization** = boiling the juice → kills bacteria BUT destroys vitamin C and changes taste.
- **Non-thermal processing** = treating the juice like a surgeon treats a patient: targeted, precise, gentle, no collateral damage.

**Real-world analogy:**
| Method | Analogy | Equipment Type |
|---|---|---|
| High Pressure | Deep ocean pressure on a submarine | HPP Vessel |
| Pulsed Electric Field | Lightning zapping a swimming pool | PEF Chamber |
| Ultrasound | Musical vibrations shaking dirt off a ring | Ultrasonic Bath/Probe |
| UV Light | Sunlight killing germs on a surface | UV Tunnel |
| Cold Plasma | Tiny lightning bolts washing the surface | Plasma Reactor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Hook for Students:** Think of non-thermal equipment as *engineered precision tools*. Just like a surgeon's scalpel versus a hammer, each device delivers a specific form of energy to a specific target (microbe, enzyme, or cell wall) without heating the bulk food.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Non-Thermal Processing Equipment (Module 2 Scope)

The Module 2 syllabus broadly covers equipment under five pillars:

1. **High Pressure Processing (HPP) Equipment**
2. **Pulsed Electric Field (PEF) Equipment**
3. **Ultrasonic Processing Equipment**
4. **Cold Plasma Equipment**
5. **Ultraviolet (UV) / Irradiation Equipment**

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** **"H-P-U-C-I"** → **H**igh, **P**ressure, **U**ltrasound, **C**old plasma, **I**rradiation (and PEF — just remember the "P" twice).

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants are **fundamental** and appear frequently in KTU numerical problems:

- **Speed of sound in water at 25 °C:** $c = 1497 \text{ m/s}$
- **Density of water:** $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101{,}325 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.1 \text{ MPa}$
- **Dielectric breakdown strength of water:** $E_{bd} \approx 30 \text{ kV/cm}$
- **Velocity of light in vacuum:** $c_0 = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$

These are used for calculations related to pressure intensification, electric field uniformity, acoustic intensity, and UV flux.

---

## 1.5 Why Equipment Design Matters in Non-Thermal Processing

The **equipment is the heart** of the process. Unlike thermal processing where heat transfer is the bottleneck, non-thermal processing depends entirely on:

- **Uniform energy distribution** across the food mass
- **Controlled residence time** in the treatment zone
- **Energy efficiency** to keep operational costs viable
- **Scalability** from lab-scale to industrial throughput

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse the *process* (e.g., pasteurization) with the *equipment* (e.g., HPP vessel). In KTU 2024 exams, question framing in Module 2 is **equipment-centric**, not process-centric. Always describe the **machine**, its components, materials of construction, and operating parameters.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 High Pressure Processing (HPP) Equipment

### 2.1.1 Major Components

A typical industrial HPP system comprises:

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Pressure Vessel** | Holds the food product, withstands up to 600 MPa |
| **Pressure Intensifier** | Boosts pump output to ultra-high pressures |
| **High-Pressure Pump** | Initial pressurization (~30–100 MPa) |
| **Pressure Transmitter** | Real-time monitoring |
| **Yoke / Closure System** | Seals the vessel against pressure loss |
| **Temperature Control Jacket** | Maintains isothermal conditions |
| **Loading/Unloading System** | Automated basket transfer |

### 2.1.2 Operational Principle

The pressure vessel is filled with food (pre-packed in flexible containers) and a pressure-transmitting medium (usually water or a water–oil mix). The intensifier compresses the medium isostatically, transmitting pressure uniformly in all directions per **Pascal's Law**.

### 2.1.3 Critical Design Parameters

- **Working pressure:** 400–600 MPa (typical industrial)
- **Cycle time:** 3–8 minutes
- **Vessel aspect ratio (L/D):** 1:1 to 4:1
- **Material of construction:** High-tensile forged steel (e.g., **Monel**, **Inconel**, **17-4 PH stainless steel**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pascal's Law Applied:** Pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted equally in all directions. This is the **physical foundation** of HPP equipment design.

---

## 2.2 Pulsed Electric Field (PEF) Equipment

### 2.2.1 System Architecture

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **High-Voltage Pulse Generator** | Creates short (μs) high-voltage pulses |
| **Treatment Chamber** | Electrodes expose the food to the electric field |
| **Cooling System** | Removes ohmic heating waste |
| **Flow Control / Pumping** | Ensures uniform residence time |
| **Control Unit / PLC** | Monitors voltage, current, pulse shape |

### 2.2.2 Electrode Configurations

- **Parallel plate (static)**
- **Coaxial / tubular (continuous flow)**
- **Co-field (colinear flow)**
- **Cross-field (perpendicular flow)**

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU exams:** Draw the electrode cross-section clearly and label anode, cathode, gap distance, and field direction. Examiners award marks for diagrams with **labeled arrow flow**.

### 2.2.3 Operating Parameters

- **Electric field strength:** 10–50 kV/cm
- **Pulse duration:** 1–10 μs
- **Number of pulses:** 5–50 per cycle
- **Specific energy input:** 50–500 kJ/kg
- **Pulse shape:** Exponential decay or square wave

---

## 2.3 Ultrasonic Processing Equipment

### 2.3.1 Types

| Type | Frequency Range | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **High-Intensity Ultrasound** | 20–100 kHz | Microbial inactivation, homogenization |
| **Low-Intensity Ultrasound** | 100 kHz – 1 MHz | Non-destructive quality analysis |

### 2.3.2 Core Components

- **Ultrasonic transducer** (piezoelectric — converts electrical energy → mechanical vibration)
- **Sonicator bath / probe / flow cell**
- **Power supply / generator**
- **Cooling jacket**

### 2.3.3 Mechanism

Acoustic cavitation is the dominant inactivation mechanism:

> [!NOTE]
> **Cavitation** is the formation, growth, and violent collapse of micro-bubbles in a liquid subjected to ultrasonic waves. The collapse generates localized "hot spots" of ~5000 K and pressures of ~100 MPa, which mechanically disrupt microbial cell walls.

---

## 2.4 Cold Plasma Equipment

### 2.4.1 Configuration Types

- **Dielectric Barrier Discharge (DBD)** — atmospheric pressure, batch
- **Atmospheric Pressure Plasma Jet (APPJ)** — continuous surface treatment
- **Glow Discharge** — low-pressure, vacuum chamber
- **Corona Discharge** — point-to-plane electrodes

### 2.4.2 Working Gas

Common feed gases: **Argon, Helium, Air, Nitrogen, O₂, or mixtures.**

### 2.4.3 Operating Parameters

- **Voltage:** 1–100 kV
- **Frequency:** 50 Hz – 50 kHz
- **Power:** 10 W – 1 kW
- **Gas flow rate:** 0.1–10 L/min

---

## 2.5 UV / Irradiation Equipment

### 2.5.1 UV Equipment Components

- **Low-pressure mercury lamp** (254 nm, monochromatic)
- **Medium-pressure mercury lamp** (200–400 nm, polychromatic)
- **Excimer lamp** (222 nm, far UV)
- **Quartz tunnel / chamber**
- **Reflective inner walls (aluminum)**

### 2.5.2 Irradiation Source

- **Gamma rays:** Cobalt-60 (1.17 and 1.33 MeV) or Cesium-137
- **Electron beam:** ≤ 10 MeV (regulatory limit)
- **X-rays:** ≤ 5 MeV

> [!WARNING]
> **Regulatory Note:** International Codex Alimentarius mandates that food irradiation doses must not exceed **10 kGy** unless specifically approved.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Variables | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = F/A$ | P = Pressure, F = Force, A = Area | Pa | HPP vessel wall stress |
| 2 | $E = U/d$ | E = Electric field, U = Voltage, d = gap | V/m | PEF chamber design |
| 3 | $I = P/A_{acoustic}$ | I = Acoustic intensity, P = Power | W/m² | Ultrasonic power density |
| 4 | $E_{sp} = U \cdot I \cdot t / m$ | Specific energy input | J/kg | PEF dose calculation |
| 5 | $W_{vol} = \int E \cdot dV$ | Electric energy density | J/m³ | PEF energy balance |
| 6 | $\sigma_{hoop} = P \cdot r / t$ | Hoop stress (thin-wall vessel) | Pa | HPP vessel thickness |
| 7 | $\sigma_{long} = P \cdot r / 2t$ | Longitudinal stress | Pa | HPP closure design |
| 8 | $t_{min} = r \sqrt{SE/P} - 0.6P$ | ASME wall thickness | varies | Vessel design code |
| 9 | $R = P / \rho c$ | Acoustic impedance (specific) | Pa·s/m³ | Cavitation threshold |
| 10 | $\lambda = c / f$ | Acoustic wavelength | m | Ultrasonic probe sizing |
| 11 | $F = \text{dose} \times \dot{m}$ | UV dose rate calculation | J/s | UV system sizing |
| 12 | $\eta_{cav} = \dot{W}_{mech} / P_{elec}$ | Cavitation efficiency | dimensionless | Sonicator performance |
| 13 | $E_{yield} = m_{plasma}/m_{input}$ | Plasma reaction yield | dimensionless | Cold plasma efficiency |
| 14 | $D_{abs} = D_0 \cdot 10^{-A \cdot z}$ | UV penetration (Beer-Lambert) | J/m² | UV reactor design |
| 15 | $Q_{remove} = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$ | Heat removal from PEF chamber | W | PEF cooling system |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Formulas 6, 7, and 8 are **frequently tested in Part B (14 marks)** for HPP equipment design. Memorize the **ASME wall thickness equation** (Eq. 8) — it is a guaranteed KTU favorite.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Equipment | Industry Application |
|---|---|
| HPP | HPP guacamole, meat, seafood, juice (commercial: **Hiperbaric, JBT Avure**) |
| PEF | Apple juice pasteurization, potato chip pretreatment |
| Ultrasound | Dairy homogenization, extraction of bioactives |
| Cold Plasma | Fresh produce shelf-life extension, surface sterilization |
| UV/Irradiation | Spices decontamination, medical device sterilization |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: HPP Vessel Wall Thickness (ASME Code)

### Problem Statement
Design a high-pressure vessel to operate at 600 MPa with inner radius 75 mm and allowable stress 250 MPa using the ASME code.

### Given Data
- Internal pressure: $P = 600 \text{ MPa} = 600 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
- Inner radius: $r = 75 \text{ mm} = 0.075 \text{ m}$
- Allowable stress: $S = 250 \text{ MPa}$
- Joint efficiency: $E = 1.0$ (assumed fully radiographed)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The **ASME BPVC Section VIII Div 3 (Ultra-High Pressure Vessel)** formula for required wall thickness is:

$$t_{min} = r \left( \sqrt{\frac{S \cdot E + P}{S \cdot E - P}} \right)$$

#### Step 1: Substitute values into the formula

$$t_{min} = 0.075 \left( \sqrt{\frac{(250 \times 10^6)(1.0) + (600 \times 10^6)}{(250 \times 10^6)(1.0) - (600 \times 10^6)}} \right)$$

#### Step 2: Compute numerator

$$S \cdot E + P = 250 \times 10^6 + 600 \times 10^6 = 850 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

#### Step 3: Compute denominator

$$S \cdot E - P = 250 \times 10^6 - 600 \times 10^6 = -350 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Negative Denominator!** Since the denominator becomes negative, the **standard ASME Div 1 formula cannot be applied**. We must use the **specialized Div 3 ultra-high pressure code** or limit operating pressure to a value where $S \cdot E > P$.

#### Step 4: Re-design with realistic parameters

Let's redesign for $P = 200 \text{ MPa}$ with $S = 1500 \text{ MPa}$ (high-strength maraging steel):

$$t_{min} = 0.075 \left( \sqrt{\frac{(1500 \times 10^6)(1.0) + (200 \times 10^6)}{(1500 \times 10^6)(1.0) - (200 \times 10^6)}} \right)$$

#### Step 5: Numerator and Denominator

$$\text{Numerator} = 1700 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$
$$\text{Denominator} = 1300 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

#### Step 6: Ratio

$$\frac{1700 \times 10^6}{1300 \times 10^6} = 1.3077$$

#### Step 7: Square root

$$\sqrt{1.3077} = 1.1435$$

#### Step 8: Final wall thickness

$$t_{min} = 0.075 \times 1.1435 = 0.0858 \text{ m} = 85.8 \text{ mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Even with maraging steel, the wall thickness required for ultra-high pressure vessels is substantial. This is why HPP equipment is heavy and expensive.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: PEF Specific Energy Input

### Problem Statement
A PEF system delivers 25 kV across a 5 mm gap for 50 pulses of 5 μs duration to a flow of 500 L/h of orange juice. Calculate specific energy input. Assume medium conductivity $\sigma = 0.4 \text{ S/m}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The **specific energy input** for PEF is:

$$E_{sp} = \frac{U^2 \cdot \sigma \cdot t_{total}}{1}$$

Where $t_{total} = N \cdot \tau$ is the total pulse-on time.

#### Step 1: Electric field strength

$$E = \frac{U}{d} = \frac{25{,}000}{0.005} = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ V/m} = 50 \text{ kV/cm}$$

#### Step 2: Total pulse-on time

$$t_{total} = N \cdot \tau = 50 \times (5 \times 10^{-6}) = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}$$

#### Step 3: Energy per unit volume

$$W_{vol} = \sigma \cdot E^2 \cdot t_{total} = 0.4 \times (5 \times 10^6)^2 \times (2.5 \times 10^{-4})$$

#### Step 4: Square and multiply

$$W_{vol} = 0.4 \times 2.5 \times 10^{13} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4} = 0.4 \times 6.25 \times 10^{9}$$

#### Step 5: Final specific energy

$$W_{vol} = 2.5 \times 10^{9} \text{ J/m}^3 = 2.5 \times 10^{6} \text{ kJ/m}^3$$

#### Step 6: Convert to per-mass basis (assuming juice density 1050 kg/m³)

$$E_{sp} = \frac{W_{vol}}{\rho} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{9}}{1050} = 2.38 \times 10^6 \text{ J/kg} \approx 2380 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** This is extremely high. Real PEF systems operate at much lower specific energy (50–500 kJ/kg). Our example is over-spec, which would heat the juice due to ohmic heating — defeating the purpose. Engineers must optimize carefully.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Ultrasonic Cavitation Threshold

### Problem Statement
Determine the minimum acoustic intensity required for cavitation inception in water at 20 kHz, 25 °C. Use the Blake threshold pressure.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The **Blake Threshold Pressure** for cavitation inception is:

$$P_{B} = P_0 + P_{atm} \cdot \left( \frac{2}{3} \right) \left( \frac{R_0}{R_e} \right)^3$$

But a simpler form using cavitation number gives:

$$\sigma_{cav} = \frac{P_0 - P_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho v^2}$$

Cavitation occurs when $\sigma_{cav} < 1$.

#### Step 1: Define parameters

- $P_0 = 101{,}325 \text{ Pa}$ (hydrostatic)
- $P_v = 2300 \text{ Pa}$ (vapor pressure of water at 25 °C)
- $\rho = 997 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $f = 20 \text{ kHz}$

#### Step 2: Threshold acoustic pressure for cavitation

The acoustic pressure amplitude needed:

$$P_A = P_0 - P_v + \sqrt{\frac{16}{27} \cdot \sigma \cdot (P_0 - P_v + \frac{3}{2} \sigma)}$$

A simplified empirical result: $P_A \approx 1.4 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$ at 20 kHz.

#### Step 3: Convert to acoustic intensity

$$I = \frac{P_A^2}{2 \rho c}$$

#### Step 4: Substitute

$$I = \frac{(1.4 \times 10^5)^2}{2 \times 997 \times 1497} = \frac{1.96 \times 10^{10}}{2.985 \times 10^6}$$

#### Step 5: Final intensity

$$I = 6566 \text{ W/m}^2 \approx 6.6 \text{ kW/m}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical implication:** Industrial ultrasonic horns typically deliver 10–100 kW/m², well above the cavitation threshold — ensuring effective microbial disruption.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Equipment Comparison

| Parameter | HPP | PEF | Ultrasound | Cold Plasma | UV |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy form** | Mechanical | Electrical | Acoustic | Ionized gas | Photonic |
| **Field strength** | 400–600 MPa | 10–50 kV/cm | 10–100 kW/m² | 10–100 kV | 0.1–10 J/cm² |
| **Penetration depth** | Whole bulk | Whole bulk | Few cm | Surface (mm) | Surface (mm) |
| **Continuous?** | Batch dominant | Continuous | Both | Both | Continuous |
| **Main kill mechanism** | Membrane rupture | Electroporation | Cavitation shear | ROS attack | DNA damage |
| **Scalability** | Capital-intensive | Moderate | Moderate | Emerging | Easy |
| **Operating cost** | High | Moderate | Moderate | Low | Low |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: UV Dose (Beer-Lambert Law)

### Problem Statement
A UV reactor processes milk at 5 L/min. The incident UV intensity at the surface is 40 W/m² at 254 nm. Estimate the dose received by a particle traveling 30 cm through the fluid. Use absorption coefficient $\alpha = 300 \text{ m}^{-1}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

#### Step 1: UV intensity at depth z

$$I(z) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha z}$$

#### Step 2: Substitute

$$I(z) = 40 \cdot e^{-(300)(0.30)} = 40 \cdot e^{-90}$$

#### Step 3: Evaluate exponential

$$e^{-90} \approx 8.2 \times 10^{-40}$$

#### Step 4: Conclusion

$$I(z) \approx 40 \times 8.2 \times 10^{-40} \approx 3.3 \times 10^{-38} \text{ W/m}^2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Conclusion:** UV is essentially **surface-only** in opaque food matrices. It cannot effectively process turbid fluids like milk in bulk — this is why UV is used mostly for transparent liquids and surface decontamination.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: HPP Equipment Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Food Basket Loading System] --> nodeB[High Pressure Vessel]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Yoke and Closure Lock]

**Block-Level Functional Flow:**
- Food in flexible packaging enters the basket (nodeA).
- Basket slides into the pressure vessel (nodeB).
- Yoke closure (nodeC) seals the vessel.
- Two-stage intensifier (nodeD, nodeE) builds pressure to 600 MPa.
- Pressure transmitter (nodeF) provides feedback to PLC (nodeH).
- Jacket (nodeG) maintains isothermal conditions.
- After hold time, decompression (nodeI) releases pressure slowly.
- Treated product discharges (nodeJ) for downstream packaging.

```

**Block-Level Functional Flow:**
- High-voltage DC supply (nodeA) charges the pulse network (nodeB).
- Switch (nodeC) releases energy into chamber (nodeD) as μs pulses.
- Flow pump (nodeF) circulates the food through the chamber continuously.
- Cooling (nodeE) removes ohmic heating waste.
- Sensors (nodeH) feed data to PLC (nodeG).

- Mains power (nodeA) feeds the ultrasonic generator (nodeB) at 20 kHz.
- PZT transducer (nodeC) converts electrical energy to mechanical vibration.
- Horn (nodeD) amplifies amplitude.
- Sonotrode (nodeE) transmits vibration into the food (nodeF).
- Cooling (nodeG) prevents thermal buildup.

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🔹 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the working of a high-pressure intensifier used in HPP equipment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A high-pressure intensifier is a device used in HPP systems to boost the pressure of a low-pressure fluid supplied by a primary pump up to ultra-high pressures (up to 600 MPa).

It works on the principle of **hydraulic pressure multiplication** using pistons of different cross-sectional areas. A low-pressure piston (large area $A_1$) is connected mechanically to a high-pressure piston (small area $A_2$). By Pascal's law:

$$P_1 \cdot A_1 = P_2 \cdot A_2$$

Therefore:

$$P_2 = P_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2}$$

If the area ratio $A_1/A_2 = 10$, then 30 MPa input becomes 300 MPa output. This enables the HPP system to reach operating pressures that primary pumps cannot achieve directly.

> (1 mark for principle, 1 mark for area ratio, 1 mark for numerical example)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**List the major components of a Pulsed Electric Field (PEF) system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **High-voltage pulse generator** — produces short-duration, high-voltage pulses.
2. **Treatment chamber with electrodes** — exposes the food to the electric field.
3. **Cooling system / heat exchanger** — removes ohmic heating.
4. **Flow pump / reservoir** — ensures continuous flow through the chamber.
5. **Control unit (PLC) and sensors** — monitors voltage, current, and process parameters.

(½ mark per component × 6 components = 3 marks)

---

## 🔹 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU Internal Choice Format — Answer ANY ONE]**

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the **major components and working of a High Pressure Processing (HPP) unit**. Discuss the **materials of construction** used for the pressure vessel and justify why each is selected. (7 marks)

**(b)** An HPP system operates at a working pressure of **500 MPa** with a vessel inner radius of **100 mm**. The allowable stress of the vessel material (maraging steel) is **1800 MPa** with a joint efficiency of **1.0**. Calculate the **minimum wall thickness** required using the ASME BPVC code. State your assumptions clearly. (7 marks)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Schematic (3 marks):**
Draw and label:
- Pressure vessel with hemispherical closure
- Yoke and closure mechanism
- Two-stage intensifier
- Pressure transmitter
- Temperature control jacket
- Loading/unloading basket
- Hydraulic power unit
- PLC control panel

**Components and Working (3 marks):**
1. **Pressure vessel:** Cylindrical forged-steel chamber rated for up to 600 MPa. Holds food in flexible pouches immersed in pressure-transmitting medium (water).
2. **Yoke and closure:** Auto-fastening system that seals the vessel against internal pressure using pre-stressed tie-rods.
3. **Intensifier:** Boosts pump output from ~50 MPa to 500+ MPa using piston area multiplication.
4. **Pressure transmitter:** Strain-gauge sensor provides real-time pressure feedback.
5. **Temperature jacket:** Surrounds the vessel to maintain isothermal conditions via circulating coolant/heating fluid.
6. **Control system:** PLC monitors pressure, temperature, hold time, and decompression.

**Materials of Construction (1 mark):**
- **Maraging steel (18Ni-300)** — excellent tensile strength (1800–2000 MPa) and toughness.
- **Inconel 718** — for high-temperature regions; corrosion-resistant.
- **17-4 PH stainless steel** — used in seals and fasteners.

**Justification:** These materials are chosen for their **high yield strength, fracture toughness, fatigue resistance, and corrosion resistance**, all critical for repeated ultra-high pressure cycles.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $P = 500 \text{ MPa}$
- $r = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
- $S = 1800 \text{ MPa}$
- $E = 1.0$

**ASME Formula (for ultra-high pressure vessels):**

$$t_{min} = r \left( \sqrt{\frac{SE + P}{SE - P}} \right)$$

**Step 1:** Numerator

$$SE + P = (1800)(1.0) + 500 = 2300 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2:** Denominator

$$SE - P = (1800)(1.0) - 500 = 1300 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3:** Ratio

$$\frac{2300}{1300} = 1.7692$$

**Step 4:** Square root

$$\sqrt{1.7692} = 1.3301$$

**Step 5:** Wall thickness

$$t_{min} = 0.1 \times 1.3301 = 0.1330 \text{ m} = 133 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answer:** The minimum wall thickness is **≈ 133 mm** (or 13.3 cm).

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumptions stated:** (i) Thin-wall approximation does not apply at this pressure; we use the **ultra-high pressure** ASME Div 3 formula. (ii) The vessel is single-forged without welds (joint efficiency = 1.0). (iii) Hoop stress is the controlling failure mode.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Describe the **types, construction, and working of ultrasonic processors** used in non-thermal food processing. Differentiate between **probe-type and bath-type** sonicators. (7 marks)

**(b)** A continuous-flow ultrasonic reactor delivers **600 W** of acoustic power to **2 L/min** of fruit juice (density 1050 kg/m³). The treatment chamber has a **cross-sectional area of 25 cm²**. Calculate:
   1. **Acoustic intensity** in the chamber (W/m²)
   2. **Specific energy input** (kJ/kg)
   3. **Residence time** if the chamber length is 30 cm (7 marks)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Ultrasonic Processor Types (2 marks):**
1. **Probe / horn-type sonicator** — direct immersion of metal probe; high intensity (up to 100 kW/m²).
2. **Bath / tank sonicator** — multiple transducers fixed to tank wall; lower intensity (1–5 kW/m²).
3. **Flow-through / continuous sonicator** — product flows through a chamber with transducers inline; suitable for industrial throughput.

**Construction (2 marks):**
- **Transducers:** Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT) piezoelectric crystals convert 20 kHz AC into mechanical vibration.
- **Booster and horn:** Titanium or aluminum alloy amplifies amplitude from 10 μm to 50–100 μm.
- **Vessel:** Stainless steel with cooling jacket.
- **Generator:** Adjustable power supply with frequency locking.

**Working (1 mark):**
Ultrasonic waves create **acoustic cavitation** — micro-bubbles form and collapse violently, generating localized extreme conditions (5000 K, 100 MPa) that mechanically disrupt microbial cells.

**Probe vs Bath Comparison (2 marks):**

| Feature | Probe Sonicator | Bath Sonicator |
|---|---|---|
| Intensity | High (10–100 kW/m²) | Low (1–5 kW/m²) |
| Volume | Small (mL) | Large (L) |
| Application | Lab scale, localized | Batch, bulk |
| Cavitation control | Better | Limited |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Power: $P = 600 \text{ W}$
- Flow rate: $\dot{V} = 2 \text{ L/min} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 3.33 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- Cross-section: $A = 25 \text{ cm}^2 = 25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$
- Density: $\rho = 1050 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Length: $L = 0.30 \text{ m}$

**1. Acoustic intensity (2 marks):**

$$I = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{600}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 240{,}000 \text{ W/m}^2 = 240 \text{ kW/m}^2$$

**2. Specific energy input (3 marks):**

First, find mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot \dot{V} = 1050 \times 3.33 \times 10^{-5} = 0.035 \text{ kg/s}$$

Then specific energy:

$$E_{sp} = \frac{P}{\dot{m}} = \frac{600}{0.035} = 17{,}143 \text{ J/kg} \approx 17.14 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**3. Residence time (2 marks):**

Volume of chamber:

$$V_{chamber} = A \cdot L = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.30 = 7.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

Residence time:

$$\tau = \frac{V_{chamber}}{\dot{V}} = \frac{7.5 \times 10^{-4}}{3.33 \times 10^{-5}} = 22.5 \text{ s}$$

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in "Equipment" Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing vessel with chamber:** HPP vessel ≠ PEF chamber. Be precise about which equipment you are describing.
> 2. **Forgetting units:** Always write **MPa, kV/cm, kHz, W/m²** with explicit units. Marks are lost silently when units are missing.
> 3. **Missing material justification:** In HPP questions, merely naming the material (e.g., "maraging steel") without justifying *why* (yield strength, fatigue, corrosion) costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping Pascal's law:** Any HPP answer without mentioning Pascal's law or isostatic pressure transmission will lose at least 1 mark.
> 5. **Formula mistakes in Eq. 8:** Students often write the **thin-wall formula** $\sigma = Pr/t$ for ultra-high pressures. Use the **ASME Div 3 formula** instead.
> 6. **Not drawing diagrams:** A KTU 14-mark answer without a clear schematic loses 2–3 marks outright.
> 7. **Ohmic heating ignored in PEF:** Always mention cooling — examiners expect the trade-off discussion.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Equipment — Module 2**

- ☐ **HPP** uses isostatic pressure (Pascal's law) — uniform in all directions.
- ☐ Industrial HPP works at **400–600 MPa**.
- ☐ **ASME BPVC Section VIII Div 3** governs ultra-high pressure vessel design.
- ☐ **Intensifier** multiplies pressure using piston area ratios: $P_2 = P_1 \cdot (A_1/A_2)$.
- ☐ **PEF** uses electric field strength $E = U/d$ — typically **10–50 kV/cm**.
- ☐ PEF critical components: pulse generator, chamber, electrodes, cooling, PLC.
- ☐ **Ultrasound** relies on **acoustic cavitation** for microbial inactivation.
- ☐ Cavitation threshold intensity ≈ **6–10 kW/m²** at 20 kHz.
- ☐ Acoustic intensity: $I = P/A$; specific energy: $E_{sp} = P/\dot{m}$.
- ☐ **Cold plasma** generates reactive oxygen and nitrogen species (ROS/RNS).
- ☐ DBD plasma uses a **dielectric barrier** (quartz) to prevent arc formation.
- ☐ **UV** is **surface-limited**; uses Beer-Lambert: $I(z) = I_0 e^{-\alpha z}$.
- ☐ **Food irradiation dose** limit = **10 kGy** (Codex Alimentarius).
- ☐ **Materials of construction:** Maraging steel, Inconel, 17-4 PH SS, titanium horns.
- ☐ **Continuous flow systems** are preferred for industrial scale-up (PEF, UV, flow-sonication).
- ☐ **Batch dominant** systems: HPP, DBD plasma.
- ☐ **KTU favorite formulas:** $t_{min} = r\sqrt{(SE+P)/(SE-P)}$, $E = U/d$, $I = P/A$, $E_{sp} = P/\dot{m}$.

---

topics.title: Principle of Microbial Inactivation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT631 - NON-THERMAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principle of Microbial Inactivation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Microbial Inactivation (KTU 2024 Definition):** Microbial inactivation is the irreversible loss of the ability of a microorganism to multiply and produce visible colonies on a suitable growth medium under defined cultural conditions, as a consequence of damage to one or more critical cellular targets — namely the **cell envelope (wall + membrane)**, the **genomic DNA**, the **ribosomal/enzymatic machinery**, or the **intracellular homeostasis apparatus** — induced by a physical, chemical, or electrical stress.

In the context of **Non-Thermal Processing (NTP)**, microbial inactivation is achieved **without the use of lethal heat**. Instead, the killing/injury is brought about by applying targeted stresses (high pressure, electric fields, ultrasound, UV light, plasma, etc.) that physically or chemically disrupt the structural and functional integrity of microbial cells while leaving the food matrix's sensory and nutritional quality relatively unaltered.

> [!NOTE]
> **Inactivation ≠ Sterilization.** Inactivation is a kinetic, probabilistic event; sterilization is an absolute condition (zero survivors). In KTU problems, "5-log reduction" means 99.999% inactivation, not sterility.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Three Locks of the Castle"

Imagine a microbial cell as a **medieval castle** protecting a precious scroll (the DNA):

| Castle Element | Microbial Equivalent | NTP Weapon that Breaks It |
|---|---|---|
| Outer stone wall | **Cell wall** (peptidoglycan) | High Pressure (HP), ultrasound |
| Inner wooden door | **Cell membrane** (lipid bilayer) | Pulsed Electric Field (PEF), HP |
| Inside guards & scribes | **Enzymes, ribosomes, DNA** | UV, cold plasma, PEF |

A **non-thermal process** is a clever siege engineer. Instead of burning the castle (thermal processing), he attacks specific weak points: he shakes the walls (HP), electrocutes the guards (PEF), or shines a blinding light that scrambles the scroll (UV). The castle falls, but the surrounding village (the food) is barely touched.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics of Microbial Inactivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Golden Numbers of Inactivation Kinetics (must be memorized):**
> - **D-value (Decimal Reduction Time):** Time (or dose) required to achieve a **1-log (90%) reduction** in viable count.
> - **z-value:** The change in process intensity required to cause a **10-fold change in D-value**.
> - **F-value:** Equivalent time at a reference temperature for a specified log reduction.

For non-thermal processes, the analogous parameters are:
- **D<sub>P</sub>**: Pressure for 1-log reduction
- **D<sub>E</sub>**: Electric field strength × time for 1-log reduction
- **D<sub>UV</sub>**: UV dose (mJ/cm²) for 1-log reduction

The **target microbes** in NTP studies are classically:
1. **Vegetative bacteria** — *E. coli*, *Listeria monocytogenes*, *Salmonella* spp.
2. **Yeasts and molds** — *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*
3. **Bacterial spores** — *Bacillus subtilis*, *Clostridium botulinum* (most resistant).
4. **Viruses** — bacteriophages, hepatitis A.
5. **Protozoan parasites** — *Cryptosporidium parvum*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Resistance Order (low → high):** Gram-negative bacteria < Gram-positive bacteria < Yeasts < Molds < Bacterial spores. This hierarchy appears in almost every KTU question on NTP.

## 1.4 Why Non-Thermal Inactivation Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Traditional thermal pasteurization/sterilization destroys nutrients (vitamins, polyphenols), alters flavor, and degrades heat-sensitive bioactives. **Non-thermal inactivation selectively targets microbial vulnerabilities** without the bulk thermal effect, enabling:
> - **Fresh-like** quality retention (cold-chain extension).
> - **Clean-label** preservation (no chemical preservatives).
> - **Energy efficiency** in selected processes.
> - **Functional food** manufacture (probiotic viability).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Cellular Targets of Inactivation

Non-thermal stresses can be classified by the cellular component they attack:

### Target 1 — Cell Wall
- **Structure:** Rigid, porous (peptidoglycan in bacteria; chitin/β-glucan in fungi).
- **Why attacked?** Compressive stresses (HP) and mechanical vibrations (ultrasound) physically collapse the wall's three-dimensional mesh.
- **Consequence:** Loss of shape; in Gram-negatives, loss of outer membrane → leakage.

### Target 2 — Cytoplasmic Membrane
- **Structure:** Phospholipid bilayer with embedded proteins; fluid mosaic.
- **Why attacked?** Electric fields (PEF) cause **electroporation** — formation of hydrophilic pores in the lipid bilayer.
- **Consequence:** Loss of proton motive force (ΔpH, ΔΨ), uncontrolled ion flux, collapse of **ATP synthesis**, leakage of intracellular ATP and metabolites.

### Target 3 — DNA / Genetic Material
- **Structure:** Double helix, supercoiled in cells.
- **Why attacked?** UV-C (254 nm) creates **cyclobutane pyrimidine dimers (CPDs)** and **6-4 photoproducts**; plasma generates reactive species that oxidize bases.
- **Consequence:** Replication failure, mutagenesis, transcription arrest → cell death after attempted division.

### Target 4 — Proteins / Ribosomes / Intracellular Enzymes
- **Why attacked?** HP denatures oligomeric proteins by altering hydrophobic and electrostatic interactions; plasma's ROS oxidize –SH groups.
- **Consequence:** Loss of enzymatic activity, ribosome dysfunction, metabolic collapse.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Whenever a question asks *"Explain the principle of microbial inactivation by PEF"*, your answer MUST identify **electroporation of the cytoplasmic membrane** as the primary mechanism — not "killing of microbes".

## 2.2 The Three Major Mechanisms of Inactivation

| Mechanism | Physical Basis | Net Effect on Cell | Classic NTP |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical disruption** | Compression, shear, cavitation | Wall/membrane rupture, cytoplasm leakage | HP, US, MS |
| **Electroporation** | Transmembrane potential > ~1 V | Pore formation in lipid bilayer | PEF, OPEF |
| **Photochemical / oxidative** | Photon absorption, ROS | DNA/Protein damage | UV, CP |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following table. It is the most-tested mathematical content in this module.

| Symbol | Name | Equation | Unit | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $N_0$ | Initial count | — | CFU/mL | All |
| $N_t$ | Survivors at time $t$ | — | CFU/mL | All |
| $\log(N_0/N_t)$ | Log reduction | — | log (dimensionless) | All |
| $D$ | Decimal reduction value | $D = t / \log_{10}(N_0/N_t)$ | min or s | Heat, HP, PEF |
| $k$ | First-order rate constant | $k = 2.303/D$ | min⁻¹ | All |
| $z$ | z-value (thermal analog) | $z = (T_2 - T_1) / \log_{10}(D_1/D_2)$ | °C or MPa | Heat, HP |
| $F_T^z$ | Equivalent process value | $F = t \cdot 10^{(T_{ref} - T)/z}$ | min | Heat |
| $P_T$ | Thermal death time | $P_T = D \cdot \log_{10}(N_0/N_t)$ | min | Heat |
| $E$ | Electric field strength | $E = V/d$ | kV/cm | PEF |
| $Q$ | UV dose | $Q = I \cdot t$ | mJ/cm² | UV |
| $\alpha$ | Weibull shape parameter | — | dimensionless | US, PEF |
| $\beta$ | Weibull scale parameter | — | s or MPa | US, PEF |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Markdown pipe rule:** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values inside tables. Never use `|x|`.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **High Pressure Processing (HPP) — 400–600 MPa:** Commercially used for guacamole, sliced ham, juices, oyster shelf-life extension. Achieves **>5-log reduction** of *Listeria* and *Salmonella*.
2. **Pulsed Electric Field (PEF) — 20–80 kV/cm:** Used for fruit juices (e.g., Genesis-style products), milk, liquid eggs. Targets membrane electroporation.
3. **UV-C Processing — 254 nm, 5–50 mJ/cm²:** Water disinfection, juice treatment, surface decontamination.
4. **Cold Plasma — atmospheric dielectric barrier discharge:** Surface decontamination of fruits, meat, packaging films.
5. **Ultrasound — 20–100 kHz:** Cavitation-induced wall disruption, often combined with mild heat (thermosonication) or pressure (manosonication).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — First-Order Log-Linear Inactivation Kinetics

The **log-linear (Bigelow) model** is the foundational kinetic equation. We derive it from the assumption that the rate of microbial inactivation is proportional to the number of viable cells present.

### Step 1 — Write the rate equation

The death rate is proportional to the surviving population:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -k \cdot N$$

where $k$ is the first-order inactivation rate constant (units: time⁻¹).

### Step 2 — Separate variables and integrate

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dN}{N} &= -k \cdot dt \\
\int_{N_0}^{N_t} \frac{dN}{N} &= -k \int_{0}^{t} dt \\
\ln(N_t) - \ln(N_0) &= -k \cdot t \\
\ln\left(\frac{N_t}{N_0}\right) &= -k \cdot t
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert natural log to base-10 log

Since $ \ln(x) = 2.303 \cdot \log_{10}(x) $:

$$2.303 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{N_t}{N_0}\right) = -k \cdot t$$

### Step 4 — Define D-value

By definition, $D$ is the time required for a 10-fold reduction, i.e., when $N_t = N_0/10$, so $\log_{10}(N_t/N_0) = -1$:

$$2.303 \cdot (-1) = -k \cdot D \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; k = \frac{2.303}{D}$$

### Step 5 — Final log-linear equation

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{N_t}{N_0}\right) = -\frac{t}{D}$$

or equivalently:

$$N_t = N_0 \cdot 10^{-t/D}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is THE most-asked equation in NTP KTU papers.** When you plot $\log(N_t)$ vs $t$, the slope is $-1/D$ and the y-intercept is $\log(N_0)$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The D-value and Process Time Formula

If a process is designed to deliver a target log reduction $n = \log_{10}(N_0/N_f)$ at a given treatment intensity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}\left(\frac{N_f}{N_0}\right) &= -\frac{t}{D} \\
-n &= -\frac{t}{D} \\
\boxed{\,t = n \cdot D\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

> A milk sample contains $N_0 = 1 \times 10^{6}$ CFU/mL of *Listeria innocua*. The D-value of the organism in the non-thermal process is $D = 0.45$ s. What is the minimum treatment time to achieve a **5-log reduction**?

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= 5 \text{ log} \\
D &= 0.45 \text{ s} \\
t &= n \cdot D = 5 \times 0.45 \\
t &= 2.25 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

The required minimum treatment time is **2.25 s**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Temperature/Pressure Dependence (z-value concept)

The D-value depends on process intensity $X$ (temperature in °C, or pressure in MPa). Empirical observation: $\log(D)$ vs $X$ is linear.

### Step 1 — Empirical linear relationship

$$\log_{10}(D) = \log_{10}(D_{ref}) - \frac{X - X_{ref}}{z}$$

### Step 2 — Solve for D at any X

$$D = D_{ref} \cdot 10^{-(X - X_{ref})/z}$$

### Step 3 — Ratio form (often used)

$$\frac{D_1}{D_2} = 10^{(X_2 - X_1)/z}$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

> At 400 MPa, the D-value of *E. coli* is 2.0 min. At 500 MPa, it is 0.25 min. Calculate $z_P$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right) &= \frac{X_2 - X_1}{z} \\
\log_{10}\left(\frac{2.0}{0.25}\right) &= \frac{500 - 400}{z} \\
\log_{10}(8) &= \frac{100}{z} \\
0.9031 &= \frac{100}{z} \\
z &= \frac{100}{0.9031} \\
z &\approx 110.7 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

The pressure $z$-value is approximately **111 MPa**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Weibull Model (for non-linear survival curves)

> [!NOTE]
> Many NTP curves show **tailing** (downward concavity) or **shoulders**. The log-linear model fails. The **Weibull distribution** fits such curves.

### Step 1 — Cumulative form

The Weibull survival function is:

$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{N_t}{N_0}\right) = -\left(\frac{t}{\beta}\right)^{\alpha}$$

where:
- $\beta$ = scale parameter (time for first log reduction, dimensionally similar to $D$).
- $\alpha$ = shape parameter (dimensionless):
  - $\alpha = 1$ → log-linear.
  - $\alpha > 1$ → concave (tailing upward) → resistant subpopulation.
  - $\alpha < 1$ → convex (shoulder) → cumulative damage.

### Step 2 — Linearize for graphical estimation

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\log_{10}\left(\frac{N_t}{N_0}\right) &= \left(\frac{t}{\beta}\right)^{\alpha} \\
\ln\!\left[-\log_{10}\left(\frac{N_t}{N_0}\right)\right] &= \alpha \cdot \ln(t) - \alpha \cdot \ln(\beta)
\end{aligned}
$$

A plot of $\ln[-\log(N_t/N_0)]$ vs $\ln(t)$ gives slope $\alpha$ and intercept $-\alpha \ln(\beta)$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Kinetic Models

| Model | Equation | Parameters | Best Fits | KTU Tip |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Log-linear (Bigelow)** | $\log(N_t/N_0) = -t/D$ | $D$ | Pure bacterial cultures, mid-range | Default model |
| **Weibull** | $\log(N_t/N_0) = -(t/\beta)^{\alpha}$ | $\alpha, \beta$ | NTP with tailing/shoulder | Most used in PEF, US |
| **Biphasic** | $N_t = (1-f)N_0 e^{-k_1 t} + f N_0 e^{-k_2 t}$ | $f, k_1, k_2$ | Mixed populations | Two sub-populations |
| **Log-linear + tail** | $\log(N_t/N_0) = \log(N_{tail}) - t/D$ | $D, N_{tail}$ | Spore inactivation | Asymptotic residual |
| **Peleg (Fermi)** | $N_t/N_0 = 1 / (1 + e^{(t-t_c)/a})$ | $t_c, a$ | Shoulders | Inverted S-shape |

---

## 3.6 Derivation 5 — Sub-lethal Injury Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A population exposed to NTP is not just **dead or alive** — there is a third class: **sub-lethally injured (SLI)** cells. These cells:
> - Cannot grow on selective media (e.g., agar with bile salts or NaCl).
> - **Can** recover and grow on non-selective media.
> - Pose a **food-safety risk** during storage.

### Quantitative expression

If $N_T$ is the total count on non-selective agar and $N_S$ is the count on selective agar, the injured fraction $I$ is:

$$I = \frac{N_T - N_S}{N_T}$$

The **sub-lethal injury ratio (SLIR)** is:

$$\text{SLIR} = 1 - \frac{N_S}{N_T} = \frac{N_T - N_S}{N_T}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Trap:** A common error is to assume that the survivor count on selective media equals the true viable population. Always specify whether the count was on **non-selective TSA (Total)** or **selective TSA + bile salts / NaCl (Stressed)**.

---

## 3.7 Spore Inactivation — Special Case

Bacterial spores (e.g., *B. subtilis*, *C. botulinum*) are the **most resistant** to NTP. They are dormant, dehydrated, with:
- Thick proteinaceous **coat**.
- **Cortex** of peptidoglycan.
- **Core** with dipicolinic acid (DPA) and Ca²⁺-DPA complex.

HPP in the 400–600 MPa range alone often **fails** to inactivate spores; the Hurdle concept is used (HP + mild heat, 60–80 °C, with or without nisin). Spore inactivation often follows:

$$\log(N_t/N_0) = -k_{spore} \cdot t^{n_{spore}}$$

where $n_{spore} > 1$ indicates a marked lag before spore germination/damage.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Microbial Cell — Targets of Inactivation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Microbial Cell"] --> B["Cell Wall: Peptidoglycan / Chitin"]
    A --> C["Cell Membrane: Lipid Bilayer"]
    A --> D["Cytoplasm: Proteins + Enzymes"]
    A --> E["Ribosomes: rRNA + Proteins"]
    A --> F["Genomic DNA: Double Helix"]
    A --> G["Spore Coat: Keratin-like"]

    B --> B1["HP / Ultrasound attack"]
    C --> C1["PEF / HP / Plasma attack"]
    D --> D1["HP / Plasma ROS attack"]
    E --> E1["Plasma / UV attack"]
    F --> F1["UV / Plasma attack"]
    G --> G1["Hurdle HP + heat attack"]

    B1 --> R["Cell Death / Sub-lethal Injury"]
    C1 --> R
    D1 --> R
    E1 --> R
    F1 --> R
    G1 --> R
```

## 4.2 Mechanism Flow — From Stress to Inactivation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Applied Stress: HP / PEF / UV / Plasma / US"] --> S2["Primary Physical Effect: Compression / Field / Photon / ROS / Cavitation"]
    S2 --> S3["Cellular Target Hit: Wall / Membrane / DNA / Protein"]
    S3 --> S4["Cellular Dysfunction: Leakage / Electroporation / Dimerization / Oxidation"]
    S4 --> S5["Loss of Cultivability: Inactivation"]
    S4 --> S6["Sub-lethal Injury: Repairable Damage"]
    S5 --> X["Microbial Inactivation Achieved"]
    S6 --> Y["Storage Risk if Repair Occurs"]
    Y --> X
```

## 4.3 Kinetic Model Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1["Observed survival curve shape?"] --> Q2{"Linear on semilog plot?"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> M1["Use Log-linear: log N vs t, slope = -1/D"]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{"Tailing or shoulder?"}
    Q3 -- Tailing --> M2["Use Weibull: alpha less than 1 or biphasic"]
    Q3 -- Shoulder --> M3["Use Weibull: alpha greater than 1 or Peleg"]
    Q4{"Mixed population?"} -- Yes --> M4["Biphasic model with fraction f"]
    Q4 -- No --> M5["Single-population Weibull"]
    M1 --> OUT["Report D-value, k"]
    M2 --> OUT2["Report alpha and beta"]
    M3 --> OUT3["Report t_c and a"]
    M4 --> OUT4["Report f, k1, k2"]
    M5 --> OUT2
```

## 4.4 Process Intensity–Log Reduction Mapping (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1["Process Inputs"] --> P2["Pulsed Electric Field: 20 to 80 kV/cm"]
    P1 --> P3["High Pressure: 300 to 600 MPa"]
    P1 --> P4["UV-C Dose: 5 to 50 mJ per cm2"]
    P1 --> P5["Ultrasound: 20 to 100 kHz"]
    P1 --> P6["Cold Plasma: 30 to 80 kV"]

    P2 --> T1["Target: Membrane Electroporation"]
    P3 --> T2["Target: Wall/Membrane Compression"]
    P4 --> T3["Target: DNA Pyrimidine Dimers"]
    P5 --> T4["Target: Cavitation Wall Rupture"]
    P6 --> T5["Target: ROS Oxidation"]

    T1 --> LR1["Log Reduction: 4 to 6 log CFU per mL"]
    T2 --> LR2["Log Reduction: 5 to 8 log CFU per mL"]
    T3 --> LR3["Log Reduction: 3 to 5 log CFU per mL"]
    T4 --> LR4["Log Reduction: 2 to 4 log CFU per mL"]
    T5 --> LR5["Log Reduction: 3 to 6 log CFU per mL"]
```
