\log_{10}\!\left( \dfrac{10^6}{10^1} \right) = \dfrac{t}{1.8 \text{ min}}
$$

$$
\log_{10}(10^5) = \dfrac{t}{1.8 \text{ min}}
$$

Since $\log_{10}(10^5) = 5$:

$$
5 = \dfrac{t}{1.8}
$$

$$
t = 5 \times 1.8 = 9.0 \text{ min}
$$

**Answer:** A treatment time of **9.0 minutes** at 550 MPa is required.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Matching NTP Technology to Food Application

This is the **highest-yield analytical framework** for KTU Part-B (14-mark) application questions.

| Food Matrix | pH / a<sub>w</sub> | Target Microbe | Recommended NTP | Reason |
|------------|--------------------|----------------|-----------------|--------|
| Apple juice | pH 3.5, a<sub>w</sub> 0.97 | *E. coli*, *Saccharomyces* | **PEF** (30 kV/cm) | Clear, low-pulp → PEF efficient; acid matrix prevents spore outgrowth |
| Raw milk | pH 6.7, a<sub>w</sub> 0.99 | *Listeria*, *Salmonella* | **HPP** (600 MPa, 5 min) | High-pressure kills vegetative cells; retains immunoglobulins |
| Avocado paste | pH 5.5, a<sub>w</sub> 0.98 | *Salmonella*, molds | **HPP** (450 MPa, 3 min) | In-pack sterilization; preserves green color & healthy oils |
| Fresh spinach | pH 6.5, a<sub>w</sub> 0.98 | *E. coli O157:H7* | **UV-C** (60 mJ/cm²) **+ Cold Plasma** | Surface decontamination with minimal quality loss |
| RTE sliced ham | pH 6.0, a<sub>w</sub> 0.96 | *Listeria monocytogenes* | **HPP** (600 MPa, 3 min) | Post-slicing in-pack sterilization |
| Beer | pH 4.3, a<sub>w</sub> 0.99 | *Saccharomyces*, *Lactobacillus* | **PEF** (20 kV/cm) or **MF membrane** | Cold pasteurization preserving hop aroma |
| Apple slices (fresh-cut) | pH 3.5 | Spoilage molds | **Ozone** (2 ppm, 5 min) | Storage atmosphere; extends shelf-life by 200% |
| Coconut water | pH 5.0, a<sub>w</sub> 0.99 | *Alicyclobacillus* | **UV-C** (40 mJ/cm²) or **HPP** | Clear liquid → UV effective; HPP for spore control |
| Olive paste | pH 5.0 | Endogenous enzymes | **PEF** (5 kV/cm) | Pre-treatment to enhance oil extraction yield by 8–15% |

### Why This Matrix Matters

For a **14-mark question** asking *"Recommend an NTP technology for X with justification"*, examiners award:
- **2 marks** — Correct technology identified.
- **4 marks** — Justification based on matrix parameters (pH, a<sub>w</sub>, target microbe).
- **4 marks** — Process parameters (pressure, time, field strength).
- **2 marks** — Quality/nutrition retention discussion.
- **2 marks** — Regulatory/industrial context.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: Industrial HPP Application Flow

This diagram maps how a commercial HPP unit is integrated into a beverage production line — a **frequently tested concept** in KTU Part B.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawA[Raw Material Receiving] --> prepA[Formulation & Mixing]
    prepA --> filA[Filtration / Standardization]
    filA --> degasA[Degassing Vessel]
    degasA --> fillA[Filling into Flexible Pouches]
    fillA --> loadA[Automated Loading into Carrier Basket]
    loadA --> hppV[High Pressure Vessel]
    hppV -- 100 to 600 MPa --> hppHold[Pressure Hold 1 to 15 min]
    hppHold --> hppRel[Decompression Step]
    hppRel --> unloadA[Unloading Carrier]
    unloadA --> labA[Quality Lab Microbial Assay]
    labA --> labDecay{Micro Result OK}
    labDecay -- No --> rejectA[Reject Batch Reprocess]
    labDecay -- Yes --> storeA[Refrigerated Storage at 4 Degree C]
    storeA --> distA[Distribution Cold Chain]
```

> **Reading Guide:** This flow demonstrates the **batch nature** of HPP — a key industrial limitation. Continuous-flow HPP exists but is rare at industrial scale.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: PEF Application Flow (Continuous Mode)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawP[Pre-treated Food Slurry] --> pumpP[Positive Displacement Pump]
    pumpP --> pipeP[Flow Through Electrode Gap 0.2 to 0.5 cm]
    pipeP -- High Voltage Pulses 20 to 80 kV per cm --> pulseP[Pulse Generator Cabinet]
    pulseP --> pulseOut[Output Stream Treated]
    pulseOut --> fillP[Aseptic Filling]
    fillP --> coldP[Refrigerated Storage]
    pulseP -. Recycle Loop .-> pumpP
```

> **Note:** Continuous PEF flow is **standard industrial configuration** for liquid foods — the food acts as the dielectric in the capacitor formed by the electrodes.

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Membrane Cascade Application (Dairy)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    milkR[Raw Milk] --> microF[Microfiltration 1.4 micron]
    microF --> branchA{Casein vs Serum Split}
    branchA -- Casein Stream --> cheeseV[Cheese Making]
    branchA -- Serum Stream --> ultraF[Ultrafiltration 10 kDa]
    ultraF --> lactPerm[Lactose Permeate]
    ultraF --> wheyProt[Concentrated Whey Protein]
    wheyProt --> nanoF[Nanofiltration 300 Da]
    nanoF --> saltRem[Desalted Whey Concentrate]
```

> **Reading Guide:** Demonstrates **sequential membrane application** — common in modern dairy plants for protein fractionation, a high-revenue bio-refinery application.

---

## 4.4 Selection Decision-Tree (For KTU Exam Practice)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startQ[New Product Application] --> q1{Is product liquid?}
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Turbidity High?}
    q1 -- No --> q3{Pressure Sensitive?}
    q2 -- No --> q4[Choose PEF or UV-C]
    q2 -- Yes --> q5[Choose HPP or MF Membrane]
    q3 -- Yes --> q6[Choose HPP or Ultrasound]
    q3 -- No --> q7[Choose Cold Plasma or Ozone]
    q4 --> endPick[Selected NTP]
    q5 --> endPick
    q6 --> endPick
    q7 --> endPick
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> *With two examples, explain how high-pressure processing (HPP) is commercially applied for shelf-stable, high-acid food products.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

High-Pressure Processing (HPP) operates at **100–600 MPa** for 1–15 minutes at ambient or mild temperature. Under isostatic pressure transmission (Pascal's Law), pressure acts uniformly throughout the food, inactivating vegetative microbial cells (especially Gram-negative bacteria and yeasts) by disrupting their cytoplasmic membrane and denaturing structural proteins.

**Two Examples:**
1. **Guacamole / Avocado Paste:** HPP at **550 MPa for 2–3 minutes** extends shelf-life from 7–10 days to **30–45 days** under refrigeration, preserving the characteristic green color (chlorophyll retained) and preventing browning (PPO enzyme mildly inactivated).
2. **Orange Juice:** HPP at **400–600 MPa for 1–3 minutes** delivers **>5-log reduction** of *E. coli* and *Saccharomyces*, while retaining vitamin C (>95%), flavor volatiles, and cloud stability — outperforming thermal pasteurization in sensory quality.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> *Differentiate between reversible and irreversible electroporation as applied in PEF processing of foods.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Reversible Electroporation | Irreversible Electroporation |
|---------|----------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Field strength** | Below **critical threshold** (typically < **2 kV/cm** for plant tissue) | Above critical threshold (**>10–15 kV/cm** for microbes) |
| **Membrane outcome** | Temporary pores; membrane self-heals | Permanent rupture; cell lysis |
| **Application** | **Extraction** enhancement — sugar beet, olive, grape | **Microbial inactivation** — fruit juice pasteurization |
| **Cell viability** | Cells remain alive | Cells die |

In PEF juice pasteurization, **irreversible electroporation** is the operative mechanism (25–35 kV/cm), whereas PEF pre-treatment of plant tissue for enhanced juice extraction uses **reversible electroporation**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - ❌ Writing only "HPP is good" without quantifying pressure, time, and log-reduction.
> - ❌ Confusing **a<sub>w</sub>** and **pH** thresholds — these are **independent** selection criteria.
> - ❌ Missing the **regulatory anchor** — every application answer must mention either FSSAI / FDA / EFSA compliance.
> - ❌ Forgetting to state **why the chosen technology** is preferred over the alternatives.
> - ❌ Not mentioning **scale-up challenges** in 14-mark questions on industrial applications.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

> *(a)* Compare the **microbiological applications of High Pressure Processing (HPP) and Pulsed Electric Field (PEF)** with respect to their inactivation mechanisms, operational parameters, and quality impacts on food. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* Design a **commercial HPP cycle for a ready-to-eat (RTE) sliced ham** ensuring a **5-log reduction of *Listeria monocytogenes***. Justify your process parameters, packaging choice, and shelf-life claim. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**1. Mechanism of microbial inactivation (2 marks):**
- **HPP:** Pressure (100–600 MPa) denatures microbial proteins, disrupts cytoplasmic membrane integrity, and damages ribosomes. Spores are highly resistant (>1000 MPa needed). Mechanism primarily **protein unfolding + membrane disruption**.
- **PEF:** Electric pulses (20–80 kV/cm, µs duration) cause **electroporation** of the microbial cytoplasmic membrane, leading to leakage of intracellular contents and lysis.

**2. Operational parameters (2 marks):**

| Parameter | HPP | PEF |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| Treatment intensity | 300–600 MPa | 20–35 kV/cm |
| Time | 1–15 min | 10–100 µs × multiple pulses |
| Temperature | 20–40 °C (mild) | < 40 °C |
| Mode | Batch (mostly) | Continuous |
| Energy per kg | ~ 25 kWh/1000 kg | ~ 5–15 kWh/1000 kg |

**3. Quality impact (2 marks):**
- Both retain vitamins, flavor, and color (no heat).
- **HPP** may inactivate beneficial enzymes and cause slight texture softening in fruit tissues.
- **PEF** has minimal texture effect but limited efficacy on spores and enzymes.

**4. Application distinction (1 mark):**
- **HPP** is preferred for **solid and packaged foods** (ham, guacamole, oysters).
- **PEF** is preferred for **pumpable liquids** (juice, milk, beer).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**1. Microbial target (1 mark):**
*Listeria monocytogenes* is a Gram-positive, psychrotrophic pathogen — it grows at refrigeration temperatures and has D-value of **~1 min at 600 MPa**.

**2. Process parameters (2 marks):**
- Pressure: **600 MPa**
- Hold time: **5 minutes** (provides > 6-log safety margin beyond the required 5-log)
- Initial temperature: **20 °C** (mild to enhance lethality without thermal damage)
- Cycles: Single cycle (sufficient for vegetative cells)

**3. Packaging choice (2 marks):**
- Use **flexible, vacuum-sealed pouches** (PET/EVOH/PE multi-layer) of **< 50 mm thickness**.
- Rigid packaging would burst under pressure; rigid cans are unsuitable.
- Packaging must have **>5% headspace** to allow pressure equalization.

**4. Shelf-life claim (2 marks):**
- HPP-treated RTE ham has a refrigerated shelf-life of **42–60 days** at 4 °C.
- **Regulatory anchor:** FDA approved HPP for RTE meats under **21 CFR 110.93**; FSSAI aligned with this in 2022.
- Label claim: *"HPP treated — no preservatives"*.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

> *(a)* Discuss the **applications of ultrasound in food processing** under three categories: (i) extraction, (ii) preservation, and (iii) structural modification. Cite specific product examples. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* Explain **membrane processing applications** in the dairy industry with reference to microfiltration, ultrafiltration, nanofiltration, and reverse osmosis. Discuss a real industrial case of **whey protein concentration**. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**1. Extraction applications (2.5 marks):**
Ultrasound-assisted extraction (UAE) uses cavitation-induced micro-jetting to rupture plant cell walls, releasing intracellular bioactive compounds.
- **Example 1:** Extraction of **polyphenols from green tea** — UAE at **20 kHz, 100 W, 30 min** increases yield by **30%** vs. conventional maceration.
- **Example 2:** Extraction of **essential oils from rosemary, ginger, and citrus peel**.

**2. Preservation applications (2.5 marks):**
- **Manothermosonication (MTS):** Combined ultrasound (20 kHz) + pressure (200–400 MPa) + mild heat (40–50 °C) for milk, fruit juices. Achieves **>5-log reduction** of *E. coli* and *Pseudomonas* while retaining sensory quality.
- **Surface decontamination** of fresh produce using probe-type ultrasonic baths.

**3. Structural modification (2 marks):**
- **Sono-crystallization:** Producing **fine, uniform ice crystals** in ice cream (improved creaminess) and **stable fat crystal networks** in chocolate.
- **Meat tenderization:** Acoustic cell disruption reduces myofibril integrity, lowering Warner-Bratzler shear force by **20–40%**.
- **Emulsification:** Nano-emulsions (droplet size < 100 nm) for clear functional beverages.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**1. Membrane classification (2 marks):**
Membranes are classified by **pore size / MWCO**:
- **Microfiltration (MF):** 0.1–10 µm — retains bacteria and suspended solids.
- **Ultrafiltration (UF):** 1–100 kDa — retains proteins.
- **Nanofiltration (NF):** 200–1000 Da — retains divalent ions, lactose.
- **Reverse Osmosis (RO):** < 200 Da — retains water and monovalent ions.

**2. Dairy applications matrix (2 marks):**

| Process | Application |
|---------|-------------|
| **MF** (1.4 µm ceramic) | Cold pasteurization of skim milk (removes 99.95% bacteria & spores) |
| **UF** (10 kDa) | Concentrating whey proteins; pre-concentration of cheese milk |
| **NF** (300 Da) | Partial demineralization of whey; lactose concentration |
| **RO** | Water removal from whey permeate; concentration of milk before spray drying |

**3. Industrial case: Whey protein concentration (3 marks):**
- **Feed:** Sweet cheese whey (6% solids, 0.8% protein).
- **Process:** UF with **10 kDa polyethersulfone spiral-wound membranes** at 50 °C, transmembrane pressure 2–4 bar.
- **Outcome:** **Whey Protein Concentrate (WPC-80)** with **80% protein** on dry basis, achieved by **diafiltration** to wash out lactose and minerals.
- **Industrial scale:** Plants like Arla Foods Ingredients (Denmark) and Davisco (USA) produce **>50,000 tonnes/year** of WPC-80.
- **Application of WPC-80:** Infant formula, sports nutrition, protein bars.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2: Applications**

- ⭐ **HPP operates at 100–600 MPa, 1–15 min, batch mode** — ideal for **packaged solid foods** (RTE meats, guacamole, oysters).
- ⭐ **PEF operates at 20–80 kV/cm, µs pulses, continuous mode** — ideal for **pumpable liquids** (juices, beer, milk).
- ⭐ **Ultrasound (20–100 kHz) → cavitation** drives extraction, emulsification, sono-crystallization, tenderization.
- ⭐ **UV-C operates at 254 nm** — effective **only in clear, low-turbidity fluids** (Beer-Lambert Law).
- ⭐ **Cold Plasma** generates **ROS/RNS** — best for **surface decontamination** of fresh produce and packaging.
- ⭐ **Ozone (E° = 2.07 V)** is **GRAS** for storage atmosphere & mycotoxin degradation.
- ⭐ **Membrane cascade**: MF → UF → NF → RO — **decreasing pore size** enables bio-refinery separation.
- ⭐ **First-order inactivation kinetics** $\log_{10}(N_0/N) = t/D$ applies to **all NTP sterilization cycles**.
- ⭐ **D-value** = time for **1-log (90%) reduction** — critical design parameter.
- ⭐ **High-acid foods (pH < 4.5)** allow safe use of milder NTP cycles (spores cannot outgrow).
- ⭐ **Low-acid foods (pH > 4.5, a<sub>w</sub> > 0.85)** require **higher intensity or combination treatments** for *Clostridium botulinum* control.
- ⭐ **Regulatory anchors** to memorize: **21 CFR 110.93** (FDA-HPP), **FSSAI 2022 NTP guidelines**, **EFSA 2016**.
- ⭐ **Key industries:** HPP dominates in **North America** (USA/Mexico guacamole); PEF in **Europe** (potato/juice); UV and Ozone in **Asia** (water & produce).
- ⭐ **Major global players:** Hiperbaric, JBT Avure (HPP); Elea, CoolWave, Pulsemaster (PEF); Hanovia, Atlantium (UV).
- ⭐ **Combined / "Hurdle" approach** is the future — HPP + PEF + essential oils; called **multi-hurdle technology**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ozone Processing and Cold Plasma
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT631 - NON-THERMAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Ozone Processing and Cold Plasma # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Ozone Processing > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition (Ozone Processing):** > *Ozone processing is a non-thermal food preservation technique that employs triatomic oxygen (O₃), a powerful oxidizing agent, to inactivate microorganisms, degrade pesticide residues, and extend the shelf-life of perishable food products. It is generated on-site and applied in gaseous or aqueous form because of its short half-life.* **Key Chemical Identity of Ozone** - Molecular formula: **O₃** (resonance-stabilized triatomic allotrope of oxygen) - Molecular weight: **48 g/mol** - Boiling point: **−112 °C** - Redox potential: **E° = +2.07 V** (one of the highest known aqueous oxidants, second only to fluorine at +2.87 V) - Aqueous half-life: ~**20 minutes** at 20 °C; even shorter in food matrices - Characteristic pungent smell detectable at **0.02–0.05 ppm** > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — The "Sun-Bleached Laundry" Effect** > Imagine hanging laundry on a clothesline on a bright, stormy afternoon. The combined action of UV rays from the sun and electrical discharge (lightning in the atmosphere) "freshens" the clothes by killing odour-causing microbes and breaking down stains. Ozone processing works on the **same principle**: a high-energy electrical discharge (corona effect) or UV-C photons split ordinary atmospheric oxygen (O₂) into atomic oxygen, which then rapidly recombines with O₂ to form O₃. The freshly made ozone is immediately bubbled through water or swept over food, where it acts as a "chemical lightning bolt" — disrupting microbial cell walls, oxidizing double bonds in lipids, and degrading pesticide molecules — before harmlessly decomposing back to oxygen. --- ## 1.2 Cold Plasma Processing > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition (Cold Plasma / Non-Thermal Plasma):** > *Cold plasma, also known as non-thermal plasma (NTP), is a partially ionized gas consisting of a quasi-neutral mixture of electrons, ions, neutral atoms/molecules, free radicals, UV photons, and electromagnetic fields, in which the electron temperature (Tₑ ≫ 1000 K) far exceeds the heavy-species (gas) temperature (T_g ≈ 300–350 K). This thermodynamic non-equilibrium enables the delivery of highly reactive chemistry to heat-sensitive biological materials at near-ambient bulk temperature.* **Plasma Taxonomy (for clarity)** | Plasma Type | Electron Temp (Tₑ) | Gas Temp (T_g) | Thermal Equil. | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Thermal (Arc) Plasma** | ~10⁴ K | ~10⁴ K | Equilibrium | | **Warm Plasma** | ~10⁴ K | ~10³–10⁴ K | Near-equil. | | **Cold (Non-Thermal) Plasma** | ~10⁴–10⁵ K | ~300–350 K | Strong non-equil. | > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — The "Cool Neon Sign" Phenomenon** > Picture the glowing tubes of a neon sign in a shop window. Although the gas inside is **electrically conducting** and packed with high-energy particles (electrons, ions, excited atoms) that emit visible light, the glass tube remains **comfortably warm to the touch** — not scalding hot. Cold plasma is the food-engineering cousin of that neon tube. By pumping a process gas (air, argon, helium, or N₂) through a high-voltage electrode gap, we generate a swarm of energetic electrons (Tₑ ≈ 10⁴ K) that collide with room-temperature gas molecules (T_g ≈ 300 K). The result is a "chemically hot, physically cool" medium — perfect for sterilizing strawberries, decontaminating spice powders, or sanitizing the inside of a sealed package, **without cooking the food**. --- ## 1.3 Why These Two Technologies Belong Together (KTU Module-2 Rationale) Both ozone and cold plasma are **Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs)** — they generate short-lived, highly reactive oxygen species (ROS) such as atomic oxygen (·O), hydroxyl radicals (·OH), superoxide (·O₂⁻), and singlet oxygen (¹O₂). Their synergy is taught in Module 2 because: 1. They share **redox-driven microbial inactivation** as a core kill mechanism. 2. They are both **regulatory-friendly** — generally recognized as safe (GRAS) for certain food applications (US FDA, 2001 for ozone). 3. They have **low energy footprints** compared to thermal pasteurization. 4. They are **complementary**: ozone excels in aqueous/wash applications, while cold plasma excels in dry, packaged, or surface treatments. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Ozone Generation — The Chemistry Behind the "Third Oxygen" ### 2.1.1 Step-Wise Generation Logic Ozone cannot be stored or transported (it decays rapidly), so it is **generated on-demand at the point of use** via one of three routes: **Route A — Corona Discharge (Dielectric Barrier Discharge, DBD) — INDUSTRIAL STANDARD** 1. Dry, oxygen-rich feed gas (medical O₂ or concentrated air) is passed between two electrodes separated by a dielectric barrier (typically glass or ceramic). 2. A high-voltage alternating field (typically **5–20 kV at 50 Hz – 20 kHz**) accelerates free electrons to kinetic energies of **1–10 eV**. 3. These energetic electrons collide with O₂ molecules and dissociate them: $$\text{O}_2 + e^{-} \longrightarrow 2\,\text{O}^\bullet + e^{-}$$ 4. The atomic oxygen radicals instantly combine with molecular oxygen (M = third-body collision partner): $$\text{O}^\bullet + \text{O}_2 + M \longrightarrow \text{O}_3 + M$$ 5. Simultaneously, ozone destruction competes (electron impact dissociation): $$\text{O}_3 + e^{-} \longrightarrow \text{O}_2 + \text{O}^\bullet + e^{-}$$ 6. **Steady-state ozone concentration** is set by the balance of formation and destruction rates. **Route B — UV Photolysis (Low-concentration, e.g., 185 nm lamps)** $$\text{O}_2 + h\nu \;(\lambda = 185\,\text{nm}) \longrightarrow 2\,\text{O}^\bullet$$ $$\text{O}^\bullet + \text{O}_2 + M \longrightarrow \text{O}_3 + M$$ **Route C — Electrolytic (Cold-plasma-electrochemical hybrid)** Water electrolysis at high overpotential on a BDD (boron-doped diamond) anode: $$3\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{O}_3 + 6\,\text{H}^{+} + 6\,e^{-}$$ ### 2.1.2 Why and How Ozone Inactivates Microbes — The Kill Mechanism - **Ozone dissolves** in the aqueous film on the cell surface (governed by Henry's law, see Formula Sheet). - It attacks the **outer membrane**: oxidizes sulfhydryl (–SH) groups in membrane proteins, peroxidizes polyunsaturated fatty acids (lipid ozonolysis). - It **degrades cytoplasmic enzymes and nucleic acids**: ozonolysis of guanine residues in DNA leads to strand scission. - It generates **reactive secondary species** (·OH, H₂O₂) via ozone decomposition in water, amplifying oxidative damage. - The result is **multi-target cell disruption** — preventing the facile development of microbial resistance. --- ## 2.2 Cold Plasma Generation — The Physics of "Cool Fire" ### 2.2.1 The Three Industrial Reactor Geometries (KTU 2024 emphasis) **Geometry 1 — DBD (Dielectric Barrier Discharge)** - Two parallel electrodes, one (or both) covered with a dielectric (glass, alumina, polymer). - Operates at **atmospheric pressure**, fed with air, O₂, N₂, or noble gases. - Produces a **filamentary microdischarge** pattern. - Most common for **direct food-surface treatment** (e.g., tomato, cheese, meat slices). **Geometry 2 — Atmospheric Plasma Jet (APPJ)** - Two concentric electrodes, with feed gas (typically He or Ar) flowing through the inner nozzle. - A high-voltage RF or pulsed-DC field ionizes the gas; the effluent plume is blown out as a "plasma pencil." - Suitable for **3-D or irregular surfaces** and **in-package treatment** of seeds/spices. **Geometry 3 — Gliding Arc Discharge** - Two diverging electrodes in a high-velocity gas flow; the arc is "blown" downstream, elongating and cooling into a non-equilibrium plasma. - Operates at higher power than DBD; suitable for **bulk gas streams** and **water treatment**. ### 2.2.2 The Reactive Species Cocktail (Why Cold Plasma Works) The energetic electron swarm (Tₑ ≈ 1–10 eV) drives the following key reactions in air-fed plasma: - Dissociation: e⁻ + O₂ → 2 O· + e⁻ - Dissociative attachment: e⁻ + H₂O → OH· + H· + e⁻ - Excitation: e⁻ + N₂ → N₂(A³Σ) + e⁻ → emits UV photons at 297–400 nm - Three-body ozone formation: O· + O₂ + M → O₃ + M **The "Four Horsemen" of Plasma-Microbe Inactivation:** 1. **Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS)** — ·O, ·OH, O₃, ¹O₂, H₂O₂ 2. **Reactive Nitrogen Species (RNS)** — ·NO, NO₂, ONOO⁻ 3. **UV photons** (especially UV-C and UV-B) — cause pyrimidine dimer formation in DNA 4. **Charged particles & electric fields** — electroporation of cell membranes --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are **exam-critical** and have appeared (in equivalent form) in KTU end-semester papers. | # | Concept | Equation | Variables & Units | Boundary / Validity | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Ozone dissolution (Henry's Law) | $C_a = k_H^{cp} \cdot P_{O_3}$ | $C_a$ = aqueous [O₃] (mg/L); $k_H^{cp}$ = Henry's constant (mg/L·Pa); $P_{O_3}$ = partial pressure (Pa) | $T = 20\,^\circ\text{C}$, dilute soln | | 2 | Ozone self-decomposition (1st order) | $\ln\!\left(\frac{C_t}{C_0}\right) = -k_d\,t$ | $C_t$ = [O₃] at time $t$; $k_d$ = decay const. (min⁻¹) | pH-dependent, $T = $ const. | | 3 | Microbial log-reduction (Chick-Watson) | $\log\!\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\frac{k \cdot C^n \cdot t}{D}$ | $N_0, N$ = initial/final CFU; $k$ = rate const.; $C$ = [O₃]; $n$ = order (≈1) | $D$ = dose parameter | | 4 | D-value (decimal reduction time) | $D = \frac{t}{\log(N_0) - \log(N)}$ | $D$ in min; $t$ = exposure time | At constant $C$ and $T$ | | 5 | Z-value (temp dependence) | $\log\!\left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right) = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{Z}$ | $Z$ in °C | Thermal analogy retained | | 6 | Electron mean energy (Boltzmann) | $\bar{\varepsilon} = \tfrac{3}{2}\,k_B\,T_e$ | $k_B$ = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K | Maxwellian EEDF assumption | | 7 | Specific input energy (SIE) | $\text{SIE} = \frac{P}{Q}$ | $P$ = power (W); $Q$ = gas flow (L/s) | J/L units; used in plasma reactor design | | 8 | Plasma energy yield for O₃ | $\eta_{O_3} = \frac{C_{O_3} \cdot Q}{P}$ | $\eta$ in g/(kWh) | Industrial: 50–150 g/kWh | | 9 | First-order kill kinetics (plasma) | $\ln\!\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -k_{pl}\,t$ | $k_{pl}$ = plasma rate const. (s⁻¹) | At fixed discharge power | | 10 | Weibull survival model (modern fit) | $\log\!\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\left(\dfrac{t}{\delta}\right)^p$ | $\delta$ = scale; $p$ = shape param. | Better fit than 1st order | **Engineering Constants Worth Memorising** - $\mathbf{k_B} = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K (Boltzmann constant) - $\mathbf{1\,\text{eV}} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J = 11600 K - **Ozone generator yield:** 50–150 g O₃ per kWh (DBD, dry air) - **Corona inception field in air:** ≈ **3 MV/m** (Paschen-related) - **Cytotoxicity threshold for surface O₃:** ≈ **0.5–2 ppm × min** for most bacteria > [!TIP] > **Real-world utility:** > - Equations (1) and (2) drive **aquaculture and fresh-cut produce wash design** (O₃ dissolved in flume water). > - Equations (3)–(5) are the **regulatory deliverables** — you must demonstrate a 5-log reduction of *L. monocytogenes* to validate a process. > - Equations (6)–(8) are used to **size a plasma reactor** (electrode area, power supply rating). > - Equation (10) is the **modern paper-curve fit** examiners love to ask about in Part B. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Ozone Concentration in a Corona Discharge Reactor > **Assumptions (state explicitly in any KTU answer):** > 1. Plug-flow reactor (PFR) with constant gas velocity. > 2. Isothermal, isobaric operation. > 3. Rate of O₃ formation is pseudo-first-order in O₂ partial pressure. > 4. Rate of O₃ destruction is pseudo-first-order in O₃ concentration. Let the elementary rates be: $$r_f = k_f \, [\text{O}_2] \, [e^{-}] \quad \text{(formation, electron-driven)}$$ $$r_d = k_d \, [\text{O}_3] \, [e^{-}] \quad \text{(destruction, electron-driven)}$$ The **net rate of ozone accumulation** in a differential slice of the reactor of length $dz$ is: $$\frac{d[\text{O}_3]}{dt} = r_f - r_d$$ At **steady state** ($d[\text{O}_3]/dt = 0$), equating the two rates: $$k_f \, [\text{O}_2] = k_d \, [\text{O}_3]_{ss}$$ Solving for the steady-state ozone concentration: $$[\text{O}_3]_{ss} = \frac{k_f}{k_d} \, [\text{O}_2]$$ Now introduce the **specific energy density (SED)** concept. Define $G$ = number of O₃ molecules formed per 100 eV of input energy. For a discharge power $P$ in a gas flow $Q$ (mol/s), the molar formation rate is: $$\dot{n}_{O_3} = \frac{P \cdot G \cdot N_A}{100 \cdot e \cdot U} \quad \text{where } U = 1\,\text{V reference}$$ In the **engineering form** (combining the above with mass balance over a PFR of length $L$): $$\frac{dC_{O_3}}{dz} = \frac{P}{Q} \cdot \frac{1}{u} \cdot \alpha - \beta \cdot C_{O_3}$$ where $u$ = gas velocity, $\alpha$ = formation coefficient, $\beta$ = destruction coefficient. Integrating from $z = 0$ (pure O₂ feed) to $z = L$ (reactor exit): $$C_{O_3}(L) = C_{O_3,\,\infty} \left(1 - e^{-\beta L/u}\right)$$ The **maximum theoretical ozone yield** $C_{O_3,\infty}$ is reached when residence time $\to \infty$: $$C_{O_3,\,\infty} = \frac{P \cdot \alpha}{Q \cdot u \cdot \beta} = \frac{\alpha}{\beta} \cdot \frac{P}{Q \cdot u}$$ > **Engineering insight:** The ratio $\alpha/\beta$ is the **branching ratio** of the discharge; modern ozone generators are engineered to maximize it via high-frequency excitation, narrow discharge gaps, and oxygen-enriched feed. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Microbial Log-Reduction from Plasma Treatment (First-Order Kinetics) > **Assumption:** Plasma treatment produces a constant flux of reactive species; bacterial inactivation follows Chick's law. Chick's law (1908) for chemical disinfection states: $$\frac{dN}{dt} = -k \cdot C \cdot N$$ For a **constant plasma power output** (hence constant $C$), separate variables: $$\frac{dN}{N} = -k\,C\,dt$$ Integrate from $N_0$ at $t = 0$ to $N$ at $t$: $$\int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN}{N} = -k\,C \int_{0}^{t} dt$$ $$\ln\!\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -k\,C\,t$$ Convert natural log to base-10 (examiners want log₁₀): $$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\frac{k\,C}{2.303} \cdot t$$ Define the **decimal reduction time** $D$ (time for 1-log reduction at a given $C$): $$D = \frac{2.303}{k\,C}$$ So the **master inactivation equation** simplifies to the familiar form: $$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\frac{t}{D}$$ For an **n-log reduction** (e.g., 5-log for *L. monocytogenes*): $$t_{n\text{-log}} = n \cdot D$$ **Numerical example (a KTU-favourite):** A plasma jet delivers a dose such that $D = 30$ s for *E. coli* on a tomato surface. To achieve a 5-log reduction: $$t_{5\text{-log}} = 5 \times 30 = 150\,\text{s} = 2.5\,\text{min}$$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Ozone vs. Cold Plasma (Head-to-Head Comparison) | Parameter | Ozone Processing | Cold Plasma Processing | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Active species** | O₃ (primary); ·OH, H₂O₂ (secondary) | ROS + RNS + UV + charged particles | | **State of food treated** | Aqueous wash / gaseous fumigation | Surface / in-package / liquid | | **Penetration depth** | Diffuses ~10–100 µm into water films | Mostly surface (µm to mm); depends on gas | | **Operating temperature** | 0–30 °C (must cool O₃ generator) | 25–60 °C bulk (non-thermal) | | **Residual toxicity** | None (decomposes to O₂) | None (transient species) | | **Effect on nutrients** | Mild oxidation of vitamin C, anthocyanins | Mild oxidation; less than ozone for vit. C | | **Effect on texture** | Minimal | Possible surface etching at long exposure | | **Equipment cost** | Low–Moderate | Moderate–High | | **Scalability** | Excellent (municipal water, flume tanks) | Good (continuous in-line or batch) | | **Regulatory status** | GRAS (US FDA, 2001) | GRAS-pending; EU novel food framework | | **5-log microbial kill time** | 1–10 min (typ.) | 30 s – 5 min (typ.) | | **KTU mark weight (Module 2)** | ~50% | ~50% | --- ## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Plasma Electron Temperature from the Boltzmann Relation In a non-thermal plasma, the electron energy distribution function (EEDF) is approximately Maxwellian. The **mean electron energy** is: $$\bar{\varepsilon} = \frac{3}{2}\,k_B\,T_e$$ The corresponding **electron temperature** $T_e$ is: $$T_e = \frac{2\,\bar{\varepsilon}}{3\,k_B}$$ If the measured mean electron energy is, e.g., $\bar{\varepsilon} = 2\,\text{eV}$: $$T_e = \frac{2 \times (2 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{J})}{3 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\,\text{J/K}}$$ $$T_e = \frac{6.408 \times 10^{-19}}{4.14 \times 10^{-23}} \approx 1.55 \times 10^{4}\,\text{K}$$ Meanwhile, the **gas (heavy species) temperature** $T_g$ remains at ~300 K. The **non-equilibrium ratio** is: $$\frac{T_e}{T_g} \approx \frac{1.55 \times 10^{4}}{300} \approx 52$$ This ratio of ~50 is the **fingerprint** of cold plasma — the electrons are "plasma-hot" while the gas is "kitchen-cold." > **Why this matters for the KTU examiner:** A 14-mark question that asks *"Explain how cold plasma can sterilize heat-sensitive foods"* earns full marks **only if** you quantify the non-equilibrium with this calculation. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Industrial Ozone Generation & Application System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Feed Gas
O2 or Dry Air] --> B[Air Dryer
and Filter] B --> C[Flow Meter
and Pressure Regulator] C --> D[High Voltage
Transformer] D --> E[Corona Discharge
Reactor DBD Cell] E --> F[Ozone Gas
Output] F --> G[Contact /
Diffuser Vessel] G --> H[Food Product
Wash Tank] H --> I[Off Gas
Ozone Destruct Unit] I --> J[Exhaust
to Atmosphere] H --> K[Treated
Food Output] style A fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366 style E fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000 style G fill:#fff5e6,stroke:#cc6600 style I fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - **Feed Gas (A → B → C):** Oxygen or air is dried to a dew point below **−60 °C** to prevent nitric acid formation in the discharge. - **Discharge Cell (E):** The high-voltage transformer steps up mains voltage to 5–20 kV; the dielectric barrier confines the microdischarges. - **Contact Vessel (G):** Ozone is dispersed via a **Venturi injector** or porous diffuser to maximize mass transfer into the water. - **Destruct Unit (I):** Residual ozone is catalytically (MnO₂) or thermally (≥ 300 °C) destroyed to meet OSHA's 0.1 ppm 8-hr TWA limit. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Cold Plasma Reactor (DBD Configuration) for Food Treatment ```mermaid flowchart TB P1[High Voltage
AC or Pulsed DC
5 to 20 kV] --> P2[Electrode
Top Plate] P3[Process Gas
Air / N2 / He / Ar] --> P4[Gas Inlet
Manifold] P4 --> P5[Discharge Gap
1 to 5 mm] P2 --> P5 P5 --> P6[Food Sample
on Grounded Plate] P6 --> P7[Ground
Electrode] P5 --> P8[Emitted Species
ROS RNS UV Photons] P8 --> P9[Microbial Cell
on Food Surface] P9 --> P10[Membrane
Peroxidation] P9 --> P11[DNA
Damage] P9 --> P12[Enzyme
Inactivation] style P5 fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000 style P8 fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#996600 style P10 fill:#e6e6ff,stroke:#333399 style P11 fill:#e6e6ff,stroke:#333399 style P12 fill:#e6e6ff,stroke:#333399 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **discharge gap (P5)** is the heart of the reactor — typical gap widths of 1–5 mm ensure uniform filament distribution. - The **food sample (P6)** is placed on the grounded lower electrode; the high-voltage upper electrode remains at a fixed stand-off. - The **emitted species cloud (P8)** is the working "antimicrobial cocktail" — simultaneously attacking three different microbial targets (P10, P11, P12), which is why resistance development is rare. --- ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Mode-of-Action Comparison Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR O3[Ozone
Processing] --> OM1[Diffuses into
Cell Water] OM1 --> OM2[Lipid and Protein
Oxidation] OM2 --> OM3[Membrane
Lysis] OM2 --> OM4[Enzyme
Denaturation] OM2 --> OM5[DNA Strand
Scission] CP[Cold Plasma
Processing] --> CM1[Reactive Species
Attack Surface] CM1 --> CM2[Etching and
Electroporation] CM1 --> CM3[UV Photon
DNA Damage] CM1 --> CM4[Radical
Oxidation] CM2 --> CM5[Cell Lysis
and Leakage] CM3 --> CM5 CM4 --> CM5 style O3 fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#003366 style CP fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#993300 style OM5 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000 style CM5 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The two technologies **converge on the same outcome** (cell lysis) but via **partially different pathways** — this is a key point in KTU essays asking for a "comparative mechanism" answer. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **"Define cold plasma. Why is it termed 'non-thermal'?" (3 marks)** **Model Answer (verbatim key style):** > **Cold plasma** is a partially ionized gas containing energetic electrons, ions, neutral species, free radicals, and UV photons. It is termed *non-thermal* because the system is in **thermodynamic non-equilibrium**: the electron temperature $T_e$ is of the order of $10^4$–$10^5$ K, while the bulk gas temperature $T_g$ remains close to ambient (300–350 K). This non-equilibrium allows delivery of plasma-driven reactive chemistry to heat-sensitive biological substrates (e.g., fresh fruits, ready-to-eat meat) without appreciable thermal damage, as confirmed by $\tfrac{T_e}{T_g} \approx 30$–$100$. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for non-equilibrium concept, 1 for the practical implication.)* --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **"List any three methods of ozone generation. Which is most commonly used in food processing?" (3 marks)** **Model Answer:** 1. **Corona Discharge (DBD):** Most widely used in food processing — high yield (50–150 g O₃/kWh), continuous operation, suited to on-site generation. 2. **UV Photolysis (185 nm Hg lamp):** Low concentration; used in air purifiers, small-scale fruit storage rooms. 3. **Electrolytic (BDD anode):** Produces ozone directly in water; used in laboratory and pharmaceutical water treatment. *(3 marks: 1 mark each. Mentioning DBD as the industrial method is the "killing" point.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern Reminder:** Module 2 contributes 50% of the paper. The 14-mark question typically has **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. **Internal choice means you must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full.** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **working principle of an ozone generation system** used in food processing. Discuss the **kill mechanism** of ozone against microorganisms. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A fresh-cut salad wash tank is treated with dissolved ozone at an aqueous concentration of **1.5 mg/L**. The Chick-Watson rate constant for *Listeria innocua* in this matrix is $k = 0.45\,\text{L·mg}^{-1}\text{·min}^{-1}$ and the concentration exponent $n = 1$. Calculate the **time required to achieve a 5-log reduction** of the initial microbial load of $10^6$ CFU/mL. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a) 1. **System description (4 marks):** Block diagram should contain — Feed gas → Filter/Dryer → Flow meter → High-voltage transformer → Corona discharge cell → Ozone output → Contact vessel → Off-gas destructor. Explain the role of the dielectric barrier, the use of dry O₂/air, and the typical operating parameters (5–20 kV, 50 Hz–20 kHz). 2. **Kill mechanism (3 marks):** Ozone dissolves in the surface water film → attacks outer membrane sulfhydryl groups and unsaturated fatty acids (lipid ozonolysis) → enters the cytoplasm → damages DNA via guanine ozonolysis and inactivates enzymes. Multi-target action makes microbial resistance unlikely. Cite the redox potential $E^\circ = +2.07\,\text{V}$ as the molecular rationale. #### Model Solution to (b) **Given:** - $C = 1.5$ mg/L - $k = 0.45$ L·mg⁻¹·min⁻¹ - $n = 1$ - Required reduction: $N/N_0 = 10^{-5}$ - $N_0 = 10^6$ CFU/mL → $N = 10^1$ CFU/mL (5-log reduction) **Apply Chick-Watson with $n = 1$:** $$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\frac{k \cdot C^n \cdot t}{2.303} \cdot 2.303$$ Wait — the standard Chick-Watson integrated form with $n=1$ is: $$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -k \cdot C^n \cdot t$$ (because $k$ in the formula sheet is already the decimal-base rate constant when $C^n \cdot t$ is in compatible units). Substituting: $$-5 = -(0.45) \times (1.5)^1 \times t$$ $$5 = 0.45 \times 1.5 \times t = 0.675\,t$$ $$t = \frac{5}{0.675} = 7.41\,\text{min}$$ **Answer:** Approximately **7.4 minutes** of contact time is required to achieve a 5-log reduction under the given conditions. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where You Lose Marks:** > 1. **Unit mismatch:** $k$ is given in L·mg⁻¹·min⁻¹ and $C$ in mg/L — the product $kC$ is in min⁻¹, so $t$ comes out in minutes. Mixing SI and CGS units will cost 2 marks. > 2. **Sign convention:** Forgetting the negative sign leads to $t = -7.4$ min, which is a common slip. Always state $t > 0$ explicitly. > 3. **Mis-reading "5-log":** Some students interpret it as $N/N_0 = 5$ instead of $10^{-5}$. Read carefully! --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **(a)** Explain the **principle of cold plasma generation using a dielectric barrier discharge (DBD) reactor**. List the **reactive species** responsible for microbial inactivation and describe the **three major kill pathways**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A cold-plasma jet operating at atmospheric pressure delivers a **mean electron energy of 2.5 eV**. Calculate the **electron temperature** and the **non-equilibrium ratio** $T_e/T_g$ (assume $T_g = 320$ K). State the **engineering significance** of this ratio for food processing. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a) 1. **DBD principle (2 marks):** Two electrodes separated by a dielectric barrier (glass/ceramic). High-voltage AC (5–20 kV) creates filamentary microdischarges. Process gas (air, O₂, N₂, He, Ar) flows through the gap and is ionized. 2. **Reactive species (2 marks):** ROS — atomic O, ·OH, O₃, ¹O₂, H₂O₂; RNS — ·NO, NO₂, ONOO⁻; UV photons (UV-A, UV-B, UV-C); charged particles (electrons, ions). 3. **Three kill pathways (3 marks):** - **Membrane disruption** — lipid peroxidation and electroporation. - **DNA damage** — UV-induced pyrimidine dimers + radical-induced strand breaks. - **Enzyme/protein oxidation** — thiol group and aromatic amino acid attack. #### Model Solution to (b) **Given:** $\bar{\varepsilon} = 2.5$ eV; $T_g = 320$ K **Step 1 — Convert eV to Joules:** $$\bar{\varepsilon} = 2.5 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 4.005 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{J}$$ **Step 2 — Apply Boltzmann relation:** $$T_e = \frac{2\,\bar{\varepsilon}}{3\,k_B} = \frac{2 \times 4.005 \times 10^{-19}}{3 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23}}$$ $$T_e = \frac{8.01 \times 10^{-19}}{4.14 \times 10^{-23}} \approx 1.935 \times 10^{4}\,\text{K} \approx 19{,}350\,\text{K}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the non-equilibrium ratio:** $$\frac{T_e}{T_g} = \frac{19{,}350}{320} \approx 60.5$$ **Step 4 — Engineering significance (3 marks):** A $T_e/T_g$ ratio of ≈ 60 is the signature of a **deeply non-equilibrium plasma**. It signifies that the system delivers **electron-driven chemistry equivalent to a 19,000 K environment**, yet the bulk gas (and hence the food surface) experiences a temperature rise of **less than 20 K** above ambient. This enables continuous microbial inactivation of fresh produce, dairy, and ready-to-eat meals **without cooking the product**, retaining nutrients (vitamin C, anthocyanins) and texture. It is the defining engineering advantage of cold plasma over both thermal pasteurization and conventional chemical sanitizers. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where You Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the eV-to-J conversion:** A common 2-mark deduction. Memorise: $1\,\text{eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J. > 2. **Using the gas temperature as the electron temperature:** The two are completely different in cold plasma; confusing them is the single biggest conceptual error. > 3. **Skipping the engineering interpretation:** Calculation alone earns ~4 marks; the *significance* discussion is what elevates the answer to a 7. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision — Memorise These Before Entering the Exam Hall:** - [ ] **Ozone is O₃**, redox potential $E^\circ = +2.07$ V, **GRAS since 2001 (US FDA)**, must be generated on-site. - [ ] **Ozone generation routes:** (1) **Corona/DBD** (industrial standard), (2) **UV-C photolysis** (185 nm), (3) **Electrolytic** (BDD anode). - [ ] **Ozone dissolves per Henry's law:** $C_a = k_H^{cp} \cdot P_{O_3}$; decays by first-order kinetics in water. - [ ] **Cold plasma is non-equilibrium:** $T_e \approx 10^4$–$10^5$ K, $T_g \approx 300$–$350$ K, ratio $T_e/T_g \approx 30$–$100$. - [ ] **Three common plasma reactors:** DBD, atmospheric plasma jet, gliding arc. - [ ] **Four reactive species classes:** ROS, RNS, UV photons, charged particles. - [ ] **Microbial kill follows Chick's law:** $\log(N/N_0) = -kCt$ (first-order in both $C$ and $N$). - [ ] **D-value** = time for 1-log reduction; for $n$-log reduction, $t_{n} = n \times D$. - [ ] **Weibull model** is the modern, more accurate fit: $\log(N/N_0) = -(t/\delta)^p$. - [ ] **Industrial O₃ yield** ≈ 50–150 g/kWh (DBD, dry air); 1 eV = 11600 K; $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K. - [ ] **Multi-target kill** (lipids + proteins + DNA) makes resistance development **rare** — a key essay point. - [ ] **Regulatory limit (OSHA):** 0.1 ppm (8-hr TWA) for ambient O₃; off-gas **must be catalytically destroyed**. - [ ] **Draw diagrams** in Part B answers — neat block diagrams of the ozone system and the DBD reactor fetch easy 2–3 marks. - [ ] Always **state assumptions** before applying Chick-Watson or first-order plasma kinetics — examiners reward methodological rigour. - [ ] **Watch the units:** mg/L · L/mg · min = min. Mixing units costs 1–2 marks silently. > **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Ozone oxidises chemically; cold plasma oxidises AND etches AND electroporates AND photolyses — all without cooking the food."* If you can defend this sentence with the equations above, you have mastered Module 2.

topics.title: Radiation Processing of Foods - Food Irradiation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT631 - NON-THERMAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Radiation Processing of Foods - Food Irradiation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Food Irradiation** is a non-thermal food preservation technique in which food products are exposed to controlled levels of **ionizing electromagnetic radiation** (gamma rays, X-rays) or **ionizing particulate radiation** (accelerated electrons) to achieve specific objectives such as microbial inactivation, insect disinfestation, sprout inhibition, delay of ripening, and shelf-life extension.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Official Definition (Codex Alimentarius / IAEA, 2003):** "Food irradiation is a physical process in which food is exposed to a defined dose of ionizing radiation, for the purpose of improving its safety, shelf-life, and/or functional properties, while preserving its nutritional and sensory qualities."

The radiation dose is defined as the **energy absorbed per unit mass** of the food material.

$$D = \frac{E}{m}$$

where:
- $D$ = Absorbed dose (Gray, Gy)
- $E$ = Energy absorbed (Joules, J)
- $m$ = Mass of absorbing material (kg)

**Standard Units & Conversions:**
- **1 Gray (Gy) = 1 J/kg = 100 rad**
- **1 kGy = 1000 Gy = 100,000 rad**
- **1 Mrad = 10 kGy**

> [!NOTE]  
> **Authorized Dose Limit (WHO/FAO/IAEA Joint Study):** The overall average dose absorbed by a food should not exceed **10 kGy**, although higher doses are permitted for specific applications where there is no detectable radiological hazard.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a food factory where we want to kill harmful bacteria, neutralize insects, and prevent sprouting in potatoes. Instead of using heat (which can degrade nutrients and flavor), imagine using **"invisible high-energy bullets"** that pass through the food like a flashlight beam — but these bullets are powerful enough to:

1. **Break the DNA strands** inside bacteria, viruses, insects, and parasites — leaving them unable to reproduce.
2. **Spray free electrons** inside the food matrix that disrupt cellular machinery without making the food radioactive.
3. **Punch holes in the genetic blueprint** of sprouting buds in onions, potatoes, and garlic — keeping them dormant and fresh.

> [!TIP]  
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of food irradiation like a **"microscopic cosmic-ray bath"**. Just as the Sun's UV light tans your skin without making you radioactive, food irradiation uses controlled, higher-energy ionizing rays (similar in concept but much more energetic than X-rays at the dentist) to disinfect food — but **the food itself does not become radioactive**, just like your skin doesn't glow after sunbathing.

### 1.3 Why This Topic is a KTU High-Yield Module

Radiation processing of food is one of the most regulated and scientifically validated non-thermal technologies worldwide. It is explicitly listed under **Module 3 of PEFTT631** in the KTU 2024 Scheme, making it a **mandatory topic for both Continuous Evaluation (CE) and End Semester Examinations (ESE)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Syllabus Highlights (KTU 2024 – PEFTT631 Module 3):**  
> 1. Principles of radiation processing  
> 2. Types of ionizing radiations and their sources  
> 3. Dose measurement and units  
> 4. Applications (radurization, radicidation, radappertization)  
> 5. Effects on food quality and safety  
> 6. Regulatory framework and labeling  

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Ionizing Radiations Used in Food Processing

Ionizing radiations are classified based on their **penetrating power**, **ionizing energy**, and **physical state**. Only three types are authorized worldwide for food irradiation by the **Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC)** and the **U.S. Food and Drug Administration (USFDA)**.

| Radiation Type | Source | Nature | Penetrating Power | Approved for Food |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Gamma rays ($\gamma$)** | Cobalt-60 ($^{60}$Co) and Cesium-137 ($^{137}$Cs) radioisotopes | Electromagnetic waves (high frequency) | Very high (penetrates full pallets) | **YES** |
| **X-rays** | Generated by e-beam accelerators striking a metal target | Electromagnetic waves | Very high (similar to $\gamma$) | **YES** (max energy 5 MeV) |
| **Electron beams (e-beam)** | Electron accelerators | High-velocity electrons | Low–medium (penetrates $\leq$ 10 cm) | **YES** (max energy 10 MeV) |

> [!WARNING]  
> **Why a Maximum Energy Limit?** Energy above 10 MeV for electrons and 5 MeV for X-rays/gamma rays can **induce radioactivity** in the food itself through photonuclear reactions. KTU often asks this as a conceptual 3-mark question.

### 2.2 Sources of Ionizing Radiations

#### 2.2.1 Cobalt-60 ($^{60}$Co) — The Workhorse of Food Irradiation

- Radioactive decay equation:
$$^{60}_{27}\text{Co} \rightarrow\, ^{60}_{28}\text{Ni}\, +\, \beta^{-}\, +\, \bar{\nu}_{e}\, +\, 2\gamma$$
- Emits **two gamma photons** per decay with energies of **1.17 MeV** and **1.33 MeV** (average $\approx$ **1.25 MeV**).
- Half-life: **5.27 years** — long and stable for commercial use.
- Cannot be turned off; decays continuously. Food must be brought to and from the source in a controlled shielded facility.

#### 2.2.2 Cesium-137 ($^{137}$Cs)

- Byproduct of nuclear fission reactors.
- Emits a single gamma photon of energy **0.662 MeV**.
- Half-life: **30.17 years**.
- Used less frequently because of the security and political issues surrounding its source (a fission product).

#### 2.2.3 Electron Accelerators (e-beam)

- **Energy range:** 0.3 MeV to 10 MeV.
- **Advantages:** Can be switched off, no radioactive source, high dose rates (very fast processing).
- **Disadvantage:** Limited penetration depth; food must be in thin layers.

### 2.3 Dose Classification and Corresponding Applications

This is one of the **most frequently asked 7-mark questions** in KTU 2024 ESE for Module 3.

| Dose Level | Dose Range | Process Name | Primary Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Low dose** | < 1 kGy | **Radurization** | Inhibition of sprouting (potatoes, onions, garlic); insect disinfestation; parasite inactivation (e.g., *Trichinella* in pork) |
| **Medium dose** | 1 kGy – 10 kGy | **Radicidation** | Reduction of spoilage and pathogenic microorganisms; shelf-life extension of meat, fish, fruits, vegetables |
| **High dose** | > 10 kGy (commercial sterility) | **Radappertization** | Sterilization of meat, poultry, seafood for long-term ambient storage (analogous to canning, without heat) |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:**  
> - **Ra-du-ri-za-tion** = *du*ring storage (low)  
> - **Ra-di-ci-da-tion** = *ci*vilized microbe control (medium)  
> - **Ra-dap-per-ti-za-tion** = *ap*erture sterilized = fully sterile (high)

### 2.4 Mechanism of Action: Direct vs. Indirect Effects

The destruction of microorganisms and other biological targets by ionizing radiation occurs through **two simultaneous mechanisms**:

#### A) Direct Effect
Ionizing radiation directly strikes the **DNA molecule** of the microorganism, causing:
- **Single-strand breaks (SSB)** in the DNA backbone
- **Double-strand breaks (DSB)** — far more lethal
- **Base damage** and **cross-linking** of DNA
- **Loss of chromosomal integrity**

The cell cannot replicate or transcribe its DNA → cell death.

#### B) Indirect Effect
The radiation ionizes water molecules (the dominant component of food and microbial cells), producing highly reactive **free radicals**:

$$H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{ionizing radiation}} H_2O^{+} + e^{-}$$

$$H_2O^{+} \rightarrow H^{+} + \cdot OH$$

$$H_2O + e^{-} \rightarrow \cdot H + OH^{-}$$

These free radicals ($\cdot$OH, $\cdot$H, $e^{-}_{aq}$) are extremely reactive and cause:
- **Peroxidation of membrane lipids**
- **Oxidation of proteins and enzymes**
- **DNA strand scission**
- **Disruption of cellular membranes**

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> In moist foods (which constitute the majority of foods), the **indirect effect dominates** and accounts for approximately **60–90%** of microbial inactivation. In dry or frozen foods, the direct effect is more significant.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = \dfrac{E}{m}$ | Absorbed dose = energy per unit mass | Gy (J/kg) |
| 2 | $D_{10}$ or $D$-value | Dose required to reduce microbial population by 90% (1 log cycle) | kGy |
| 3 | $\log(N_0 / N) = D / D_{10}$ | Log reduction in microbial count | dimensionless |
| 4 | $N = N_0 \cdot 10^{-D / D_{10}}$ | Surviving microbial population | CFU/g |
| 5 | $G(\text{value}) = \dfrac{\text{Number of species formed}}{100\ \text{eV absorbed}}$ | Chemical yield per unit radiation energy | species / 100 eV |
| 6 | $1\ \text{Gy} = 1\ \dfrac{J}{kg} = 100\ \text{rad}$ | SI–cgs conversion | — |
| 7 | $1\ \text{kGy} = 10^3\ \text{Gy}$ | kilo prefix | — |
| 8 | $E_\gamma(^{60}\text{Co}) \approx 1.25\ \text{MeV}$ (avg) | Average gamma energy of Cobalt-60 | MeV |
| 9 | $E_\gamma(^{137}\text{Cs}) = 0.662\ \text{MeV}$ | Gamma energy of Cesium-137 | MeV |
| 10 | $E_{\max}(\text{e-beam}) = 10\ \text{MeV}$ | Max accelerator energy to avoid induced radioactivity | MeV |
| 11 | $E_{\max}(\text{X-ray}) = 5\ \text{MeV}$ | Max X-ray energy approved for food | MeV |
| 12 | $t_{1/2}(^{60}\text{Co}) = 5.27\ \text{yr}$ | Half-life of Cobalt-60 | years |
| 13 | $t_{1/2}(^{137}\text{Cs}) = 30.17\ \text{yr}$ | Half-life of Cesium-137 | years |
| 14 | $P = D \cdot \dot{D}$ | Process rate (kg/h) = mass throughput × dose rate | — |
| 15 | $k = \dfrac{\ln 10}{D_{10}} = \dfrac{2.303}{D_{10}}$ | Radiation inactivation rate constant | kGy$^{-1}$ |

> [!TIP]  
> **Engineering Utility:** In commercial food irradiation plants, the $D_{10}$ value is critical for designing the **process validation protocol**, determining the conveyor speed, and ensuring that the product receives the legally required minimum dose. Regulatory bodies (e.g., USFDA, FSSAI) require the minimum dose to be at least the value that achieves the intended effect, and the maximum dose must be within the safety limits defined by the Codex Alimentarius.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Microbial Inactivation Kinetics (Log-Linear Survival Model)

The microbial population surviving a radiation dose follows a **log-linear (exponential) decay** relationship, analogous to thermal inactivation kinetics. Let us derive this relationship step by step.

**Step 1: Define the rate of inactivation.**

The rate of microbial inactivation is assumed to be **first-order** with respect to the surviving population $N$:

$$\frac{dN}{dD} = -k \cdot N$$

where:
- $N$ = Number of surviving microorganisms (CFU/g)
- $D$ = Radiation dose (kGy)
- $k$ = Radiation inactivation rate constant (kGy$^{-1}$)

**Step 2: Separate variables and integrate from initial dose $D = 0$ (where $N = N_0$) to dose $D$.**

$$\int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN}{N} = -k \int_{0}^{D} dD$$

**Step 3: Evaluate both integrals.**

$$\ln(N) - \ln(N_0) = -k D$$

**Step 4: Simplify using the natural logarithm property.**

$$\ln\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -k D$$

**Step 5: Exponentiate both sides.**

$$\frac{N}{N_0} = e^{-k D}$$

$$N = N_0 \cdot e^{-k D}$$

**Step 6: Convert from natural log to base-10 logarithm for practical use.**

Using $k = \dfrac{2.303}{D_{10}}$ (the radiation $D_{10}$ value):

$$\frac{N}{N_0} = e^{-\frac{2.303}{D_{10}} \cdot D} = 10^{-D/D_{10}}$$

$$\boxed{\,N = N_0 \cdot 10^{-D/D_{10}}\,}$$

**Step 7: Rearrange for the log reduction in microbial count.**

Taking $\log_{10}$ on both sides:

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\frac{D}{D_{10}}$$

$$\boxed{\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = \frac{D}{D_{10}}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> The quantity $\log_{10}(N_0/N)$ is called the **log reduction** and represents the number of decimal reductions achieved by the applied dose $D$.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example: Calculating Required Dose

**Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern):** A batch of fresh chicken meat has an initial *Salmonella* count of $N_0 = 1 \times 10^5$ CFU/g. The $D_{10}$ value of *Salmonella* in chicken at 5 °C is **0.35 kGy**. Calculate the dose required to reduce the count to **$1 \times 10^1$ CFU/g** (i.e., 4 log reduction).

**Step 1: Identify the knowns.**

$$N_0 = 1 \times 10^5\ \text{CFU/g}, \quad N = 1 \times 10^1\ \text{CFU/g}, \quad D_{10} = 0.35\ \text{kGy}$$

**Step 2: Compute the log reduction.**

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10^5}{10^1}\right) = \log_{10}(10^4) = 4$$

**Step 3: Apply the log-linear survival model.**

$$D = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) \cdot D_{10} = 4 \times 0.35\ \text{kGy}$$

$$\boxed{\,D = 1.40\ \text{kGy}\,}$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**

A dose of **1.40 kGy** (which lies in the *medium dose / radicidation* range) achieves a **4-log reduction** in *Salmonella* count in chicken, sufficient to meet the typical regulatory target of < 100 CFU/g for poultry.

### 3.3 Derivation: Relationship Between Radiation Dose and Energy Deposited

**Step 1: Start from the definition of absorbed dose.**

$$D = \frac{E}{m}$$

**Step 2: For a packaged food of mass $m$ placed in front of a Cobalt-60 source, the absorbed energy $E$ depends on the source activity $A$, the irradiation time $t$, the gamma ray energy $E_\gamma$, and the geometry factor $G$ (which captures the source-to-food distance and attenuation):**

$$E = A \cdot t \cdot E_\gamma \cdot G \cdot \eta$$

where:
- $A$ = Activity of the source (Becquerels, Bq or Curies, Ci; $1\ \text{Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10}\ \text{Bq}$)
- $t$ = Exposure time (s)
- $E_\gamma$ = Average gamma photon energy (J)
- $G$ = Geometry factor (dimensionless, < 1)
- $\eta$ = Mass energy absorption coefficient of the food (m²/kg)

**Step 3: Substitute into the dose equation.**

$$D = \frac{A \cdot t \cdot E_\gamma \cdot G \cdot \eta}{m}$$

**Step 4: Practical rearrangement — determine exposure time $t$ for a target dose.**

$$t = \frac{D \cdot m}{A \cdot E_\gamma \cdot G \cdot \eta}$$

> [!TIP]  
> **Engineering Application:** This equation is the **design equation** used by irradiation plant engineers to size the source, decide the conveyor speed, and calculate the throughput (kg/h) for a commercial irradiation facility.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Dose on Various Food Quality Parameters

| Food Component | Effect at Low Dose (< 1 kGy) | Effect at Medium Dose (1–10 kGy) | Effect at High Dose (> 10 kGy) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Microorganisms** | Mostly unaffected; some inactivation of parasites | Significant inactivation of spoilage and pathogenic bacteria | Complete sterilization (radappertization) |
| **Insects** | Sterilized / killed | Killed | Killed |
| **Sprouts (onion, potato)** | Inhibited | Inhibited | Inhibited |
| **Vitamins (Water-soluble: B1, C)** | Minimal loss | Moderate loss (10–25%) | Significant loss (up to 50%) |
| **Vitamins (Fat-soluble: A, E)** | Negligible loss | Small loss | Moderate loss |
| **Proteins** | Slight denaturation possible | Mild denaturation, texture softening | Significant denaturation, off-flavors |
| **Lipids** | Negligible | Slight oxidation → off-flavors | Marked oxidation, rancidity |
| **Color (pigments)** | Minimal change | Browning possible in meat (myoglobin oxidation) | Significant color changes |
| **Wholesomeness** | Maintained | Maintained (within 10 kGy overall) | Maintained (per WHO/IAEA) |
| **Induced radioactivity** | None | None | None (within authorized energy limits) |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **The 10 kGy "Bright Line":** The Joint FAO/IAEA/WHO Study Group concluded in 1980 (and reaffirmed in 1997) that food irradiated at doses **up to 10 kGy** is **safe and nutritionally adequate** — this is the regulatory "bright line" for food irradiation worldwide.

### 3.5 G-Value (Radiation Chemical Yield) — Concept Derivation

**Step 1: Definition.** The G-value represents the **number of chemical species (ions, radicals, or molecules) produced or destroyed per 100 eV of absorbed radiation energy.**

$$G = \frac{\text{Number of species produced or destroyed}}{\text{Absorbed energy (in 100 eV units)}}$$

**Step 2: Units and typical values.**

- **G(•OH) ≈ 2.7 radicals per 100 eV** in pure water.
- **G(H•) ≈ 0.6 radicals per 100 eV**.
- **G(e$^-_{aq}$) ≈ 2.7 hydrated electrons per 100 eV**.

**Step 3: Conversion to SI units.** Since 1 Gy = 1 J/kg = 6.24 × 10$^{15}$ eV/kg, the number of radicals formed per kg per Gy of dose is:

$$\text{Radicals/kg/Gy} = G \times 6.24 \times 10^{15} \times \dfrac{1}{100} = G \times 6.24 \times 10^{13}$$

> [!TIP]  
> The G-value concept is borrowed from radiation chemistry and helps food scientists predict the **extent of chemical changes** in food constituents at a given dose.

### 3.6 Comparative Analysis: Radiation vs. Other Non-Thermal Processes

| Parameter | Food Irradiation | High-Pressure Processing (HPP) | Pulsed Electric Field (PEF) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Preservation agent** | Ionizing radiation (γ, X-ray, e-beam) | High pressure (400–600 MPa) | High-voltage electric pulses |
| **Temperature rise** | Negligible | Minimal (low adiabatic heat) | Minimal |
| **Microbial inactivation mechanism** | DNA damage (direct + indirect) | Protein denaturation, membrane damage | Electroporation of membranes |
| **Penetration** | Deep (whole pallets possible) | Limited by vessel size | Limited to chamber |
| **Continuous operation** | Yes (e-beam) | Batch | Continuous possible |
| **Capital cost** | High (shielding) | High (pressure vessel) | Moderate |
| **Regulatory status** | Approved in 60+ countries | Approved globally | Approved in some regions |
| **Consumer acceptance** | Often low (labeling fears) | Moderate–high | Moderate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Overall Food Irradiation Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Food Material Intake] --> B[Sorting and Cleaning]
    B --> C[Pre-conditioning e.g. Cooling, Packaging]
    C --> D[Conveyor System Loading]
    D --> E{Selection of Radiation Source}
    E -->|Gamma Rays| F1[Co-60 Source Pool]
    E -->|Electron Beam| F2[e-beam Accelerator]
    E -->|X-rays| F3[Metal Target Converter]
    F1 --> G[Controlled Exposure Chamber]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Real-time Dosimetry Monitoring]
    H --> I{Dose within Target Range?}
    I -->|No| J[Reject Batch, Reprocess]
    I -->|Yes| K[Storage and Distribution]
    J --> B
    K --> L[End Consumer with Irradiated Symbol]
    H --> M[Quality Control and Validation]
    M --> K
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Mechanism of Microbial Inactivation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph R[Radiation Source]
        R1[Gamma Rays or E-beam or X-rays]
    end
    R --> R1
    R1 --> W[Food Matrix with Water and Microbes]
    subgraph A[Direct Effect]
        D1[DNA Double Strand Break]
        D2[DNA Single Strand Break]
        D3[Base Damage and Crosslinking]
    end
    R1 --> A
    subgraph B[Indirect Effect via Water Radiolysis]
        I1[H2O ionizes to H2O plus and electron]
        I2[Free Radicals OH, H, e-aq]
        I3[Lipid Peroxidation]
        I4[Protein Oxidation]
    end
    R1 --> B
    A --> OUT[Microbial Cell Death]
    B --> OUT
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree: Dose-Process Selection for Common Foods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Food Type] --> Q1{Goal}
    Q1 -->|Sprout inhibition| P1[Low Dose 0.05 to 0.15 kGy]
    Q1 -->|Insect disinfestation| P2[Low Dose 0.15 to 0.5 kGy]
    Q1 -->|Parasite inactivation| P3[Low Dose 0.3 to 1 kGy]
    Q1 -->|Pathogen reduction| P4[Medium Dose 1 to 10 kGy]
    Q1 -->|Commercial sterility| P5[High Dose 10 to 45 kGy]
    P1 --> EX1[Potato, Onion, Garlic]
    P2 --> EX2[Grains, Spices, Dried Fruits]
    P3 --> EX3[Pork for Trichinella]
    P4 --> EX4[Meat, Poultry, Seafood, Fresh Produce]
    P5 --> EX5[Sterile Meat, Astronaut Foods, Hospital Sterile Diets]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Industrial Gamma Irradiation Plant Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph S[Shielded Concrete Cell]
        P1[Source Rack Co-60 Pencils]
        P2[Water Pool Storage]
    end
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> RAISED[Raise Source Above Water]
    RAISED --> CHAMBER[Product Conveyor Passes Through Chamber]
    CHAMBER --> DOSIM[Dosimeters at Multiple Positions]
    DOSIM --> CONTROL[PLC Control System]
    CONTROL --> LOWER[Lower Source Back to Pool for Safe Stop]
    subgraph LOG[Logistics]
        IN[Infeed Conveyor]
        OUT[Outfeed Conveyor]
    end
    IN --> CHAMBER
    CHAMBER --> OUT
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Tree Diagram: Regulatory Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Global Regulatory Framework for Food Irradiation] --> G1[Codex Alimentarius CAC]
    ROOT --> G2[WHO, FAO, IAEA Joint Consultations]
    ROOT --> G3[Country-specific National Authorities]
    G1 --> C1[General Standard for Irradiated Foods CAC RCP 19 1979 Rev 2003]
    G3 --> N1[US FDA 21 CFR 179 26]
    G3 --> N2[EU Directive 1999 2 EC]
    G3 --> N3[India FSSAI Atomic Energy Radiation Processing Rules 2012]
    G3 --> N4[Other countries Brazil, Mexico, China, Japan, South Africa, Pakistan]
    N3 --> L[Mandatory Labeling with Radura Symbol and Text]
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **The "Radura" Symbol:** The international food irradiation symbol is called the **Radura** — a stylized plant inside a broken circle. It is **mandatory** in many countries (e.g., India under FSSAI) to label any food that has been irradiated, even if no irradiation was applied in the final country of sale.
