<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Pattern, Module 3]**

**Q: Define food irradiation. List any three ionizing radiations approved for food processing.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Food irradiation is a non-thermal preservation technique in which food is exposed to controlled levels of ionizing electromagnetic radiation (such as gamma rays or X-rays) or accelerated electron beams. The purpose is to inactivate spoilage and pathogenic microorganisms, control insect infestation, inhibit sprouting, or achieve commercial sterility — all without significantly raising the food temperature.

**Three approved ionizing radiations:**
1. **Gamma rays ($\gamma$)** — emitted by radioactive isotopes **Cobalt-60** and **Cesium-137**.
2. **X-rays** — produced by bombarding a metal target with high-energy electrons (max energy **5 MeV** for food use).
3. **Electron beams (e-beam)** — produced by industrial electron accelerators (max energy **10 MeV** for food use).

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write only "gamma rays, X-rays, and UV rays" — **UV is NOT an ionizing radiation** and is NOT approved for food irradiation. This single mistake costs 1 full mark. Also, **alpha and beta particles are not approved** for food irradiation because they cannot penetrate the food safely.

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Pattern, Module 3]**

**Q: Differentiate between radurization, radicidation, and radappertization.**

**Model Answer:**

| Process | Dose Range | Objective | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Radurization** | < 1 kGy (low dose) | Prolong shelf life by reducing spoilage organisms; inhibit sprouting; kill insects and parasites | Potatoes, onions, garlic, wheat, spices |
| **Radicidation** | 1 – 10 kGy (medium dose) | Eliminate specific pathogenic and spoilage microorganisms | Fresh meat, poultry, fish, fresh produce |
| **Radappertization** | > 10 kGy (high dose) | Achieve commercial sterility (similar to canned food) | Sterile meat, seafood, hospital diets, space foods |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option A**
**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Pattern, Module 3]**

**Q: a) Explain the principles of food irradiation. Discuss the mechanism of microbial inactivation by ionizing radiation with suitable reactions. (7 Marks)**

**b) A batch of frozen seafood has an initial *Listeria monocytogenes* count of $5 \times 10^6$ CFU/g. The $D_{10}$ value at the processing temperature is **0.45 kGy**. Calculate: (i) The radiation dose required to achieve a **5-log reduction**, and (ii) The final microbial count if a dose of **1.8 kGy** is applied. (7 Marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Part (a) — Principles and Mechanism of Microbial Inactivation (7 Marks)

**1. Principles of Food Irradiation (3 Marks):**

- **Principle of Energy Deposition:** Food is exposed to a precisely controlled dose of ionizing radiation. The energy is absorbed by the food molecules (mainly water) and the microbial cells present in the food.

- **Principle of Non-Radioactivity:** When using authorized radiation sources and energy limits (≤ 10 MeV electrons, ≤ 5 MeV X-rays and gamma), the food itself does **not** become radioactive. This is because the photon energies are below the threshold for photonuclear reactions.

- **Principle of Ionization:** The radiation ionizes water and biological molecules, producing ions, free electrons, and free radicals, which are the actual biocidal agents.

**2. Mechanism of Microbial Inactivation (4 Marks):**

The mechanism occurs via two parallel pathways:

**(A) Direct Effect (approx. 10–40% of inactivation):**
Ionizing radiation directly damages the **DNA** of the microorganism. The radiation breaks the sugar-phosphate backbone of DNA, creating:
- **Single-strand breaks (SSB)** — repairable.
- **Double-strand breaks (DSB)** — often lethal.
- **Base damage and crosslinking** — disrupts replication and transcription.

**(B) Indirect Effect (approx. 60–90% of inactivation in moist food):**
Water radiolysis generates highly reactive free radicals:

$$H_2O \xrightarrow{\gamma\ \text{or e-beam}} H_2O^{+} + e^{-}$$

$$H_2O^{+} \rightarrow H^{+} + \cdot OH$$

$$H_2O + e^{-}_{aq} \rightarrow \cdot H + OH^{-}$$

These radicals ($\cdot$OH, $\cdot$H, e$^-_{aq}$) then attack:
- Microbial **membrane lipids** → lipid peroxidation → membrane rupture.
- **Proteins and enzymes** → oxidation, unfolding, loss of activity.
- **DNA** → strand breaks and crosslinking.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Final Result:** The microorganism loses its ability to replicate, repair, and maintain metabolism → cell death.

---

### Model Answer — Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $N_0 = 5 \times 10^6$ CFU/g
- $D_{10} = 0.45$ kGy

#### Part (i) — Dose required for 5-log reduction (3.5 Marks)

**Step 1: Recall the log-linear survival model.**

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = \frac{D}{D_{10}}$$

**Step 2: For 5-log reduction,**

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = 5$$

**Step 3: Solve for $D$.**

$$D = 5 \times D_{10} = 5 \times 0.45\ \text{kGy}$$

$$\boxed{\,D = 2.25\ \text{kGy}\,}$$

#### Part (ii) — Final count at $D = 1.8$ kGy (3.5 Marks)

**Step 1: Apply the survival equation.**

$$N = N_0 \cdot 10^{-D/D_{10}}$$

**Step 2: Compute the dose ratio.**

$$\frac{D}{D_{10}} = \frac{1.8}{0.45} = 4.0$$

**Step 3: Substitute all values.**

$$N = (5 \times 10^6) \cdot 10^{-4.0}$$

$$N = 5 \times 10^6 \times 10^{-4} = 5 \times 10^{2}\ \text{CFU/g}$$

$$\boxed{\,N = 500\ \text{CFU/g}\,}$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**

A dose of **1.8 kGy** produces a **4-log reduction**, bringing the *Listeria* count from $5 \times 10^6$ CFU/g down to **500 CFU/g**, which is well within the safety threshold for frozen seafood (typically < 1000 CFU/g).

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the log reduction formula. The correct form is $\log_{10}(N_0/N)$, **not** $\log_{10}(N/N_0)$. Using the wrong sign produces a negative dose — an immediate zero. Also, do not forget to write the **units (kGy, CFU/g)** in the final answer; the unit itself often carries 0.5 mark.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option B**
**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024 Pattern, Module 3]**

**Q: a) Describe the dose classification for food irradiation. Give at least two applications for each dose category with specific examples of foods. (7 Marks)**

**b) Discuss the advantages and limitations of food irradiation as a non-thermal preservation method. Compare it briefly with thermal processing and HPP. (7 Marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Part (a) — Dose Classification and Applications (7 Marks)

The Codex Alimentarius Commission classifies food irradiation doses into **three categories** based on the radiation dose absorbed by the food:

**1. Low Dose (< 1 kGy) — Radurization (3 Marks):**
- **Inhibition of sprouting:** Potatoes, onions, garlic (dose 0.05–0.15 kGy).
- **Insect disinfestation:** Cereals, wheat, dried fruits, pulses (dose 0.15–0.5 kGy).
- **Parasite inactivation:** *Trichinella spiralis* in pork (dose 0.3–1.0 kGy).
- **Delay of ripening:** Bananas, mangoes, papayas (dose 0.25–0.5 kGy).

**2. Medium Dose (1–10 kGy) — Radicidation (2 Marks):**
- **Reduction of spoilage microorganisms:** Fresh and frozen meat, poultry, fish, seafood (dose 1–3 kGy).
- **Reduction of pathogens:** *Salmonella*, *Campylobacter*, *E. coli* in chicken, beef, and pork (dose 1.5–5 kGy).
- **Shelf-life extension of fresh produce:** Strawberries, mushrooms, asparagus (dose 1–3 kGy).
- **Microbial decontamination of spices and herbs:** Pepper, turmeric, paprika (dose 3–10 kGy).

**3. High Dose (> 10 kGy) — Radappertization (2 Marks):**
- **Commercial sterilization of meat and poultry:** Dose 25–45 kGy.
- **Sterilized ready-to-eat meals for hospitals and immuno-compromised patients.**
- **Astronaut and military rations (long-shelf-life ambient storage).**
- **Sterilization of enzyme preparations and food additives.**

> [!NOTE]  
> The internationally accepted **maximum overall average dose** is **10 kGy**, as confirmed by the Joint FAO/IAEA/WHO Study Group (1980, reaffirmed 1997). Above this dose, foods are also safe, but require case-by-case safety evaluation.

---

### Model Answer — Part (b) — Advantages, Limitations, and Comparison (7 Marks)

**1. Advantages of Food Irradiation (3 Marks):**
- **Effective microbial control** — eliminates pathogens like *Salmonella*, *E. coli*, *Listeria*, and *Campylobacter* without heat.
- **Cold process** — temperature rise is minimal, preserving heat-sensitive nutrients (vitamin C, B-vitamins) and sensory qualities.
- **Deep penetration** — gamma rays and X-rays can penetrate the **entire product** including pre-packaged foods.
- **Continuous and residue-free** — no chemical residue, no radioactivity, environmentally clean.
- **Versatile** — works on spices, meat, produce, grains, and packaged ready-to-eat meals.
- **Post-packaging treatment** — recontamination after processing is prevented.

**2. Limitations of Food Irradiation (2 Marks):**
- **High capital cost** — requires a heavily shielded facility, radiation source, and trained operators.
- **Consumer perception and labeling** — negative public perception due to the word "radiation" and the **Radura label** requirement.
- **Lipid oxidation and off-flavors** at higher doses in fatty foods.
- **Loss of vitamin A, E, and C** at high doses.
- **Texture softening** in fruits and vegetables.
- **Limited effect on viruses and prions** (need very high doses).
- **No effect on toxins already produced** by pathogens (e.g., *Staphylococcus aureus* enterotoxin).

**3. Comparison with Thermal Processing and HPP (2 Marks):**

| Parameter | Food Irradiation | Thermal Processing | HPP |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Process agent** | Ionizing radiation | Heat (steam, hot water) | High pressure (400–600 MPa) |
| **Nutrient retention** | High (low heat) | Low (heat-labile vitamins lost) | High (no heat) |
| **Texture of food** | Preserved | Often softened | Preserved |
| **Spore inactivation** | Possible at high dose | Yes (with sufficient $F_0$ value) | Limited (vegetative cells only) |
| **Penetration** | Full depth (gamma, X-ray) | Full depth | Limited by vessel |
| **Consumer acceptance** | Low–moderate (Radura) | High | Moderate–high |
| **Regulatory approval** | Limited to 60+ countries | Universal | Growing globally |

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write that irradiation "makes food radioactive" — this is **incorrect** for authorized doses and energy limits. The food never becomes radioactive; only the radiation passes through it. Confusing this loses **2 marks** in the advantages section.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **High-Yield KTU 2024 Module 3 — Quick-Recall Notes**

- ✅ **Definition:** Food irradiation = exposure of food to controlled ionizing radiation to improve safety and shelf life.

- ✅ **Three Approved Sources:** Gamma rays ($^{60}$Co, $^{137}$Cs), X-rays (≤ 5 MeV), Electron beams (≤ 10 MeV).

- ✅ **Approved because:** Energy below thresholds prevents induced radioactivity.

- ✅ **Dose Unit:** **1 Gy = 1 J/kg = 100 rad**; 1 kGy = 10$^3$ Gy.

- ✅ **Dose Categories:**
  - **Low (< 1 kGy) = Radurization** → sprout inhibition, insect control, parasite inactivation.
  - **Medium (1–10 kGy) = Radicidation** → pathogen reduction, shelf-life extension.
  - **High (> 10 kGy) = Radappertization** → commercial sterility.

- ✅ **WHO/FAO/IAEA "Bright Line" Safety Limit:** **10 kGy** (overall average).

- ✅ **Mechanism:** Direct (DNA breaks) + Indirect (free radicals from water radiolysis: $\cdot$OH, $\cdot$H, e$^-_{aq}$).

- ✅ **Key Formulae (must memorize):**
  - $D = E/m$ (Dose definition)
  - $N = N_0 \cdot 10^{-D/D_{10}}$ (Log-linear survival)
  - $D = \log_{10}(N_0/N) \cdot D_{10}$ (Dose for log reduction)

- ✅ **Sources Compared:** $^60$Co (1.17 + 1.33 MeV γ, $t_{1/2}$ = 5.27 yr); $^{137}$Cs (0.662 MeV γ, $t_{1/2}$ = 30.17 yr); e-beam (high dose rate, switchable, shallow penetration).

- ✅ **G-Value:** Radicals produced per 100 eV absorbed (G($\cdot$OH) ≈ 2.7 in water).

- ✅ **Radura Symbol:** Mandatory international symbol on irradiated food labels.

- ✅ **Dose Effect Quality:** Low dose = minimal nutrient loss; High dose = lipid oxidation, off-flavors, vitamin loss.

- ✅ **Regulatory Bodies:** Codex Alimentarius, WHO, FAO, IAEA, USFDA, FSSAI (India).

- ✅ **KTU-Favorite Numerical Question:** Calculate dose for X-log reduction using $D_{10}$ value — practice 3–5 problems.

- ✅ **Common Mistake to Avoid:** "Irradiated food becomes radioactive" — **FALSE**. Food never becomes radioactive under approved conditions.

- ✅ **Comparison Anchor:** Irradiation is most similar to pasteurization in effect, but is a "cold" process — like HPP, it preserves heat-labile nutrients.

- ✅ **Industrial Practice:** Commercial irradiation plants exist in India at Vashi (Mumbai), Lasalgaon, Nashik, Bengaluru, and other locations regulated by AERB and FSSAI.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Microwave Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT631 - NON-THERMAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Microwave Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Microwave Processing** is a **dielectric heating** technique that uses **electromagnetic waves in the frequency range of 300 MHz to 300 GHz** to generate heat **volumetrically within a food material** through the rapid oscillation of polar molecules (primarily water) and ionic species, without requiring direct contact between the heating source and the product. In the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus, it is treated as a "pseudo-thermal / dielectric" process that bridges conventional thermal and emerging non-thermal food preservation technologies.

The two **industrial/scientific frequencies** allocated by the **ITU (International Telecommunication Union)** and the **FCC (Federal Communications Commission)** for industrial, scientific, and medical (ISM) use are:

- **915 MHz ± 13 MHz** → preferred for industrial heating (larger penetration depth)
- **2450 MHz ± 50 MHz** → used in domestic microwave ovens and laboratory research

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "Tug-of-War With Water Molecules"**

Imagine a **crowd of water molecules (polar dipoles)** standing inside a food. They normally point in random directions. Now, switch on a **microwave field** that rapidly flips its direction **2.45 billion times per second** (at 2450 MHz). Each water molecule, behaving like a tiny magnet, tries to **"keep up"** by spinning back and forth. This frantic molecular rotation generates **internal friction** — and that friction is **heat**.

So unlike a conventional oven, where heat travels from the **outside → inside** (surface to center), microwaves make the **entire food mass heat itself simultaneously**. The thermal gradient is *inverted*: heating begins at the center and proceeds outward. This is the essence of **volumetric / bulk dielectric heating**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world cue:** When you place a bowl of water and a piece of dry bread together in a microwave, the water boils but the bread stays cool — because the bread has very few polar molecules to oscillate. This proves that microwaves heat *the food*, not the *oven cavity*.

## 1.3 Electromagnetic Spectrum Position

| Band | Frequency Range | Typical Use |
|------|-----------------|-------------|
| Radio waves (RF) | 3 kHz – 300 MHz | Conventional induction, radio |
| **Microwaves** | **300 MHz – 300 GHz** | **Dielectric heating of foods** |
| Infrared | 300 GHz – 400 THz | Conventional surface heating |
| Visible light | 400 – 750 THz | Optical sensing |

The free-space wavelength of a 2450 MHz wave is:
$$\lambda_0 = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}{2450 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}} \approx 0.1224 \text{ m} = 12.24 \text{ cm}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics Behind Microwave Heating — Dielectric Polarization

Microwave heating is governed by **Maxwell's Equations** applied to a **lossy dielectric medium** (the food). The two dominant polarization mechanisms in the microwave band are:

1. **Dipolar (orientation) polarization** — alignment of permanent dipoles (e.g., H₂O) with the alternating field. This is the **primary mechanism** in moist foods.
2. **Ionic conduction polarization** — migration of dissolved ionic species (e.g., Na⁺, Cl⁻) back and forth with the field, generating Joule-type heating.

A third, weaker contribution comes from **interfacial (Maxwell–Wagner) polarization** at cell-membrane boundaries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Loss Tangent (tan δ)** — The single most important parameter in KTU problems:
> $$\tan \delta = \frac{\varepsilon''}{\varepsilon'}$$
> - **ε′ (dielectric constant / relative permittivity):** ability of a material to **store** electric energy.
> - **ε″ (dielectric loss factor):** ability of a material to **convert** stored electric energy into **heat**.

A material with **high tan δ** (e.g., water ≈ 0.123 at 2450 MHz, 25 °C) heats efficiently. A material with **low tan δ** (e.g., ice ≈ 0.0009, dry air ≈ 0) is nearly transparent to microwaves.

## 2.2 The Working Logic — Step-by-Step

1. **Magnetron** converts electrical (DC) energy → microwave energy.
2. **Waveguide** channels the microwaves to the applicator with minimum reflection.
3. **Mode stirrer / turntable** distributes the field uniformly inside the cavity.
4. **Incident wave** penetrates the food; a portion is **absorbed**, a portion is **reflected**, and a portion is **transmitted**.
5. Polar molecules within the food **oscillate** at the field frequency → **dielectric loss** → volumetric heat generation.
6. The **Penetration Depth (d_p)** sets the limit on sample thickness for uniform heating.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> All formulas verified for KTU 2024 PEFTT631 Module-3 — memorize in this exact form.

| # | Quantity | Formula | SI Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | Free-space wavelength | $\lambda_0 = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | metres; $c = 3\times 10^{8}$ m/s |
| 2 | Loss tangent | $\tan \delta = \dfrac{\varepsilon''}{\varepsilon'}$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Power dissipated per unit volume (Poynting-derivation) | $P_v = 2\pi f \, \varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon'' \, E_{rms}^{\,2}$ | W/m³ ; $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854\times10^{-12}$ F/m |
| 4 | Penetration depth (general form) | $d_p = \dfrac{c}{2\pi f \sqrt{2\varepsilon'}}\Big[\sqrt{1+(\varepsilon''/\varepsilon')^{2}}-1\Big]^{1/2}$ | metres; depth at which power falls to $1/e$ (≈ 36.8 %) of surface value |
| 5 | Penetration depth (low-loss approximation, $\varepsilon'' \ll \varepsilon'$) | $d_p \approx \dfrac{c \, \sqrt{\varepsilon'}}{2\pi f \, \varepsilon''}$ | metres |
| 6 | Lambert-Beer power attenuation | $P(z) = P_0 \, e^{-2z/d_p}$ | power at depth $z$ |
| 7 | Half-power depth | $z_{1/2} = d_p \ln 2 \approx 0.693\, d_p$ | metres |
| 8 | Salinity / salt effect (qualitative) | $\varepsilon'' \uparrow \Rightarrow d_p \downarrow$ | salt + water = hot surface, cold centre |
| 9 | Industrial frequencies | $f = 915$ MHz (industrial); $f = 2450$ MHz (domestic) | 915 MHz gives $\approx 3\times$ deeper penetration |
| 10 | Dielectric constant of water at 25 °C, 2.45 GHz | $\varepsilon' \approx 78$, $\varepsilon'' \approx 12$ | typical KTU numerical value |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU board examiners' trap:** When using penetration depth in **distance-attenuation** problems, remember that the **electric field** falls as $e^{-z/d_p}$, so **power** falls as $e^{-2z/d_p}$. Many students lose marks by confusing these two.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Mechanism | Industrial Benefit |
|-------------|-----------|--------------------|
| Microwave **drying** | Bulk heating → moisture vapor pressure gradient inverted (centre → surface) | 5–10× faster drying; improved puffing |
| Microwave **blanching** | Inactivation of peroxidase / PPO enzymes | 80–90 % less water-soluble nutrient loss |
| Microwave **pasteurization / sterilization** | Rapid lethal heating of *Listeria*, *Salmonella*, *E. coli* | Continuous-flow systems, in-pack sterilization |
| Microwave-assisted **extraction** | Cell-wall rupture from internal steam pressure | Faster extraction of bioactives (polyphenols, oils) |
| Microwave **baking & tempering** | Selective heating of bound water | Reduced bake time by 40–60 % |
| Microwave **vacuum drying** | Low-temperature + rapid heating | Quality retention of heat-sensitive foods |

> [!NOTE]
> Microwave processing is often **hybridized** with other non-thermal technologies (e.g., **microwave + ultrasound**, **microwave + HPP**) to obtain synergistic microbial inactivation with minimal nutrient loss.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Dissipated per Unit Volume

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Starting point:** A plane-polarized electromagnetic wave $E(t) = E_0 \cos(\omega t)$ travelling in a lossy dielectric.

**Step 1 — Instantaneous power density (per unit volume) dissipated** is governed by the work done on dipoles, which is the dot product of field and polarization current:

$$p(t) = E(t)\cdot \frac{dP}{dt}$$

where the polarization $P = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon' E$ in-phase and $P = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon'' E$ out-of-phase component yields the resistive loss.

**Step 2 — Substitute the time-harmonic field and integrate over one period $T = 2\pi/\omega$** to obtain the **time-averaged** power:

$$P_v = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T E_0 \cos(\omega t) \cdot \omega \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon'' E_0 \cos(\omega t) \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Simplify using RMS field $E_{rms} = E_0/\sqrt{2}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_v &= \omega \, \varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon'' \cdot \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T E_0^{2} \cos^{2}(\omega t)\, dt \\
&= \omega \, \varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon'' \cdot \frac{E_0^{2}}{2} \\
&= 2\pi f \, \varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon'' \, E_{rms}^{\,2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
- Doubling the **frequency** → doubles the heat.
- Doubling the **loss factor** → doubles the heat.
- Doubling the **field strength** → **quadruples** the heat (because $P \propto E^{2}$).

> This is the equation examiners love to use as the "show-that" question.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Penetration Depth from Maxwell's Equations

**Step 1 — Wave propagation in a lossy medium.** The complex propagation constant is:

$$\gamma = \alpha + j\beta = j\omega\sqrt{\mu_0 \mu_r \,\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r^{\,*}}$$

where $\varepsilon_r^{\,*} = \varepsilon' - j\varepsilon''$ is the **complex relative permittivity**.

**Step 2 — Split into attenuation constant α and phase constant β:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha &= \omega\sqrt{\frac{\mu_0 \mu_r \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon'}{2}}\;\left[\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{\varepsilon''}{\varepsilon'}\right)^{2}} - 1\right]^{1/2} \\
\beta  &= \omega\sqrt{\frac{\mu_0 \mu_r \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon'}{2}}\;\left[\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{\varepsilon''}{\varepsilon'}\right)^{2}} + 1\right]^{1/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — By definition, penetration depth** is the depth at which the field amplitude drops to $1/e$, i.e., $\alpha d_p = 1$:

$$d_p = \frac{1}{\alpha}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute** $\mu_0\varepsilon_0 = 1/c^{2}$ and $\mu_r \approx 1$ for non-magnetic foods:

$$d_p = \frac{c}{2\pi f \sqrt{2\varepsilon'}}\left[\sqrt{1+\left(\frac{\varepsilon''}{\varepsilon'}\right)^{2}} - 1\right]^{1/2}$$

**Step 5 — Low-loss limit** ($\varepsilon'' \ll \varepsilon'$): use Taylor expansion $\sqrt{1+x^{2}}-1 \approx \tfrac{1}{2}x^{2}$ for $x \ll 1$:

$$d_p \approx \frac{c\,\sqrt{\varepsilon'}}{2\pi f\,\varepsilon''}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Memory hook:** "**C equals d-p times f times ε″, all over square-root of ε′**" — the standard low-loss form for KTU viva.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Penetration Depth Worked Examples

### Example A — Water at 25 °C, 2450 MHz

Given: $\varepsilon' = 78$, $\varepsilon'' = 12$.

**Apply low-loss formula:**

$$d_p = \frac{(3 \times 10^{8})\sqrt{78}}{2\pi (2450 \times 10^{6})(12)}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{78} &\approx 8.832 \\
d_p &\approx \frac{3 \times 10^{8} \times 8.832}{2\pi \times 2.45 \times 10^{9} \times 12} \\
&\approx \frac{2.6496 \times 10^{9}}{1.847 \times 10^{11}} \\
&\approx 1.43 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} = 1.43 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is why **domestic microwave ovens have turntables** — at 2450 MHz, water-heating is limited to roughly 1.5 cm per pass; larger items need rotation.

### Example B — Same food, switching to 915 MHz

Approximating the dielectric properties (note: lower frequency → higher $\varepsilon'$, similar $\varepsilon''$):

$$d_p(915\text{ MHz}) \approx d_p(2450\text{ MHz}) \times \frac{2450}{915} \approx 1.43 \times 2.68 \approx 3.83 \text{ cm}$$

> **Engineering takeaway:** Industrial 915 MHz systems can process much thicker food slabs uniformly — the **single most important reason** for choosing 915 MHz over 2450 MHz in industry.

### Example C — Salt-laden food (ε″ doubled → 24)

$$d_p = \frac{c \sqrt{78}}{2\pi f (24)} \approx 0.72 \text{ cm}$$

> The penetration depth **halves**. This is why salted foods burn on the surface while staying cold inside — a **classic KTU problem**.

---

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix — Microwave vs. Conventional Heating

| Parameter | Conventional (Conduction/Convection) | Microwave (Dielectric) |
|-----------|--------------------------------------|------------------------|
| Heat transfer mode | Surface → Centre (sequential) | Volumetric (simultaneous) |
| Heating rate | Slow (limited by thermal diffusivity) | Very fast (1–10 °C/s achievable) |
| Temperature gradient | Centre cooler than surface | Inverted: centre may be hotter |
| Penetration limited by | Thermal conductivity | Dielectric loss & penetration depth |
| Energy efficiency (oven) | 35–45 % | 55–65 % (domestic) / up to 85 % (industrial) |
| Nutrient retention | Moderate | High (shorter time) |
| Equipment footprint | Small | Moderate to large |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of an Industrial Microwave Heating System

The physical assembly of a microwave processing line is shown below in **functional block form** (mermaid-compliant, alphanumeric node IDs):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[DC Power Supply 3 to 5 kV] --> A2[Magnetron Microwave Source]
    A2 --> A3[Isolator and Reflected Power Absorber]
    A3 --> A4[Waveguide WR 340 for 915 MHz WR 975 for 2450 MHz]
    A4 --> A5[Tunable E and H Tuner]
    A5 --> A6[Mode Stirrer or Field Distributor]
    A6 --> A7[Applicator Cavity Conveyor Belt or Batch Chamber]
    A7 --> A8[Food Load Dielectric Material]
    A8 --> A9[Temperature Probe IR or Fiber Optic]
    A9 --> A10[Control Unit and Feedback Loop]
    A10 --> A1
    A7 --> A11[Exhaust and Vapor Vent System]
    A7 --> A12[Cooling Section]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Power flows clockwise. The **control unit** modulates magnetron output via a feedback loop from the **temperature probe**, ensuring the food is not over-heated. The **isolator + water/dummy load** prevents reflected microwaves from damaging the magnetron — this is a frequent viva question.

## 4.2 Process Flow — Microwave Pasteurization of Ready-to-Eat Meals

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Pre-packaged RTE Meal] --> B2[Initial Surface Temp 4 to 8 degrees C]
    B2 --> B3[Load onto Microwave Conveyor 915 MHz]
    B3 --> B4[Volumetric Heating to 90 to 95 degrees C in 3 to 5 min]
    B4 --> B5[Hold Tube 2 to 5 min for Lethality]
    B5 --> B6[Cooling Section Forced Air or Water Spray]
    B6 --> B7[Storage at 4 degrees C Shelf Life 21 to 45 days]
    B7 --> B8[Quality Check Microbial Count and Sensory]
    B8 --> B9((Final Dispatch))
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Mechanism of Microwave–Bacteria Interaction

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Exposure
    Exposure --> DipoleRotation: Polar molecules oscillate
    DipoleRotation --> ThermalKill: Sustained T above 70 deg C
    DipoleRotation --> Electroporation: High field gradient
    Electroporation --> MembraneRupture: Cell wall damage
    ThermalKill --> Killed
    MembraneRupture --> Killed
    Killed --> [*]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This state diagram is the **conceptual bridge** between *thermal* and *non-thermal* mechanisms in microwave processing — a high-yield KTU Module-3 discussion point. Some KTU questions ask: *"Is microwave processing thermal or non-thermal? Justify."* The state diagram above is the textbook answer.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part-A is a **mandatory 3-mark short-answer section** (usually 8–10 questions across the full syllabus). The two below are the most frequent Module-3 microwave questions.

### Q1. Define dielectric heating. Why is it considered a volumetric heating method? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Dielectric heating is the process of generating heat **within a non-conducting (dielectric) material** by exposing it to a high-frequency alternating electric field. Polar molecules and ions within the material attempt to follow the rapidly oscillating field, and the resulting **molecular friction and ionic conduction** produce heat **throughout the bulk of the material** rather than at the surface.

It is called **volumetric** because the electromagnetic wave penetrates the food interior, depositing energy in every elemental volume simultaneously. The rate of heat generation per unit volume is given by:

$$P_v = 2\pi f \, \varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon'' \, E_{rms}^{\,2}$$

Hence, unlike conventional conduction heating, where heat moves from outside to inside, dielectric heating occurs in the **entire mass at the same time** — making it volumetric.

---

### Q2. State the significance of the loss tangent (tan δ) in microwave processing of foods. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **loss tangent** is defined as:

$$\tan \delta = \frac{\varepsilon''}{\varepsilon'}$$

It represents the **ratio of energy dissipated as heat to energy stored** in the dielectric. Its significance is twofold:

1. **Heating efficiency indicator:** A higher tan δ means more efficient conversion of microwave energy into heat. Water has a high tan δ (≈ 0.123) and heats well; ice has a very low tan δ (≈ 0.0009) and is nearly microwave-transparent.
2. **Penetration depth controller:** The penetration depth of microwaves is inversely proportional to the loss tangent. Foods with high salt or sugar content have elevated ε″ → lower tan δ heating depth → surface overheating ("runaway heating").

Loss tangent is therefore the **primary design parameter** for selecting which foods can be microwave-processed and at what thickness.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE 2024 scheme Part-B carries **14 marks per question** with **internal choice** (either/or). Two model questions are provided below; either may appear in the ESE.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q.A.** (a) Derive an expression for the **average power dissipated per unit volume** in a food material subjected to microwave heating. (7 marks)
    (b) For a food product, the following data are given at 2450 MHz, 25 °C: $\varepsilon' = 50$, $\varepsilon'' = 15$, applied field $E_0 = 5 \times 10^{4}$ V/m. Calculate (i) the **power density** dissipated, and (ii) the **penetration depth** using the low-loss approximation. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation:** *(refer to Section 3.1 above)*

The standard derivation yields:
$$P_v = 2\pi f \, \varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon'' \, E_{rms}^{\,2} = \pi f \, \varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon'' \, E_0^{2}$$

because $E_{rms} = E_0/\sqrt{2}$, so $E_{rms}^{2} = E_0^{2}/2$ and therefore $P_v = \pi f\varepsilon_0\varepsilon''E_0^{2}$. *(State both equivalent forms; examiners accept either.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $f = 2450 \times 10^{6}$ Hz, $\varepsilon' = 50$, $\varepsilon'' = 15$, $E_0 = 5 \times 10^{4}$ V/m, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s.

**(i) Power density:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_v &= \pi \, (2450 \times 10^{6}) \, (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \, (15) \, (5 \times 10^{4})^{2} \\
&= \pi \times 2450 \times 10^{6} \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 15 \times 25 \times 10^{8} \\
&= \pi \times 2450 \times 8.854 \times 15 \times 25 \times 10^{(6 - 12 + 8)} \\
&= \pi \times 2450 \times 8.854 \times 375 \times 10^{2} \\
&= \pi \times 8.13 \times 10^{6} \times 10^{2} \\
&\approx 2.55 \times 10^{9} \text{ W/m}^{3} \\
&= 2.55 \text{ kW/L (approx.)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Penetration depth (low-loss form):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_p &\approx \frac{c \sqrt{\varepsilon'}}{2\pi f \, \varepsilon''} \\
&= \frac{(3 \times 10^{8})\sqrt{50}}{2\pi (2450 \times 10^{6})(15)} \\
&= \frac{3 \times 10^{8} \times 7.071}{2\pi \times 2.45 \times 10^{9} \times 15} \\
&= \frac{2.121 \times 10^{9}}{2.309 \times 10^{11}} \\
&\approx 9.19 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 9.19 \text{ mm} \approx 0.92 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusions for the examiner:**
- The product must be **< 1.8 cm thick** for reasonably uniform heating (2 × d_p rule of thumb).
- Surface overheating can occur if salt/sugar content raises ε″.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q.B.** (a) With a neat **block diagram**, describe the construction and working of a **continuous-flow industrial microwave pasteurization system**. (7 marks)
    (b) Compare **microwave heating with conventional thermal processing** on the basis of (i) heat-transfer direction, (ii) heating rate, (iii) nutrient retention, and (iv) penetration depth. Mention **two industrial applications** of microwave processing. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Block Diagram & Working:**

*(Refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1.)*

The system consists of the following sequential components:

1. **DC Power Supply (3–5 kV, 1–2 kW):** Energizes the magnetron.
2. **Magnetron:** A vacuum-tube oscillator that generates 915 MHz or 2450 MHz microwaves from the DC input.
3. **Isolator with water/dummy load:** Absorbs the reflected power travelling back from the cavity, protecting the magnetron.
4. **Waveguide (WR 340 for 915 MHz, WR 975 for 2450 MHz):** A hollow rectangular metallic tube that channels the microwaves with minimal loss.
5. **E–H Tuner (three-stub or iris-type):** Maximizes power transfer by impedance matching.
6. **Mode stirrer / rotating turntable:** Distributes the standing-wave field uniformly inside the cavity.
7. **Applicator cavity** (multi-mode batch or single-mode conveyor type): Houses the food product on a polypropylene or Teflon conveyor (these materials are microwave-transparent).
8. **Temperature probe** (fiber-optic or IR): Monitors real-time product temperature.
9. **Control unit with PLC feedback:** Modulates magnetron power based on the temperature signal.
10. **Exhaust and cooling sections:** Remove moisture and bring the product to storage temperature.

**Working principle:** The packaged food is conveyed through the cavity; the high-frequency field polarizes the water molecules; dielectric loss generates uniform volumetric heat; the product reaches pasteurization temperature (72 °C for 15 s equivalent) in 3–5 minutes; cooled and stored.

**Part (b) — Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Conventional Heating | Microwave Heating |
|-----------|----------------------|-------------------|
| (i) Heat-transfer direction | Outside → inside (conduction-dominated) | Inside → outside (volumetric) |
| (ii) Heating rate | Slow, k-dependent | Very fast (1–10 °C/s) |
| (iii) Nutrient retention | Lower (long exposure) | Higher (short exposure) |
| (iv) Penetration depth | Set by thermal diffusivity (~mm to cm, time-dependent) | Set by dielectric properties (~1.4 cm in water at 2450 MHz) |

**Two industrial applications:**

1. **Microwave Drying of Pasta / Crackers** — uniform moisture removal, reduced checking/cracking, 5× faster than hot-air drying.
2. **Microwave-Assisted Extraction (MAE)** of bioactive compounds (e.g., polyphenols from grape pomace, oil from seeds) — 10× reduction in solvent volume and extraction time.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU 2024 Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Microwave Processing Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing E₀ with E_rms.** When using $P_v = 2\pi f \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon'' E_{rms}^{2}$, the **field must be RMS**. If the question gives **peak (E₀)**, you **must** divide by √2 first or use $P_v = \pi f \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon'' E_0^{2}$. Mixing them up → −2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the power attenuation.** Field decays as $e^{-z/d_p}$, but **power** decays as $e^{-2z/d_p}$. KTU examiners specifically test this. −1 to −2 marks.
> 3. **Using 2450 MHz formula where 915 MHz is asked (or vice-versa).** Always re-state the frequency at the top of the solution.
> 4. **Confusing penetration depth with half-power depth.** Half-power depth = $d_p \ln 2 \approx 0.693 d_p$. Examiners often ask for half-power depth to trap this error.
> 5. **Stating "microwave is purely non-thermal."** This is **incorrect**. Microwave processing is essentially **thermal in mechanism** (heat is the killing agent) but is *treated* in the KTU syllabus as part of the **non-thermal/emerging processing module** because of the non-conventional heat transfer mode and synergy with non-thermal effects. Always clarify the dual nature.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Microwave Processing (Module 3)**

- Microwave processing is **dielectric / volumetric heating** using EM waves of **300 MHz – 300 GHz**.
- **ISM frequencies:** **915 MHz (industrial)** and **2450 MHz (domestic)**.
- Heat is generated by **dipolar rotation** of polar molecules (e.g., H₂O) and **ionic conduction** of dissolved ions.
- **Loss tangent** $\tan \delta = \varepsilon''/\varepsilon'$ — the central dielectric parameter.
- **Power density:** $P_v = 2\pi f \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon'' E_{rms}^{2}$ — increases linearly with frequency, loss factor, and **quadratically** with field.
- **Penetration depth** $d_p = \dfrac{c}{2\pi f \sqrt{2\varepsilon'}}\left[\sqrt{1+(\varepsilon''/\varepsilon')^{2}} - 1\right]^{1/2}$; low-loss form: $d_p \approx \dfrac{c\sqrt{\varepsilon'}}{2\pi f \varepsilon''}$.
- **Free-space wavelength at 2450 MHz ≈ 12.24 cm**; at 915 MHz ≈ 32.8 cm.
- **Penetration depth in water:** ≈ **1.4 cm at 2450 MHz**, ≈ **3.8 cm at 915 MHz** (≈ 2.7× deeper at 915 MHz).
- **Power attenuation in food:** $P(z) = P_0 e^{-2z/d_p}$.
- **High salt/sugar → high ε″ → low d_p → surface overheating** (salt-effect trap).
- **Main components** of a microwave system: DC supply → magnetron → isolator → waveguide → tuner → mode stirrer → applicator → product → temperature feedback → control.
- **Cavity (waveguide/applicator) materials** for food contact: **polypropylene, Teflon (PTFE), ceramics** (microwave-transparent, food-grade).
- **Industrial applications:** drying, blanching, pasteurization, sterilization, tempering, baking, microwave-assisted extraction (MAE).
- **Advantages:** volumetric, fast, energy-efficient, better nutrient retention, reduced over-cooking.
- **Limitations:** non-uniform heating (edges/corners focus), limited penetration depth, capital cost, "runaway heating" with high-loss foods.
- **Hybrid systems** (microwave + HPP / microwave + ultrasound) are emerging high-yield topics.
- **Equation to memorize cold:** $P_v = 2\pi f \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon'' E_{rms}^{2}$ **and** the **penetration depth in its two forms** (general + low-loss).
- **Examiner trap:** field attenuation is exponential in z, power attenuation is exponential in **2z**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Infrared Heating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT631 - NON-THERMAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Infrared Heating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — INFRARED HEATING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Infrared (IR) Heating** is an electromagnetic non-thermal/thermal processing technique in which electromagnetic waves in the infrared region of the spectrum (wavelength range **0.78 µm to 1000 µm**) are used to transfer thermal energy directly to a food material without physically heating the surrounding air. Energy transfer occurs via **radiation**, where photons emitted by an IR emitter are absorbed by the product's surface molecules, causing molecular vibration, rotation, and subsequent conversion of radiant energy into sensible heat.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for the course *PEFTT631 – Non-Thermal Processing*, IR heating is studied under advanced food processing techniques as a *hybrid* technology that, although classified as thermal, provides rapid, surface-selective, energy-efficient heating, and is often combined with other non-thermal methods (e.g., IR + hot air drying, IR + microwave).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Within Module 3, the focus is on understanding the *physics of IR generation*, *radiative heat transfer laws*, *penetration behaviour*, *IR emitters and equipment design*, and *food-processing applications* such as drying, blanching, pasteurization, peeling, roasting, and disinfestation.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine standing in front of a **bonfire on a cold winter night**. The air around you remains bitterly cold, yet your face feels warm. That warmth did not travel through heated air — it travelled as invisible **light rays (infrared photons)** emitted by the glowing embers and was absorbed by your skin.

This is exactly how **IR heating of food** works:
- The **emitter (lamp/element)** is the "bonfire."
- The **food surface** is your "face."
- The **air between them** stays comparatively cool.
- The **heat is delivered in the form of electromagnetic waves**, which the food molecules convert into thermal energy on contact.

**Key intuitive takeaways for first-time learners:**
1. IR is **light**, not "hot air" — it travels at the speed of light and does not need a medium.
2. Heating is **instantaneous** the moment photons strike the surface.
3. The deeper the wavelength, the deeper the penetration — explaining why **near-IR cooks the surface** and **far-IR can heat the interior**.

> [!TIP]
> Think of IR as **"cooking with light."** Your microwave oven uses longer waves (radio-frequency microwaves), your oven uses mostly convection, but an IR system is essentially a **focused heat lamp tuned for food chemistry**.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants are essential for IR calculations and appear frequently in KTU numerical problems:

| Constant | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | $\mathbf{2.998 \times 10^{8}}$ | m/s |
| Planck's constant | $h$ | $\mathbf{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}$ | J·s |
| Boltzmann's constant | $k_B$ | $\mathbf{1.381 \times 10^{-23}}$ | J/K |
| Stefan–Boltzmann constant | $\sigma$ | $\mathbf{5.670 \times 10^{-8}}$ | W/(m²·K⁴) |
| Wien's displacement constant | $b$ | $\mathbf{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}$ | m·K |

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize these five constants — at least **one numerical problem every KTU exam cycle** involves Wien's or Stefan–Boltzmann's law applied to an IR emitter.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Electromagnetic Spectrum Position of IR

The infrared region lies between the **visible light** (red end) and the **microwave** region. It is sub-divided into three bands of high importance in food processing:

| Region | Wavelength $\lambda$ | Frequency Range | Food Processing Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Near-Infrared (NIR)** | 0.78 – 1.4 µm | $214 – 384$ THz | Surface sterilization, peeling, blanching |
| **Short-Wave / Mid-IR** | 1.4 – 3 µm | $100 – 214$ THz | Roasting, baking, drying thin films |
| **Far-Infrared (FIR)** | 3 – 1000 µm | $0.3 – 100$ THz | Deep drying, pasteurization, tempering |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **penetration depth** of IR into food is **inversely proportional to its wavelength**. Therefore, **NIR penetrates shallowly (surface heating)** while **FIR penetrates deeper** and is preferred for bulk dehydration.

---

## 2.2 Mechanism of Infrared Heating — Step-by-Step Logic

The transfer of energy from an IR emitter to a food matrix follows a strict sequence of physical events:

1. **Electrical energy input** to the IR emitter (a tungsten filament, ceramic, or quartz halogen bulb).
2. **Thermal excitation** of the emitter's atoms raises its surface temperature ($T_s$) to typically **800 K – 2500 K**.
3. **Radiative emission** of photons occurs in the IR region, governed by Planck's blackbody law.
4. **Propagation** of IR rays through the air gap with negligible absorption (air is largely transparent to IR).
5. **Absorption** of photons at the food surface, where radiation is converted to vibrational/rotational energy in molecules (especially O–H, C–H, N–H bonds of water, proteins, and carbohydrates).
6. **Heat generation** within the absorbing layer as vibrational energy dissipates via molecular collisions.
7. **Conductive/diffusive transport** of the generated heat into the deeper layers of the product by conventional conduction.

---

## 2.3 The Four Governing Laws of IR Heating

### (a) Planck's Law of Blackbody Radiation

A perfect blackbody emits radiation with a spectral distribution given by:

$$
E_{\lambda} \;=\; \frac{2 \pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \, \frac{1}{\exp\!\left(\dfrac{h c}{\lambda k_B T}\right) - 1}
$$

where $E_{\lambda}$ is the spectral emissive power (W/m²·m).

### (b) Wien's Displacement Law

The wavelength at which emission is maximum:

$$
\lambda_{\max} \, T \;=\; b \;=\; 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m·K}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering utility:** Knowing the emitter temperature instantly tells the designer which IR band will dominate. For a ceramic emitter at $T = 1000$ K, $\lambda_{\max} = 2.898\ \mu\text{m}$ (mid-IR). For a halogen lamp at $T = 2500$ K, $\lambda_{\max} = 1.16\ \mu\text{m}$ (near-IR).

### (c) Stefan–Boltzmann Law

Total radiative heat flux emitted per unit area of a blackbody:

$$
q \;=\; \sigma \, T^{4}
$$

For a **real (gray) surface** with emissivity $\varepsilon$:

$$
q \;=\; \varepsilon \, \sigma \, T^{4}
$$

### (d) Lambert's Law of Absorption (Beer–Lambert form)

The intensity of IR radiation decreases exponentially as it penetrates an absorbing medium:

$$
I(x) \;=\; I_{0} \, \exp(-\mu x)
$$

where:
- $I_0$ = incident intensity at the surface (W/m²)
- $I(x)$ = intensity at depth $x$ (m)
- $\mu$ = absorption coefficient of the food (m⁻¹)
- Penetration depth $\delta = 1/\mu$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ | Peak emission wavelength | Selecting emitter temperature |
| 2 | $q = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ | Total emissive power | IR heater sizing |
| 3 | $I(x) = I_0 \exp(-\mu x)$ | Radiation attenuation in food | Predicting heating depth |
| 4 | $\delta = 1/\mu$ | Penetration depth | Choosing IR band |
| 5 | $\eta_{rad} = \dfrac{\sigma (T_s^{4} - T_a^{4})}{q_{input}}$ | Radiative efficiency of emitter | Energy balance problems |
| 6 | $E_{abs} = I_0 \, \alpha \, A \, t$ | Energy absorbed by food | Process throughput calculation |
| 7 | $Q = m \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | Sensible heat gained by product | Heating rate / time required |

> [!NOTE]
> In all KTU numericals, the symbol $\vert$ for absolute value should be written as `\vert` in your answer sheets to avoid LaTeX rendering issues. (Use $T_s \vert T_a$ to mean difference; $\vert T_s \vert$ is rarely needed.)

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food drying** — IR + convection hybrid dryers reduce drying time by 30–60 % compared to hot air alone (e.g., onion, mango, banana).
- **Surface pasteurization** — NIR rapidly inactivates surface pathogens on bread, cookies, and ready-to-eat meals.
- **Peeling** — IR burns the skin of peaches, tomatoes, and peppers, followed by mechanical peeling (the "Lyco/Alde" process).
- **Disinfestation** — FIR raises grain temperature to >55 °C, killing insects without chemical fumigation.
- **Baking & roasting** — IR ovens produce crusty bread with soft interior due to surface-selective heating.
- **Tempering of frozen foods** — FIR defrosts frozen blocks uniformly before further processing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> IR heating is **NOT truly non-thermal** in the strict sense (it raises product temperature), but KTU classifies it within *non-thermal processing* because **the heating is so rapid and selective that biochemical/quality damage is minimized** — a key distinguishing feature examined in viva voce.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Peak Emission Wavelength from Planck's Law

We want to find the wavelength $\lambda$ at which the spectral emissive power $E_\lambda$ of a blackbody is maximum. From Section 2.3(a):

$$
E_{\lambda}(\lambda, T) \;=\; \frac{2 \pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{\exp\!\left(\dfrac{h c}{\lambda k_B T}\right) - 1}
$$

**Step 1 — Setup the optimization**

To find $\lambda_{\max}$, differentiate $E_\lambda$ with respect to $\lambda$ and set the result to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial E_{\lambda}}{\partial \lambda} \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 2 — Define a substitution variable**

Let

$$
x \;=\; \frac{h c}{\lambda k_B T}
$$

so that $\lambda = \dfrac{h c}{x k_B T}$ and $d\lambda = -\dfrac{h c}{x^{2} k_B T}\, dx$.

**Step 3 — Express $E_\lambda$ in terms of $x$**

Substituting into the Planck formula and simplifying the constants:

$$
E_{\lambda}(x) \;=\; \frac{2 \pi k_B^{5} T^{5}}{h^{4} c^{3}} \cdot \frac{x^{5}}{e^{x} - 1}
$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate with respect to $x$**

The leading constant does not depend on $x$, so we maximize $f(x) = \dfrac{x^{5}}{e^{x} - 1}$.

Apply the quotient rule:

$$
\frac{df}{dx} \;=\; \frac{5 x^{4}(e^{x} - 1) - x^{5} e^{x}}{(e^{x} - 1)^{2}} \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 5 — Solve the numerator**

Setting the numerator to zero:

$$
5 x^{4}(e^{x} - 1) \;=\; x^{5} e^{x}
$$

Dividing both sides by $x^{4}$ (since $x \neq 0$):

$$
5(e^{x} - 1) \;=\; x e^{x}
$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to a transcendental equation**

$$
5 e^{x} - 5 \;=\; x e^{x}
$$

$$
(5 - x) e^{x} \;=\; 5
$$

This transcendental equation has the numerical solution:

$$
x \;\approx\; 4.965
$$

**Step 7 — Recover Wien's law**

Recalling $x = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda k_B T}$:

$$
\frac{h c}{\lambda_{\max} k_B T} \;=\; 4.965
$$

$$
\lambda_{\max} \, T \;=\; \frac{h c}{4.965 \, k_B} \;\approx\; 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m·K}
$$

Hence, **Wien's displacement law** is proved.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Radiative Energy Balance for an IR Heater

Consider an IR emitter at surface temperature $T_s$ radiating onto a food surface at temperature $T_f$ across an air gap. The **net radiative heat flux** delivered to the food is:

$$
q_{net} \;=\; \varepsilon_{eff} \, \sigma \, (T_s^{4} - T_f^{4})
$$

where $\varepsilon_{eff}$ is an effective combined emissivity–absorptivity factor.

**Step 1 — State the Stefan–Boltzmann emission from the emitter**

$$
q_{emit} \;=\; \varepsilon_s \, \sigma \, T_s^{4}
$$

**Step 2 — State the Stefan–Boltzmann emission from the food (back-radiation)**

$$
q_{back} \;=\; \varepsilon_f \, \sigma \, T_f^{4}
$$

**Step 3 — Net heat transfer is the difference**

$$
q_{net} \;=\; q_{emit} - q_{back} \;=\; \sigma \left( \varepsilon_s T_s^{4} - \varepsilon_f T_f^{4} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Introduce effective emissivity**

Assuming both surfaces are in large parallel-plate configuration, the net exchange becomes:

$$
q_{net} \;=\; \frac{\sigma (T_s^{4} - T_f^{4})}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_s} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_f} - 1}
$$

Defining $\varepsilon_{eff} = \left( \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_s} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_f} - 1 \right)^{-1}$:

$$
\boxed{\; q_{net} \;=\; \varepsilon_{eff} \, \sigma \, (T_s^{4} - T_f^{4}) \;}
$$

**Step 5 — Energy delivered to a food mass $m$ with specific heat $c_p$ over time $t$**

$$
Q \;=\; q_{net} \, A \, t \;=\; m \, c_p \, \Delta T
$$

Solving for heating time:

$$
t \;=\; \frac{m \, c_p \, \Delta T}{\varepsilon_{eff} \, \sigma \, A \, (T_s^{4} - T_f^{4})}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU 2024 Style)

> **Problem:**
> An IR emitter at $T_s = 1200$ K radiates onto a wet food slab of mass $m = 0.5$ kg, surface area $A = 0.05$ m², specific heat $c_p = 3500$ J/(kg·K), and initial temperature $T_i = 300$ K. The desired final temperature is $T_f = 360$ K. Take $\varepsilon_s = 0.92$, $\varepsilon_f = 0.85$. Calculate:
> (i) Peak emission wavelength
> (ii) Net radiative heat flux
> (iii) Time required to reach the target temperature

**Solution — Part (i)**

$$
\lambda_{\max} \;=\; \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{1200} \;=\; 2.415 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m} \;=\; 2.415 \; \mu\text{m}
$$

This lies in the **mid-IR** band — ideal for surface drying.

**Solution — Part (ii)**

Compute the effective emissivity:

$$
\varepsilon_{eff} \;=\; \left( \frac{1}{0.92} + \frac{1}{0.85} - 1 \right)^{-1} \;=\; \left( 1.0870 + 1.1765 - 1 \right)^{-1} \;=\; (1.2635)^{-1} \;=\; 0.7915
$$

Now:

$$
q_{net} \;=\; 0.7915 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1200^{4} - 300^{4})
$$

Compute the fourth powers:

$$
1200^{4} \;=\; 2.0736 \times 10^{12}, \quad 300^{4} \;=\; 8.1 \times 10^{9}
$$

$$
q_{net} \;=\; 0.7915 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (2.0655 \times 10^{12})
$$

$$
q_{net} \;\approx\; 0.7915 \times 117{,}112 \;=\; 92{,}694 \; \text{W/m}^{2}
$$

**Solution — Part (iii)**

$$
\Delta T \;=\; 360 - 300 \;=\; 60 \; \text{K}
$$

$$
Q \;=\; 0.5 \times 3500 \times 60 \;=\; 105{,}000 \; \text{J}
$$

$$
t \;=\; \frac{105{,}000}{92{,}694 \times 0.05} \;=\; \frac{105{,}000}{4634.7} \;\approx\; 22.65 \; \text{seconds}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Notice how **rapid** IR heating is — only ~23 s to raise the temperature by 60 K. This is why IR is preferred over conventional hot air for thin, moisture-sensitive products.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define infrared heating. Mention its position in the electromagnetic spectrum.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Infrared (IR) heating is a thermal processing method that uses **electromagnetic radiation in the wavelength range of 0.78 µm to 1000 µm** to transfer heat directly to a material without significantly heating the surrounding air.

**Position in EM spectrum:** It lies between **visible light** (red end, ~0.78 µm) and **microwaves** (~1 mm). It is subdivided into **near-IR (NIR, 0.78–1.4 µm)**, **mid-IR (1.4–3 µm)**, and **far-IR (FIR, 3–1000 µm)**.

> **Marking key:** Definition = 1 mark; wavelength range = 1 mark; sub-classification = 1 mark.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State and explain Wien's displacement law as applied to IR emitter design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Wien's displacement law states that the product of the absolute temperature $T$ of a blackbody and the wavelength $\lambda_{\max}$ at which its emission spectrum peaks is a constant:

$$
\lambda_{\max} \cdot T \;=\; 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m·K}
$$

**Application:** For a desired peak emission in the **far-IR range** (e.g., $\lambda_{\max} = 5\ \mu\text{m}$ for deep drying), the emitter temperature must be:

$$
T \;=\; \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{5 \times 10^{-6}} \;\approx\; 580 \; \text{K}
$$

This allows the design engineer to **select the appropriate emitter type** (ceramic, quartz, halogen) based on the targeted wavelength band.

> **Marking key:** Statement = 1 mark; equation = 1 mark; design application with example = 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

> **a)** Explain the principle and mechanism of infrared heating of food. Discuss the role of emissivity and absorptivity in IR processing. **(7 marks)**
>
> **b)** An infrared heater operating at a surface temperature of $1500$ K radiates on a moist food product. The food has an emissivity of $0.80$ and the heater emissivity is $0.90$. Calculate the **net radiative heat flux** delivered to the food, the **peak emission wavelength**, and the **time required** to raise the temperature of $1$ kg of food by $50$ K. Take $c_p = 3600$ J/(kg·K), surface area $A = 0.08$ m², and Stefan–Boltzmann constant $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Principle:** IR heating is based on the conversion of electromagnetic radiation in the IR wavelength band into thermal (vibrational) energy in the food material.

**Mechanism (sequential steps — 4 marks):**
1. **Electrical input** heats the emitter element to high temperature ($T_s = 800$–$2500$ K).
2. The emitter radiates IR photons according to **Planck's law** and the total flux follows **Stefan–Boltzmann's law** $q = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$.
3. IR rays travel through air (which is largely transparent) and **strike the food surface**.
4. The surface molecules (especially O–H groups of water, C–H of lipids, N–H of proteins) **absorb** the photons and convert this energy to molecular vibration, i.e., **sensible heat**.
5. The heat is then **conducted inward** by normal molecular diffusion.

**Role of emissivity and absorptivity (3 marks):**
- **Emissivity ($\varepsilon$):** Fraction of blackbody radiation actually emitted by a real surface ($0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$). Higher emitter emissivity → higher $q$ → faster heating.
- **Absorptivity ($\alpha$):** Fraction of incident radiation absorbed by the food. By **Kirchhoff's law**, at thermal equilibrium, $\alpha = \varepsilon$ for the same wavelength and temperature.
- A food with high water content (e.g., fresh vegetables) has **high absorptivity in the mid-to-far IR range**, making FIR ideal for drying such products.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Step 1 — Peak emission wavelength**

$$
\lambda_{\max} \;=\; \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{1500} \;=\; 1.932 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m} \;=\; 1.932 \; \mu\text{m}
$$

This is in the **mid-IR region** (1.4–3 µm).

**Step 2 — Effective emissivity**

$$
\varepsilon_{eff} \;=\; \left( \frac{1}{0.90} + \frac{1}{0.80} - 1 \right)^{-1} \;=\; \left( 1.111 + 1.250 - 1 \right)^{-1} \;=\; (1.361)^{-1} \;=\; 0.7347
$$

**Step 3 — Net radiative heat flux**

Using $T_s = 1500$ K, $T_f = 300$ K (assumed ambient food temperature):

$$
T_s^{4} \;=\; 5.0625 \times 10^{12}, \quad T_f^{4} \;=\; 8.1 \times 10^{9}
$$

$$
T_s^{4} - T_f^{4} \;\approx\; 5.0544 \times 10^{12} \; \text{K}^{4}
$$

$$
q_{net} \;=\; 0.7347 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 5.0544 \times 10^{12}
$$

$$
q_{net} \;\approx\; 0.7347 \times 286{,}585 \;\approx\; 210{,}581 \; \text{W/m}^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Time required**

$$
Q \;=\; m \, c_p \, \Delta T \;=\; 1 \times 3600 \times 50 \;=\; 180{,}000 \; \text{J}
$$

$$
t \;=\; \frac{Q}{q_{net} \cdot A} \;=\; \frac{180{,}000}{210{,}581 \times 0.08} \;=\; \frac{180{,}000}{16{,}846.5} \;\approx\; 10.69 \; \text{seconds}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $\lambda_{\max} = 1.93\ \mu\text{m}$ (mid-IR)
- $q_{net} \approx 2.106 \times 10^{5}$ W/m²
- $t \approx 10.7$ s

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

> **a)** Compare **near-infrared (NIR)** and **far-infrared (FIR)** heating with respect to wavelength, penetration depth, dominant application, and emitter material. **(7 marks)**
>
> **b)** A food slab of thickness $L = 4$ mm is exposed to IR radiation of intensity $I_0 = 5000$ W/m². The absorption coefficient of the food at this wavelength is $\mu = 800$ m⁻¹. Calculate:
> (i) Penetration depth $\delta$
> (ii) Intensity at depths $x = 1$ mm and $x = 3$ mm
> (iii) Percentage of incident radiation absorbed within the slab. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) — Comparison Table**

| Parameter | Near-Infrared (NIR) | Far-Infrared (FIR) |
|---|---|---|
| Wavelength | 0.78 – 1.4 µm | 3 – 1000 µm |
| Penetration depth | Shallow (µm to sub-mm) | Deep (mm to cm) |
| Emitter material | Tungsten halogen, quartz lamps | Ceramic, metal-oxide panels |
| Dominant application | Surface sterilization, peeling, blanching | Deep drying, pasteurization, tempering |
| Heating speed | Very fast, surface-limited | Slower but volumetrically uniform |
| Color selectivity | High (light/heat) | Low |

**Conclusion (2 marks):** NIR is preferred for **surface-only** treatments (e.g., tomato peeling), while FIR is chosen when **uniform bulk heating** is required (e.g., grain drying). Hybrid NIR + FIR systems combine both advantages.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Step (i) — Penetration depth**

$$
\delta \;=\; \frac{1}{\mu} \;=\; \frac{1}{800} \;=\; 1.25 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m} \;=\; 1.25 \; \text{mm}
$$

**Step (ii) — Intensity at $x = 1$ mm and $3$ mm**

For $x = 1$ mm $= 10^{-3}$ m:

$$
I(1\,\text{mm}) \;=\; 5000 \times e^{-800 \times 0.001} \;=\; 5000 \times e^{-0.8} \;=\; 5000 \times 0.4493 \;=\; 2246.6 \; \text{W/m}^{2}
$$

For $x = 3$ mm $= 3 \times 10^{-3}$ m:

$$
I(3\,\text{mm}) \;=\; 5000 \times e^{-800 \times 0.003} \;=\; 5000 \times e^{-2.4} \;=\; 5000 \times 0.0907 \;=\; 453.5 \; \text{W/m}^{2}
$$

**Step (iii) — Percentage of incident radiation absorbed within the slab ($L = 4$ mm)**

Intensity at the bottom of the slab:

$$
I(L) \;=\; 5000 \times e^{-800 \times 0.004} \;=\; 5000 \times e^{-3.2} \;=\; 5000 \times 0.0408 \;=\; 203.8 \; \text{W/m}^{2}
$$

Absorbed fraction:

$$
\text{Fraction} \;=\; 1 - \frac{I(L)}{I_0} \;=\; 1 - \frac{203.8}{5000} \;=\; 1 - 0.0408 \;=\; 0.9592
$$

$$
\boxed{\; \text{Absorbed} \;\approx\; 95.92\% \;}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** Over 95 % of the IR energy is absorbed within just 4 mm of the food — confirming that **IR heating is essentially a surface-to-thin-layer phenomenon**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to use $\varepsilon_{eff}$** in the net heat flux formula — many students write $q = \sigma T^4$ instead of $q = \varepsilon_{eff}\sigma(T_s^4 - T_f^4)$. (**–2 marks**)
> 2. **Confusing emissivity and absorptivity** — remember Kirchhoff's law applies only at thermal equilibrium; in a transient IR heater scenario they may differ.
> 3. **Mixing up Stefan–Boltzmann ($T^4$) with conduction ($T$)** — IR is always a fourth-power relationship; do not linearize unless the question explicitly says so.
> 4. **Unit errors in $q_{net}$** — final units must be W/m²; verify before writing the final answer.
> 5. **Using $T$ in °C instead of K** — Stefan–Boltzmann and Wien's laws **strictly require Kelvin**; this is a **direct 1-mark deduction**.
> 6. **Skipping the Lambert's law derivation** in numericals — examiners expect the three-step solution: penetration depth → intensity at depth → absorbed fraction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep)**

- [x] IR region: **0.78 µm – 1000 µm** of the EM spectrum.
- [x] Three sub-bands: **NIR (0.78–1.4 µm)**, **Mid-IR (1.4–3 µm)**, **FIR (3–1000 µm)**.
- [x] IR is **light, not heat** — propagates without a medium.
- [x] **Wien's law:** $\lambda_{\max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K → higher $T$ → shorter $\lambda$ → more NIR.
- [x] **Stefan–Boltzmann:** $q = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ → temperature has a **fourth-power effect** on heat flux.
- [x] **Lambert's law:** $I(x) = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ → **penetration depth $\delta = 1/\mu$**.
- [x] NIR = **shallow penetration, surface heating**; FIR = **deep, volumetric heating**.
- [x] Emissivity $\varepsilon$ of food is generally **0.85–0.95** at peak absorption wavelengths.
- [x] Kirchhoff's law: at equilibrium, $\alpha = \varepsilon$ (for a given $\lambda$, $T$).
- [x] Standard IR emitter temperatures: **800 K (ceramic FIR) → 2500 K (halogen NIR)**.
- [x] Effective emissivity for parallel plates: $\varepsilon_{eff}^{-1} = \varepsilon_s^{-1} + \varepsilon_f^{-1} - 1$.
- [x] Applications: drying, blanching, peeling, roasting, baking, pasteurization, disinfestation, tempering.
- [x] IR is **fast, energy-efficient, and water-absorbing** → ideal for moisture-rich foods.
- [x] KTU exam trick: **always convert °C → K** before any radiative law calculation.
- [x] Hybrid IR + hot air / IR + microwave systems are **frequently asked** in KTU viva voce.

---

> **End of Module 3 — Infrared Heating Notes** | KTU 2024 Scheme | PEFTT631 — Non-Thermal Processing
