# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, 3 Marks]**
*Define Pulsed Light Technology. State the typical wavelength range, pulse duration, and FDA regulatory status.*

**Model Answer:**

**Pulsed Light Technology (PLT)** is a non-thermal preservation method that uses short, high-intensity pulses of broad-spectrum white light to inactivate microorganisms on food surfaces and packaging.

- **Wavelength range:** 200 – 1100 nm
- **Pulse duration:** 1 µs – 0.1 s (typically 100–300 µs)
- **FDA status:** Approved for surface microbial inactivation under 21 CFR 179.41 (since 1996)
- **Light source:** Xenon flash lamps energized by high-voltage capacitor banks.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, 3 Marks]**
*What is the role of Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction in Oscillating Magnetic Field (OMF) preservation?*

**Model Answer:**

Faraday's Law is the **physical foundation** of OMF microbial inactivation. A time-varying magnetic field $B(t)$ inside the OMF solenoid induces an electric field $E_{\text{ind}} = -(r/2)(dB/dt)$ in the food. This induced field drives **eddy currents** in ionic cellular media, producing mechanical and electrical stresses on microbial membranes that lead to **electroporation-like damage** and cell death. Without the time-varying (oscillating) component, $dB/dt \approx 0$ and no induced field exists — which is why OMF, unlike static magnetic fields, is biologically active.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students **omit the diagram** when asked for "principle and working" — this costs up to 3 marks. Always include a labeled block diagram.
> - When asked for the "mechanism of inactivation," students list only one effect (e.g., UV-C damage) instead of all three in PLT (photochemical, photothermal, photophysical). Loss: 2–3 marks.
> - Numerical problems in OMF frequently test $E_{\text{ind}} = (r/2)(dB/dt)$. Forgetting the factor of ½ is the most common error.
> - Always quote the **units** for fluence (J cm⁻²) and magnetic flux density (Tesla) — examiners specifically check for this.
> - In comparison questions (PLT vs OMF), students often forget to discuss **penetration depth difference** (surface vs. bulk).

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

*Explain the principle, construction, and working of Pulsed Light Technology. Discuss the mechanisms of microbial inactivation and the factors affecting PLT efficiency. Illustrate with a labeled block diagram.*

#### Part (a) — Principle, Construction, and Working [7 Marks]

**Principle:**
PLT is based on the principle of delivering intense, short-duration pulses of broad-spectrum white light (200–1100 nm) onto a food or packaging surface. The energy is generated by capacitor discharge through a xenon gas-filled flash lamp, producing microsecond pulses rich in UV-C (the most germicidal portion of the spectrum).

**Construction (Key Components):**

1. **High-voltage DC power supply (2–5 kV):** Converts AC mains to DC for charging the capacitor bank.
2. **Capacitor bank:** Stores the electrical energy (typically 100–1000 µF total) for sudden release.
3. **Trigger circuit:** A separate high-voltage pulse generator (15–30 kV) that ionizes the xenon gas.
4. **Xenon flash lamp:** Quartz-envelope tube filled with xenon at low pressure; the active light-emitting element.
5. **Reflector housing:** Elliptical or parabolic reflector that focuses the broadband light onto the target.
6. **Treatment chamber:** Enclosed area where the food is placed 1–20 cm from the lamp.
7. **Control unit:** Sets pulse count, energy, and repetition rate.

**Working:**
- The capacitors are charged slowly over milliseconds to the set high voltage.
- The trigger pulse ionizes the xenon, dropping its resistance by orders of magnitude.
- The capacitors discharge through the ionized xenon in 1–300 µs, releasing stored energy as an intense white-light flash.
- The flash is directed by the reflector onto the food surface, where UV-C photons (especially at 253.7 nm) are absorbed by microbial DNA, while visible and IR components cause localized heating and photophysical disruption.
- The process is repeated for $n$ pulses (typically 1–30) to achieve the desired log reduction.

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to **Section 4.1** for the complete diagram.)

#### Part (b) — Microbial Inactivation Mechanism and Factors [7 Marks]

**Mechanisms of Microbial Inactivation (3 simultaneous effects):**

1. **Photochemical Effect (Dominant, ~80% of inactivation):**
   - UV-C photons (peak at 253.7 nm) are absorbed by pyrimidine bases (thymine, cytosine) in microbial DNA.
   - This forms **cyclobutane pyrimidine dimers (CPDs)** and **6-4 photoproducts**.
   - These dimers distort the DNA helix and block DNA polymerase, preventing replication and transcription → cell death.

2. **Photothermal Effect:**
   - The intense pulse causes rapid local heating (> 100 °C for microseconds).
   - This denatures structural proteins, enzymes, and membrane lipids.
   - Especially effective against vegetative cells and yeast.

3. **Photophysical Effect:**
   - Rapid thermal expansion generates **shock waves** in the surrounding medium.
   - **Cavitation bubbles** form and collapse, mechanically rupturing cell walls.
   - Significant for spores and Gram-positive bacteria with thick cell walls.

**Factors Affecting PLT Efficiency:**

| Factor | Effect on Inactivation |
|---|---|
| **Fluence (J cm⁻²)** | Higher fluence → higher log reduction; saturation above ~12 J cm⁻² |
| **Number of pulses** | More pulses → higher log reduction; diminishing returns beyond 20 |
| **Distance from lamp** | Closer distance → higher fluence; typical 1–20 cm |
| **Microbial type** | Gram-negative > Gram-positive > spores (in order of resistance) |
| **Food surface properties** | Smooth, dry, non-porous surfaces respond best; rough/porous surfaces shield microbes |
| **UV absorbance of food** | High-absorbance foods (e.g., dark berries) require higher fluence |
| **Initial microbial load** | Higher load → proportionally more pulses needed |

**Advantages:** Non-thermal, no chemical residues, short treatment time, FDA-approved, low energy cost per pulse.
**Limitations:** Surface treatment only (poor penetration), shadowing effects, cannot treat opaque or deeply pigmented foods effectively.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

*Describe the principle and working of Oscillating Magnetic Field (OMF) preservation. With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the mechanism of microbial inactivation. Discuss the factors influencing OMF efficacy and its industrial applications.*

#### Part (a) — Principle, Construction, and Working [7 Marks]

**Principle:**
OMF preservation is based on the principle that a **time-varying high-intensity magnetic field** (typically 5–50 Tesla, oscillating at 1–100 kHz) induces an **internal electric field** in the food via Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction. This induced field exerts mechanical and electrical stresses on microbial cell membranes and biomolecules, leading to inactivation without significant bulk heating.

**Construction (Key Components):**

1. **High-voltage DC power supply:** Charges the capacitor bank to several kV.
2. **Capacitor bank:** Stores the energy required for a single pulse train.
3. **Thyristor / IGBT switching module:** Routes the stored energy into the treatment coil at the desired frequency.
4. **Treatment coil (solenoid):** A water-cooled copper coil (often hollow conductor) with $n$ turns per unit length, defining the working volume.
5. **LC resonant circuit:** Determines the oscillation frequency $f = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$.
6. **Treatment chamber:** Located inside the bore of the solenoid, holding the food (typically liquid or semi-solid).
7. **Cooling system:** Removes resistive (I²R) heat from the coil to allow repetitive pulsing.
8. **Control and monitoring unit:** Sets field strength, frequency, pulse count, and temperature.

**Working:**
- The capacitor bank is charged to high voltage (typically 2–5 kV).
- Upon triggering, the capacitor discharges through the solenoid via the switching module, producing a damped sinusoidal current at the resonant frequency.
- This current generates an axial magnetic field $B = \mu_0 n I$ that oscillates (reverses polarity) at the oscillation frequency.
- The food, placed inside the solenoid bore, experiences a rapidly time-varying $B$-field.
- By Faraday's Law, an azimuthal induced electric field $E_{\text{ind}} = (r/2)(dB/dt)$ is generated inside the food.
- This field drives eddy currents in ionic cellular media, exerting mechanical and electrical stress on microbial membranes and biomolecules.
- The treatment is repeated for $n$ pulse trains (typically 1–100) to achieve the target log reduction.
- The total energy input is low, so bulk food temperature rises by less than 2–5 °C.

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to **Section 4.2** for the complete diagram.)

#### Part (b) — Mechanism, Factors, and Applications [7 Marks]

**Mechanism of Microbial Inactivation:**

The inactivation of microorganisms in OMF arises from a combination of physical effects:

1. **Electroporation of Cell Membranes:**
   - The induced electric field $E_{\text{ind}}$ creates a transmembrane potential $\Delta V_m = E_{\text{ind}} \cdot d$, where $d$ is the membrane thickness (~5 nm).
   - When $\Delta V_m$ exceeds a critical threshold (~1 V), the lipid bilayer rearranges, forming **aqueous pores**.
   - These pores allow uncontrolled ion and metabolite flux, disrupting cellular homeostasis and causing **cell lysis**.

2. **Magnetomechanical Effects:**
   - Diamagnetic and paramagnetic molecules (e.g., proteins with iron-sulfur clusters, membrane lipids) experience a **magnetic torque** $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{m} \times \mathbf{B}$.
   - The high-frequency oscillation (kHz range) drives these molecules into **resonance**, deforming protein tertiary structure and membrane integrity.

3. **Induction of Eddy Currents and Localized Heating:**
   - Eddy currents dissipate as **Joule heat** ($I^2 R$) in the ionic cellular medium.
   - Localized microscopic heating (~tens of mK) can denature membrane-bound enzymes.
   - This effect is *non-thermal at the bulk level* but *micro-thermal at the cellular level*.

4. **Disruption of Ion Channel Function:**
   - Voltage-gated ion channels (Ca²⁺, K⁺, Na⁺) require precise transmembrane potentials to function.
   - The induced fields depolarize these channels, collapsing the membrane potential and triggering apoptosis-like pathways in some microbes.

**Factors Influencing OMF Efficacy:**

| Factor | Effect on Inactivation |
|---|---|
| **Magnetic flux density (B)** | Higher B → higher $dB/dt$ → higher $E_{\text{ind}}$ → greater inactivation |
| **Oscillation frequency (f)** | Higher f → higher $dB/dt$ → more pulses per second → more membrane stress |
| **Number of pulse trains (n)** | More trains → cumulative damage → higher log reduction; saturation above ~50 |
| **Sample radius (r)** | Larger radius → higher peripheral $E_{\text{ind}}$ but lower at center (radial gradient) |
| **Electrical conductivity of food** | Higher conductivity → stronger eddy currents → greater inactivation, but also more heating |
| **Microbial species and growth phase** | Vegetative cells > spores; exponential-phase cells most sensitive |
| **Treatment temperature** | Moderate pre-heating (~40–50 °C) synergistically improves inactivation |

**Industrial Applications:**

- Preservation of **liquid foods**: fruit juices, milk, beer, wine, and liquid egg.
- **Shelf-life extension** of fresh produce (post-harvest treatment).
- **Inactivation of pathogens** including *Escherichia coli*, *Salmonella Typhimurium*, *Listeria monocytogenes*, and spoilage yeasts (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*).
- Combined hurdle technology with mild heat, low pH, or natural antimicrobials.
- Continuous-flow OMF systems for high-throughput industrial processing.

**Advantages:** Truly non-thermal (minimal quality loss), uniform treatment of bulk liquids, no chemical additives, energy-efficient.
**Limitations:** Limited efficacy on solid foods, requires uniform field geometry, scaling up to industrial throughput remains a challenge, regulatory approval is still under evaluation in some regions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION BULLETS — Must Memorize for KTU ESE:**

- **PLT** = Broad-spectrum (200–1100 nm), pulsed (1 µs – 0.1 s), xenon flash-based surface preservation.
- **OMF** = Time-varying magnetic field (5–50 T, 1–100 kHz) for bulk liquid preservation; *static* fields are ineffective.
- **Three inactivation mechanisms of PLT:** Photochemical (UV-C → DNA dimers), Photothermal (local heating), Photophysical (shock waves / cavitation).
- **DNA absorption peak:** 253.7 nm (thymine dimer formation).
- **Fluence formula:** $H = nE_p/A$ (in J cm⁻²).
- **FDA approval for PLT:** 21 CFR 179.41, since 1996.
- **Induced field in OMF:** $E_{\text{ind}} = (r/2)(dB/dt)$ — linear in radius, peaks at the food periphery.
- **D-value formula:** $D = 2.303 / k$; log reduction = $k_{10} \cdot t$.
- **PLT penetration depth:** Surface only (a few mm); opaque or pigmented foods limit efficacy.
- **OMF penetration depth:** Bulk (entire volume), but radial non-uniformity exists.
- **Peak PLT power:** Megawatt class (e.g., 720 J in 200 µs = 3.6 MW).
- **Capacitor energy:** $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$; PLT typically uses 2–5 kV charging.
- **Lorentz force on ions in OMF:** $\mathbf{F} = q\mathbf{v} \times \mathbf{B}$.
- **Resonant frequency of OMF coil:** $f = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$.
- **Typical log reductions:** PLT = 5–9 log (surfaces); OMF = 3–5 log (liquids).
- **Bulk temperature rise:** PLT < 5 °C; OMF < 2 °C — *true non-thermal* processes.
- **Key difference from PEF:** PEF uses *external* high-voltage electrodes; OMF uses *induced* fields from a magnetic coil.
- **Industrial leaders:** PLT — Claranor, Xenon Corp.; OMF — pilot scale, several research institutions.
- **Diagram is mandatory** in any 7- or 14-mark question — losing it costs 2–3 marks.
- **Always quote units:** J cm⁻² for fluence, Tesla for B, V m⁻¹ for induced E-field, Hz for frequency.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equipment and its Functioning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT631 - NON-THERMAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Equipment and its Functioning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining Non-Thermal Processing Equipment

**Non-Thermal Processing (NTP) Equipment** refers to the specialized industrial machinery, reactors, and process lines engineered to inactivate microorganisms, extend shelf-life, and modify structural properties of food and biological materials **without relying on heat as the primary lethal agent**. These systems preserve sensory, nutritional, and functional attributes that are typically degraded by conventional thermal pasteurization or sterilization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 4 Focus):**
> Non-thermal processing equipment comprises **pressure vessels, electrical discharge systems, acoustic reactors, and advanced plasma/irradiation chambers** that deliver physical or electromagnetic energy to food matrices under controlled conditions, achieving microbial inactivation and bio-preservation while maintaining a processing temperature generally below **70 °C** (or with a minimal temperature rise of less than **5–10 °C** above the initial product temperature).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Gentle Giant"

Imagine you have a **delicate, hand-painted silk scarf** covered with dust and germs. You want to clean it, but a washing machine (heat/water/chemicals) would ruin the colors. Instead, you place it in a **hydraulic press chamber** and apply **uniform pressure from all sides** (like the crushing pressure of the deep ocean). The dust and germs are physically ruptured — but the silk and its colors remain untouched.

> **That is the essence of non-thermal processing equipment.** It uses **physical vectors** (pressure, electric fields, sound waves, light, plasma) as the "cleaning tool" instead of heat.

## 1.3 Classification of Non-Thermal Processing Equipment

| S.No | Equipment Category | Primary Physical Principle | Typical Operating Range |
|:----:|:-------------------|:---------------------------|:------------------------|
| 1 | **High Pressure Processing (HPP)** | Isostatic Pressure Transmission | **100 – 1000 MPa** |
| 2 | **Pulsed Electric Field (PEF)** | Electroporation via Electric Pulses | **10 – 80 kV/cm** |
| 3 | **Ultrasound (US) Processor** | Acoustic Cavitation | **20 – 100 kHz** |
| 4 | **Ozone Generator** | Oxidative Disinfection by O₃ | **0.5 – 5 ppm** |
| 5 | **UV-C Sterilizer** | DNA/RNA Damage at 254 nm | **10 – 200 mJ/cm²** |
| 6 | **Cold Plasma Reactor** | Reactive Species Generation | **10⁻³ – 10² Pa** |
| 7 | **Ionizing Irradiation** | Gamma/X-ray/E-beam | **0.1 – 10 kGy** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Trend (2024 Scheme):** The examiner frequently tests students on **equipment schematic components, operating parameters, and the relationship between process intensity and microbial log reduction**. Memorize the bolded units and energy vectors above.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 High Pressure Processing (HPP) Equipment — "The Pressure Vessel"

### 2.1.1 Core Components
- **Pressure Vessel (Autoclave):** A thick-walled, forged steel cylinder (often **Yongson-steel or Monel alloy**) capable of withstanding **600–1000 MPa** internal pressure.
- **Yoke Frame & Closure System:** A self-sealing threaded closure that clamps the lid using hydraulic force.
- **Intensifier Pump:** Converts low-pressure hydraulic fluid (**~30 MPa**) into ultra-high pressure using a **piston area ratio**.
- **Pressure Transmission Medium:** Deionized water, food-grade glycol mixtures, or castor oil.
- **Thermocouples & Pressure Transducers:** Real-time monitoring sensors.

### 2.1.2 Working Principle
According to the **Le Chatelier Principle** and **Pressure–Temperature–Time (PTT)** concept, isostatic pressure acts *instantaneously and uniformly* throughout the food (Pascal's Law), independent of geometry. Microbial inactivation follows first-order kinetics:

$$ N = N_0 \cdot e^{-k_P \cdot t} $$

where $N$ = surviving count (CFU/mL), $N_0$ = initial count, $k_P$ = pressure-dependent inactivation rate constant (min⁻¹), and $t$ = hold time (min).

### 2.1.3 Operational Cycle
1. **Loading** — Pre-packaged food (in flexible pouches) placed in carrier basket.
2. **Closure** — Vessel sealed, residual air removed.
3. **Pressurization** — Water pumped in, intensifier achieves target pressure.
4. **Hold** — Pressure maintained for 1–15 minutes.
5. **Depressurization** — Rapid release; cycle ends.

## 2.2 Pulsed Electric Field (PEF) Equipment

### 2.2.1 Components
- **High-Voltage Pulse Generator** (capacitor bank discharging through a thyratron or IGBT switch).
- **Treatment Chamber** (co-field, coaxial, or parallel plate geometry).
- **Cooling System** (heat exchangers prevent Joule heating).
- **Control & Data Acquisition Unit.**

### 2.2.2 Theory — Electroporation
The applied field $E$ (kV/cm) creates a transmembrane potential $U_m$ that disrupts the lipid bilayer when it exceeds the critical value (**~1 V**):

$$ U_m = 0.75 \cdot r \cdot E \cdot \cos\theta $$

where $r$ = cell radius (m), $\theta$ = angle from field direction.

> **Why it works:** When $U_m > U_{crit}$, the membrane forms **irreversible pores**, leading to cell leakage and death.

## 2.3 Ultrasound Processing Equipment

### 2.3.1 Components
- **Ultrasonic Transducer** (piezoelectric: lead zirconate titanate, **PZT**).
- **Horn/Probe** (Stepped or Booster horn, titanium).
- **Reactor Vessel** (batch or flow-through).
- **Power Supply** (typically **20 kHz, 50–2000 W**).

### 2.3.2 Cavitation Energy Release
Acoustic energy density:

$$ E_{acoustic} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot v^2 \cdot c_s^2 \cdot t $$

where $\rho$ = medium density (kg/m³), $v$ = particle velocity, $c_s$ = speed of sound in medium (~1480 m/s in water), $t$ = sonication time.

Cavitation bubble collapse generates **localized hotspots** of **~5000 K** and pressures of **~100 MPa**, but bulk temperature remains low.

## 2.4 Ozone Generation Equipment

- **Corona Discharge Reactor** is the industrial standard.
- Dry air or oxygen passes through a high-voltage gap (**6–20 kV**), splitting O₂ into atomic O, which combines with O₂ to form **O₃**.
- Ozone is then **bubbled into water** or applied to food surfaces.

## 2.5 UV-C Sterilization Equipment

- **Low-pressure mercury vapor lamps** emit monochromatic radiation at **253.7 nm**.
- Inactivation kinetics follow the **Chick-Watson model**:

$$ \log\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -k_{UV} \cdot I \cdot t $$

where $I$ = UV intensity (mW/cm²), $k_{UV}$ = microbial UV susceptibility constant.

## 2.6 Cold Plasma Equipment

- Two main types: **Dielectric Barrier Discharge (DBD)** and **Atmospheric Pressure Plasma Jet (APPJ)**.
- Operating gas: **Helium, Argon, or Air**, often with feed gas additives.
- Generates **Reactive Oxygen and Nitrogen Species (RONS)**: O, O₃, OH•, NO, NO₂.

## 2.7 Irradiation Equipment

| Irradiator Type | Radioactive Source | Penetration |
|:----------------|:-------------------|:-------------|
| **Gamma Irradiator** | Co-60 / Cs-137 | **Very high** |
| **X-ray Irradiator** | Electron-accelerated target | **Very high** |
| **E-beam Irradiator** | Electron Accelerator (5–10 MeV) | **Low–Medium** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The minimum dose for pasteurization is **1 kGy**, and for sterilization is **≥ 25 kGy** (per Codex Alimentarius).

## 2.8 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `|` inside table cells to avoid markdown breaking.**

| Formula | Description | Units | Equation # |
|:-------|:------------|:------|:-----------|
| $N = N_0 e^{-k_P t}$ | First-order microbial inactivation kinetics | dimensionless | 1 |
| $U_m = 0.75 \, r E \cos\theta$ | Transmembrane potential (PEF) | V | 2 |
| $E_{acoustic} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho v^2 c_s^2 t$ | Acoustic energy density | J/m³ | 3 |
| $\log(N/N_0) = -k_{UV} I t$ | Chick-Watson UV inactivation | log CFU | 4 |
| $D_0 = \log^{-1} e \cdot k^{-1}$ | D-value calculation | min or s | 5 |
| $z_p = \frac{T_{ref} - T_2}{\log(D_1/D_2)}$ | Pressure z-value | MPa | 6 |
| $P_{intensifier} = P_{low} \cdot (A_1/A_2)$ | Pressure amplification | MPa | 7 |
| $W_{PEF} = E^2 \cdot \sigma \cdot t$ | Energy per pulse | J | 8 |
| $O_3 \, yield = \frac{m_{O_3}}{E_{input}} \cdot 1000$ | Ozone yield | g/kWh | 9 |
| $D_{abs} = \dot{D} \cdot t$ | Absorbed irradiation dose | kGy | 10 |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Pressure Amplification in HPP Intensifier Pump

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup
An HPP system uses a **hydraulic intensifier** to convert low-pressure input ($P_{in}$) into ultra-high pressure output ($P_{out}$). The intensifier has a **low-pressure piston of area $A_1$** and a **high-pressure piston of area $A_2$**. Derive the relationship.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Apply the principle of force balance.**
The hydraulic force on both pistons must be in equilibrium during quasi-static operation (neglecting friction and dynamic losses):

$$ F_1 = F_2 $$

$$ P_{in} \cdot A_1 = P_{out} \cdot A_2 $$

**Step 2: Solve for the output pressure.**

$$ P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2} $$

**Step 3: Interpret the area ratio.**
Define the **intensification ratio** $\beta$ as:

$$ \beta = \frac{A_1}{A_2} $$

Therefore:

$$ P_{out} = \beta \cdot P_{in} $$

**Step 4: Numerical example.**
If $P_{in} = 30$ MPa, $A_1 = 200$ cm², and $A_2 = 10$ cm²:

$$ P_{out} = 30 \cdot \frac{200}{10} = 600 \text{ MPa} $$

**Step 5: Mechanical work consideration.**

$$ W = P_{in} \cdot V_1 = P_{out} \cdot V_2 $$

$$ P_{in} \cdot A_1 \cdot x_1 = P_{out} \cdot A_2 \cdot x_2 $$

where $x_1$ and $x_2$ are piston displacements.

**Step 6: Volumetric constraint.**

$$ A_1 \cdot x_1 = A_2 \cdot x_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_2 = \frac{A_1}{A_2} \cdot x_1 = \beta \cdot x_1 $$

> **Result:** A 20:1 area ratio amplifies pressure by 20× and increases piston displacement by 20×. This explains why HPP pumps are **mechanically slow but capable of enormous pressure output**.

## 3.2 Derivation — PEF Energy per Pulse

### 3.2.1 Problem
A PEF treatment chamber has a volume $V$ of 5 mL, an electrical conductivity $\sigma$ of 0.5 S/m, an applied field strength $E$ of 30 kV/cm, and a pulse width $\tau$ of 5 μs. Calculate the energy per pulse.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Convert units to SI.**
- $E = 30 \text{ kV/cm} = 30 \times 10^5 \text{ V/m} = 3 \times 10^6 \text{ V/m}$
- $V = 5 \text{ mL} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$
- $\sigma = 0.5 \text{ S/m}$
- $\tau = 5 \text{ μs} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$

**Step 2: Apply the PEF energy equation:**

$$ W_{PEF} = E^2 \cdot \sigma \cdot t \cdot V $$

Wait — the correct volumetric energy density form is:

$$ w = \sigma \cdot E^2 \cdot t \quad \text{(J/m}^3\text{)} $$

**Step 3: Compute energy density:**

$$ w = 0.5 \cdot (3 \times 10^6)^2 \cdot (5 \times 10^{-6}) $$

$$ w = 0.5 \cdot 9 \times 10^{12} \cdot 5 \times 10^{-6} $$

$$ w = 0.5 \cdot 45 \times 10^{6} $$

$$ w = 22.5 \times 10^{6} \text{ J/m}^3 = 22.5 \text{ J/mL} $$

**Step 4: Compute total energy per pulse:**

$$ W_{PEF} = w \cdot V = 22.5 \cdot 5 = 112.5 \text{ J} $$

> **Result:** Each pulse delivers **112.5 J** to the food. For 10 pulses, total energy is **1125 J** ≈ **1.125 kJ**.

## 3.3 Derivation — Acoustic Energy in Ultrasound Reactor

### 3.3.1 Problem
An ultrasonic bath operates at 40 kHz in water. The probe tip oscillates with peak velocity $v = 5$ m/s. Calculate the acoustic intensity.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Acoustic intensity formula:**

$$ I = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot c_s \cdot v^2 $$

**Step 2: Substitute values (water at 25 °C):**
- $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³
- $c_s = 1480$ m/s
- $v = 5$ m/s

**Step 3: Compute:**

$$ I = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1000 \cdot 1480 \cdot 25 $$

$$ I = 0.5 \cdot 1000 \cdot 1480 \cdot 25 $$

$$ I = 0.5 \cdot 3.7 \times 10^7 $$

$$ I = 1.85 \times 10^7 \text{ W/m}^2 $$

> **Result:** Acoustic intensity ≈ **18.5 MW/m²**, sufficient to drive violent cavitation.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameter Limits

| Equipment | Min Operational Limit | Max Operational Limit | Critical Failure Threshold |
|:----------|:---------------------|:----------------------|:---------------------------|
| HPP Vessel | 100 MPa | 1000 MPa | **> 1.5 × P_rated** → seal rupture |
| PEF Strength | 5 kV/cm | 80 kV/cm | **Dielectric breakdown** of food |
| Ultrasound | 20 kHz | 100 kHz | **Cavitation erosion** on probe |
| Ozone | 0.1 ppm | 5 ppm | **> 5 ppm** → toxic for workers |
| UV-C Dose | 5 mJ/cm² | 200 mJ/cm² | **> 1000 mJ/cm²** → tissue damage |
| Cold Plasma | 10 W | 1000 W | **Arcing** at electrodes |
| Gamma Irradiation | 0.1 kGy | 50 kGy | **> 50 kGy** → polymer degradation |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Integrated Non-Thermal Processing Line

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawMat[Raw Food Material] --> sort[Sorting and Grading]
    sort --> wash[Washing Station]
    wash --> prep[Pre-conditioning]
    
    prep --> choice1{NTP Selection}
    
    choice1 --> hpp[HPP Vessel Module]
    choice1 --> pef[PEF Chamber Module]
    choice1 --> us[Ultrasonic Reactor]
    choice1 --> o3[Ozone Injection Unit]
    choice1 --> uv[UV C Tunnel]
    choice1 --> cp[Cold Plasma Reactor]
    choice1 --> irrad[Irradiation Chamber]
    
    hpp --> hppMon[Pressure and Temp Monitor]
    pef --> pefMon[Voltage and Pulse Logger]
    us --> usMon[Frequency and Power Monitor]
    o3 --> o3Mon[Ozone Concentration Sensor]
    uv --> uvMon[UV Intensity Meter]
    cp --> cpMon[Emission Spectrometer]
    irrad --> irradMon[Dosimeter Reading]
    
    hppMon --> pack[Packaging Section]
    pefMon --> pack
    usMon --> pack
    o3Mon --> pack
    uvMon --> pack
    cpMon --> pack
    irradMon --> pack
    
    pack --> cold[Refrigerated Storage]
    cold --> dist[Distribution]
    
    style rawMat fill:#FFF8DC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style choice1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px
    style pack fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style dist fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 HPP Equipment — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feed[Food Pouches] --> basket[Loading Basket]
    basket --> vessel[Pressure Vessel]
    
    hydPump[Hydraulic Pump] --> intPump[Intensifier]
    intPump --> vessel
    vessel --> medCirc[Pressure Medium Recirculation]
    
    trans[Pressure Transducer] --> vessel
    thermo[Thermocouple Array] --> vessel
    plc[PLC Controller] --> hydPump
    plc --> intPump
    plc --> display[Operator HMI Display]
    
    vessel --> decomp[Decompression Valve]
    decomp --> unload[Unloading Station]
    unload --> nextLine[To Packaging]
    
    style vessel fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px
    style intPump fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style plc fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 PEF Equipment — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    hvu[High Voltage DC Supply] --> capBank[Capacitor Bank]
    capBank --> switch[Thyratron or IGBT Switch]
    switch --> chamber[Treatment Chamber]
    
    foodIn[Liquid Food Inlet] --> chamber
    chamber --> foodOut[Processed Food Outlet]
    
    coolSys[Cooling Heat Exchanger] --> chamber
    pMeter[Voltage Probe] --> chamber
    iMeter[Current Probe] --> chamber
    osci[Oscilloscope] --> pMeter
    osci --> iMeter
    osci --> dataAcq[Data Acquisition PC]
    
    style capBank fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style chamber fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style osci fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Cold Plasma Reactor — DBD Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    gasCyl[Gas Cylinder He or Ar] --> mfm[Mass Flow Meter]
    mfm --> dbdReactor[DBD Reactor Chamber]
    
    hva[High Voltage AC Source] --> upperEl[Upper Electrode]
    lowerEl[Lower Electrode] --> ground[Ground Connection]
    upperEl --> dielectric[Quartz Dielectric Barrier]
    dielectric --> dbdReactor
    lowerEl --> dbdReactor
    
    sample[Food Sample] --> dbdReactor
    dbdReactor --> plasmaOut[Plasma Treated Sample]
    
    oes[Optical Emission Spectrometer] --> dbdReactor
    ronsDet[RONS Detector] --> dbdReactor
    
    style dbdReactor fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:3px
    style dielectric fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style plasmaOut fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Notice:** All node identifiers are alphanumeric (no `end`, `style`, `subgraph`, or `graph` keywords misused). Labels are clean uppercase text without markdown bold/italic tags to ensure clean rendering.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**List the main components of a High Pressure Processing (HPP) system and explain the role of the intensifier pump.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The main components of an HPP system are:
1. **Pressure vessel (autoclave)** – thick-walled steel chamber that holds the food and transmits isostatic pressure (**1 mark**).
2. **Yoke frame and closure system** – seals the vessel under ultra-high pressure (**0.5 marks**).
3. **Intensifier pump** – amplifies low-pressure hydraulic input (e.g., 30 MPa) to ultra-high pressure (e.g., 600 MPa) using piston area ratio amplification: $P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot A_1/A_2$ (**1 mark**).
4. **Pressure transmission medium and sensors** – deionized water + thermocouples/pressure transducers (**0.5 marks**).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Distinguish between Dielectric Barrier Discharge (DBD) and Atmospheric Pressure Plasma Jet (APPJ) reactors used in cold plasma processing.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | DBD | APPJ |
|:----------|:----|:-----|
| **Electrode geometry** | Two parallel electrodes separated by dielectric | Concentric needle-nozzle configuration (**1 mark**) |
| **Plasma generation** | Microdischarges across dielectric barrier | Continuous plasma jet ejected through nozzle (**1 mark**) |
| **Sample treatment** | Direct contact in chamber | Remote exposure via carrier gas stream (**1 mark**) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[Internal Choice as per KTU 2024 Scheme: Answer EITHER Question A OR Question B]**

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **construction, working principle, and key components of a High Pressure Processing (HPP) system**. List any **five commercial food products** processed by HPP. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **pressure amplification equation** for an HPP intensifier pump. An HPP system operates with a low-pressure input of **25 MPa** and an intensifier piston area ratio of **1:25**. Calculate the **output pressure** delivered to the vessel. **(7 Marks)**

### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — HPP System Description (7 Marks)

**Construction and Components (4 Marks):**

1. **Pressure Vessel (Autoclave)** — Forged from high-tensile steel, lined with corrosion-resistant alloy. Operates at **300–600 MPa** (commercial) up to **1000 MPa** (research).
2. **Closure System** — A self-sealing threaded lid clamped by a hydraulic yoke. The seal uses Buna-N or PTFE O-rings rated for ultra-high pressure.
3. **Intensifier Pump** — Dual-piston assembly that amplifies pressure from 25–35 MPa to 600 MPa.
4. **Pressure Transmission Medium** — Deionized water (preferred) or propylene glycol-water mixtures to prevent freezing under adiabatic compression.
5. **Instrumentation** — Strain-gauge pressure transducers, Type-T thermocouples, and PLC-based control.
6. **Loading/Unloading System** — Baskets and conveyors for pre-packaged food pouches.

**Working Principle (2 Marks):**

Food in flexible, vacuum-sealed packaging is loaded into the vessel. The vessel is sealed and filled with pressure medium. The intensifier raises pressure to the target value within **2–5 minutes**. Pressure is held for **1–15 minutes** depending on the log-reduction requirement (typically **5-log reduction** for pasteurization). Depressurization is rapid; the food experiences **uniform isostatic compression** because pressure transmits equally in all directions (Pascal's Law).

**Five Commercial HPP Products (1 Mark):**
- HPP guacamole (avocado paste)
- HPP packaged deli meats (turkey, ham)
- HPP cold-pressed fruit juices
- HPP seafood (oysters, ready-to-eat shrimp)
- HPP hummus and ready meals

#### Part (b) — Pressure Amplification Derivation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $P_{in} = 25$ MPa
- Area ratio: $A_1/A_2 = 25/1$

**Derivation (4 Marks):**

Apply force balance across the two pistons of the intensifier:

$$ P_{in} \cdot A_1 = P_{out} \cdot A_2 $$

Solve for output pressure:

$$ P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2} $$

Define intensification ratio $\beta = A_1/A_2$:

$$ P_{out} = \beta \cdot P_{in} $$

**Numerical Calculation (2 Marks):**

$$ P_{out} = 25 \text{ MPa} \cdot 25 = 625 \text{ MPa} $$

**Result & Conclusion (1 Mark):**

The system delivers **625 MPa**, which falls within the commercial HPP operating range (**400–600 MPa** for pasteurization). The vessel design rating must be **at least 1.25 × P_max = 781 MPa** to comply with the **ASME BPVC Section VIII** safety factor.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Describe the **construction and working principle of a Pulsed Electric Field (PEF) treatment system**. Explain the concept of **electroporation** with a suitable transmembrane potential equation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A PEF chamber processes **100 mL of liquid food** at an applied field strength of **35 kV/cm** and pulse width of **4 μs**. The food has an electrical conductivity of **0.6 S/m**. Calculate the **total energy delivered per pulse** and the **average power** if the system delivers **10 pulses per second**. **(7 Marks)**

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — PEF System Description (7 Marks)

**Construction (3 Marks):**

1. **High-Voltage Pulse Generator** — A DC power supply charges a capacitor bank (**0.5–10 μF**). A high-speed switch (thyratron, IGBT, or MOSFET) discharges the bank through the treatment chamber, generating short, high-intensity pulses.
2. **Treatment Chamber** — The food flows between two electrodes (parallel plate, coaxial, or co-field design). Chamber volume is typically **10–100 mL** for continuous systems.
3. **Cooling System** — Heat exchangers or refrigerated jackets remove Joule heating ($Q = I^2 R t$) to maintain product temperature below **40 °C**.
4. **Control System** — Monitors voltage, current, pulse shape, and frequency.

**Working Principle & Electroporation (4 Marks):**

A typical PEF process applies **1–20 pulses** of microsecond duration at field strengths of **20–80 kV/cm**. Each pulse creates a transmembrane potential $U_m$ given by:

$$ U_m = 0.75 \cdot r \cdot E \cdot \cos\theta $$

where $r$ is the cell radius and $\theta$ is the angle between the field vector and the membrane normal. When $U_m$ exceeds the **critical value of ~1 V**, **irreversible electroporation** occurs: hydrophilic pores form in the lipid bilayer, leading to leakage of cytoplasmic contents, pH collapse, and cell death. Since the food temperature remains low, **vitamins, flavor compounds, and color pigments are preserved**.

#### Part (b) — PEF Energy and Power Calculation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $V = 100$ mL $= 100 \times 10^{-6}$ m³ $= 10^{-4}$ m³
- $E = 35$ kV/cm $= 35 \times 10^5$ V/m $= 3.5 \times 10^6$ V/m
- $\sigma = 0.6$ S/m
- $\tau = 4$ μs $= 4 \times 10^{-6}$ s
- Pulse frequency $f = 10$ Hz

**Step 1: Compute volumetric energy density (2 Marks):**

$$ w = \sigma \cdot E^2 \cdot \tau $$

$$ w = 0.6 \cdot (3.5 \times 10^6)^2 \cdot (4 \times 10^{-6}) $$

$$ w = 0.6 \cdot 1.225 \times 10^{13} \cdot 4 \times 10^{-6} $$

$$ w = 0.6 \cdot 4.9 \times 10^7 $$

$$ w = 2.94 \times 10^7 \text{ J/m}^3 = 29.4 \text{ J/mL} $$

**Step 2: Compute energy per pulse (2 Marks):**

$$ W_{pulse} = w \cdot V = 29.4 \cdot 100 = 2940 \text{ J} = 2.94 \text{ kJ} $$

**Step 3: Compute average power (2 Marks):**

$$ P_{avg} = W_{pulse} \cdot f = 2.94 \text{ kJ} \cdot 10 = 29.4 \text{ kW} $$

**Step 4: Interpretation (1 Mark):**

The system delivers **2.94 kJ per pulse** at an average power draw of **29.4 kW**, which is comparable to industrial-scale PEF systems used in fruit juice pasteurization. Energy efficiency is generally **60–80%**, with the remainder lost as heat.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — KTU Board Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks in Module 4 — Equipment & Functioning:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing energy equations** — PEF uses $E^2 \sigma t$ (electric), not $E_{acoustic} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v^2 c_s^2 t$ (acoustic). Mixing these two will cost **3 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units** — $E$ in kV/cm must be converted to V/m before squaring. Mark deduction: **2 marks**.
> 3. **Skipping the derivation steps** — KTU examiners allocate **3–4 marks** purely for the *algebraic transitions*, not just the final answer.
> 4. **Wrong intensification ratio direction** — Students often write $P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot A_2/A_1$ (inverted). Remember: **large area → low-pressure side; small area → high-pressure side**.
> 5. **Ignoring safety/regulatory aspects** — Mentioning **ASME BPVC**, **Codex Alimentarius** (for irradiation), or **FDA FCN approval** earns an extra **1 mark** for "engineering professionalism."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 — Equipment and its Functioning: Last-Minute Revision Notes**

- ✅ **HPP**: Pressure vessel + intensifier + water medium; **100–1000 MPa**; 5-log pasteurization; preserves vitamins, flavor.
- ✅ **PEF**: Capacitor bank + chamber + cooling; **10–80 kV/cm**; electroporation; **$U_m = 0.75 r E \cos\theta$**.
- ✅ **Ultrasound**: PZT transducer + horn; **20–100 kHz**; **cavitation** at ~5000 K localized.
- ✅ **Ozone**: Corona discharge; **0.5–5 ppm**; strong oxidant; residue-free (degrades to O₂).
- ✅ **UV-C**: 254 nm mercury lamp; Chick-Watson kinetics; surface sterilization only (low penetration).
- ✅ **Cold Plasma**: DBD or APPJ; **RONS species**; emerging for fresh produce.
- ✅ **Irradiation**: Co-60 gamma or e-beam; **1 kGy pasteurization, 25 kGy sterilization**.
- ✅ **Master equation**: $N = N_0 e^{-kt}$ for any first-order inactivation; $W_{PEF} = E^2 \sigma t V$.
- ✅ **Commercial HPP brands**: Hiperbaric, JBT Avure, Stansted Fluid Power.
- ✅ **Commercial PEF brands**: Elea, Pulsemaster, CoolWave.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 mantra**: *Process intensity × residence time = microbial log reduction.*
- ✅ **Always include** units, a sketch (block diagram), and a real-world example in long answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Extruders
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT631 - NON-THERMAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Extruders

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **extruder** is a continuous, high-shear mechanical processing device that forces a material (typically food biopolymers, proteins, starches, or dough) through a die of defined cross-section under controlled conditions of pressure, temperature, moisture, and shear, thereby transforming raw ingredients into shaped, texturized, or cooked products. In the specific context of **non-thermal processing (NTP)**, extruders are repurposed as *mechano-thermal* reactors where structural, textural, and rheological transformations are achieved predominantly by **mechanical shear and pressure**, with thermal input acting as a secondary, residual effect rather than the primary processing agent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Although extrusion is technically a *thermo-mechanical* process, it is included in non-thermal processing modules because the transformations (gelatinization, protein denaturation, texturization) can be achieved at much lower residence times and temperatures than conventional thermal processing, and the emphasis is on **mechanical disruption of cellular and molecular structures**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a giant industrial **"mechanical syringe"** combined with a **"play-doh press"**:

- You feed raw dough (or meal) into a wide barrel at one end (the throat).
- A rotating **screw** (like a giant drill bit) inside the barrel acts like an **Archimedes' screw** or the rotating auger of a *sausage stuffer* — it grabs the material, pushes it forward, compresses it, shears it, and squeezes it out through a small opening (the **die**).
- The resulting product emerges as a continuous shaped strand — just like toothpaste coming out of a tube, or pasta coming out of a pasta machine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** The process is functionally similar to squeezing a piping bag (icing bag) used in baking — the manual pressure on the bag (screw rotation) forces the soft material out through a shaped nozzle (die) to form decorative patterns. In extruders, this process is automated, pressurized, and includes heating, mixing, and texturization.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The fundamental engineering metrics used to characterize extruder operation in food/non-thermal processing are:

- **L/D Ratio (Length-to-Diameter):** Ranges typically from **10:1 to 30:1** for food extruders.
- **Screw Speed (N):** Typically **100 to 800 rpm**.
- **Compression Ratio (CR):** Generally **1:1 to 5:1** for single-screw extruders.
- **Barrel Temperature:** Usually **80 °C to 200 °C** in the cooking zone.
- **Throughput (Q):** Ranges from **50 kg/h (lab scale) to >10,000 kg/h (industrial)**.
- **Specific Mechanical Energy (SME):** **50 to 1500 Wh/kg** is the typical operating window.
- **Die Pressure:** Typically **2 to 15 MPa**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The classification of extruders in KTU Module 4 is based on: (1) number of screws, (2) screw rotation direction, (3) screw intermeshing, (4) moisture content of feed, and (5) application objective (cooking, forming, texturizing).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Extruders

Extruders in non-thermal food processing are classified along the following hierarchy:

**A. Based on Number of Screws:**
1. **Single-Screw Extruder (SSE)**
2. **Twin-Screw Extruder (TSE)**

**B. Based on Screw Rotation Direction (Twin-Screw):**
1. **Co-rotating Twin-Screw Extruder** — both screws rotate in the same direction.
2. **Counter-rotating Twin-Screw Extruder** — screws rotate in opposite directions.

**C. Based on Screw Intermeshing (Twin-Screw):**
1. **Intermeshing (Engaging) Twin-Screw**
2. **Non-Intermeshing (Non-engaging) Twin-Screw**

**D. Based on Moisture Content:**
1. **Low-Moisture Extrusion** (< 25% moisture) — for snacks, cereals, pellets.
2. **High-Moisture Extrusion Cooking (HMEC)** (> 40% moisture) — for meat analogues, TVP.

**E. Based on Application:**
1. **Cold/Forming Extruder** — pasta forming, pet food shaping.
2. **Cooking Extruder** — expanded snacks.
3. **Texturizing Extruder** — meat analogues.

### 2.2 Single-Screw Extruder (SSE) — Deep Analysis

#### Operational Logic Steps:

1. **Feeding (Zone 1 — Feed/Solids Conveying Zone):** Raw material is fed via a gravimetric or volumetric feeder into the hopper. The deep-flighted section of the screw conveys loose solid particles forward.

2. **Mixing and Compression (Zone 2 — Transition/Kneading Zone):** Flight depth decreases along the screw. This **compression zone** squeezes out air, compacts the material, and begins to generate pressure. Mechanical energy input rises sharply.

3. **Cooking/Shear (Zone 3 — Metering Zone):** Here, the flight depth is shallowest. Material experiences maximum shear and pressure. **Starch gelatinization**, **protein denaturation**, and **Maillard-type reactions** occur here.

4. **Die Zone (Zone 4 — Discharge Zone):** The molten viscoelastic mass is forced through the die. As it exits, the sudden **pressure drop** causes superheated water to flash into steam, leading to **puffing/expansion** — a hallmark of extrusion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three zones?** The single-screw geometry simulates the three classical engineering unit operations in series: **conveying → compressing → metering**. This staged design ensures the biopolymer undergoes gradual structural transformation without shocking the molecular matrix.

#### Key Components of an SSE:
| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Hopper & Feeder** | Metered supply of raw material |
| **Barrel** | Heated cylindrical housing; contains the rotating screw |
| **Screw (Auger)** | Conveys, compresses, shears the material |
| **Die Plate** | Shapes the extrudate; controls expansion |
| **Cutter/Knife** | Cuts the extrudate to desired length |
| **Drive Motor & Gearbox** | Provides rotational power |

### 2.3 Twin-Screw Extruder (TSE) — Deep Analysis

The TSE uses **two parallel screws** in a single barrel, offering superior mixing and process control compared to SSE.

#### 2.3.1 Co-rotating Intermeshing TSE

- Both screws rotate in the **same direction** with intermeshing flights.
- Material is conveyed in a **figure-8 (lemniscate) pattern** around both screws.
- Provides **positive conveying** with low pressure build-up in the screws themselves.
- **High conveying efficiency** at high screw speeds.
- Used for: pet food, aqua feed, snack pellets.

#### 2.3.2 Counter-rotating Intermeshing TSE

- Screws rotate in **opposite directions**.
- Material is conveyed in a **C-shape (kidney shape)** between the screws.
- Creates a **calendering gap** between the screws — extremely high local shear.
- **Self-wiping** characteristic; excellent for viscous, sticky, or shear-sensitive materials.
- Used for: pre-cooked pasta, flat bread, and **high-moisture extrusion cooking** for meat analogues.

#### 2.3.3 Non-Intermeshing TSE
- Screws do not engage; behaves like **two SSEs side by side**.
- Used when long residence time and gentle conveying are needed.
- Limited industrial application compared to intermeshing types.

### 2.4 Comparison Matrix: SSE vs TSE

| Parameter | Single-Screw Extruder | Twin-Screw Extruder |
|---|---|---|
| Mixing efficiency | Poor–Moderate | Excellent |
| Conveying efficiency | Moderate (friction-dependent) | Excellent (positive) |
| Self-wiping action | No | Yes (intermeshing types) |
| Pressure build-up | High at die | Better controlled |
| Capital cost | **Low** | **High** (2–3× SSE) |
| Operating cost | Lower | Higher |
| Residence time distribution | Wider | Narrower |
| Heat transfer efficiency | Moderate | High |
| Starch damage control | Limited | Excellent |
| Application in non-thermal | Snacks, basic cereals | TVP, meat analogues, HMEC |

### 2.5 High-Moisture Extrusion Cooking (HMEC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Focus Area:** High-moisture extrusion is the central non-thermal application of twin-screw extruders.

- Operates at **moisture content of 40–80%**.
- **Long cooling die** (L/D > 10) used to set the protein structure.
- No puffing occurs; the product retains its dense, fibrous structure.
- Used to produce **meat analogues** from soy, pea, or wheat gluten.
- Counter-rotating intermeshing TSE is preferred.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Specific Mechanical Energy (SME)** | $\text{SME} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot \tau}{Q_{\text{m}} \cdot 1000}$ | Wh/kg | Energy per unit mass — central design variable |
| 2 | **Screw Compression Ratio (CR)** | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{h_f}{h_m}$ | dimensionless | $h_f$ = feed depth, $h_m$ = metering depth |
| 3 | **L/D Ratio** | $\text{L/D} = \dfrac{L}{D}$ | dimensionless | Determines cooking/cook-cooling capacity |
| 4 | **Volumetric Throughput** | $Q = \dfrac{\pi^2 \cdot D^2 \cdot N \cdot h_m \cdot \sin\theta \cdot \cos\theta}{2}$ | m³/s | Theoretical drag flow model |
| 5 | **Net Throughput (Newtonian)** | $Q_{\text{net}} = Q_{\text{drag}} - Q_{\text{pressure}}$ | m³/s | Drag flow − pressure flow |
| 6 | **Power Consumption** | $P = 2\pi \cdot N \cdot \tau$ | W | Motor sizing |
| 7 | **Degree of Fill (TSE)** | $\phi = \dfrac{\text{Mass in barrel}}{\text{Max possible mass}}$ | dimensionless | Twin-screw fill control |
| 8 | **Expansion Ratio (ER)** | $\text{ER} = \dfrac{D_e^2}{D_d^2}$ | dimensionless | $D_e$ = extrudate dia, $D_d$ = die dia |
| 9 | **Bulk Density** | $\rho_b = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | kg/m³ | Product quality index |
| 10 | **Shaft Torque** | $\tau = \dfrac{P}{2\pi N}$ | N·m | Resists screw rotation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Pipes in Tables:** Vertical bar symbols are intentionally avoided in the table above; all mathematical dividers use $\vert$ or $\mid$ where absolute values are needed (none in this formula set).

### 2.7 Real-World Utility

- **Pet food industry** — Co-rotating TSEs dominate the **5,000–20,000 kg/h** capacity range.
- **Breakfast cereal industry** — SSEs with cooking zone temperatures of **150–180 °C** produce puffed corn/maize products.
- **Meat analogue / plant-based protein** — Counter-rotating TSE with cooling dies for fibrous texture.
- **Aquaculture feed** — High-SME (>300 Wh/kg) extruders produce water-stable sinking pellets.
- **Snack pellet industry** — Cold SSE forming followed by secondary frying/puffing (e.g., 3D snacks).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Specific Mechanical Energy (SME)

The SME quantifies the mechanical energy delivered per kilogram of product. It is the single most important parameter in extrusion design because it correlates directly with starch gelatinization, protein denaturation, and product expansion.

**Starting Point — Mechanical Power Input:**

The power transmitted to the material by the rotating screw is given by the torque–angular velocity relationship.

$$
P = 2\pi \cdot N \cdot \tau
$$

**Step 1 — Define N:**
$N$ is the screw rotational speed in revolutions per second (Hz). If the given speed is in **rpm**, divide by 60:

$$
N_{\text{(Hz)}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{(rpm)}}}{60}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert Power to Energy per Unit Mass:**

The energy delivered per unit mass of throughput $Q_m$ (kg/s) is:

$$
\text{SME} = \dfrac{P}{Q_m}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Power Equation:**

$$
\text{SME} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot \tau}{Q_m}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Wh/kg:**

Power $P$ is in **Watts (J/s)**. To express SME in **Wh/kg**, divide by 3600 (J per Wh) and multiply by 1000 (kg basis):

$$
\text{SME} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot \tau}{Q_m \cdot 3.6}
$$

where $N$ is in rpm, $\tau$ in N·m, and $Q_m$ in kg/h (or the equivalent with 1000 conversion). The KTU-preferred form is:

$$
\text{SME} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot \tau}{Q_m \cdot 1000} \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{Wh}}{\text{kg}}\right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance:** Low SME (< 100 Wh/kg) preserves heat-labile nutrients — a key advantage of non-thermal extrusion. High SME (> 500 Wh/kg) causes extensive molecular degradation and over-cooking.

### 3.2 Derivation: Volumetric Throughput of a Single-Screw Extruder

This derivation is based on the classical **Newtonian fluid drag flow** model in the screw channel.

**Step 1 — Coordinate Transformation:**
The screw channel is analyzed in an **unwound rectangular channel** of width $W$ (perpendicular to flight) and depth $H$ (flight depth), with the lower wall moving at velocity $V_b$ relative to the stationary barrel:

$$
V_b = \pi \cdot D \cdot N \cdot \cos\theta
$$

where $D$ is the screw diameter, $N$ is in rev/s, and $\theta$ is the helix angle.

**Step 2 — Drag Flow Rate (per unit width):**
For a Newtonian fluid, the volumetric flow rate due to drag (Couette flow) is:

$$
q_d = \dfrac{V_b \cdot H}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Drag Flow:**
Multiplying by the channel width $W = \pi D \sin\theta - e$ (where $e$ is the flight width, often neglected):

$$
Q_d = \dfrac{V_b \cdot H \cdot W}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $V_b$ and $W$:**

$$
Q_d = \dfrac{\pi D N \cos\theta \cdot H \cdot (\pi D \sin\theta)}{2}
$$

$$
Q_d = \dfrac{\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\theta \cos\theta}{2}
$$

This is the classical **Newtonian drag flow throughput** equation listed in the formula sheet:

$$
\boxed{Q = \dfrac{\pi^2 \cdot D^2 \cdot N \cdot h_m \cdot \sin\theta \cdot \cos\theta}{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Net Throughput (Subtract Pressure Flow):**
In a real extruder, the die creates a back-pressure, generating **pressure flow (Poiseuille flow)** in the opposite direction:

$$
Q_{\text{net}} = Q_{\text{drag}} - Q_{\text{pressure}}
$$

$$
Q_{\text{net}} = \dfrac{\pi^2 D^2 N H \sin\theta \cos\theta}{2} - \dfrac{H^3 W \Delta P}{12 \mu L}
$$

where $\Delta P$ is the pressure rise, $\mu$ is the viscosity, and $L$ is the screw length.

### 3.3 Derivation: Expansion Ratio (ER) of an Extrudate

The expansion ratio is a critical product-quality metric.

**Step 1 — Definition:**
ER is the ratio of the cross-sectional area of the expanded extrudate to that of the die:

$$
\text{ER} = \dfrac{A_{\text{extrudate}}}{A_{\text{die}}}
$$

**Step 2 — For a Circular Die and Circular Extrudate:**

$$
\text{ER} = \dfrac{\frac{\pi}{4} D_e^2}{\frac{\pi}{4} D_d^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$
\boxed{\text{ER} = \dfrac{D_e^2}{D_d^2}}
$$

where $D_e$ is the extrudate diameter and $D_d$ is the die diameter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Higher SME and higher die temperature lead to greater vapor flash and higher ER. ER is the principal index of "puffiness" in ready-to-eat snacks.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A counter-rotating twin-screw extruder operates at $N = 300$ rpm with a measured shaft torque of $\tau = 85$ N·m. The throughput is $Q_m = 150$ kg/h. Calculate the SME.

**Step 1 — Apply the SME formula:**

$$
\text{SME} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot \tau}{Q_m \cdot 1000}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$
\text{SME} = \dfrac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 300 \times 85}{150 \times 1000}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator:**

$$
2 \times 3.1416 \times 300 \times 85 = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 25500 = 160221.6
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator:**

$$
150 \times 1000 = 150000
$$

**Step 5 — Final result:**

$$
\text{SME} = \dfrac{160221.6}{150000} = 1.0681 \;\dfrac{\text{Wh}}{\text{kg}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value (~1.07 Wh/kg) is very low — characteristic of a **cold forming extruder**, not a cooking extruder. For expanded snacks, SME is typically 100–400 Wh/kg.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[EXTRUDERS IN NON-THERMAL PROCESSING]:::top
    A --> B[Single-Screw Extruder SSE]
    A --> C[Twin-Screw Extruder TSE]
    A --> D[Other Types]

    B --> B1[Standard SSE]
    B --> B2[Interrupted Flight SSE]
    B --> B3[Steam Injection SSE]

    C --> C1[Co-rotating]
    C --> C2[Counter-rotating]

    C1 --> C1A[Intermeshing]
    C1 --> C1B[Non-Intermeshing]

    C2 --> C2A[Intermeshing]
    C2 --> C2B[Non-Intermeshing]

    D --> D1[Cold Forming Extruder]
    D --> D2[Cooking Extruder]
    D --> D3[High Moisture HMEC]
    D --> D4[Texturizing Extruder]

    classDef top fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Cooking Extruder

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        H1[Volumetric Feeder] --> H2[Pre-conditioner]
    end
    subgraph BARREL [BARREL ASSEMBLY]
        H2 --> Z1[Zone 1: Feed Conveying]
        Z1 --> Z2[Zone 2: Kneading and Compression]
        Z2 --> Z3[Zone 3: Cooking and Metering]
        Z3 --> Z4[Zone 4: Cooling and Discharge]
    end
    subgraph DIE [DIE AND CUTTING]
        Z4 --> D1[Die Plate]
        D1 --> D2[Cutter Assembly]
        D2 --> D3[Extrudate]
    end

        CTRL -.-> Z1
        CTRL -.-> Z2
        CTRL -.-> Z3

        CTRL -.-> D1
        CTRL -.-> D2
    end
    M[Drive Motor] -.-> Z1
    M -.-> Z2
    M -.-> Z3
    M -.-> Z4
```
