<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Define an extruder. List any two advantages of a twin-screw extruder over a single-screw extruder.**

**Model Answer:**

An **extruder** is a continuous mechanical processing device that forces a material through a die under controlled pressure, temperature, and shear, producing a shaped and/or texturized product. In non-thermal food processing, it serves as a thermo-mechanical reactor where structural transformations (starch gelatinization, protein denaturation) are achieved primarily by mechanical shear rather than prolonged thermal exposure.

**Two advantages of TSE over SSE:**
1. **Superior mixing and self-wiping** action ensures uniform temperature and moisture distribution.
2. **Positive conveying** of material, independent of friction, leading to better process control and reduced residence time distribution.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark each for advantages)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**
**Differentiate between co-rotating and counter-rotating twin-screw extruders.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Co-rotating TSE | Counter-rotating TSE |
|---|---|---|
| Rotation direction | Same direction | Opposite directions |
| Material flow | Figure-8 path | C-shaped (kidney) path |
| Shear intensity | Moderate | Very high (calendering gap) |
| Self-wiping | Yes | Yes |
| Typical application | Pet food, snacks, aqua feed | HMEC, meat analogues, pasta |

*(3 marks: 1.5 marks for direction/flow distinction + 1.5 marks for application difference)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module 4 — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students often **confuse CR (compression ratio) with L/D ratio** — these are different parameters.
> - The formula for SME is often written without unit conversion — remember the factor of 1000 in the denominator.
> - Do not write "high-pressure extruder" as a category — this is not a standard KTU classification.
> - Always mention **"self-wiping action"** when describing intermeshing TSE — it is a key term examiners look for.
> - For HMEC, students forget to mention the **long cooling die** — significant mark loss if missed.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) Classify the different types of extruders used in non-thermal food processing with suitable examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Extruders used in non-thermal food processing can be classified as follows:

**1. Based on Number of Screws:**
- **Single-Screw Extruder (SSE):** Contains one screw in the barrel. Example: snack food extruder, cold pasta forming.
- **Twin-Screw Extruder (TSE):** Contains two parallel screws. Example: pet food cookers, high-moisture extruders.

**2. Based on Screw Rotation (TSE only):**
- **Co-rotating TSE:** Both screws rotate in the same direction. Material moves in a figure-8 path. Used for pet food, aqua feed.
- **Counter-rotating TSE:** Screws rotate in opposite directions. Material moves in a C-shape. Used for meat analogues, high-moisture extrusion cooking.

**3. Based on Intermeshing:**
- **Intermeshing TSE:** Screw flights engage each other — enables self-wiping.
- **Non-Intermeshing TSE:** Screws do not engage — behaves like two SSEs.

**4. Based on Moisture Content:**
- **Low-Moisture Extrusion (< 25% moisture):** Produces puffed and expanded products like corn curls, third-generation snacks.
- **High-Moisture Extrusion (40–80%):** Produces dense, fibrous, meat-analogue products.

**5. Based on Application:**
- **Cold/Forming Extruder:** Pasta, snack pellets (no cooking in extruder).
- **Cooking Extruder:** Breakfast cereals, expanded snacks.
- **Texturizing Extruder:** Texturized vegetable protein (TVP), meat analogues.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per main category, with example)*

---

**(b) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a single-screw cooking extruder. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram:**
```
Hopper → Feeder → Barrel (Zone 1: Feed) → Zone 2: Compression → Zone 3: Cooking/Metering → Die → Cutter → Extrudate
```

**Working Principle:**

A single-screw cooking extruder operates in **four functional zones**:

1. **Feeding Zone (Zone 1):** Raw material (e.g., corn meal) is fed from the hopper via a gravimetric feeder. The deep flight depth in this zone ensures loose solid conveying with low pressure. The feed is essentially a particulate solid.

2. **Compression/Kneading Zone (Zone 2):** The flight depth gradually decreases along the screw. This **compression** compacts the material, expels trapped air, and initiates the formation of a viscoelastic melt. Mechanical energy input begins to rise due to shearing of the material between screw flights and the barrel wall.

3. **Cooking/Metering Zone (Zone 3):** The flight depth is at its minimum. Material experiences **maximum shear, pressure, and temperature** (typically 120–180 °C). Starch granules gelatinize, proteins denature, and water acts as a plasticizer. The molten mass is pressurized to 2–10 MPa.

4. **Die and Cutting Section:** The pressurized melt is forced through a die of defined cross-section. Upon exiting, the sudden pressure drop causes superheated water to flash into steam, **puffing the extrudate**. A rotating cutter slices the continuous strand into defined lengths.

**Key Parameters:**
- L/D ratio: 20:1 to 30:1
- Compression ratio: 2:1 to 4:1
- Screw speed: 200–600 rpm

*(7 marks: 2 marks for diagram + 5 marks for explanation of zones)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]

**(a) Explain the working principle of a counter-rotating intermeshing twin-screw extruder with a focus on high-moisture extrusion cooking (HMEC). (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:**

A **counter-rotating intermeshing TSE** has two parallel screws rotating in opposite directions within a single barrel. The intermeshing of the screw flights creates a self-wiping action and a narrow **calendering gap** between the screws where extreme local shear is generated.

The material follows a **C-shaped (kidney-shaped) path** as it is conveyed from the hopper to the die. The counter-rotation drags the material into the gap between the screws, exposing it to intense mechanical shear and pressure.

**High-Moisture Extrusion Cooking (HMEC):**

HMEC is a specialized application of counter-rotating intermeshing TSE used to produce **meat analogues and texturized vegetable protein (TVP)**. Key features include:

- **Moisture content:** 40–80% in the feed.
- **Barrel temperature:** 100–180 °C; the final zones are often **cooled** to 60–80 °C to fix the structure.
- **Long cooling die:** L/D > 10, with a temperature-controlled jacket to set the fibrous protein structure.
- **No puffing:** The product does not expand upon exit because the moisture does not flash (water is in excess).
- **Screw speed:** Low to moderate (100–250 rpm) to preserve fiber alignment.

**Mechanism of Texturization:**
1. Protein (e.g., soy isolate) is plasticized in the cooking zone.
2. The melt is forced through the cooling die under laminar flow.
3. Protein molecules align along the flow direction.
4. Cooling sets the structure into a fibrous, anisotropic mass resembling meat.

**Applications:** Plant-based meat, fish analogues, pet food with fibrous texture.

*(7 marks: 3 marks for TSE principle + 4 marks for HMEC specifics)*

---

**(b) Define Specific Mechanical Energy (SME). A counter-rotating TSE operates at 250 rpm with a torque of 90 N·m and a throughput of 200 kg/h. Calculate the SME in Wh/kg. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):**

**Specific Mechanical Energy (SME)** is the mechanical energy delivered by the screw to the material per unit mass of product. It is the most important design and process parameter in extrusion because it directly correlates with starch gelatinization, protein denaturation, expansion, and overall product texture.

$$
\text{SME} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot \tau}{Q_m \cdot 1000} \quad \text{(Wh/kg)}
$$

where:
- $N$ = screw speed in rpm
- $\tau$ = shaft torque in N·m
- $Q_m$ = throughput in kg/h

**Calculation (5 marks):**

**Given:**
- $N = 250$ rpm
- $\tau = 90$ N·m
- $Q_m = 200$ kg/h

**Step 1 — Apply the formula:**

$$
\text{SME} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot \tau}{Q_m \cdot 1000}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values:**

$$
\text{SME} = \dfrac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 250 \times 90}{200 \times 1000}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator:**

$$
2 \times 3.1416 \times 250 \times 90 = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 22500 = 141372
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator:**

$$
200 \times 1000 = 200000
$$

**Step 5 — Final answer:**

$$
\text{SME} = \dfrac{141372}{200000} = 0.7069 \;\dfrac{\text{Wh}}{\text{kg}} \approx 0.71 \;\dfrac{\text{Wh}}{\text{kg}}
$$

**Interpretation:** This very low SME value (~0.71 Wh/kg) indicates a **cold forming or low-shear process**, typical of pasta forming or gentle mixing applications, not of cooking extrusion. For expanded snacks, SME would be 100–400 Wh/kg.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for definition + 5 marks for stepwise calculation)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (for KTU 2024 Module 4)**

- **Extruder** = continuous mechanical device that forces material through a die under pressure, shear, and temperature.
- **Two main classes:** Single-Screw (SSE) and Twin-Screw (TSE).
- **TSE subtypes:** Co-rotating (figure-8 flow) and Counter-rotating (C-shaped flow).
- **Intermeshing** → **Self-wiping** action is a key advantage of TSE.
- **Counter-rotating TSE** → preferred for **High-Moisture Extrusion Cooking (HMEC)** and meat analogues.
- **Co-rotating TSE** → preferred for **pet food, aqua feed, and snack pellets**.
- **Single-screw extruder zones:** Feed → Compression → Cooking/Metering → Die.
- **L/D ratio** typically **20:1 to 30:1** for cooking extruders.
- **Compression ratio (CR)** = feed flight depth / metering flight depth, typically **2:1 to 4:1**.
- **Specific Mechanical Energy (SME)** is the **single most important design parameter** in extrusion.
- **SME formula:** $\text{SME} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot N \cdot \tau}{Q_m \cdot 1000}$ in Wh/kg.
- **SME range:**
  - Cold forming: < 50 Wh/kg
  - Snacks/expanded products: 100–400 Wh/kg
  - High-shear meat analogues: 200–500 Wh/kg
- **Expansion Ratio (ER)** = $D_e^2 / D_d^2$ — index of puffiness.
- **HMEC conditions:** moisture 40–80%, long cooling die (L/D > 10), no puffing, low screw speed.
- **Puffing** occurs at the die due to **flashing of superheated water** into steam.
- **Non-thermal aspect** of extrusion: structural transformations (gelatinization, denaturation) achieved primarily by **mechanical shear**, minimizing thermal damage to heat-labile nutrients.
- **Key applications in NTP:** Meat analogues (plant-based protein), breakfast cereals, snack pellets, pet food, aqua feed, modified starches.
- **Volumetric throughput (Newtonian drag flow):** $Q = \dfrac{\pi^2 D^2 N h_m \sin\theta \cos\theta}{2}$.
- **Net throughput** = Drag flow − Pressure flow (pressure flow opposes drag flow at the die).
- **Degree of fill ($\phi$)** in TSE is a critical control variable — it determines whether the screw acts as a conveyer, mixer, or cooker.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Extrusion on Foods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT631 - NON-THERMAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Effect of Extrusion on Foods # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Extrusion** in food processing is a **thermo-mechanical process** in which food material is forced to flow, under controlled conditions, through a designed die (orifice) of specific geometry, under high pressure (~3–25 MPa) and elevated temperature (typically **100 °C–200 °C**), where it is subjected simultaneously to high shear, compression, and thermal energy. The combined action of **mechanical shear, heat, and pressure** induces profound physical, chemical, nutritional, structural, and microbiological transformations in the food matrix. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Although this course focuses on *Non-Thermal Processing*, Module 4 places extrusion in a **comparative framework** — to highlight how a *thermo-mechanical* process produces effects (gelatinization, protein cross-linking, microbial inactivation, anti-nutrient destruction) that are often *complementary* or *superior* to purely non-thermal techniques. Students must be able to **contrast** extrusion effects with HPP, PEF, ultrasound, and cold plasma. Mathematically, the specific mechanical energy (SME) input during extrusion is defined as: $$\text{SME} = \frac{\omega \cdot \tau \cdot L}{m_s \cdot D} \quad \left[\frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg}}\right]$$ where: - $\omega$ = angular velocity of the screw (rad/s) - $\tau$ = torque on the screw shaft (N·m) - $L/D$ = length-to-diameter ratio of the barrel - $m_s$ = mass flow rate of the feed (kg/s) > [!NOTE] > **Classification of Extrusion Effects** (KTU Module 4 – must remember): > 1. **Physical Effects** – texturization, expansion, density reduction > 2. **Chemical Effects** – starch gelatinization, protein denaturation, Maillard browning, lipid oxidation > 3. **Nutritional Effects** – reduction of anti-nutritional factors (ANFs), protein digestibility changes > 4. **Microbiological Effects** – microbial inactivation, enzyme inactivation > 5. **Rheological & Functional Effects** – WHC, OHC, emulsifying capacity, gel strength --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine pushing a thick paste (like wet clay or a dough) through a small hole in a wall using a heavy piston. As the paste is forced through: - The **pressure is enormous** (think of a stressed balloon squeezing through a needle hole). - The **friction against the wall** heats it up dramatically. - The **sudden drop in pressure** outside causes the paste to puff up (like opening a fizzy drink bottle). - The **glue-like starches and proteins** inside get "melted" and re-bonded in new shapes. This is exactly what happens inside an **extruder**. The food is pushed forward by a rotating **screw** through a heated **barrel**, encountering increasing resistance. When it exits through the **die**, the pressure suddenly drops to atmospheric, water flashes to steam, and the product **expands** into a porous, crispy, ready-to-eat form (e.g., breakfast cereals, snacks, pasta). > [!TIP] > **Student Quick-Recall Mnemonic — "SHEAR-FORCE-HEAT-POP":** > **S**hear breaks cells → **H**eat cooks starch → **E**nergy of pressure released → **A**ll bonds re-form → **R**estructured matrix → … result: a **POP**ped expanded product. > [!NOTE] > **Standard KTU Bench-Mark Values (must memorize for exams):** > - Feed moisture content: **12%–28% (w.b.)** > - Barrel temperature: **100 °C–200 °C** > - Screw speed: **100–400 rpm** > - SME: **100–500 kJ/kg** (low) up to **1500+ kJ/kg** (high-shear) > - Die pressure: **3–25 MPa** > - Expansion ratio: **2× to 25×** depending on feed and conditions --- ## 1.3 Why Study "Effects" of Extrusion? Understanding the *effects* is what differentiates a food *engineer* from a mere machine *operator*. By manipulating: - **Temperature** (T) - **Moisture** (MC) - **Screw speed** (N) - **Screw configuration** (compression ratio) - **Die geometry** (diameter, L/D) …the processor can **tailor-make** the final product characteristics: crunchy vs chewy, dense vs airy, soluble vs insoluble fiber, low- vs high-protein, etc. # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Extrusion Effects — Structured Logic ### Pillar 1: Physical Effects (Texturization & Expansion) | Sub-Effect | Mechanism | Engineering Importance | |---|---|---| | **Expansion (Puffing)** | Sudden vapor flashing of superheated water at die exit | Reduces bulk density → breakfast cereals, snacks | | **Porosity formation** | Air cell walls set by cooled starch-protein matrix | Crispiness, water absorption | | **Density reduction** | $\rho_{extrudate} = 0.1 - 0.5 \text{ g/cm}^3$ | Lower shipping cost per volume | | **Hardness / Crispiness** | Glassy amorphous state of starch | Sensory quality | | **Shrinkage on cooling** | Recrystallization (retrogradation) | Dimensional stability | The **Expansion Ratio (ER)** is the most quoted physical metric: $$\text{ER} = \frac{D_e^2}{D_d^2}$$ where $D_e$ = diameter of extrudate, $D_d$ = diameter of die. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Learning Outcome:** Students must be able to **explain** how moisture and SME **inversely** affect expansion, and how melt temperature **directly** affects expansion up to a critical point (~140 °C for starchy feeds). --- ### Pillar 2: Chemical Effects #### (a) Starch Gelatinization & Dextrinization - Native starch granules are **semi-crystalline**. - Extrusion's high T + shear + moisture disrupt crystallinity → **irreversible gelatinization** (often *complete*, much faster than batch cooking). - Higher T and SME → **dextrinization** (partial hydrolysis) → increased solubility, reduced viscosity. > Degree of starch gelatinization (DG) by DSC or enzymatic method is the **gold standard** KTU question. #### (b) Protein Denaturation & Cross-linking - Tertiary/quaternary structures unfold under heat + shear. - Reactive side chains (–SH, $\varepsilon$-NH$_2$ of lysine) form new **intermolecular cross-links** (disulfide bridges, isopeptide bonds). - Functional effects: altered WHC, emulsification, gelation. #### (c) Maillard Reaction & Caramelization - Reducing sugars + amine groups → melanoidins (browning). - L* value of extrudate drops; a* and b* rise (instrumental color change). - *Caution:* Maillard also **destroys lysine** → nutritional concern. #### (d) Lipid Oxidation - High shear may rupture oil bodies. - Polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFAs) can undergo **lipid peroxidation**, generating off-flavors. - *Benefit:* Inactivates **lipoxygenase** enzyme, thus reducing *post-extrusion* oxidation. #### (e) Dietary Fiber Modification - Soluble dietary fiber (SDF) **increases** at the expense of insoluble dietary fiber (IDF). - Improved fermentability, cholesterol-binding capacity. --- ### Pillar 3: Nutritional Effects | Nutrient | Effect of Extrusion | Direction | |---|---|---| | **Lysine** (limiting AA) | Destroyed by Maillard | ↓ | | **Cystine, Methionine** | Partial destruction | ↓ | | Available lysine | Significantly reduced (especially at T > 180 °C) | ↓↓ | | Starch digestibility | Increases (gelatinization) | ↑ | | Protein digestibility | Generally increases (denaturation unfolds chains) | ↑ | | Anti-nutrients (phytates, tannins, trypsin inhibitors, lectins) | **Destroyed** | ↓↓ (Good!) | | Vitamins (B group, C) | Heat-labile, partially lost | ↓ | | Minerals | Mostly retained | ≈ | > [!NOTE] > **KTU-Favorite Concept:** Extrusion is widely used in **legume-based RTE snacks** (e.g., soy-fortified corn puffs) precisely because extrusion **destroys 70%–95% of trypsin inhibitors and phytic acid**, dramatically improving protein bioavailability. --- ### Pillar 4: Microbiological Effects - High T + low $a_w$ post-extrusion → significant **microbial inactivation** (>3–4 log reduction of vegetative bacteria, yeasts, molds). - **Spores** are more resistant; extrusion alone is **not** a sterilization process. - Inactivates *Aspergillus* molds producing **aflatoxins** — major benefit in **maize and peanut** products. --- ### Pillar 5: Rheological & Functional Effects - **Water Holding Capacity (WHC)** changes due to altered porous structure. - **Water Solubility Index (WSI)** rises sharply (starch dextrinization). - **Water Absorption Index (WAI)** peaks at moderate SME, drops at very high SME (over-cooking). - **Emulsifying capacity** of proteins is altered — usually *improved* up to a point. - **Oil Holding Capacity (OHC)** rises due to capillary pores. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Typical Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\text{SME} = \dfrac{\omega \cdot \tau}{m_s} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D}$ | Specific Mechanical Energy (energy per unit mass of feed) | $\text{kJ/kg}$ | | 2 | $\text{ER} = \dfrac{D_e^2}{D_d^2}$ | Expansion Ratio (cross-sectional basis) | dimensionless | | 3 | $\text{SI} = \dfrac{\Delta m_{\text{dry}}}{\Delta m_{\text{wet}}} \times 100$ | Sectional / Longitudinal Expansion Index | % | | 4 | $\rho_b = \dfrac{m_{\text{extrudate}}}{V_{\text{displaced}}}$ | Bulk / Apparent Density of extrudate | $\text{g/cm}^3$ | | 5 | $\text{WAI} = \dfrac{m_{\text{gel}}}{m_{\text{dry sample}}}$ | Water Absorption Index | g/g | | 6 | $\text{WSI} = \dfrac{m_{\text{dry supernatant}}}{m_{\text{dry sample}}} \times 100$ | Water Solubility Index | % | | 7 | $\text{WHC} = \dfrac{m_{\text{water retained}}}{m_{\text{dry sample}}}$ | Water Holding Capacity | g/g | | 8 | $\text{TDR} = 1 - \dfrac{\text{PDI}_{\text{final}}}{\text{PDI}_{\text{initial}}}$ | Total Digestibility Reduction (for protein) | – | | 9 | $\text{DG} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{native}} - \Delta H_{\text{extrudate}}}{\Delta H_{\text{native}}} \times 100$ | Degree of Starch Gelatinization (DSC method) | % | | 10 | $P_{\text{die}} = \dfrac{8 \eta L_v Q}{\pi R^4}$ | Hagen-Poiseuille die pressure (laminar model) | Pa | | 11 | $\eta_a = \dfrac{\tau_{\text{apparent}}}{\dot{\gamma}_{\text{apparent}}}$ | Apparent viscosity inside barrel | Pa·s | | 12 | $T_{m} = T_b + \dfrac{\text{SME}}{C_p \cdot m_s}$ | Melt temperature rise (energy balance) | °C | > [!WARNING] > **LaTeX formatting note for KTU answer sheets:** In handwritten exams, always write 'SME' as **"S.M.E."** or **"Specific Mechanical Energy"** on first use. Examiners deduct marks for ambiguous symbols. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Breakfast Cereal Industry** — corn flakes, wheat flakes, rice crisps: controlled expansion, crispiness, sugar coating. 2. **Pet Food & Aquafeed** — high SME for starch gelatinization → sinking pellets, better digestibility. 3. **Snack Industry** — direct-expanded (DX) and pellet-fried products. 4. **Protein Texturization** — TVP (Texturized Vegetable Protein) from soy, pea: layered, fibrous, meat-like texture for vegans. 5. **Modified Starch Production** — pre-gelatinized starch, cold-water-soluble starch for instant soups. 6. **Functional Fiber** — resistant starch (RS) formation for low-GI products. 7. **Legume Processing** — deactivation of ANFs, expanded snack market in India (Bikaneri bhujia extruded versions). # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Specific Mechanical Energy (SME) from First Principles **Starting Concept:** Power delivered to the food by the rotating screw equals torque × angular velocity. **Step 1:** The mechanical power transferred from the motor to the food material via the screw: $$P_{\text{mech}} = \omega \cdot \tau \quad [\text{W} = \text{J/s}]$$ **Step 2:** The total energy delivered per unit mass of feed is energy divided by mass flow rate, but corrected for the screw's geometry (the energy is distributed along the length of the barrel): $$\text{SME} = \frac{P_{\text{mech}}}{m_s} \cdot \frac{L}{D}$$ **Step 3:** Substituting Step 1 into Step 2: $$\text{SME} = \frac{\omega \cdot \tau}{m_s} \cdot \frac{L}{D}$$ **Step 4:** Resolving units: $$\left[\text{SME}\right] = \frac{[\text{rad/s}] \cdot [\text{N·m}]}{[\text{kg/s}]} = \frac{\text{J/s}}{\text{kg/s}} = \frac{\text{J}}{\text{kg}}$$ > So SME has units of $\text{J/kg}$ (or $\text{kJ/kg}$ when scaled by 1000). > **Conversion logic:** Power is torque × angular velocity (basic rotational mechanics). Dividing by the mass flow rate converts total energy into *energy per unit mass*. The factor $L/D$ is included because the screw's ability to impart energy scales with its *length-to-diameter ratio* — a longer barrel at the same rpm delivers more energy. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Expansion Ratio (ER) from Die Geometry **Starting Concept:** The product exits the die as a continuous cylinder. After expansion, the cross-section grows. **Step 1:** Cross-sectional area of extrudate (circular): $$A_e = \frac{\pi D_e^2}{4}$$ **Step 2:** Cross-sectional area of die (circular): $$A_d = \frac{\pi D_d^2}{4}$$ **Step 3:** Definition of Expansion Ratio (cross-sectional): $$\text{ER} = \frac{A_e}{A_d} = \frac{\pi D_e^2/4}{\pi D_d^2/4}$$ **Step 4:** The $\pi$ and the 4 cancel: $$\text{ER} = \frac{D_e^2}{D_d^2}$$ **Step 5:** The **longitudinal (length) expansion index** is similarly: $$\text{LEI} = \frac{L_e - L_d}{L_d} \times 100\%$$ > **Conversion logic:** Expansion is a *volumetric* phenomenon, but because extrudate diameter is easy to measure and the product is constrained in length by the cutter, the *diameter-squared ratio* is the standard, easily measured proxy. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Die Pressure from Hagen–Poiseuille Law (for Newtonian flow) **Starting Concept:** A viscous melt flows in a tube under a pressure gradient. **Step 1:** Volumetric flow rate through a cylindrical tube of radius $R$ and length $L_v$: $$Q = \frac{\pi R^4 \Delta P}{8 \eta L_v}$$ **Step 2:** Solving for the pressure drop $\Delta P$ (which equals die pressure $P_d$ if inlet = die and outlet ≈ atmospheric): $$P_d = \frac{8 \eta L_v Q}{\pi R^4}$$ **Step 3:** This shows $P_d \propto \eta$ (viscosity) and $P_d \propto Q$ (flow rate), but $P_d \propto 1/R^4$ — die diameter has a *dramatic* effect. > **Conversion logic:** Poiseuille's law (1838) was derived for laminar flow in a tube. Food melts in a short die approach this regime, especially in the metering section. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Degree of Starch Gelatinization (DSC Method) **Starting Concept:** Native starch shows a gelatinization endotherm ($\Delta H$). If extrusion gelatinizes some starch, the residual endotherm shrinks. **Step 1:** Enthalpy of gelatinization of native (uncooked) starch: $$\Delta H_{\text{native}} \;\; [\text{J/g}]$$ **Step 2:** Enthalpy of gelatinization remaining in the *extrudate* (measured by DSC): $$\Delta H_{\text{extrudate}} \;\; [\text{J/g}]$$ **Step 3:** Fraction of starch that has *already* been gelatinized by extrusion: $$x_{\text{gelatinized}} = \frac{\Delta H_{\text{native}} - \Delta H_{\text{extrudate}}}{\Delta H_{\text{native}}}$$ **Step 4:** Expressing as a percentage (KTU answer form): $$\text{DG} (\%) = \frac{\Delta H_{\text{native}} - \Delta H_{\text{extrudate}}}{\Delta H_{\text{native}}} \times 100$$ > **Conversion logic:** DSC measures the energy absorbed during the *order–disorder* transition (gelatinization). After extrusion, the crystalline order is already lost, so the residual endotherm shrinks. The fractional drop in enthalpy equals the fractional degree of gelatinization. This is the gold-standard KTU examination answer. --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > **Problem:** A co-rotating twin-screw extruder runs at 300 rpm with a torque of 80 N·m on a feed of 50 kg/h. The barrel has L/D = 25. Find (a) SME, (b) percent increase in SME if screw speed is raised to 450 rpm with all else constant. **Solution (a):** Step 1: $\omega = 300 \text{ rpm} = 300 \times \dfrac{2\pi}{60} = 31.416 \text{ rad/s}$ Step 2: $m_s = 50 \text{ kg/h} = \dfrac{50}{3600} = 0.01389 \text{ kg/s}$ Step 3: SME = $\dfrac{31.416 \times 80 \times 25}{0.01389}$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{SME} &= \frac{31.416 \times 80 \times 25}{0.01389} \\ &= \frac{62{,}832}{0.01389} \\ &= 4{,}523{,}758 \;\; \text{J/kg} \\ &\approx 4524 \;\; \text{kJ/kg} \end{aligned} $$ > This is a **very high SME**, typical of *high-shear* extrusion used for texturized vegetable protein (TVP). **Solution (b):** SME is directly proportional to $\omega$. So new SME: $$\text{SME}_{\text{new}} = 4524 \times \frac{450}{300} = 6786 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$\%\text{ increase} = \frac{6786 - 4524}{4524} \times 100 = 50\%$$ > **Answer:** SME = 4524 kJ/kg; a 50% increase occurs. --- ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Process Parameters on Food Quality Attributes | Process Parameter ↑ (increase) | Expansion | Density | Starch DG | Lysine Loss | Crispness | WSI | WAI | |---|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:| | **Barrel Temperature** | ↑↑ (up to ~140°C) | ↓↓ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ then ↓ | | **Moisture (feed)** | ↓↓ | ↑ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ | | **Screw Speed (N)** | ↑ (initially) | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | | **SME** | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑↑ | ↑ then ↓ | | **Die Diameter** | ↑ | ↓ | – | – | ↓ | – | – | | **Residence Time** | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | – | > **Reading the matrix:** Most product-quality parameters are **inversely related to feed moisture** and **directly related to SME/T**. # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Effect-of-Extrusion Causal Map ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Feed Material
Starch, Protein, Fiber, Lipids] --> B[Extruder Barrel
High T, Pressure, Shear] B --> C1[Physical Effects] B --> C2[Chemical Effects] B --> C3[Nutritional Effects] B --> C4[Microbiological Effects] B --> C5[Rheological Effects] C1 --> D1[Expansion, Puffing
Porosity, Crispness] C2 --> D2[Starch Gelatinization
Protein Denaturation
Maillard Browning] C3 --> D3[ANF Reduction
Lysine Loss
Improved Digestibility] C4 --> D4[Bacteria, Yeast
Mold Inactivation
Aflatoxin Reduction] C5 --> D5[WHC, OHC, WSI
Emulsifying Capacity] D1 --> E[Final RTE Product
Snack, Cereal, TVP] D2 --> E D3 --> E D4 --> E D5 --> E E --> F[Consumer Quality
Texture, Taste, Nutrition] ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pathway of Starch Transformation in Extruder ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Native Starch Granule
Semi-crystalline] -->|Heat, Moisture, Shear| S2[Swollen Granule
H-bond Breakage] S2 -->|Continue Shear at T>100C| S3[Amorphous Gel
Starch Gelatinized] S3 -->|High SME > 1000 kJ/kg| S4[Partially Hydrolyzed
Dextrin Formed] S3 -->|Cooling at Die Exit| S5[Glass Transition
Porous, Crisp Matrix] S4 -->|Cooling| S6[Soluble, Crisp
High WSI] S5 --> P1[Breakfast Cereal] S5 --> P2[Direct Expanded Snack] S6 --> P3[Instant Powder
Soup, Sauce] ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Anti-Nutritional Factor Destruction Map ```mermaid flowchart TD leg[Legume / Oilseed Feed
Soybean, Peanut, Lentil] --> pt[Phytates] leg --> ti[Trypsin Inhibitors] leg --> lec[Lectins] leg --> tan[Tannins] pt -->|Extrusion T>120C| redP[Reduced 50-70 percent
Phosphorus Bioavailability Up] ti -->|High SME + T>140C| redT[Reduced 80-95 percent
Protein Digestibility Up] lec -->|T>110C| redL[Destroyed Completely
Toxicity Eliminated] tan -->|High SME| redTan[Reduced 40-60 percent
Bitter Taste Lowered] redP --> nutr[Improved Nutritional Quality] redT --> nutr redL --> nutr redTan --> nutr ``` --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Comparative Effects: Extrusion vs Other Processes (KTU Module 4 Emphasis) ```mermaid flowchart TD SP[Food Matrix] --> EX[Extrusion
Thermo-Mechanical] SP --> HPP[High Pressure Processing
Non-Thermal] SP --> PEF[Pulsed Electric Field
Non-Thermal] SP --> US[Ultrasound
Non-Thermal] EX --> E1[Starch Fully Gelatinized] EX --> E2[Proteins Texturized] EX --> E3[ANFs Destroyed] EX --> E4[Significant Microbial Reduction] EX --> E5[Permanent Texture Change] HPP --> H1[Starch Partially Modified] HPP --> H2[Proteins Unfolded Reversibly] HPP --> H3[ANFs Mildly Reduced] HPP --> H4[Excellent Microbial Inactivation] HPP --> H5[Texture Mostly Retained] PEF --> P1[Starch Unchanged] PEF --> P2[Proteins Mildly Modified] PEF --> P3[ANFs Reduced Moderately] PEF --> P4[Microbes Inactivated] PEF --> P5[Texture Preserved] US --> U1[Starch Mildly Disrupted] US --> U2[Proteins Sonicated] US --> U3[ANFs Somewhat Reduced] US --> U4[Microbes Partially Killed] US --> U5[Texture Slightly Altered] ``` > **Interpretation:** Extrusion delivers the **deepest structural change** at the cost of *thermal* damage to some heat-sensitive nutrients, whereas non-thermal processes preserve native structure and nutrients but have limited textural effect. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **List the major chemical changes that occur in food during extrusion cooking. Briefly explain any two.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** During extrusion, the major chemical changes are: 1. **Starch gelatinization and dextrinization** – Native starch granules lose crystalline order under heat, moisture, and shear, forming an amorphous gel. At high SME, partial hydrolysis produces dextrins. 2. **Protein denaturation and cross-linking** – Tertiary structure unfolds; disulfide bonds and isopeptide linkages form, altering functional properties. 3. **Maillard reaction** – Reducing sugars react with free amino groups (especially lysine $\varepsilon$-NH$_2$), producing brown melanoidins. 4. **Lipid oxidation** – Shear ruptures oil bodies, exposing unsaturated fatty acids to oxygen. 5. **Dietary fiber conversion** – Insoluble fiber partially converts to soluble fiber, improving fermentability. *(Any two with one-line explanation = 3 marks: 1.5 marks per change with explanation.)* --- ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **How does extrusion affect the anti-nutritional factors (ANFs) present in legumes and oilseeds?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Extrusion significantly reduces ANFs in legumes and oilseeds: 1. **Trypsin inhibitors** – Reduced by 80–95% at barrel temperature > 140 °C and high SME, dramatically improving protein digestibility. 2. **Phytic acid** – Reduced 50–70% by extrusion, increasing bioavailability of bound minerals (Zn, Fe, Ca). 3. **Lectins** – Completely destroyed above 110 °C, eliminating their haemagglutinating toxicity. 4. **Tannins** – Reduced 40–60% by high SME conditions, lowering astringency and improving palatability. *(Each ANF + percentage reduction + mechanism = approx 0.75 mark. Total 3 marks.)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question has internal choice. Solve either **OR**. Each is split into two sub-parts of **7 marks** each. --- ### **Question A** (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Set B]** **(a)** Explain the **physical and textural changes** that occur in food during extrusion cooking. How do barrel temperature, feed moisture, and SME influence these changes? *(7 marks)* **(b)** Discuss the **nutritional implications** of extrusion on proteins, vitamins, and anti-nutritional factors with suitable examples. *(7 marks)* --- #### **Model Solution for Q-A (a) — 7 marks** **1. Physical/Textural changes during extrusion** (4 marks): | Change | Cause | Consequence | |---|---|---| | **Expansion (puffing)** | Superheated water flashes to steam at die exit | Porous, low-density product | | **Porosity** | Air cell walls set by cooling starch-protein matrix | Crispiness, faster rehydration | | **Density reduction** | Mass diluted by air voids | $\rho$ drops from ~1.0 to 0.1–0.5 g/cm³ | | **Crispness** | Glassy amorphous state | Sensory appeal | | **Color change** | Maillard browning | Golden-brown surface | | **Chewiness/hardness** | Protein cross-linking + dextrin formation | Texture control | **2. Influence of process parameters** (3 marks): - **Barrel temperature ↑** → starch melts earlier, viscosity ↓, vapor pressure ↑, expansion ↑ (up to 140 °C). - **Feed moisture ↑** → water acts as plasticizer, reduces SME, lowers expansion, increases density. - **SME ↑** → more molecular disruption, smaller air cells, denser, more texturized product (e.g., TVP requires SME > 1000 kJ/kg). --- #### **Model Solution for Q-A (b) — 7 marks** **1. Effect on proteins** (2 marks): - **Denaturation** – unfolds tertiary/quaternary structure → improved digestibility. - **Cross-linking** – new S–S bonds and isopeptide linkages; can reduce solubility but improve fibrous texture (TVP). - **Lysine destruction** – Maillard reaction between $\varepsilon$-NH$_2$ of lysine and reducing sugars → loss of *available lysine* (the most KTU-asked loss). **2. Effect on vitamins** (2 marks): - **B-group vitamins** (thiamine, riboflavin, niacin) – partially destroyed, especially thiamine (up to 30% loss). - **Vitamin C (ascorbic acid)** – significantly degraded due to heat and oxidation. - **Fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E)** – relatively stable but may oxidize if lipids oxidize. - Example: *Extruded corn-based breakfast cereals retain ~80% of added B-vitamins after fortification*. **3. Effect on ANFs** (2 marks): - **Phytates, trypsin inhibitors, lectins, tannins, oxalates** all reduced. - Example: *Extruded soy flour shows 90% reduction in trypsin inhibitor activity, allowing safe use in infant formulations.* **4. Conclusion** (1 mark): Extrusion improves protein digestibility and destroys ANFs, but causes heat-labile vitamin loss and lysine damage — *fortification* compensates for these losses in commercial RTE products. --- ### **Question B (Alternative choice)** (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Set A]** **(a)** Describe the **rheological and functional changes** in starch and protein as affected by extrusion. Use the WSI, WAI, and WHC indices to explain. *(7 marks)* **(b)** With a neat flow diagram, explain the **mechanism of starch gelatinization and dextrinization** during extrusion. How do you measure the **degree of gelatinization** by DSC? *(7 marks)* --- #### **Model Solution for Q-B (a) — 7 marks** **1. Starch changes** (3.5 marks): - Native starch granules (semi-crystalline) → amorphous gel under heat + shear. - Polymer chains fragment → **dextrins** of lower molecular weight. - Increased **WSI** (Water Solubility Index) — due to smaller, soluble fragments. - WAI peaks at moderate SME; drops at very high SME due to over-degradation. - Loss of pasting peak viscosity in RVA (Rapid Visco Analyzer). - Example: *Pre-gelatinized starch is cold-water-soluble, used in instant puddings.* **2. Protein changes** (3.5 marks): - Denaturation unfolds globular proteins → exposes hydrophobic groups. - Formation of fibrous, anisotropic structures (TVP). - Changes in **WHC** (↑ due to porous structure) and **OHC** (↑ due to capillary pores). - Emulsifying capacity (EAI) and foaming capacity (FC) — usually increase to a point, then drop. - Example: *Extruded soy protein concentrate shows 25% higher WHC than native soy flour.* > **Connecting to formulae:** WSI = (mass of dried supernatant / mass of dry sample) × 100, indicating the fraction of soluble material; WAI = (mass of gel / mass of dry sample), indicating swelling power. --- #### **Model Solution for Q-B (b) — 7 marks** **1. Starch gelatinization mechanism** (3 marks) — supported by diagram: ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Native Starch Granule
Semi-crystalline] -->|T 60-70C + Water| B[Reversible Swelling] B -->|T 80-90C + Shear| C[Irreversible Swelling
Birefringence Lost] C -->|T 100C+ High Shear| D[Granule Rupture
Amylose Leaches Out] D -->|Cooling| E[Amorphous Gel Matrix
Set on Cooling] E -->|High SME| F[Dextrin Formation
Soluble Fragments] ``` **Explanation:** - **Stage 1** (T < 60 °C): reversible water uptake. - **Stage 2** (T 60–90 °C): hydrogen bonds in crystalline regions break, **birefringence lost** — point of gelatinization onset. - **Stage 3** (T > 100 °C + shear): granules rupture, amylose leaches out, viscosity peaks. - **Stage 4** (in extruder): continuous high shear + T → dextrinization (chain scission). - **Stage 5** (at die): sudden pressure drop → water flashes → expansion; starch matrix sets in **glassy state** on cooling. **2. Dextrinization** (1 mark): - At SME > 1000 kJ/kg and T > 150 °C, glycosidic bonds break. - Produces maltodextrins, oligosaccharides. - Result: **cold-water solubility**, low paste viscosity. **3. DSC measurement of Degree of Gelatinization** (3 marks): - **Principle:** Differential Scanning Calorimetry measures the energy (enthalpy, $\Delta H$) absorbed during the order-to-disorder transition of starch. - **Steps:** 1. Weigh 3–5 mg of native starch (reference) in DSC pan; add water (1:3 ratio). 2. Heat from 20 °C to 110 °C at 5 °C/min — record $\Delta H_{\text{native}}$ (peak around 60–80 °C). 3. Repeat for extrudate sample — record $\Delta H_{\text{extrudate}}$. 4. Calculate using: $$\text{DG}\,(\%) = \frac{\Delta H_{\text{native}} - \Delta H_{\text{extrudate}}}{\Delta H_{\text{native}}} \times 100$$ - **Interpretation:** A *lower* $\Delta H_{\text{extrudate}}$ means *more* starch was already gelatinized during extrusion. A DG of ~95–100% indicates *complete* gelatinization, typical of high-SME extrusion. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout — Effects of Extrusion:** > > 1. **Don't confuse SME with residence time** — SME is energy per kg; residence time is time spent. Both matter but are independent parameters. > 2. **Don't claim "extrusion is non-thermal"** — It IS thermal (100–200 °C). Module 4 places it for *comparative* study, not as a non-thermal technique. > 3. **Always specify which anti-nutritional factor** when quoting a percentage reduction — examiners deduct 1 mark for vague statements. > 4. **Units in SME problems**: Final answer should be in **kJ/kg**, not J/kg, for readability. > 5. **Maillard browning ≠ caramelization** — both produce brown color, but Maillard requires amino groups; caramelization is sugar-only. > 6. **In DSC gelatinization questions, always state the heating rate** (typically 5 °C/min or 10 °C/min) — examiners expect it. > 7. **Do not write "extrusion kills all microbes"** — it kills vegetative cells, NOT bacterial spores. Use the term *"microbial load reduction"*. --- ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield 10-Point Rapid Checklist (Memorize for KTU):** - ☐ Extrusion is a **thermo-mechanical** process — not purely non-thermal — but is studied in Module 4 for *comparative* purposes. - ☐ The **5 pillars of extrusion effects**: Physical, Chemical, Nutritional, Microbiological, Rheological. - ☐ **SME (Specific Mechanical Energy)** = $\dfrac{\omega \cdot \tau \cdot L}{m_s \cdot D}$, units **kJ/kg**. - ☐ **Expansion Ratio (ER)** = $D_e^2 / D_d^2$ (cross-sectional basis, dimensionless). - ☐ **Degree of Starch Gelatinization (DG)** is measured by **DSC** as the fractional loss of gelatinization enthalpy. - ☐ **Starch gelatinization** is *irreversible* in extrusion (vs reversible at low T); at high SME, **dextrinization** occurs. - ☐ **Lysine is the most-damaged amino acid** (via Maillard reaction); methionine and cystine are next. - ☐ **ANFs destroyed**: trypsin inhibitors (80–95%), phytates (50–70%), lectins (>110 °C), tannins (40–60%). - ☐ **WSI ↑** and **WAI peaks** at moderate SME then drops; **WHC and OHC ↑** due to porosity. - ☐ **Microbial inactivation**: significant for vegetative cells, *not* spores; aflatoxin-producing molds are inactivated. > [!TIP] > **One-line exam-ready summary (write this for full marks in 2-mark questions):** > *"Extrusion cooking is a high-temperature, high-shear, short-time (HTST) process that simultaneously gelatinizes starch, denatures proteins, destroys anti-nutritional factors, inactivates vegetative microbes, and produces an expanded, porous, crispy food matrix — making it a cornerstone of modern snack, cereal, and texturized-protein industries."*

topics.title: Membrane Technologies in Food Processing - Overview of Membrane Technology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT631 - NON-THERMAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Membrane Technologies in Food Processing - Overview of Membrane Technology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Membrane Technology** is a physical separation process that employs a **semi-permeable barrier (membrane)** to selectively separate components of a feed mixture based on differences in molecular size, shape, charge, chemical affinity, or vapor pressure, when a driving force (pressure, concentration, temperature, or electrical potential) is applied across the membrane.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Membrane technology is a *non-thermal* separation technique that uses the principle of **selective permeability** to fractionate, concentrate, or purify components in liquid or gaseous food streams, without altering their thermal state.

In the context of **Food Processing**, membrane technology is classified under **Non-Thermal Processing (NTP)** because it accomplishes concentration, clarification, fractionation, and demineralization at ambient or near-ambient temperatures, thereby preserving heat-labile nutrients, bioactive compounds, flavors, colors, and vitamins.

## 1.2 Key Constituents of a Membrane System

A membrane separation system consists of three fundamental elements:

1. **Membrane (The Barrier):** A thin, selective layer that permits passage of certain species (permeate) while retaining others (retentate or concentrate).
2. **Driving Force (The Engine):** A thermodynamic potential gradient — typically **Pressure ($\Delta P$)**, **Concentration ($\Delta C$)**, **Temperature ($\Delta T$)**, or **Electrical Potential ($\Delta E$)** — that pushes specific molecules through the membrane.
3. **Feed / Permeate / Retentate Streams (The Outputs):**
   - **Feed ($F$)** – the input mixture introduced to the membrane module.
   - **Permeate ($P$)** – the fraction that passes through the membrane.
   - **Retentate ($R$)** – the fraction rejected by the membrane, returned to the feed tank.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Coffee Filter Principle ☕**
>
> Imagine pouring a cup of filter coffee. The **filter paper** is your **membrane**. The ground coffee particles (large) are *retained* on the filter — this is the **retentate**. The aromatic liquid (small molecules: water, caffeine, flavor) passes through — this is the **permeate**. The **pressure** from gravity (and your hand) is the **driving force**.
>
> Now, scale this concept down to **nanometers** and replace the paper with a polymer or ceramic film that can discriminate between molecules of different sizes (from microns down to ions). That is membrane technology in food processing!

Think of the membrane as an **intelligent molecular sieve** — it does not destroy or chemically alter food components; it simply *filters* them based on physical and chemical selectivity.

## 1.4 Historical Context (Board-Exam Favorite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - The first commercial synthetic membrane was developed by **Bechhold (1907)** using nitrocellulose.
> - **Zsigmondy (1922)** developed the first commercial ultrafiltration membrane.
> - The modern era began with the development of **asymmetric membranes by Loeb and Sourirajan (1960)**, enabling reverse osmosis for seawater desalination.
> - In food processing, membrane technology gained industrial prominence in the **1970s**, particularly for **cheese whey protein recovery** and **milk standardization**.

## 1.5 Standard Units and Metrics (Bolded for Board Recall)

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|-----------|--------|----------------|
| **Membrane Flux** | $J$ | **L · m⁻² · h⁻¹ (LMH)** |
| **Transmembrane Pressure** | $\Delta P$ or **TMP** | **bar** or **kPa** |
| **Rejection Coefficient** | $R$ | **dimensionless (%)** |
| **Membrane Pore Size** | $d_p$ | **nanometer (nm) / micrometer (µm)** |
| **Molecular Weight Cut-Off** | **MWCO** | **Dalton (Da)** |
| **Membrane Area** | $A_m$ | **m²** |
| **Permeability** | $L_p$ | **L · m⁻² · h⁻¹ · bar⁻¹** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Membrane Processes

Membrane processes are classified based on the **pore size of the membrane** and the **magnitude of the driving force**. This is the **most frequently asked classification topic** in KTU examinations.

### 2.1.1 Classification by Pore Size (Separation Spectrum)

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Pearl:** *"From large to small — MF → UF → NF → RO → ED"* — memorize this descending pore size order.

| Process | Pore Size / MWCO | Particles Retained | Driving Force | Typical Application in Food |
|---------|------------------|---------------------|---------------|------------------------------|
| **Microfiltration (MF)** | **0.1 – 10 µm** | Bacteria, yeast, suspended solids | $\Delta P$ = 0.1–2 bar | **Cold pasteurization of milk, juice clarification** |
| **Ultrafiltration (UF)** | **1 – 100 nm (1k–500k Da)** | Proteins, macromolecules, colloids | $\Delta P$ = 1–10 bar | **Whey protein concentration, milk standardization** |
| **Nanofiltration (NF)** | **0.5 – 5 nm (200–1000 Da)** | Divalent salts, lactose, small organics | $\Delta P$ = 10–40 bar | **Partial demineralization of whey, lactose concentration** |
| **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** | **< 0.5 nm (< 200 Da)** | Monovalent ions, low MW solutes | $\Delta P$ = 15–80 bar | **Concentration of fruit juices, water recovery** |
| **Electrodialysis (ED)** | Ion-exchange membrane | Ions (cations & anions) | $\Delta E$ = 1–2 V/cell | **Demineralization of whey, salt adjustment** |
| **Pervaporation (PV)** | Dense membrane | Selective organic vapors | $\Delta p$ (vapor partial pressure) | **Ethanol recovery, aroma extraction** |

### 2.1.2 Classification by Membrane Morphology

1. **Symmetric (Isotropic) Membranes:** Uniform pore structure throughout the thickness (e.g., MF membranes).
2. **Asymmetric (Anisotropic) Membranes:** A thin, dense top *skin layer* (responsible for selectivity) supported by a thicker, porous sub-layer (mechanical support). Developed by **Loeb–Sourirajan (1960)**.
3. **Composite Membranes:** A thin selective layer (e.g., polyamide) coated on a separate porous support (e.g., polysulfone) — most common in RO/NF.

## 2.2 Membrane Materials

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"List four membrane materials used in food processing."*

| Material | Type | Key Property | Application |
|----------|------|--------------|-------------|
| **Cellulose Acetate (CA)** | Polymeric | Hydrophilic, low fouling, low pH tolerance | RO, UF |
| **Polyethersulfone (PES)** | Polymeric | High thermal & chemical stability | UF, MF |
| **Polyvinylidene Fluoride (PVDF)** | Polymeric | Hydrophobic, high mechanical strength | MF |
| **Polyamide (PA)** | Polymeric | High salt rejection, thin-film composite | RO, NF |
| **Polysulfone (PSf)** | Polymeric | Robust, broad pH tolerance | UF |
| **Ceramic (Al₂O₃, ZrO₂, TiO₂)** | Inorganic | Extremely durable, steam-cleanable, expensive | MF, UF (high-temp) |
| **Stainless Steel / Sintered Metal** | Metallic | Ultra-durable, used in harsh conditions | MF |

## 2.3 Membrane Configurations (Module Geometry)

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic: "PlaST Hollow" → Plate, Spiral, Tubular, Hollow fiber**

| Configuration | Description | Packing Density (m²/m³) | Application |
|---------------|-------------|--------------------------|-------------|
| **Plate-and-Frame** | Flat membranes stacked in cassettes | 100–400 | Lab, dairy UF |
| **Spiral Wound** | Membrane envelopes wound around a permeate tube | 500–1000 | RO, NF, large-scale |
| **Tubular** | Membranes cast on inner wall of tubes (diameter > 6 mm) | Low (~100) | Viscous feeds (e.g., starch, fruit pulp) |
| **Hollow Fiber** | Self-supporting fibers (diameter < 1 mm) with dense skin | Very high (1000–10000) | RO, UF, MF |
| **Pleiaded (Submerged)** | Hollow fibers immersed directly in feed tank | High | MBR, juice clarification |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for ESE and module tests.**

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Form |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Membrane Flux** | Volumetric permeate flow per unit area | $J = \dfrac{V_p}{A_m \cdot t}$ |
| 2 | **Permeability (Pure Water)** | Flux per unit pressure | $L_p = \dfrac{J}{\Delta P}$ |
| 3 | **Flux (Pressure-Driven)** | For UF/MF | $J = L_p \cdot \Delta P$ |
| 4 | **Flux (RO with Osmotic Pressure)** | Solvent transport (solution-diffusion) | $J_w = A_w(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$ |
| 5 | **Solute Flux (RO)** | For salt/organic transport | $J_s = B_s \cdot (C_f - C_p)$ |
| 6 | **Rejection Coefficient** | Fraction of solute rejected | $R = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}$ |
| 7 | **Observed/Apparent Rejection** | Including concentration polarization | $R_{obs} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_b}$ |
| 8 | **Intrinsic Rejection** | At membrane surface | $R_{int} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_w}$ |
| 9 | **Volumetric Recovery (Yield)** | Fraction of feed recovered as permeate | $Y = \dfrac{V_p}{V_f}$ |
| 10 | **Selectivity Factor (for binary)** | Ratio of permeabilities | $\alpha_{ij} = \dfrac{(C_i/C_j)_p}{(C_i/C_j)_f}$ |
| 11 | **Concentration Polarization (Film Theory)** | Boundary-layer mass balance | $J = k \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_w - C_p}{C_b - C_p}\right)$ |
| 12 | **Resistance-in-Series Model** | Flux in fouled membrane | $J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot (R_m + R_f + R_{cp})}$ |
| 13 | **TMP (Crossflow Module)** | Average of inlet/outlet pressures | $TMP = \dfrac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_p$ |
| 14 | **Mass Balance (Overall)** | Component balance | $F \cdot C_f = P \cdot C_p + R \cdot C_r$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** In the table above, vertical bars inside the concentration polarization expression use $\vert$ notation to prevent markdown table formatting conflicts.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Membrane technology is extensively used in the following food sectors:

1. **Dairy Industry** — Whey protein concentration (UF), lactose concentration (NF), milk standardization, cheese-making, demineralization (ED/NF).
2. **Beverages & Fruit Juices** — Apple, orange, and grape juice clarification (UF); concentration of juices (RO).
3. **Wine & Beer** — Cold stabilization, clarification, alcohol adjustment (NF/RO), dealcoholization.
4. **Sugar Industry** — Purification of cane/beet sugar juice, color removal.
5. **Edible Oils** — Degumming, deacidification, solvent recovery.
6. **Water Treatment** — RO and NF for process water reuse in food plants.
7. **Biotechnology** — Enzyme recovery, fermentation broth clarification, vaccine concentration.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Membrane technology is **modular and scalable** — capacity can be increased by simply adding more membrane area. It also has a **small footprint**, low energy footprint (compared to evaporation), and operates continuously, making it the preferred choice in modern food plants.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Membrane Flux Equation

The volumetric membrane flux $J$ is defined as the volume of permeate ($V_p$) collected per unit membrane area ($A_m$) per unit time ($t$).

**Starting Definition:**

$$J = \frac{V_p}{A_m \cdot t}$$

**Step 1 — Express in terms of permeate linear velocity:**
The permeate flow rate $Q_p = V_p / t$. So the equation becomes:

$$J = \frac{Q_p}{A_m}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Darcy's Law (for pressure-driven processes):**
For a pressure-driven membrane process, the volumetric flux is proportional to the transmembrane pressure difference $\Delta P$, inversely proportional to the dynamic viscosity $\mu$ of the permeate, and inversely proportional to the total membrane resistance $R_{total}$:

$$J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_{total}}$$

**Step 3 — Expand total resistance (Resistance-in-Series Model):**
The total resistance includes the clean membrane resistance $R_m$, the fouling resistance $R_f$ (deposited solids, biofilm), and the concentration polarization resistance $R_{cp}$ (accumulated solutes at the membrane surface):

$$R_{total} = R_m + R_f + R_{cp}$$

**Step 4 — Final Form:**

$$\boxed{\,J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot (R_m + R_f + R_{cp})}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Flux *decreases* over time as $R_f$ and $R_{cp}$ increase (membrane fouling). Recovery requires periodic **cleaning-in-place (CIP)** protocols.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Rejection Coefficient

**Step 1 — Define feed, permeate, and retentate concentrations:**
- $C_f$ = concentration of solute in the feed
- $C_p$ = concentration of solute in the permeate
- $C_b$ = concentration in the bulk feed
- $C_w$ = concentration at the membrane wall (polarization layer)

**Step 2 — Define true (intrinsic) rejection:**
The intrinsic rejection depends on the membrane's inherent selectivity, measured at the membrane surface:

$$R_{int} = \frac{C_w - C_p}{C_w} = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_w}$$

**Step 3 — Define observed (apparent) rejection:**
This is what we actually measure experimentally using bulk concentrations:

$$R_{obs} = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_b}$$

**Step 4 — Relationship via concentration polarization:**
Since $C_w > C_b > C_p$ (solutes accumulate at the membrane wall), we have:

$$R_{int} > R_{obs}$$

This means the **observed rejection is always lower than the intrinsic rejection** because concentration polarization dilutes the true selectivity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Tip:** Always specify *which* rejection you are calculating — $R_{int}$ (membrane property) or $R_{obs}$ (process measurement).

## 3.3 Derivation of the Concentration Polarization Equation (Film Theory)

**Step 1 — Set up the boundary layer:**
At steady state, convective transport of solute toward the membrane equals the sum of solute passage through the membrane and back-diffusion into the bulk.

**Step 2 — Mass balance over a control volume of thickness $\delta$ (the boundary layer):**

$$J \cdot C - J \cdot C_p + D \cdot \frac{dC}{dy} = 0$$

where $D$ is the diffusion coefficient, $y$ is the coordinate normal to the membrane.

**Step 3 — Integrate from bulk ($C_b$, $y=0$) to wall ($C_w$, $y=\delta$):**

$$\int_{C_b}^{C_w} \frac{dC}{C - C_p} = -\frac{J}{D} \int_{0}^{\delta} dy$$

**Step 4 — Solve the integral:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{C_w - C_p}{C_b - C_p}\right) = \frac{J \cdot \delta}{D}$$

**Step 5 — Define the mass transfer coefficient $k = D / \delta$:**

$$\boxed{\,J = k \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_w - C_p}{C_b - C_p}\right)\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **classic Film Theory equation** (Michaels & Zidney, 1968). It is used to compute the wall concentration $C_w$ — the parameter governing fouling — from measurable $J$, $C_b$, and $C_p$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Operating Variables

| Variable | Increase Variable → | Effect on Flux ($J$) | Effect on Rejection ($R$) | Practical Implication |
|----------|--------------------|----------------------|----------------------------|------------------------|
| **$\Delta P$ (TMP)** | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ linearly (UF/MF) | Slight $\uparrow$ | Higher energy cost |
| **Crossflow Velocity** | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ (via reduced $R_{cp}$) | Slight $\uparrow$ | Higher pumping cost |
| **Temperature** | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ (lower $\mu$, higher $D$) | Variable | Risk of microbial growth; not for heat-sensitive |
| **Feed Concentration** | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ (via $R_{cp}$ and $R_f$) | $\downarrow$ | Increased fouling |
| **Membrane Pore Size** | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ | Selectivity lost |
| **Feed pH** | Near protein pI | $\downarrow$ | Variable | Protein aggregation at pI |
| **Fouling** | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ sharply | Variable | Frequent cleaning needed |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**Define membrane technology. List the four major pressure-driven membrane processes in increasing order of pore size.**

**Model Answer:**

**Membrane technology** is a non-thermal separation technique that uses a *semi-permeable membrane* to selectively separate components of a feed mixture under an applied driving force (pressure, concentration, electrical potential, or temperature), while preserving the physico-chemical integrity of the food.

**Four Pressure-Driven Processes (Ascending Pore Size — increasing pore size):**

1. **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** — pore size < 0.5 nm
2. **Nanofiltration (NF)** — pore size 0.5–5 nm
3. **Ultrafiltration (UF)** — pore size 1–100 nm
4. **Microfiltration (MF)** — pore size 0.1–10 µm

> [!TIP]
> **Remember:** "RO → NF → UF → MF" — *largest particles in MF, smallest ions in RO.*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Differentiate between symmetric and asymmetric membranes with a suitable example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Symmetric Membrane | Asymmetric Membrane |
|---------|---------------------|----------------------|
| **Structure** | Uniform pore size and composition throughout the entire membrane thickness | A thin, dense *skin layer* (selective) supported by a thicker, porous *sub-layer* (mechanical) |
| **Flux** | Low — entire thickness offers resistance | High — resistance concentrated in thin skin |
| **Example** | Microfiltration membrane (e.g., track-etched polycarbonate) | Loeb–Sourirajan RO/UF membrane (e.g., cellulose acetate) |
| **Developed by** | Early filtration | Loeb & Sourirajan (1960) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students must answer **one full 14-mark question** (with sub-parts). We provide **two alternative choices (A and B)** to practice both.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat labeled diagram, explain the working principle of a **crossflow membrane separation system**. Define the terms *flux*, *rejection*, and *recovery*, giving their mathematical expressions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A dairy plant processes **1000 L/h** of cheese whey through an ultrafiltration unit with a membrane area of **20 m²**. The permeate collected in **one hour** is **750 L**, containing **0.2% (w/v) protein**, while the feed contains **1.0% (w/v) protein**.
Calculate:
1. Volumetric flux $J$ (in LMH)
2. Observed rejection $R$
3. Volumetric recovery $Y$
4. Permeate protein mass flow rate (kg/h)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Crossflow Membrane System:**

In **crossflow filtration**, the feed stream flows *tangentially* (parallel) along the membrane surface. The applied pressure (driving force) drives part of the feed through the membrane as **permeate**, while the remaining feed (now more concentrated) exits as **retentate** and is recycled to the feed tank.

The hydrodynamic shear from crossflow continuously sweeps retained solutes away, minimizing **concentration polarization** and **fouling**, leading to sustained high flux — a major advantage over *dead-end* filtration.

```
              ┌─────────── Retentate (R) ──────────┐
              │                                    │
              │       ┌──── Membrane ────┐         │
   Feed (F) ──┼──────▶│ ◄─── Crossflow ──▶│────────┘
              │       └───────────────────┘
              │              ↓
              │       ┌─────────────┐
              │       │  Permeate   │
              │       │    (P)      │
              │       └─────────────┘
```

**Mathematical Definitions:**

1. **Flux:**
$$J = \frac{V_p}{A_m \cdot t} \quad \left[\text{L} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}\right]$$

2. **Rejection (Observed):**
$$R = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_f} \quad \text{[dimensionless]}$$

3. **Recovery (Yield):**
$$Y = \frac{V_p}{V_f} \quad \text{[dimensionless, often expressed as \%]}$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Feed flow rate: $F = 1000$ L/h
- Membrane area: $A_m = 20$ m²
- Permeate volume in 1 h: $V_p = 750$ L
- Permeate protein concentration: $C_p = 0.2\%$ (w/v)
- Feed protein concentration: $C_f = 1.0\%$ (w/v)
- Time: $t = 1$ h

**Step 1 — Volumetric Flux $J$:**

$$J = \frac{V_p}{A_m \cdot t} = \frac{750 \text{ L}}{20 \text{ m}^2 \times 1 \text{ h}} = 37.5 \; \text{L} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Observed Rejection $R$:**

$$R = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_f} = 1 - \frac{0.2}{1.0} = 1 - 0.2 = 0.8 = 80\%$$

**Step 3 — Volumetric Recovery $Y$:**

$$Y = \frac{V_p}{V_f} = \frac{750}{1000} = 0.75 = 75\%$$

**Step 4 — Permeate Protein Mass Flow Rate $\dot{m}_{p}$:**

$$\dot{m}_{p} = V_p \times C_p = 750 \text{ L} \times 0.2\% \text{ (w/v)}$$

Converting 0.2% w/v → 0.2 g per 100 mL = 2 g/L:

$$\dot{m}_{p} = 750 \text{ L} \times 2 \text{ g/L} = 1500 \text{ g/h} = 1.5 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Summary of Results:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Value |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| **Volumetric Flux** | $J$ | **37.5 LMH** |
| **Observed Rejection** | $R$ | **80 %** |
| **Volumetric Recovery** | $Y$ | **75 %** |
| **Permeate Protein Mass Flow** | $\dot{m}_p$ | **1.5 kg/h** |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the major membrane processes based on **pore size and driving force**, listing the typical pore size, MWCO range, driving force, and one food-industry application of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the phenomenon of **concentration polarization** and **membrane fouling** in ultrafiltration. Discuss the **resistance-in-series model** and write the flux equation incorporating all resistances.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Classification Table:**

| Process | Pore Size | MWCO | Driving Force | Food Application |
|---------|-----------|------|----------------|------------------|
| **Microfiltration (MF)** | 0.1 – 10 µm | > 500 kDa | $\Delta P$ = 0.1–2 bar | **Cold pasteurization of milk, beer clarification** |
| **Ultrafiltration (UF)** | 1 – 100 nm | 1k – 500k Da | $\Delta P$ = 1–10 bar | **Whey protein recovery, milk concentration** |
| **Nanofiltration (NF)** | 0.5 – 5 nm | 200 – 1000 Da | $\Delta P$ = 10–40 bar | **Lactose concentration, partial desalination** |
| **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** | < 0.5 nm | < 200 Da | $\Delta P$ = 15–80 bar | **Fruit juice concentration, water reuse** |
| **Electrodialysis (ED)** | Ion-exchange | — | $\Delta E$ = 1–2 V/cell | **Whey demineralization** |
| **Pervaporation (PV)** | Dense | — | $\Delta p$ (vapor) | **Aroma recovery, ethanol removal** |

**Part (b) — Concentration Polarization and Fouling:**

**Concentration Polarization (CP):** When a feed is filtered under pressure, the solvent and small solutes pass through the membrane, while rejected solutes accumulate at the membrane surface. This creates a thin boundary layer with concentration $C_w$ (wall) much higher than the bulk $C_b$. The result is a diffusive back-flow of solute into the bulk — the system reaches **steady state** when convective transport toward the membrane equals the sum of permeate transport and back-diffusion.

CP is described by the **Film Theory equation** (derived in Section 3.3):

$$J = k \cdot \ln\left(\frac{C_w - C_p}{C_b - C_p}\right)$$

**Membrane Fouling:** When $C_w$ exceeds the *solubility limit* or *gel concentration* of the rejected solute, a **gel layer** forms on the membrane. This is irreversible (or only partially reversible) and consists of:
- **Pore blockage** (small solutes lodged in pores)
- **Pore constriction** (solutes adsorbing onto pore walls)
- **Cake/gel layer formation** (deposits on the surface)
- **Biofouling** (microbial film growth)

**Resistance-in-Series Model:**

The flux in a fouled membrane is governed by **Darcy's law** applied across a stack of resistances:

$$J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot (R_m + R_f + R_{cp})}$$

| Resistance | Symbol | Cause | Reversibility |
|------------|--------|-------|---------------|
| **Membrane resistance** | $R_m$ | Intrinsic resistance of clean membrane | N/A (baseline) |
| **Fouling resistance** | $R_f$ | Pore blocking, adsorption, cake | Partially reversible (CIP) |
| **Concentration polarization resistance** | $R_{cp}$ | Solute accumulation at wall | Reversible (relaxation, backwash) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mitigation Strategies:** (i) Increased crossflow velocity (sweeps away CP), (ii) Backwashing, (iii) Chemical cleaning (CIP), (iv) Pretreatment (microfiltration, activated carbon), (v) Use of low-fouling hydrophilic membranes (PES, regenerated cellulose), (vi) Pulsed feed/relaxation cycles.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Traps in Membrane Technology Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $R_{int}$ with $R_{obs}$** — Examiners specifically test whether you know that observed rejection uses bulk concentration $C_b$, while intrinsic uses wall concentration $C_w$. Mixing these up costs **2–3 marks**.
>
> 2. **Wrong unit for Flux** — Flux must be in **L·m⁻²·h⁻¹ (LMH)**, NOT L/h or m³/s. Always check unit consistency.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Osmotic Pressure in RO** — The driving force in RO is $(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$, not just $\Delta P$. This is a frequently tested KTU trap.
>
> 4. **Omitting TMP formula** — In crossflow modules, the relevant pressure is the average transmembrane pressure, NOT the inlet pressure:
> $$TMP = \frac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_p$$
>
> 5. **Conflating "Rejection" with "Recovery"** — Rejection is about *solute* retention; recovery is about *volumetric* permeate yield.
>
> 6. **Skipping diagrams in long answers** — KTU valuation keys award **1–2 marks** for a *neat, labeled schematic*. Always include a simple block diagram.
>
> 7. **Not mentioning the non-thermal advantage** — In PEFTT631 (Non-Thermal Processing), always emphasize that membrane technology preserves heat-sensitive nutrients and bioactive compounds — this aligns with the course's central theme.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this in the final 10 minutes before your exam.**

### 🔹 Core Concepts
- Membrane technology is a **non-thermal, physical, size-based separation** process using a semi-permeable barrier and a driving force.
- The **three output streams** are: Feed ($F$), Permeate ($P$), and Retentate ($R$).
- The **four pressure-driven processes** in increasing pore size: **RO → NF → UF → MF**.

### 🔹 Driving Forces
- **MF, UF, NF, RO** → Pressure ($\Delta P$)
- **Pervaporation** → Vapor partial pressure difference
- **Electrodialysis** → Electrical potential ($\Delta E$)
- **Membrane Distillation** → Temperature ($\Delta T$)

### 🔹 Membrane Types
- **Polymeric:** CA, PES, PSf, PVDF, PA (cheap, common).
- **Inorganic (Ceramic/Metal):** Al₂O₃, ZrO₂, TiO₂ (expensive, durable, steam-sterilizable).
- **Morphology:** Symmetric (uniform) vs. Asymmetric (thin skin + porous support).

### 🔹 Configurations
- **Plate-and-Frame** (low packing), **Spiral Wound** (high packing, RO/NF), **Tubular** (viscous feeds), **Hollow Fiber** (highest packing, MF/UF).
- **Crossflow > Dead-end** for sustained operation.

### 🔹 Key Performance Equations (Must Memorize)
- $J = V_p / (A_m \cdot t)$
- $L_p = J / \Delta P$
- $R = 1 - C_p / C_f$
- $Y = V_p / V_f$
- $J = \Delta P / [\mu(R_m + R_f + R_{cp})]$
- $J = k \cdot \ln[(C_w - C_p)/(C_b - C_p)]$
- $TMP = (P_{in} + P_{out})/2 - P_p$

### 🔹 Industrial Food Applications
- **Dairy:** Whey UF, milk standardization, cheese.
- **Juices:** Apple/Orange clarification (UF), concentration (RO).
- **Beverages:** Beer/wine clarification, dealcoholization.
- **Sugar:** Decolorization, purification.
- **Oils:** Degumming, deacidification.

### 🔹 Advantages
- Non-thermal → preserves nutrients, flavor, color.
- Low energy vs. evaporation.
- Continuous, modular, scalable.
- No phase change (except pervaporation).
- Environmentally friendly (no chemical additives needed).

### 🔹 Limitations
- **Membrane fouling** (flux decline) → CIP required.
- **Concentration polarization** → reduced selectivity.
- **Limited membrane lifetime** (3–5 years typical).
- **High capital cost** for ceramic/specialty membranes.
- **Not suitable for high-viscosity feeds** (plugging).

### 🔹 Quick Numerical Practice
For a UF unit: $F = 500$ L/h, $V_p = 350$ L/h, $A_m = 10$ m², $C_f = 0.8\%$, $C_p = 0.1\%$.
- $J = 35$ LMH; $R = 87.5\%$; $Y = 70\%$.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Microfiltration (MF)** is a pressure-driven membrane separation process that employs **semi-permeable porous membranes** with nominal pore diameters ranging from **0.1 µm to 10 µm** to physically separate suspended particles, microorganisms (bacteria, yeast, molds), emulsified droplets, and macromolecular aggregates from a fluid stream (liquid or gas), without undergoing a phase change or significant temperature elevation.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context of **Non-Thermal Processing** of foods, MF is classified as a **physical, non-thermal membrane technology** that achieves *cold pasteurization* or *cold sterilization* by exploiting a size-exclusion mechanism governed purely by the membrane's mean pore diameter ($d_p$) and the hydrodynamic size of the target species.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Fine Sieve in the Kitchen Sink**
> 
> Imagine pouring a mixture of sand and water through a tea strainer held over a glass. The water passes through effortlessly (the **permeate**), while the larger sand grains are retained on top of the mesh (the **retentate/concentrate**). Microfiltration is essentially this exact concept, but engineered with sub-micron precision:
> - The **membrane** acts as an extremely fine sieve with millions of precisely laser-drilled pores per cm².
> - The **fluid** is forced through using a gentle pressure differential (typically **0.1 – 2.0 bar**) — far milder than what you would use to crush a tomato.
> - Unlike heating (which destroys vitamins and alters flavor), MF is purely *mechanical*, preserving the heat-labile nutrients, aroma compounds, and bioactive molecules of juices, milk, and wine.

### 1.3 Position in the Membrane Separation Spectrum

MF occupies the **largest pore-size** end of the pressure-driven membrane cascade. The hierarchy is critical for exam questions:

| Process | Pore Size | Driving Pressure | Retains |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Microfiltration (MF)** | **0.1 – 10 µm** | **0.1 – 2 bar** | Bacteria, yeast, suspended solids |
| Ultrafiltration (UF) | 1 – 100 nm | 1 – 10 bar | Proteins, macromolecules |
| Nanofiltration (NF) | 0.5 – 5 nm | 5 – 40 bar | Divalent salts, lactose |
| Reverse Osmosis (RO) | < 0.5 nm | 10 – 100 bar | Monovalent ions, water |

### 1.4 Typical Application Spectrum in Food Engineering

- **Cold pasteurization of beer, wine, and cider** (clarification + bio-stabilization)
- **Bacteria removal from milk** for extended shelf-life without heat
- **Clarification of fruit juices** (apple, grape, citrus — removal of pectin, pulp, tartrate crystals)
- **Recovery of microorganisms** in fermentation broths (yeast harvesting)
- **Sterile filtration of pharmaceutical water** and heat-sensitive beverages
- **Pre-treatment to UF/RO** to reduce fouling load

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** MF is specifically positioned in Module 4 as the *entry-level* membrane technology to be studied before progressing to UF, since the governing equations (Darcy's law, resistance-in-series model) form the **mathematical foundation** for all subsequent membrane processes.
