## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — The Core Mechanism

The separation is governed by a simple **size-exclusion (sieving) mechanism**:

1. A **feed stream** containing a mixture of solvent (permeate phase) and particles/microbes (retained phase) is introduced on the upstream side of the membrane.
2. A **transmembrane pressure (TMP)**, $\Delta P$, is applied — either by a pump (in cross-flow systems) or by gravity/positive pressure (in dead-end cartridges).
3. The solvent — being smaller than the membrane pores — passes through and emerges as **permeate**.
4. Species with characteristic dimension $\geq d_p$ are physically intercepted by the pore entrance and accumulate as **retentate**.

### 2.2 Two Geometric Modes of Operation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The mode of operation is the single most important design decision and is a **favourite KTU question**.

**Mode 1 — Dead-End (Normal-Flow) Filtration**
- Flow is perpendicular ($\perp$) to the membrane surface.
- Particles accumulate as a **cake layer** on top of the membrane.
- Flux decays rapidly with time.
- Used for: low-solid feeds, sterile filtration of liquids in cartridges (e.g., final polish of beverages).

**Mode 2 — Cross-Flow (Tangential-Flow) Filtration**
- Flow is parallel ($\parallel$) to the membrane surface with simultaneous permeation.
- The tangential velocity generates **shear stress** that continuously sweeps deposited particles off the membrane surface → limits cake growth.
- Flux reaches a **steady-state value** ($J_{ss}$).
- Used for: industrial juice clarification, milk processing, biotech harvest.

### 2.3 Membrane Morphology — Symmetric vs Asymmetric

- **Symmetric (isotropic) membranes**: uniform pore structure throughout thickness (e.g., track-etched polycarbonate, sintered metals). Used for Dead-End.
- **Asymmetric (anisotropic) membranes**: thin, dense skin ($\sim$ 1 µm) on top of a thicker porous sublayer ($\sim$ 100–200 µm). The skin does the actual separation; the sublayer provides mechanical strength. Used for Cross-Flow. Examples: polymeric MF membranes in **polyethersulfone (PES), polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), polyamide, regenerated cellulose**.

### 2.4 Membrane Characterization Parameters

> [!NOTE]
> These three quantities are the standard vocabulary of MF and must be memorized for Part A questions.

- **Mean Pore Diameter ($d_p$)** — units µm. Determined by bubble-point, mercury porosimetry, or SEM image analysis.
- **Membrane Permeability ($L_p$)** — the slope of flux vs. TMP for a clean membrane in pure water. Units: **m³/(m²·s·Pa)** or **L/(m²·h·bar)**.
- **Logarithmic Reduction Value (LRV)** — the log₁₀ ratio of feed microbial count to permeate count. For MF, $\text{LRV} \geq 7$ means a sterile-grade membrane.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All equations below are mathematically exact and reproduced without any `vert` symbol to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Symbols & Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $J = \dfrac{1}{A}\dfrac{dV}{dt}$ | $J$ = flux (m/s or L/m²·h); $V$ = permeate volume; $A$ = membrane area | **Permeate flux** definition |
| 2 | $J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu R_{tot}}$ | $\Delta P$ = TMP (Pa); $\mu$ = viscosity (Pa·s); $R_{tot}$ = total resistance (m⁻¹) | **Darcy's Law for Membranes** |
| 3 | $R_{tot} = R_m + R_c + R_f$ | $R_m$ = membrane; $R_c$ = cake; $R_f$ = fouling (all in m⁻¹) | **Resistance-in-Series Model** |
| 4 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_{perm}$ | $P_{in}, P_{out}$ = inlet/outlet feed pressures; $P_{perm}$ = permeate pressure | **Transmembrane Pressure** |
| 5 | $J_w = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu R_m}$ | $J_w$ = pure-water flux of clean membrane | **Clean-water permeability** |
| 6 | $R_c = \alpha \cdot C_b \cdot V / A$ | $\alpha$ = specific cake resistance (m/kg); $C_b$ = bulk concentration | **Cake resistance** (constant pressure) |
| 7 | $J = J_0 \exp(-k_c t)$ | $J_0$ = initial flux; $k_c$ = cake mass-transfer coefficient | **Flux-decay (Hermia-like) model** |
| 8 | $\beta = \dfrac{P_{in} - P_{out}}{P_{in}}$ (typically) | — | Recovery / pressure-drop ratio |
| 9 | $J_{ss} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu(R_m + R_c^{ss})}$ | $J_{ss}$ = steady-state flux in cross-flow | Steady-state design equation |
| 10 | $LRV = -\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{N_{perm}}{N_{feed}}\right)$ | $N$ = microbial count (CFU/mL) | **Logarithmic reduction** |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Dairy industry**: MF of skim milk at 50 °C with 1.4 µm ceramic membranes removes **99.5% of bacteria and 99.9% of somatic cells** while letting native casein micelles (0.02–0.4 µm) pass → produces **bacteria-free native whey** for premium protein concentrates.
- **Beverage industry**: Cross-flow MF of apple juice using **0.2 µm PP hollow fibres** delivers visually clear, shelf-stable product without enzymes or gelatin fining.
- **Biotech**: MF (0.45 µm / 0.22 µm) is the **final sterile filter** before filling of injectables — confirms the LRV ≥ 7 benchmark.
- **Pre-treatment**: MF of seawater at 0.1 µm before RO reduces **SDI₁₅** (Silt Density Index) below the SWRO limit of **< 3**, extending RO membrane life by 2–3 years.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Darcy's Law Applied to a Microfiltration Membrane

We start from the general form of **Darcy's Law** for volumetric flow through a porous medium and adapt it for a thin membrane.

**Starting Equation (Darcy, 1856):**

$$
\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_{tot}}
$$

**Step 1 — Recognize the Definition of Flux.** By definition, volumetric flux $J$ is volumetric flow rate per unit membrane area:

$$
J \;=\; \dfrac{1}{A}\dfrac{dV}{dt}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into Darcy's Equation.** Divide both sides by $A$:

$$
J \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \, R_{tot}}
$$

**Step 3 — Define Total Resistance $R_{tot}$.** During MF, fluid encounters multiple resistive layers in series:

- $R_m$ → intrinsic resistance of the clean membrane
- $R_c$ → resistance of the deposited cake/particulate layer
- $R_f$ → resistance from pore-blocking/internal fouling

Hence:

$$
R_{tot} \;=\; R_m \;+\; R_c \;+\; R_f
$$

**Step 4 — Final Governing Flux Equation:**

$$
\boxed{\,J \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \,(R_m + R_c + R_f)}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Doubling $\Delta P$ doubles $J$ *only if* $R_{tot}$ stays constant. In reality, MF is **strongly flux-limited**: beyond a critical pressure, $R_c$ rises and the linear relation breaks down.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Transmembrane Pressure from Feed-Side Manometry

The TMP is not simply the inlet gauge pressure; it is the **mean effective driving force** averaged along the membrane length.

**Step 1 — Local TMP at any axial position $x$:**

$$
\Delta P(x) \;=\; P(x) - P_{perm}
$$

**Step 2 — Since permeate pressure $P_{perm}$ is essentially atmospheric and constant** ($P_{perm} \approx 0$ gauge), and the feed pressure drops linearly from inlet to outlet due to frictional losses, the **arithmetic mean** approximation is valid for low-recovery cross-flow systems:

$$
\Delta P \;=\; \dfrac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_{perm}
$$

**Step 3 — Example numerical evaluation.** Suppose $P_{in} = 3.0$ bar, $P_{out} = 2.2$ bar, $P_{perm} = 0.1$ bar:

$$
\Delta P \;=\; \dfrac{3.0 + 2.2}{2} - 0.1 \;=\; 2.6 - 0.1 \;=\; 2.5 \text{ bar}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cake Resistance at Constant-Pressure Dead-End Filtration

**Step 1 — Mass Balance on the Cake.** Let $C_b$ be the bulk particle concentration (kg/m³), $V$ the cumulative permeate volume (m³), and $A$ the membrane area (m²). The mass of particles deposited per unit area is:

$$
M \;=\; \dfrac{C_b \, V}{A}
$$

**Step 2 — Empirical Cake-Rheology Relation.** For an incompressible cake of thickness $\delta_c$, Darcy's law applied to the cake alone gives:

$$
R_c \;=\; \dfrac{\delta_c}{k_c}
$$

where $k_c$ is the cake permeability. Empirical observation (Carman-Kozeny) shows that $R_c$ scales linearly with deposited mass through a constant of proportionality $\alpha$ (specific cake resistance, m/kg):

$$
R_c \;=\; \alpha \, M \;=\; \alpha \, \dfrac{C_b \, V}{A}
$$

**Step 3 — Combined Flux Expression:**

$$
\boxed{\,J \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \left(R_m + \alpha \, C_b \, V/A\right)}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Reciprocal-Flux Form (useful for linearized plots):**

$$
\dfrac{1}{J} \;=\; \dfrac{\mu R_m}{\Delta P} \;+\; \dfrac{\mu \alpha C_b}{\Delta P \cdot A}\,V
$$

This is the famous **Rushton plot**: plotting $1/J$ vs. $V$ yields a straight line; the slope gives $\alpha$ and the intercept gives $R_m$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Steady-State Flux in Cross-Flow MF

**Step 1 — Force Balance on a Single Particle.** At steady-state, the convective drag of permeate (towards membrane) is balanced by the **shear-induced back-diffusion** (away from membrane).

$$
\text{Drag} = \text{Lift} + \text{Shear-induced diffusion}
$$

**Step 2 — Shear-Induced Diffusion Model (Zydney & Colton, 1986).** The particle back-transport velocity is given empirically as:

$$
v_b \;=\; 0.07 \, \gamma_0 \!\left(\dfrac{a^4}{l^3}\right)
$$

where $\gamma_0$ is the wall shear rate (s⁻¹), $a$ is particle radius, and $l$ is inter-particle spacing.

**Step 3 — Mass-Transfer Coefficient:**

$$
k_c \;=\; 0.07 \!\left(\dfrac{\dot{\gamma}_w \, a^4}{L}\right)^{1/3}
$$

where $L$ is membrane length. The steady-state flux is then:

$$
\boxed{\,J_{ss} \;=\; k_c \,\ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_w}{C_b}\right)\,}
$$

where $C_w$ is the particle concentration at the membrane wall (often taken as the gel/cake concentration $C_g$).

**Step 4 — Numerical Demonstration.** Let $a = 2$ µm, $\dot{\gamma}_w = 1000$ s⁻¹, $L = 1$ m:

$$
k_c \;=\; 0.07 \!\left(\dfrac{1000 \times (2\times 10^{-6})^4}{1}\right)^{1/3} \;\approx\; 8.4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m/s}
$$

Converting to LMH: $8.4\times 10^{-6} \times 3.6\times 10^6 \approx 30$ **L/(m²·h)** — a typical industrial MF flux.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — MF Performance vs UF/RO

| Parameter | Microfiltration (MF) | Ultrafiltration (UF) | Reverse Osmosis (RO) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pore size** | 0.1–10 µm | 1–100 nm | < 0.5 nm |
| **TMP (bar)** | 0.1–2 | 1–10 | 10–100 |
| **Flux (L/m²·h)** | 500–1500 | 50–300 | 10–30 |
| **Dominant resistance** | Cake ($R_c$) | Gel polarization ($R_g$) | Osmotic pressure ($\pi$) |
| **Boundary layer** Negligible? | No (significant) | Yes (gel-polarized) | Yes (concentration-polarized) |
| **Fouling control** | Backflush, cross-flow | Backpulse, chemical CIP | CIP, antiscalants |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Cross-Flow Microfiltration Plant

The diagram below maps every subsystem, sensor, and feedback loop of a complete industrial MF unit. This is a **functional architecture** rather than a physical drawing, which makes it more useful for control-engineering and P&ID reasoning.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Feed Tank] --> P1[Feed Pump Centrifugal]
    P1 --> H1[Inlet Heat Exchanger]
    H1 --> M1[Membrane Module Hollow Fibre]
    M1 -->|Permeate| PT1[Permeate Tank]
    M1 -->|Retentate| RT1[Retentate Recycle Loop]
    RT1 --> RV1[Retentate Valve Back-pressure]
    RV1 --> P1
    PT1 --> QS1[Quality Sensor Turbidity / Conductivity]
    QS1 --> QC1{Quality Met}
    QC1 -->|Yes| OK1[Permeate Discharge OK]
    QC1 -->|No| RW1[Re-work / Reprocess]
    F1 -->|Control signal| PLC1[PLC Controller]
    H1 -->|Temperature feedback| PLC1
    P1 -->|Flow / Pressure feedback| PLC1
    PLC1 --> P1
    PLC1 --> H1
    PLC1 --> RV1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **F1 → P1 → H1 → M1** is the *forward* process stream (feed to membrane).
- **M1 splits** into two output streams: **permeate** (clean filtrate) and **retentate** (concentrate, partially recycled).
- **QS1 → QC1** is the *quality check* branch.
- **PLC1** is the central *controller* that receives telemetry from temperature, flow, and pressure sensors and modulates pump speed, heater duty, and the retentate back-pressure valve.
- **RV1** is the **transmembrane pressure regulator** — critical because MF is flux-limited.

---

### 4.2 Pore-Scale Schematic — Sieving vs Cross-Flow Shear

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AX[Cross-Flow Shear Layer]
        dir1[Feed flow direction parallel to membrane]
        shear[Shear stress sweeps particles back to bulk]
    end
    subgraph MB[Membrane Skin Layer]
        pore1[Pore 1 µm]
        pore2[Pore 0.45 µm]
        pore3[Pore 0.22 µm]
    end
    subgraph AP[Permeate Channel]
        dir2[Pure solvent flux perpendicular to membrane]
    end
    AX --> MB
    MB --> AP
    AP -.->|Concentration polarization| AX
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram emphasizes the **orthogonality** of the two flow directions — the feed flows *across* while permeate flows *through*. The dashed return arrow at the bottom represents the **concentration polarization boundary layer**, the build-up of rejected species near the membrane wall that limits achievable flux.

---

### 4.3 Decision Tree — Mode Selection in MF

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Feed solids concentration?}
    Q1 -->|Low < 0.1 percent| M1[Use Dead-End MF]
    Q1 -->|High >= 0.1 percent| M2[Use Cross-Flow MF]
    Q1 -->|Very high >= 5 percent| M3[Use Diafiltration or Pre-concentrate]
    M2 --> Q2{Target product in permeate or retentate?}
    Q2 -->|Permeate e.g. clarification| P1[Pore size >> particle size]
    Q2 -->|Retentate e.g. cell harvest| P2[Pore size << particle size]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]**
**Q.** Define microfiltration. State any two applications of MF in the food industry.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> [!NOTE]
> *Microfiltration (MF) is a pressure-driven membrane separation process using porous membranes with pore diameters in the range **0.1 µm to 10 µm** to separate suspended particles, bacteria, and macromolecular aggregates from a fluid stream by size exclusion, under a **transmembrane pressure of 0.1–2 bar**.* **(1.5 marks)**
> 
> *Applications: (i) Cold pasteurization of beer and wine, removing yeast and spoilage bacteria to extend shelf-life; (ii) Clarification of fruit juices such as apple and grape, removing pulp, pectin, and tartrate crystals without heat treatment.* **(1.5 marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Q2]**
**Q.** Compare **dead-end** and **cross-flow** microfiltration modes with respect to flux behaviour and fouling.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> *In **dead-end MF**, the feed flows perpendicular to the membrane, particles accumulate as a cake, and flux decays continuously with time (≈ exponential decay), reaching zero at saturation.* **(1.5 marks)**
> 
> *In **cross-flow MF**, the feed flows parallel to the membrane, shear-induced back-transport limits cake growth, and flux stabilizes at a steady-state value ($J_{ss}$). Cross-flow MF therefore yields higher sustainable throughput for feeds with high solids content.* **(1.5 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module-Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, the examiner provides **internal choice** within a module. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Both versions cover identical COs and Bloom's levels, but differ in technical emphasis.

---

#### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — *Theory-Derivation Emphasis*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the **cross-flow microfiltration** process. Discuss the role of transmembrane pressure (TMP) and the **resistance-in-series model** in determining the steady-state permeate flux. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ceramic MF membrane (mean pore diameter 0.2 µm, area 0.5 m²) is used to clarify orange juice. The clean membrane has resistance $R_m = 5 \times 10^{10}$ m⁻¹. Juice viscosity $\mu = 1.1 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, TMP = 1.5 bar. After 30 min of operation at constant TMP, the cumulative permeate volume is 35 L and the cake resistance $R_c = 8 \times 10^{11}$ m⁻¹. Assuming $R_f = 0$:
   (i) Calculate the **average flux** during the 30 min.
   (ii) Calculate the **specific cake resistance** $\alpha$, given the bulk juice solids concentration $C_b = 4$ kg/m³.
   (iii) Comment on the practical significance of your results. **(7 marks)**

**Complete Model Solution:**

**(a) Theoretical Part (7 marks)**

Cross-flow microfiltration is depicted in the functional diagram already shown in Section 4.1. The key elements are:

1. **Feed introduction** by a centrifugal pump (1) raises the feed to operating pressure.
2. The feed flows **tangentially** across the membrane surface, with a fraction $J$ passing through as permeate and the rest continuing as retentate.
3. **TMP** is defined as $\Delta P = \dfrac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_{perm}$.
4. **Steady-state flux** is governed by the resistance-in-series model:

$$
J_{ss} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu\,(R_m + R_c + R_f)}
$$

5. $R_m$ is constant (membrane property); $R_c$ depends on cake thickness and porosity; $R_f$ accounts for irreversible adsorption into pores.
6. Increasing $\Delta P$ linearly increases $J$ **until** the *critical flux* is exceeded, beyond which $R_c$ grows rapidly and flux plateaus or declines.
7. The **flux plateau** is the engineering design point — operating above it wastes pumping energy without throughput gain.

**(Marks: Definition 1 + Diagram 2 + Equation 1 + Discussion 3 = 7 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Part (7 marks)**

**(i) Average Flux**

$$
J_{avg} \;=\; \dfrac{V}{A \cdot t} \;=\; \dfrac{0.035 \text{ m}^3}{0.5 \text{ m}^2 \times 1800 \text{ s}} \;=\; \dfrac{0.035}{900}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,J_{avg} \;\approx\; 3.89 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s} \;\approx\; 140 \text{ L/(m²·h)}\,}
$$

**(ii) Specific Cake Resistance**

Using $R_c = \alpha \dfrac{C_b \, V}{A}$:

$$
\alpha \;=\; \dfrac{R_c \cdot A}{C_b \, V} \;=\; \dfrac{8\times 10^{11} \times 0.5}{4 \times 0.035}
$$

$$
\alpha \;=\; \dfrac{4 \times 10^{11}}{0.14} \;\approx\; \boxed{\,2.86 \times 10^{12} \text{ m/kg}\,}
$$

**(iii) Practical Significance**

- The flux of 140 LMH is consistent with industrial cross-flow MF of viscous fruit juices.
- The very high $\alpha$ indicates a **dense, low-permeability cake** — typical of pectin-rich juices. This implies that **enzyme pretreatment** (e.g., pectinase) before MF could dramatically reduce $R_c$ and double or triple $J_{avg}$.
- Operating at TMP > 1.5 bar would be counterproductive: $R_c$ would rise via compression and the flux would decline.

**(Marks: (i) 2 + (ii) 2 + (iii) 3 = 7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — *Application-Fouling Emphasis*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**(a)** Explain the **mechanisms of membrane fouling** in microfiltration. Differentiate between **cake formation**, **pore blocking**, and **adsorption fouling**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dairy plant uses 0.8 µm tubular ceramic MF membranes to remove bacteria from skim milk at 50 °C. Operating data:
   - Membrane area = 12 m²
   - Initial clean-water flux $J_0 = 2200$ L/m²·h at TMP = 0.5 bar
   - After 4 h of milk processing, the flux has decayed to $J_4 = 700$ L/m²·h at the same TMP
   - Viscosity of milk at 50 °C, $\mu = 0.9$ mPa·s

Calculate:
   (i) The **membrane resistance** $R_m$ from clean-water data.
   (ii) The **total resistance** $R_{tot}$ after 4 h.
   (iii) The **percentage contribution** of fouling resistance $R_f$ to $R_{tot}$ after 4 h.
   (iv) State **two engineering strategies** to mitigate this fouling in industrial practice. **(7 marks)**

**Complete Model Solution:**

**(a) Theoretical Part (7 marks)**

Membrane fouling in MF arises from four interacting mechanisms:

1. **Cake formation** — particles larger than $d_p$ accumulate on the surface as a porous layer whose thickness grows with time; resistance scales as $R_c = \alpha C_b V/A$.
2. **Pore blocking (constriction)** — particles of similar size to $d_p$ lodge at pore entrances, partially sealing them; reduces effective membrane area.
3. **Adsorption fouling** — macromolecules (proteins, polysaccharides) deposit on pore walls through hydrophobic/electrostatic interactions; this is often **irreversible** without chemical cleaning.
4. **Biofouling** — microbial adhesion and EPS secretion (especially relevant in dairy MF).

Differentiation:

| Mechanism | Reversibility | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Cake | Reversible | Cross-flow, backflush |
| Pore blocking | Partly reversible | Backflush, sizing choice |
| Adsorption | Largely irreversible | CIP, surface modification |

**(Marks: 1 + 1.5 + 1.5 + 3 = 7 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Part (7 marks)**

**(i) Clean membrane resistance**

Convert units carefully:
$J_0 = 2200$ L/m²·h $= \dfrac{2200 \times 10^{-3}}{3600}$ m/s $= 6.11 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s.

TMP $= 0.5$ bar $= 5\times 10^4$ Pa.

$$
R_m \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu J_0} \;=\; \dfrac{5 \times 10^4}{0.9\times 10^{-3} \times 6.11 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,R_m \;\approx\; 9.09 \times 10^{10} \text{ m}^{-1}\,}
$$

**(ii) Total resistance after 4 h**

$J_4 = 700$ L/m²·h $= 1.94 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s.

$$
R_{tot} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu J_4} \;=\; \dfrac{5 \times 10^4}{0.9\times 10^{-3} \times 1.94 \times 10^{-4}} \;\approx\; \boxed{\,2.86 \times 10^{11} \text{ m}^{-1}\,}
$$

**(iii) Fouling resistance contribution**

$$
R_f \;=\; R_{tot} - R_m \;=\; 2.86 \times 10^{11} - 0.91 \times 10^{11} \;=\; 1.95 \times 10^{11} \text{ m}^{-1}
$$

$$
\% \text{ contribution of } R_f \;=\; \dfrac{1.95 \times 10^{11}}{2.86 \times 10^{11}} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\approx 68\%\,}
$$

**(iv) Mitigation strategies (any two):**
1. **Periodic backflushing** (reverse flow of permeate for 5–10 s every 10–15 min) — dislodges the cake layer.
2. **Increasing cross-flow velocity** to raise wall shear stress and limit cake thickness.
3. **Chemical Cleaning-In-Place (CIP)** with alkaline (NaOH, 60 °C) and acidic (HNO₃) sequences at the end of every processing run.
4. **Operating at the critical flux** (≤ $J_{crit}$) using the *flux-stepping method* to avoid irreversible compaction.

**(Marks: (i) 1.5 + (ii) 1.5 + (iii) 1.5 + (iv) 2.5 = 7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Traps in MF Numericals (KTU Examiner Observations)**
> 
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors** — Mixing LMH with m/s. The conversion is $1$ L/m²·h $= 2.778 \times 10^{-7}$ m/s. Many students lose 1–2 marks here.
> 
> 2. **Confusing $R_m$ and $R_f$** — A *clean membrane* has only $R_m$. Any measurement after feed exposure contains $R_m + R_c + R_f$. The clean-water baseline must be measured with **pure water**, not with feed.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting the logarithmic mean TMP** — Although many textbooks use the arithmetic mean approximation, the rigorous expression is $\Delta P = \dfrac{P_{in}-P_{out}}{\ln(P_{in}/P_{out})}$. Examiners accept arithmetic mean but you **must show the formula**.
> 
> 4. **Neglecting permeate pressure** — For industrial cross-flow systems $P_{perm} \approx 0$, but in lab setups with restrictive permeate tubing it can be significant. Always write it.
> 
> 5. **Saying "MF removes all bacteria"** — Wrong. MF only retains cells larger than $d_p$. *Mycoplasma* (0.2 µm) can pass through a 0.45 µm membrane. Always state "MF removes vegetative bacteria and yeast but not all viruses".
> 
> 6. **Cross-flow and dead-end diagrams missing arrows** — In KTU's block-diagram questions, missing flow direction arrows lose 1 full mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist — read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- **Definition:** MF is a *pressure-driven, size-exclusion, non-thermal* membrane process with $d_p \in [0.1, 10]$ µm and TMP $\in [0.1, 2]$ bar.
- **Membrane hierarchy:** MF > UF > NF > RO (decreasing pore size, increasing pressure).
- **Modes:** Dead-End (perpendicular flow, cake-limited, batch) vs Cross-Flow (tangential flow, shear-limited, continuous).
- **Governing equation (must be memorized):** $J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu (R_m + R_c + R_f)}$
- **TMP formula:** $\Delta P = \dfrac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_{perm}$
- **Cake-resistance model:** $R_c = \alpha \dfrac{C_b V}{A}$
- **Steady-state cross-flow flux:** $J_{ss} = k_c \ln(C_w / C_b)$
- **Rushton plot:** $\dfrac{1}{J}$ vs. $V$ is linear; slope yields $\alpha$, intercept yields $R_m$.
- **Flux-decay model:** $J = J_0 \exp(-k_c t)$
- **Pure-water permeability test:** Use this to find $R_m$ — critical for separating $R_f$ from $R_m$ in subsequent experiments.
- **LRV:** $-\log_{10}(N_{perm}/N_{feed})$; sterile MF has LRV $\geq 7$.
- **Industrial foods where MF dominates:** Apple juice, beer, wine, milk (cold pasteurization), vinegar, fermentation broth clarification.
- **Critical flux concept:** Operating above $J_{crit}$ gives negligible flux gain and accelerated fouling — always design at or below.
- **Cleaning hierarchy:** Physical (backflush, backpulse) → Mechanical (air-scouring) → Chemical CIP (alkali → acid → enzyme).
- **Membrane materials to remember:** **PES, PVDF, polyamide, regenerated cellulose** (polymeric); **Al₂O₃, ZrO₂, TiO₂** (ceramic).
- **Key unit conversions to memorize cold:** 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa; 1 LMH = $2.778 \times 10^{-7}$ m/s; 1 µm = $10^{-6}$ m.
- **CO Mapping (KTU 2024):** This topic primarily addresses **CO4 — Apply principles of membrane-based non-thermal processes for food preservation.**
- **Bloom's level distribution:** Part A tests *Remember*/*Understand*; Part B tests *Apply*/*Analyze*/*Evaluate*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ultrafiltration (UF)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT631 - NON-THERMAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ultrafiltration (UF)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ULTRAFILTRATION (UF) – Module 4 | Section 1: Foundation & Intuition

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Ultrafiltration (UF)** is a **pressure-driven membrane separation process** in which a semi-permeable membrane with defined pore size is used to **concentrate, purify, or fractionate** macromolecular species (proteins, polysaccharides, colloids, emulsified fats, and microbial cells) from a liquid feed, under a **transmembrane pressure (TMP) typically between 1 and 10 bar**.

The defining feature of UF, in the spectrum of pressure-driven membrane processes, is that the **separation mechanism is primarily sieving (size exclusion)** at the membrane surface, supplemented by secondary interactions such as adsorption and charge effects. The membrane's **Molecular Weight Cut-Off (MWCO)** — expressed in **Daltons (Da)** — is the universal design parameter.

### Position of UF in the Membrane Process Hierarchy

| Process | Driving Force | Pore / Cut-off | Typical TMP | Retained Species |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Microfiltration (MF)** | Pressure (0.1–2 bar) | 0.1 – 10 μm | Low | Suspended solids, bacteria, yeast |
| **Ultrafiltration (UF)** | Pressure (1–10 bar) | 1 – 100 nm (**1 kDa – 500 kDa**) | Moderate | Proteins, colloids, viruses, emulsions |
| **Nanofiltration (NF)** | Pressure (10–40 bar) | < 2 nm (**200 – 1000 Da**) | High | Divalent salts, lactose, small organics |
| **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** | Pressure (15–80 bar) | Non-porous | Very High | Monovalent ions, water |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Plain-English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **"UF is like a microscopic colander — but for molecules, not pasta."**

Imagine pouring a bucket of milk through a fine kitchen sieve. The sieve's mesh is so fine that it catches cheese curds (large casein micelles) but lets the watery **whey** slip through, along with all dissolved salts, sugars (lactose), and tiny water molecules. Now scale that sieve down a million times — the holes are now so small that they trap **proteins, fat globules, and microorganisms**, while allowing water, minerals, and small sugars to pass.

- The bucket of milk = **Feed** ($F$)
- The fine sieve = **UF Membrane** (with a precise MWCO)
- What passes through = **Permeate** ($P$) — clarified, low-molecular-weight stream
- What stays behind in the sieve = **Retentate** ($R$) — concentrated, high-molecular-weight stream

That is essentially the entire logic of ultrafiltration. The "fineness" of the sieve is called the **MWCO**, and the rate at which liquid passes through is the **Permeate Flux ($J$)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Unlike Microfiltration (which removes visible particles) and Reverse Osmosis (which removes ions), UF is the *Goldilocks zone* for processing **biological macromolecules** — it is the workhorse technology of the modern dairy, beverage, and bioproduct industries.

---

## 1.3 Key Operational Metrics (Standard KTU Board Definitions)

- **Molecular Weight Cut-Off (MWCO):** The molecular weight of a solute (typically a globular protein such as **cytochrome-c, BSA, or dextran**) that is **90% rejected** by the membrane. Expressed in **Daltons (Da)**.
- **Permeate Flux ($J$):** Volumetric flow rate of permeate per unit membrane area. Units: **L·m⁻²·h⁻¹ (LMH)** or **m³·m⁻²·s⁻¹**.
- **Rejection Coefficient ($R$):** Fractional measure of how well the membrane retains a solute. Dimensionless, between 0 and 1.
- **Transmembrane Pressure (TMP or $\Delta P$):** Net pressure driving force pushing permeate through the membrane. Units: **bar** or **Pa**.
- **Concentration Polarization:** Build-up of retained solute at the membrane–feed interface, which depresses flux and alters effective selectivity.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sieving Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The UF separation process can be broken down into **five sequential physical events**:

1. **Feed Introduction** — The feed stream is delivered to the membrane module at a controlled cross-flow velocity ($v$).
2. **Solvent Convection** — Under TMP, the solvent (typically water) and all species smaller than the membrane pores are **convected** through the membrane.
3. **Size Exclusion at Pore Mouth** — Solutes whose hydrodynamic diameter exceeds the membrane pore size are physically **rejected** at the surface.
4. **Permeate Collection** — The clarified stream (permeate) exits on the low-pressure side.
5. **Retentate Recycle / Discharge** — The concentrated stream is either recycled for further concentration or discharged.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept:** The selectivity of a UF membrane is governed by the **ratio of solute diameter to pore diameter** — not just by MWCO. Two proteins of identical molecular weight can have very different rejection profiles if one is **globular** and the other is **rod-shaped or highly hydrated**.

---

## 2.2 Permeate Flux — The Core Transport Equation

The volumetric permeate flux across a UF membrane is universally described by **Darcy's Law for Membranes**:

$$J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_t}$$

Where:

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $J$ | Permeate flux | $\text{L} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$ (LMH) |
| $\Delta P$ | Transmembrane pressure (TMP) | Pa or bar |
| $\mu$ | Dynamic viscosity of permeate | Pa·s |
| $R_t$ | Total hydraulic resistance | m⁻¹ |

---

## 2.3 The Resistance-in-Series Model (Critical KTU Concept)

The total resistance $R_t$ is the sum of **three independent resistance terms** acting in series:

$$R_t = R_m + R_{cp} + R_f$$

| Resistance | Origin | Reversibility |
|---|---|---|
| $R_m$ | Intrinsic resistance of the clean membrane | Constant (cleaning can recover it) |
| $R_{cp}$ | Concentration polarization gel layer at membrane surface | Reversible (reducible by cross-flow) |
| $R_f$ | Fouling — pore plugging, irreversible adsorption | Largely **irreversible** |

The expanded flux equation therefore becomes:

$$J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \, (R_m + R_{cp} + R_f)}$$

---

## 2.4 Rejection Coefficient (Observed vs. Intrinsic)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Observed Rejection ($R_{obs}$)** is what experiments measure, but it is *depressed* by concentration polarization. The **Intrinsic Rejection ($R_{int}$)** is the true membrane property.

$$R_{obs} = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_b}$$

$$R_{int} = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_m}$$

Where:
- $C_p$ = solute concentration in permeate
- $C_b$ = solute concentration in bulk feed
- $C_m$ = solute concentration at membrane surface (always $C_m \geq C_b$)

---

## 2.5 Concentration Polarization — Gel Layer Model (Critical KTU Derivation Topic)

Under steady-state operation, the convective transport of solute to the membrane equals the sum of solute passage through the membrane and back-diffusion from the membrane surface into the bulk:

$$J \cdot C_b = J \cdot C_p + D \cdot \frac{dC}{dx}$$

Integrating across the boundary layer of thickness $\delta$:

$$J = k \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{C_m - C_p}{C_b - C_p}\right)$$

Where $k = D / \delta$ is the **mass transfer coefficient**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** For high-rejection UF (where $R \to 1$, i.e., $C_p \to 0$), the equation collapses to $J = k \cdot \ln(C_m / C_b)$. Students forget to set $C_p = 0$ in the denominator and lose marks.

At the **limiting flux ($J_{lim}$)**, $C_m$ reaches the **gel concentration ($C_g$)** — a maximum physically retainable solute concentration:

$$J_{lim} = k \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{C_g}{C_b}\right)$$

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the single most important reference for solving any UF numerical problem in KTU examinations. Memorize it.

| # | Formula | Meaning | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $J = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_t}$ | Darcy flux | All UF flux problems |
| 2 | $R_t = R_m + R_{cp} + R_f$ | Resistance-in-series | Fouling / polarization analysis |
| 3 | $R_{obs} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_b}$ | Observed rejection | Single-pass experiments |
| 4 | $R_{int} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_m}$ | Intrinsic rejection | Membrane characterization |
| 5 | $J = k \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_m - C_p}{C_b - C_p}\right)$ | Film theory (general) | Concentration polarization |
| 6 | $J_{lim} = k \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_g}{C_b}\right)$ | Limiting flux ($R \to 1$) | High-rejection UF |
| 7 | $\sigma = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_{ret}}$ | Sieving coefficient | Alternative to rejection |
| 8 | $R_{recovery} = \dfrac{J_{cleaned}}{J_{initial}} \times 100\%$ | Flux recovery | Cleaning efficiency |
| 9 | $V_c = J_{lim} \cdot A \cdot t$ | Volume of permeate | Batch process design |
| 10 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{P_{in} + P_{out}}{2} - P_p$ | TMP definition | Module pressure drop |

**Units Conversion Reminder:**
- $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 100 \text{ kPa}$
- $1 \text{ LMH} = \dfrac{1 \text{ L}}{1 \text{ m}^2 \cdot 1 \text{ h}} = 2.778 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m/s}$
- **Daltons (Da)** is g/mol; **1 kDa = 1000 Da**

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

UF is the **backbone technology** of food bioprocessing because it is **non-thermal** (preserves heat-sensitive nutrients, vitamins, and bioactives). Major KTU-relevant applications:

- **Dairy:** Whey protein concentration (WPC 35, WPC 80), milk protein standardization, lactose reduction, cheese pre-concentration.
- **Beverages:** Apple, grape, and pomegranate juice clarification (replaces diatomaceous earth filtration).
- **Biotech:** Enzyme recovery, antibiotic purification, vaccine concentration.
- **Water:** Potable water production, wastewater protein recovery.
- **Oils & Fats:** Degumming, deacidification of vegetable oils.
- **Fermentation Broths:** Cell harvesting and broth clarification.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — Resistance-in-Series Flux Equation (Starting from Darcy's Law)

> [!NOTE]
> The following is the full KTU-board derivation. Every line is shown explicitly with no algebraic leaps.

**Step 1 — Start with Darcy's Law for porous media flow:**

$$v = -\frac{K}{\mu} \cdot \frac{dP}{dx}$$

**Step 2 — For a thin membrane of thickness $\Delta x$, the pressure drop is $dP = \Delta P$ over $dx = L$. Substitute:**

$$v = \frac{K \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that permeate flux $J$ is volumetric flow per unit area, so $J = v$. Define the intrinsic membrane resistance as $R_m = L / K$:**

$$J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_m}$$

**Step 4 — When the membrane operates in a process stream, additional resistance layers deposit on the surface: a concentration polarization layer ($R_{cp}$) and a fouling layer ($R_f$). Because these are physical layers in series, their resistances add linearly:**

$$R_t = R_m + R_{cp} + R_f$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the flux equation to obtain the working form:**

$$J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \, (R_m + R_{cp} + R_f)}$$

**Step 6 — Solving for the unknown resistance (e.g., $R_f$ after measuring $J$ experimentally) is the basis of every UF characterization lab:**

$$R_f = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot J} - R_m - R_{cp}$$

**Numerical example for clarity:**

> A UF membrane has $R_m = 1.2 \times 10^{12} \text{ m}^{-1}$. During processing, $R_{cp} = 0.4 \times 10^{12} \text{ m}^{-1}$ and $R_f = 0.8 \times 10^{12} \text{ m}^{-1}$. The TMP is 2 bar = $2 \times 10^5$ Pa, and $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. Calculate $J$.

$$J = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{(1.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times (1.2 + 0.4 + 0.8) \times 10^{12}}$$

$$J = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{2.4 \times 10^{9}} = 8.33 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m/s}$$

Converting to LMH: $J = 8.33 \times 10^{-5} \times 3.6 \times 10^6 = 300 \text{ LMH}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Concentration Polarization (Film Theory)

**Step 1 — Write the steady-state mass balance on the solute at the membrane surface. Solute convected to the surface = solute permeating + solute diffusing back:**

$$J \cdot C_x = J \cdot C_p + \left(-D \cdot \frac{dC}{dx}\right)$$

Where the negative sign on the diffusion term indicates flux back into the bulk.

**Step 2 — Rearrange:**

$$J \cdot (C_x - C_p) = D \cdot \frac{dC}{dx}$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables and integrate from $x = 0$ (membrane surface, $C = C_m$) to $x = \delta$ (edge of boundary layer, $C = C_b$):**

$$J \int_{0}^{\delta} dx = D \int_{C_m}^{C_b} \frac{dC}{C - C_p}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$J \cdot \delta = D \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{C_b - C_p}{C_m - C_p}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Define the mass transfer coefficient as $k = D / \delta$:**

$$J = k \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{C_m - C_p}{C_b - C_p}\right)$$

**Step 6 — For high-rejection UF ($R \to 1$, $C_p \to 0$):**

$$J = k \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{C_m}{C_b}\right)$$

**Step 7 — At the limiting flux, $C_m$ saturates at $C_g$ (gel concentration):**

$$J_{lim} = k \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{C_g}{C_b}\right)$$

**Worked numerical example:**

> A UF membrane processes a protein solution. The bulk concentration is $C_b = 10$ kg/m³, the gel concentration is $C_g = 300$ kg/m³, and the mass transfer coefficient is $k = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s. Calculate the limiting flux.

$$J_{lim} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \cdot \ln(300/10)$$

$$J_{lim} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \cdot \ln(30) = 2 \times 10^{-5} \cdot 3.401 = 6.80 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s}$$

Convert to LMH: $J_{lim} = 6.80 \times 10^{-5} \times 3.6 \times 10^6 = 244.9 \text{ LMH}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Intrinsic vs Observed Rejection Linkage

**Step 1 — Start with definitions:**

$$R_{obs} = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_b} \quad ; \quad R_{int} = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_m}$$

**Step 2 — Solve each for $C_p$:**

$$C_p = C_b \,(1 - R_{obs}) = C_m \,(1 - R_{int})$$

**Step 3 — Equate and rearrange:**

$$C_m = C_b \cdot \frac{1 - R_{obs}}{1 - R_{int}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $C_m$ into the film-theory expression to obtain the relationship linking observable flux, rejection, and intrinsic membrane properties:**

$$J = k \cdot \ln\!\left[\frac{C_b \,(1 - R_{obs}) / (1 - R_{int}) - C_p}{C_b - C_p}\right]$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Insight:** This is the equation from which **$C_m$ is never directly measured**. It must always be inferred from measurable quantities ($C_b$, $C_p$, $J$, $k$). Students who write $C_m = C_b$ without justification lose full marks.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Influence of Operating Variables on UF Performance

| Operating Variable | Effect on $J$ | Effect on Rejection $R$ | Practical Engineering Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ TMP ($\Delta P$) | ↑ (linear in clean regime) | ↓ slightly at high TMP | Operate below the **limiting flux** $J_{lim}$ |
| ↑ Cross-flow velocity ($v$) | ↑ (reduces $R_{cp}$) | ↑ (slightly) | Use turbulent flow (Re > 4000) |
| ↑ Feed concentration ($C_b$) | ↓ (increases $R_{cp}$) | ↑ | Dilute feed or use diafiltration |
| ↑ Temperature ($T$) | ↑ (lowers $\mu$) | ↓ slightly | Operate at $45$–$55$°C (also reduces viscosity) |
| ↑ MWCO of membrane | ↑ | ↓ | Select MWCO based on target protein size |
| ↑ Membrane fouling | ↓↓ | ↑ then erratic | CIP (Clean-in-Place) protocols |
| ↑ Feed viscosity ($\mu$) | ↓ | Variable | Pre-heat, depectinize (for juices) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — UF Process Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    feed[FEED TANK] --> pump1[FEED PUMP]
    pump1 --> heater[HEAT EXCHANGER]
    heater --> mod1[UF MODULE INLET]
    mod1 --> perm1[PERMEATE OUTLET]
    mod1 --> ret1[RETENTATE OUTLET]
    perm1 --> permtank[PERMEATE STORAGE TANK]
    ret1 --> retflow{RETENTATE PATH}
    retflow -->|Recycle| pump1
    retflow -->|Discharge| retdrain[CONCENTRATE COLLECTION]
    permtank --> down[Downstream Processing]
    retdrain --> down
    classDef ioNode fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    classDef processNode fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    classDef storageNode fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px
    class feed,permtank,retdrain ioNode
    class pump1,heater,mod1 processNode
    class retflow,down storageNode
```

**Reading Guide:** The feed is pressurized, optionally tempered, and sent to the UF module. The module splits the stream into **permeate** (low-MW, passes through) and **retentate** (high-MW, retained). Retentate is partly recycled to maximize conversion and partly sent to downstream concentration.

---

## 4.2 Cross-Sectional Schematic — Sieving at the Membrane Surface

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BULK[FEED SIDE - BULK LAYER]
        bulkmol[Feed Solute Molecules]
        bulksolv[Water and Small Molecules]
    end
    subgraph POLAR[BOUNDARY LAYER]
        polmol[Concentrated Solute Layer]
        polnote[Concentration Polarization]
    end
    subgraph MEMB[MEMBRANE STRUCTURE]
        topmat[Top Dense Skin MWCO Defined]
        submat[Porous Sublayer Support]
    end
    subgraph PERM[PERMEATE SIDE]
        permflow[Permeate Stream Out]
    end
    BULK --> POLAR
    POLAR --> MEMB
    MEMB --> PERM
    bulkmol -.Rejected.-> polmol
    bulksolv --> permflow
    permnote[Permeate flux J]
    POLAR --> permnote
    classDef sideBlock fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px
    classDef layerBlock fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#f57c00,stroke-width:2px
    classDef memBlock fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px
    classDef permBlock fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px
    class BULK,PERM sideBlock
    class POLAR layerBlock
    class MEMB memBlock
    class permflow permBlock
```

**Reading Guide:** The diagram illustrates the four key zones — bulk feed, polarized boundary layer, asymmetric membrane (dense skin + porous sublayer), and permeate side. The dense skin is where size exclusion occurs; the sublayer is purely mechanical support.

---

## 4.3 Module Configuration Comparison (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HF[HOLLOW FIBER]
        hff[Thousands of hollow fibers]
        hfin[Feed flows inside fibers]
        hfout[Permeate collects in shell]
    end
    subgraph SW[SPIRAL WOUND]
        swf[Two membrane sheets glued]
        swin[Feed flows axially]
        swout[Permeate spirals to center tube]
    end
    subgraph PF[PLATE AND FRAME]
        pff[Flat membranes on plates]
        pfin[Feed flows between plates]
        pfout[Permeate exits through ports]
    end
    subgraph TU[TUBULAR]
        tuf[Single large diameter tubes]
        tuin[Feed flows through tube]
        tuout[Permeate exits through porous wall]
    end
    HF --> SW
    SW --> PF
    PF --> TU
    classDef cfgNode fill:#f1f8e9,stroke:#558b2f,stroke-width:2px
    class HF,SW,PF,TU cfgNode
```

**Module Selection Criteria (KTU Board Relevance):**

| Configuration | Packing Density (m²/m³) | Fouling Resistance | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hollow Fiber** | Very High (up to 10,000) | Low | Clear feeds, water |
| **Spiral Wound** | High (500–1000) | Moderate | Whey, RO/UF of clean streams |
| **Plate & Frame** | Low (100–400) | High | Viscous / fouling feeds (juices) |
| **Tubular** | Very Low (50–200) | Very High | Heavy fouling (fermentation broth) |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**

**Q1. Define Molecular Weight Cut-Off (MWCO) of an ultrafiltration membrane. How is it experimentally determined?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Molecular Weight Cut-Off (MWCO)** of a UF membrane is defined as the molecular weight of a reference solute (typically a globular protein such as **bovine serum albumin, cytochrome-c, or a dextran standard**) that is rejected by the membrane to an extent of **90%** under standardized test conditions. It is determined experimentally by performing a **solute rejection test** with a series of reference solutes of known molecular weight, plotting the **rejection coefficient ($R$) vs. molecular weight** on a semi-log plot, and reading off the MW corresponding to $R = 0.90$. MWCO is reported in **Daltons (Da)**. [3 marks]

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Differentiate between concentration polarization and membrane fouling in ultrafiltration. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Concentration Polarization | Membrane Fouling |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Reversible accumulation of rejected solute at the membrane surface | Irreversible deposition of solute within pores or on surface |
| **Reversibility** | Fully reversible by reducing TMP or increasing cross-flow | Partially or fully irreversible |
| **Location** | Bulk-side boundary layer | Pore interior and/or surface |
| **Time scale** | Seconds to minutes | Hours to days |
| **Mitigation** | Higher cross-flow velocity, turbulence promoters | CIP, backflushing, surface modification |
| **Impact on $J$** | Reversible decline | Permanent decline |

[3 marks]

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, ESE Module 4, Question 8]**

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**Q8(a)** With the help of the **resistance-in-series model**, derive the expression for the steady-state permeate flux in ultrafiltration. Clearly define each term. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

The permeate flux through a UF membrane under the influence of transmembrane pressure is governed by **Darcy's Law for porous media**. The derivation proceeds in the following steps:

**Step 1:** Darcy's Law for volumetric flux through a porous medium:

$$v = -\frac{K}{\mu} \cdot \frac{dP}{dx}$$

where $K$ is the permeability of the porous medium and $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity of the permeating fluid.

**Step 2:** For a thin membrane of thickness $L$ across which the total pressure drop is $\Delta P$ (the transmembrane pressure):

$$J = v = \frac{K \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$$

**Step 3:** Defining the **intrinsic membrane resistance** $R_m = L / K$:

$$J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_m}$$

**Step 4:** During operation, two additional resistance layers develop on the membrane: (i) the **concentration polarization layer** with resistance $R_{cp}$, formed by reversible accumulation of retained solute, and (ii) the **fouling layer** with resistance $R_f$, formed by irreversible deposition of solute within and on the membrane.

**Step 5:** Since these resistances act **in series**, the total resistance is their arithmetic sum:

$$R_t = R_m + R_{cp} + R_f$$

**Step 6:** Substituting into the flux expression:

$$\boxed{J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \, (R_m + R_{cp} + R_f)}}$$

where:
- $J$ = permeate flux (LMH or m/s)
- $\Delta P$ = transmembrane pressure (Pa or bar)
- $\mu$ = permeate viscosity (Pa·s)
- $R_m$ = clean membrane resistance (m⁻¹)
- $R_{cp}$ = concentration polarization resistance (m⁻¹)
- $R_f$ = fouling resistance (m⁻¹) [7 marks]

---

**Q8(b)** A UF module processes skim milk at TMP = 3 bar, viscosity = $1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s. The following resistances are measured: $R_m = 1.5 \times 10^{12}$ m⁻¹, $R_{cp} = 0.6 \times 10^{12}$ m⁻¹, $R_f = 0.9 \times 10^{12}$ m⁻¹. Calculate: (i) the steady-state permeate flux $J$ in LMH, and (ii) the percentage of total resistance contributed by the fouling layer. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Part (i) — Calculation of $J$:**

Total resistance:
$$R_t = R_m + R_{cp} + R_f$$
$$R_t = (1.5 + 0.6 + 0.9) \times 10^{12} = 3.0 \times 10^{12} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

TMP conversion: $\Delta P = 3 \text{ bar} = 3 \times 10^5$ Pa.

Applying the resistance-in-series flux equation:

$$J = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_t} = \frac{3 \times 10^5}{(1.2 \times 10^{-3}) \times (3.0 \times 10^{12})}$$

$$J = \frac{3 \times 10^5}{3.6 \times 10^{9}} = 8.33 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s}$$

Converting to LMH:
$$J = 8.33 \times 10^{-5} \times 3.6 \times 10^6 = 300 \text{ LMH}$$

**Part (ii) — Percentage fouling contribution:**

$$\% R_f = \frac{R_f}{R_t} \times 100 = \frac{0.9 \times 10^{12}}{3.0 \times 10^{12}} \times 100 = 30\%$$

**Answer:** $J = 300$ LMH, and fouling accounts for **30%** of the total resistance — indicating a significant cleaning need. [7 marks]

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, ESE Module 4, Question 9]**

**Q9(a)** Explain the **gel polarization model** for ultrafiltration. Derive the limiting flux equation and discuss how operating parameters influence the limiting flux. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Statement of the model:**
The gel polarization model postulates that when a high-rejection UF membrane processes a macromolecular solution, the rejected solute accumulates at the membrane surface, forming a **concentration gradient** across a thin boundary layer of thickness $\delta$. The concentration at the membrane surface ($C_m$) is always greater than the bulk concentration ($C_b$). As TMP is increased, $C_m$ rises until it saturates at a maximum physically retainable value called the **gel concentration ($C_g$)**. Beyond this point, additional TMP cannot increase flux — the membrane is operating at its **limiting flux ($J_{lim}$)**.

**Step 2 — Derivation of the limiting flux equation:**

Steady-state mass balance:
$$J \cdot C_b = J \cdot C_p - D \cdot \frac{dC}{dx}$$

Integrating across the boundary layer (assuming $C_p \to 0$ for high rejection):
$$J_{lim} = k \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{C_g}{C_b}\right)$$

where $k = D / \delta$ is the mass transfer coefficient.

**Step 3 — Influence of operating parameters:**

| Parameter | Effect on $J_{lim}$ | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| ↑ Cross-flow velocity | ↑ | Reduces $\delta$ → increases $k$ |
| ↑ Temperature | ↑ | Increases $D$ and decreases $\mu$ → increases $k$ |
| ↑ Feed concentration $C_b$ | ↓ | Higher $C_b$ reduces the ln ratio |
| ↑ Membrane MWCO | ↓ (for target solute) | Solute may enter pores |

[7 marks]

---

**Q9(b)** A UF membrane is used to concentrate a protein solution. The gel concentration of the protein is $C_g = 250$ kg/m³, the bulk feed concentration is $C_b = 8$ kg/m³, and the mass transfer coefficient is $k = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}$ m/s. If the membrane area is $A = 4$ m², calculate: (i) the limiting flux in LMH, and (ii) the volume of permeate produced in 2 hours. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Part (i) — Limiting flux:**

$$J_{lim} = k \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{C_g}{C_b}\right)$$

$$J_{lim} = 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \times \ln\!\left(\frac{250}{8}\right)$$

$$J_{lim} = 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \times \ln(31.25)$$

$$J_{lim} = 1.8 \times 10^{-5} \times 3.442 = 6.20 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s}$$

Converting to LMH:
$$J_{lim} = 6.20 \times 10^{-5} \times 3.6 \times 10^6 = 223.1 \text{ LMH}$$

**Part (ii) — Volume of permeate in 2 hours:**

$$V_p = J_{lim} \times A \times t = 6.20 \times 10^{-5} \times 4 \times 7200$$

$$V_p = 1.7856 \text{ m}^3 = 1785.6 \text{ L}$$

**Answer:** $J_{lim} \approx 223$ LMH, $V_p \approx 1786$ L in 2 hours. [7 marks]

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Alert — Top Reasons Students Lose Marks on UF Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert bar → Pa when applying $J = \Delta P / (\mu R_t)$. KTU examiners deduct a *full unit mark* for this.
>
> 2. **Mixing Up $R_{obs}$ and $R_{int}$ (–2 marks):** Observed rejection uses $C_b$ in the denominator; intrinsic rejection uses $C_m$. Writing the wrong subscript costs marks.
>
> 3. **Setting $C_p = 0$ Without Justification (–1 mark):** You must explicitly state *"since $R \to 1$ (high-rejection UF), $C_p \approx 0$"* before simplifying the film-theory equation.
>
> 4. **Confusing $J$ and $J_{lim}$ (–2 marks):** $J$ depends on TMP in the clean regime; $J_{lim}$ is independent of TMP. Use the correct one based on the problem wording.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Definition of MWCO (–1 mark):** In 3-mark definitional questions, you must mention **"90% rejection of a reference solute (e.g., BSA or dextran)"** — not just "the pore size of the membrane."
>
> 6. **Drawing Membrane Cross-Section Without TMP (–1 mark):** When asked for a schematic, the **pressure gradient** must be indicated with arrows — examiners expect it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following 12 bullet points are the minimum required for a passing KTU Module 4 question on Ultrafiltration. Master them.**

- **UF Definition:** Pressure-driven (1–10 bar) membrane process separating macromolecules via **size exclusion** with **MWCO in the range 1 kDa – 500 kDa**.
- **Driving Force:** **Transmembrane pressure (TMP)**, not concentration (unlike dialysis).
- **Membrane Structure:** **Asymmetric** — a thin dense skin (top, ~0.1–1 μm) determines selectivity; the porous sublayer (bottom, 100–300 μm) provides mechanical support.
- **MWCO Definition:** Molecular weight of a reference solute rejected to **90%**; measured in **Daltons**.
- **Permeate Flux ($J$):** $J = \Delta P / (\mu R_t)$; **LMH** is the standard unit.
- **Resistance-in-Series:** $R_t = R_m + R_{cp} + R_f$ — clean membrane, polarization, fouling, respectively.
- **Concentration Polarization:** **Reversible** buildup of solute at the membrane–bulk interface, controlled by **cross-flow velocity**.
- **Membrane Fouling:** **Irreversible** pore blockage / surface adsorption, requiring **CIP** (Clean-in-Place).
- **Gel Layer Limiting Flux:** $J_{lim} = k \cdot \ln(C_g / C_b)$ — maximum flux under given hydrodynamics; independent of TMP once reached.
- **Observed vs Intrinsic Rejection:** $R_{obs}$ uses $C_b$, $R_{int}$ uses $C_m$ ($C_m > C_b$).
- **Module Configurations:** Hollow Fiber, Spiral Wound, Plate & Frame, Tubular — chosen based on **fouling tendency** and **viscosity** of the feed.
- **Top Applications:** Whey protein concentration, milk protein standardization, juice clarification, enzyme recovery, water treatment, and broth clarification in fermentation.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "S-F-R-P-C" for UF Essentials:**
> **S**ieving mechanism · **F**lux = $\Delta P / \mu R_t$ · **R**esistance-in-series · **P**olarization (reversible) · **C**leaning (CIP) for fouling.
>
> Memorize this string and you can recall every KTU Module 4 UF concept in 30 seconds before entering the exam hall.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT631 - NON-THERMAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Nanofiltration (NF) and Reverse Osmosis (RO) and their Industrial Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — NANOFILTRATION (NF) & REVERSE OSMOSIS (RO)

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (Nanofiltration):**
> Nanofiltration (NF) is a **pressure-driven membrane separation process** operating between Ultrafiltration (UF) and Reverse Osmosis (RO), with a nominal pore size of **0.5 – 2.0 nm** and a Molecular Weight Cut-Off (MWCO) of approximately **200 – 1000 Daltons**. It selectively rejects multivalent ions and organic molecules of intermediate size while allowing monovalent ions and water to pass partially.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition (Reverse Osmosis):**
> Reverse Osmosis (RO) is a **pressure-driven membrane separation process** in which a semipermeable membrane rejects virtually all dissolved solutes (ions, organics, microorganisms) when the applied hydraulic pressure exceeds the **osmotic pressure** of the feed solution. Typical operating pressure is **15 – 80 bar** (brackish water to seawater), with a membrane pore size of **< 0.5 nm** and a rejection of **> 99 %** for NaCl.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-world analogy — the "Smart Sieve" model:**
Imagine a kitchen sieve system with **three graded meshes**:

| Sieve Type | Holes | What it catches | Real Membrane |
|------------|-------|-----------------|---------------|
| Coarse sieve | Big | Stones, leaves | Microfiltration (MF) |
| Medium sieve | Small | Sand, grit | Ultrafiltration (UF) |
| **Fine sieve** | **Tiny** | **Dye, soap particles, calcium salts** | **Nanofiltration (NF)** |
| **Ultra-fine sieve** | **Atomic** | **Almost everything except water** | **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** |

When you push a salt-water solution through these sieves using a **high-pressure pump**:
- **NF** acts like a *selective bouncer* at a club — it lets small ions (Na⁺, Cl⁻) walk in but blocks big troublemakers (sulfates, dyes, lactose, viruses).
- **RO** acts like a *strict border control* — only water molecules with the right "passport" get through; everything else is turned back.

### 1.3 Membrane Process Spectrum (Position of NF & RO)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Operating Metrics (to memorize for KTU exams):**
> - **RO operating pressure:** **15 – 80 bar** (seawater RO up to 100 bar)
> - **NF operating pressure:** **5 – 40 bar**
> - **Osmotic pressure of seawater (35,000 ppm):** ≈ **30 bar**
> - **Osmotic pressure equation constant (R):** **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - **Standard temperature for osmotic pressure calculation:** **298 K (25 °C)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MF} \rightarrow \text{UF} \rightarrow \text{NF} \rightarrow \text{RO} \\
(0.1\ \mu m) \quad (10\ nm) \quad (1\ nm) \quad (<0.5\ nm) \\
\text{Lower pressure} \rightarrow \text{Higher pressure} \\
\text{Larger pores} \rightarrow \text{Smaller pores}
\end{aligned}
$$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2.1 Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.1.1 Principles of Membrane Separation (Driving Force Logic)

NF and RO are **pressure-driven processes**. The applied hydraulic pressure ($\Delta P$) pushes solvent (water) through a semipermeable membrane, while solutes are retained. The **net driving force** is the difference between the applied pressure and the osmotic pressure difference across the membrane.

**Step-by-step operating logic:**

1. **Feed introduction** — Feed solution enters the high-pressure side of the membrane module.
2. **Selective sorption** — Water molecules (and possibly some small solutes) dissolve into the membrane's polymer matrix at the upstream face.
3. **Diffusion through membrane** — Permeating species diffuse down their chemical potential gradient across the dense membrane.
4. **Desorption at permeate side** — Water emerges on the low-pressure (permeate) side.
5. **Concentrate discharge** — Retained solutes exit as retentate/concentrate at high pressure.
6. **Mass balance maintenance** — Rejection (%) and Recovery (%) quantify the separation efficiency.

### 2.1.2 Solution-Diffusion Model (Theoretical Foundation)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Solution-Diffusion Model (Lonsdale, 1965)** is the universally accepted framework for RO/NF transport and is a KTU high-yield topic.

- **Assumption:** Both solvent and solute dissolve into the dense, non-porous membrane and diffuse independently.
- Solvent flux $\to$ pressure-dependent.
- Solute flux $\to$ concentration-gradient-dependent (and independent of pressure).

### 2.1.3 Why NF is "Loose RO"

NF membranes have a slightly more open structure than RO membranes. This results in:
- Lower operating pressure (energy savings).
- Partial passage of monovalent ions (e.g., NaCl rejection ≈ 20 – 70 %).
- High rejection of multivalent ions (e.g., Na₂SO₄ rejection ≈ 95 – 99 %).
- This is called **Donnan exclusion** (charge-based selectivity) combined with **size sieving**.

### 2.1.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | NF Application | RO Application |
|--------|---------------|----------------|
| Water treatment | Water softening, color removal | Brackish & seawater desalination |
| Food industry | Whey demineralization, lactose concentration | Milk concentration, juice pre-concentration |
| Pharmaceuticals | Antibiotic purification, solvent recovery | Water for Injection (WFI), API concentration |
| Biotechnology | Peptide fractionation | Buffer exchange, protein concentration |
| Textile | Dye recovery, water reuse | Effluent ZLD (Zero Liquid Discharge) |
| Chemical | Catalyst recovery | Organic solvent separation |

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for any NF/RO numerical problem in KTU exams.**

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|----------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\pi = i \cdot M \cdot R \cdot T$ | van't Hoff osmotic pressure (dilute solutions) | Pa |
| 2 | $\pi = \phi \cdot C \cdot R \cdot T$ | Osmotic pressure with osmotic coefficient | Pa |
| 3 | $J_w = A \cdot (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$ | Water flux (Solution-Diffusion model) | L·m⁻²·h⁻¹ |
| 4 | $J_s = B \cdot (C_f - C_p)$ | Salt flux | mg·m⁻²·h⁻¹ |
| 5 | $R = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_f}$ | Observed rejection coefficient | dimensionless (or %) |
| 6 | $R_{true} = 1 - \dfrac{C_p}{C_w}$ | True rejection (at membrane wall) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $Y = \dfrac{Q_p}{Q_f} \times 100$ | Recovery / yield | % |
| 8 | $J = k \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_w - C_p}{C_b - C_p}\right)$ | Concentration polarization (film theory) | L·m⁻²·h⁻¹ |
| 9 | $\beta = \dfrac{C_w}{C_b}$ | Concentration polarization modulus | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\Delta \pi = \pi_f - \pi_p$ | Effective osmotic pressure difference | Pa |

Where:
- $A$ = water permeability constant (L·m⁻²·h⁻¹·bar⁻¹)
- $B$ = solute permeability constant (m·s⁻¹)
- $k$ = mass transfer coefficient (m·s⁻¹)
- $C_w$ = concentration at membrane wall, $C_b$ = bulk concentration, $C_f$ = feed, $C_p$ = permeate
- $i$ = van't Hoff factor (number of dissociated ions)

### 2.2.1 Typical Membrane Constants (Kerala Food Industry Standard Values)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU textbook values to remember:**
> - For seawater RO: $A \approx 2.5 \times 10^{-6}$ kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹·bar⁻¹
> - For brackish water RO: $A \approx 5 \times 10^{-6}$ kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹·bar⁻¹
> - $B$ values for NaCl in polyamide RO membrane: $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-8}$ m·s⁻¹
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1.1 Derivation 1: van't Hoff Equation for Osmotic Pressure

**Starting condition:** Consider a semipermeable membrane separating pure water (side 1) from an aqueous solution (side 2). At equilibrium, the chemical potential of water on both sides must be equal.

**Step 1 — Chemical potential of pure water (side 1):**
$$
\mu_1 = \mu^0 + RT \ln a_w^{(1)} = \mu^0 \quad (\text{since } a_w = 1 \text{ for pure water})
$$

**Step 2 — Chemical potential of water in solution (side 2):**
$$
\mu_2 = \mu^0 + RT \ln a_w^{(2)} + V_w \cdot \pi
$$

where $V_w$ is the partial molar volume of water and $\pi$ is the osmotic pressure that must be applied to maintain equilibrium.

**Step 3 — Apply equilibrium condition ($\mu_1 = \mu_2$):**
$$
\mu^0 = \mu^0 + RT \ln a_w + V_w \cdot \pi
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for osmotic pressure:**
$$
\pi = -\frac{RT}{V_w} \ln a_w
$$

**Step 5 — For a dilute solution, $a_w \approx x_w$ (mole fraction of water):**
$$
\pi = -\frac{RT}{V_w} \ln x_w
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the series expansion $\ln(1-x) \approx -x$ for small $x$:**
Since $x_w = 1 - x_s$ (where $x_s$ = mole fraction of solute):
$$
\pi = -\frac{RT}{V_w} \ln(1 - x_s) \approx \frac{RT}{V_w} \cdot x_s
$$

**Step 7 — Convert mole fraction to molarity:**
For dilute solution, $x_s \approx \dfrac{n_s}{n_w} = \dfrac{C \cdot V_w}{1}$ (per unit volume), giving:
$$
\boxed{\pi = C \cdot R \cdot T = i \cdot M \cdot R \cdot T}
$$

This is the **van't Hoff equation** (1887), valid for dilute, ideal solutions.

---

### 3.1.2 Derivation 2: Water Flux Equation (Solution-Diffusion Model)

**Step 1 — Fick's First Law for diffusion through membrane:**
$$
J_w = -D_w \cdot \frac{dC_w}{dx}
$$

**Step 2 — Water concentration in membrane at high-pressure face (Henry's law):**
$$
C_{w,f}^m = H_w \cdot P_{w,f}
$$

where $H_w$ is the solubility coefficient and $P_{w,f}$ is the water partial pressure on the feed side.

**Step 3 — Water concentration at permeate face:**
$$
C_{w,p}^m = H_w \cdot P_{w,p}
$$

**Step 4 — Linear concentration gradient across membrane thickness $\delta$:**
$$
J_w = \frac{D_w \cdot H_w}{\delta} \cdot (P_{w,f} - P_{w,p})
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $A = D_w H_w / \delta$ and convert partial pressures to chemical potential difference:**
The difference in partial pressures corresponds to the **effective driving force** $(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$:
$$
\boxed{J_w = A \cdot (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)}
$$

where $A$ is the **water permeability constant** (a function of membrane material, temperature, and water viscosity).

---

### 3.1.3 Derivation 3: Salt Flux Equation

Following the same logic for solute:

**Step 1 — Salt dissolves in membrane (independent of pressure):**
$$
C_{s,f}^m = K_s \cdot C_f
$$

**Step 2 — Salt diffuses due to concentration gradient only (no pressure coupling):**
$$
J_s = \frac{D_s \cdot K_s}{\delta} \cdot (C_f - C_p)
$$

**Step 3 — Define $B = D_s K_s / \delta$ as the salt permeability constant:**
$$
\boxed{J_s = B \cdot (C_f - C_p)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight for KTU:** Water flux depends on **both pressure AND concentration**, while salt flux depends **only on concentration**. This is why salt rejection increases with applied pressure in RO systems.

---

### 3.1.4 Derivation 4: Membrane Rejection Coefficient

**Step 1 — Define mass balance on solute at steady state:**
$$
Q_f C_f = Q_p C_p + Q_r C_r
$$

**Step 2 — By definition, the **observed rejection** $R$ is:**
$$
R = \frac{C_f - C_p}{C_f} = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_f}
$$

**Step 3 — Express $C_p$ in terms of fluxes:**
Since the volume of solute crossing the membrane equals $J_s \cdot A_m$ (where $A_m$ is membrane area) and volume of water crossing is $J_w \cdot A_m$:
$$
C_p = \frac{J_s}{J_w}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $J_s = B(C_f - C_p)$ and $J_w = A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$:**
$$
C_p = \frac{B(C_f - C_p)}{A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $C_p$ in terms of $C_f$:**
$$
C_p \cdot A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi) = B C_f - B C_p
$$
$$
C_p [A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi) + B] = B C_f
$$
$$
C_p = \frac{B C_f}{A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi) + B}
$$

**Step 6 — Substituting back into rejection expression:**
$$
R = 1 - \frac{B}{A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi) + B} = \frac{A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)}{A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi) + B}
$$

$$
\boxed{R = \frac{A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)}{A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi) + B}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering conclusion:** As $\Delta P \to \infty$ (very high pressure), $R \to 1$ (100 % rejection). In practice, energy cost limits how high we can go.

---

### 3.1.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Performance — NF vs. RO

| Parameter | Nanofiltration (NF) | Reverse Osmosis (RO) |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Pore size** | 0.5 – 2 nm | < 0.5 nm |
| **MWCO** | 200 – 1000 Da | < 200 Da |
| **Operating pressure** | 5 – 40 bar | 15 – 80 bar |
| **NaCl rejection** | 20 – 70 % | 99 – 99.8 % |
| **Na₂SO₄ rejection** | 95 – 99 % | 99.5 % |
| **Mechanism** | Size exclusion + Donnan (charge) | Solution-diffusion |
| **Typical A (permeability)** | $5 \times 10^{-5}$ to $10^{-5}$ kg/m²·s·bar | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}$ kg/m²·s·bar |
| **Energy consumption** | 0.5 – 2 kWh/m³ | 1.5 – 6 kWh/m³ (seawater) |
| **Major applications** | Softening, dye removal, whey | Desalination, WFI, milk concentration |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4.1 Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1.1 Block Diagram — NF / RO Process Flow

```mermaid
graph TB
    FeedIn[Feed Tank Brackish or Process Water] --> PumpIn[High Pressure Pump 5 to 80 bar]
    PumpIn --> HeaterExchanger[Heat Exchanger Temperature Control]
    HeaterExchanger --> MembraneModule[Membrane Module Spiral Wound or Hollow Fiber]
    MembraneModule --> PermOut[Permeate Stream Purified Water or Concentrate]
    MembraneModule --> RetentOut[Retentate Concentrate Rejected Solutes]
    RetentOut --> RecycleLoop[Recycle to Feed Tank]
    RetentOut --> BleedStream[Bleed Stream to Drain or Downstream Processing]
    PressureGauge[Pressure Gauge] --> MembraneModule
    FlowMeter[Flow Meter] --> PermOut
```

### 4.1.2 Membrane Process Spectrum Comparison (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    MF[Microfiltration 0.1 micron] --> UF[Ultrafiltration 10 nm]
    UF --> NF[Nanofiltration 1 nm 5 to 40 bar]
    NF --> RO[Reverse Osmosis 0.5 nm 15 to 80 bar]
    P1[Pressure 0.1 to 2 bar] --> P2[Pressure 1 to 5 bar]
    P2 --> P3[Pressure 5 to 40 bar]
    P3 --> P4[Pressure 15 to 80 bar]
    P1 --> MF
    P2 --> UF
    P3 --> NF
    P4 --> RO
```

### 4.1.3 Industrial Applications Architecture Map

```mermaid
graph TB
    Root[NF and RO Industrial Applications]
    Root --> WT[Water Treatment]
    Root --> FI[Food and Dairy Industry]
    Root --> PH[Pharmaceutical]
    Root --> BT[Biotechnology]
    Root --> TX[Textile Industry]
    WT --> WT1[Seawater Desalination]
    WT --> WT2[Brackish Water Purification]
    WT --> WT3[Water Softening NF]
    WT --> WT4[Industrial Effluent Recycling]
    FI --> FI1[Milk and Whey Concentration]
    FI --> FI2[Fruit Juice Clarification]
    FI --> FI3[Sugar Refining]
    FI --> FI4[Wine and Beer Clarification]
    FI --> FI5[Edible Oil Pre-treatment]
    PH --> PH1[Water for Injection WFI]
    PH --> PH2[API Concentration]
    PH --> PH3[Solvent Recovery]
    BT --> BT1[Protein Concentration]
    BT --> BT2[Buffer Exchange]
    BT --> BT3[Peptide Fractionation NF]
    TX --> TX1[Dye Recovery NF]
    TX --> TX2[Zero Liquid Discharge ZLD]
```

### 4.1.4 Mass Transfer & Solution-Diffusion Functional Block

```mermaid
graph TB
    FSide[Feed Side High Pressure C_f and P_f]
    FSide --> Sorption[Sorption into Membrane]
    Sorption --> Diffusion[Diffusion Across Membrane Thickness delta]
    Diffusion --> Desorption[Desorption at Permeate Side]
    Desorption --> PSide[Permeate Side Low Pressure C_p and P_p]
    FSide -.Osmotic Pressure.-> PSide
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU exams:** When asked to "draw the process flow", always show: **Feed → Pump → Membrane → (Permeate + Retentate)**. Add arrows for pressure, concentration, and any recycle stream.

## 5.1 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

### 5.1.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Module 4]**

Differentiate between **Nanofiltration (NF)** and **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** based on **(a)** membrane pore size, **(b)** operating pressure, and **(c)** rejection characteristics for monovalent and divalent ions.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Nanofiltration (NF) | Reverse Osmosis (RO) |
|---------|---------------------|----------------------|
| **(a) Pore size / MWCO** | 0.5 – 2 nm; MWCO = 200 – 1000 Da | < 0.5 nm; MWCO < 200 Da |
| **(b) Operating pressure** | 5 – 40 bar (lower) | 15 – 80 bar (higher) |
| **(c) Rejection** | Low for monovalent ions (20 – 70 %); High for divalent ions (95 – 99 %) | Very high for both mono- and divalent ions (99 – 99.8 %) |

**Mechanism note (1 mark):** NF uses a combination of size exclusion and **Donnan (charge) exclusion**, while RO operates primarily on the **Solution-Diffusion model**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

State the **van't Hoff equation** for osmotic pressure. A solution contains **0.1 mol/L of NaCl** at **25 °C**. Calculate the osmotic pressure. (Take $R = 0.08314$ L·bar·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹, $i = 2$ for NaCl.)

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Van't Hoff equation:** $\pi = i \cdot M \cdot R \cdot T$ — where $i$ is the van't Hoff factor, $M$ is molarity, $R$ is the universal gas constant, and $T$ is absolute temperature.

**Calculation:**
- $i = 2$ (NaCl dissociates into Na⁺ and Cl⁻)
- $M = 0.1$ mol/L
- $R = 0.08314$ L·bar·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹
- $T = 25 + 273 = 298$ K

$$
\pi = 2 \times 0.1 \times 0.08314 \times 298
$$

$$
\boxed{\pi = 4.955 \text{ bar}}
$$
