### 5.1.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, students answer ONE question of 14 marks from Module 4 (internal choice). Each sub-part carries 7 marks.**

---

### 5.1.3 QUESTION A — [14 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Solution-Diffusion model** for transport through Reverse Osmosis membranes. Derive the expressions for **water flux** and **salt flux**, and discuss how the rejection coefficient depends on applied pressure.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Part a):**

**Step 1 — Concept of Solution-Diffusion (2 marks):**
The Solution-Diffusion model (Lonsdale, 1965) treats the dense RO/NF membrane as a **non-porous polymer** in which both water and solute dissolve at the upstream face, diffuse through the membrane under a chemical potential gradient, and desorb at the permeate side. Unlike porous membranes (used in MF/UF), no bulk convective flow occurs.

**Step 2 — Water flux derivation (2 marks):**
Using Fick's law across membrane thickness $\delta$ with Henry's law sorption:
$$
J_w = \frac{D_w H_w}{\delta} (P_{w,f} - P_{w,p})
$$
Defining water permeability $A = D_w H_w / \delta$ and substituting the effective driving force $(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$:
$$
\boxed{J_w = A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)}
$$
**Water flux increases linearly with applied pressure** (after offsetting osmotic pressure).

**Step 3 — Salt flux derivation (2 marks):**
Solute diffuses independently and its flux is **pressure-independent**:
$$
\boxed{J_s = B(C_f - C_p)}
$$
where $B$ is the salt permeability constant.

**Step 4 — Rejection dependence on pressure (1 mark):**
The observed rejection $R$ is given by:
$$
R = \frac{A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)}{A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi) + B}
$$
As $\Delta P$ increases, $R \to 1$ (100 %), so **higher pressure yields higher salt rejection** — a fundamental reason why seawater RO uses 60 – 80 bar.

---

**(b)** A **seawater RO system** processes feed with **TDS = 35,000 mg/L** at an applied pressure of **70 bar**. The membrane water permeability constant is $A = 2.5 \times 10^{-6}$ kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹·bar⁻¹, and the osmotic pressure of the feed is **30 bar**. The permeate is essentially salt-free. Calculate the **water flux** in **L·m⁻²·h⁻¹**.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Part b):**

**Given:**
- $A = 2.5 \times 10^{-6}$ kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹·bar⁻¹
- $\Delta P = 70$ bar
- $\Delta \pi = 30$ bar (permeate salt-free, so $\pi_p \approx 0$)
- $\Delta P - \Delta \pi = 70 - 30 = 40$ bar

**Step 1 — Apply water flux equation:**
$$
J_w = A (\Delta P - \Delta \pi)
$$
$$
J_w = 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 40
$$
$$
J_w = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to L·m⁻²·h⁻¹ (assuming density of water ≈ 1 kg/L):**

Since 1 kg = 1 L and 1 hour = 3600 s:
$$
J_w = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600 \text{ L·m}^{-2}\text{·h}^{-1}
$$
$$
J_w = 0.36 \text{ L·m}^{-2}\text{·h}^{-1}
$$

$$
\boxed{J_w = 0.36 \text{ L·m}^{-2}\text{·h}^{-1}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on real-world values:** Commercial seawater RO plants operate at higher flux (10 – 15 L·m⁻²·h⁻¹) using membranes with higher A values. The low A value used here gives a conservative academic result.

---

### 5.1.4 QUESTION B — [14 Marks] (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Discuss the **industrial applications** of Nanofiltration and Reverse Osmosis in **(i)** water treatment, **(ii)** dairy and food processing, and **(iii)** pharmaceutical industries. Give at least **two specific examples per industry**.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Part a):**

**(i) Water Treatment (2 marks):**
- **Seawater desalination (RO):** Produces drinking water from seawater in coastal regions (e.g., Kerala's coastal belts). Modern SWRO plants in Kerala (e.g., Kerala Water Authority) produce 100 – 200 MLD of potable water at 3 – 5 kWh/m³.
- **Brackish water treatment (RO):** Used for inland saline groundwater with TDS 1,000 – 10,000 mg/L.
- **Water softening (NF):** Removes hardness (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺) from groundwater by rejecting divalent ions while leaving monovalent ions — a low-energy alternative to ion exchange.
- **Industrial effluent recycling (NF + RO):** Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD) systems in textile and dye industries.

**(ii) Dairy & Food Processing (2.5 marks):**
- **Whey demineralization (NF):** NF removes 30 – 60 % of ash (minerals) from cheese whey while retaining valuable proteins and lactose — produces whey protein concentrate for infant formula.
- **Milk concentration (RO):** Pre-concentration of milk from 12 % to 22 – 25 % total solids before evaporation, saving **60 – 80 % of evaporation energy**.
- **Fruit juice clarification & concentration (NF/UF + RO):** Apple, orange, and grape juice concentration at ambient temperature, preserving heat-sensitive vitamins and flavor compounds.
- **Sugar refining (NF):** Removal of color bodies and multivalent ions from cane sugar juice.
- **Wine & beer clarification:** Cold stabilization and alcohol adjustment.

**(iii) Pharmaceutical & Biotechnology (2.5 marks):**
- **Water for Injection (WFI) via RO:** Multi-stage RO systems produce WFI-grade water, replacing energy-intensive distillation.
- **API concentration:** Reverse osmosis concentrates heat-sensitive antibiotics (e.g., penicillin, erythromycin) without thermal degradation.
- **Peptide fractionation (NF):** Separation of peptides based on molecular weight.
- **Protein concentration & buffer exchange (UF/NF):** Downstream processing of monoclonal antibodies and enzymes.
- **Solvent recovery in API synthesis** (Nanofiltration of organic solvents — "Organic Solvent Nanofiltration, OSN").

---

**(b)** An **RO plant** processes **1000 m³/day** of brackish water. The **permeate flow rate** is **750 m³/day** with **permeate TDS = 200 mg/L**. The **feed TDS = 3500 mg/L**. Calculate:
- (i) The **Recovery (Y)** in %
- (ii) The **Rejection (R)** in %
- (iii) The **retentate flow rate** $Q_r$ and **retentate TDS** $C_r$.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Part b):**

**Given:**
- $Q_f = 1000$ m³/day, $Q_p = 750$ m³/day
- $C_f = 3500$ mg/L, $C_p = 200$ mg/L

**Step 1 — Recovery (Y):**
$$
Y = \frac{Q_p}{Q_f} \times 100 = \frac{750}{1000} \times 100
$$
$$
\boxed{Y = 75 \%}
$$

**Step 2 — Rejection (R):**
$$
R = 1 - \frac{C_p}{C_f} = 1 - \frac{200}{3500}
$$
$$
R = 1 - 0.0571 = 0.9429
$$
$$
\boxed{R = 94.29 \%}
$$

**Step 3 — Retentate flow rate $Q_r$:**
$$
Q_r = Q_f - Q_p = 1000 - 750
$$
$$
\boxed{Q_r = 250 \text{ m}^3\text{/day}}
$$

**Step 4 — Retentate TDS $C_r$ by overall mass balance:**

Total salt in feed = Total salt in permeate + Total salt in retentate:
$$
Q_f C_f = Q_p C_p + Q_r C_r
$$
$$
1000 \times 3500 = 750 \times 200 + 250 \times C_r
$$
$$
3{,}500{,}000 = 150{,}000 + 250 \cdot C_r
$$
$$
C_r = \frac{3{,}350{,}000}{250} = 13{,}400 \text{ mg/L}
$$
$$
\boxed{C_r = 13{,}400 \text{ mg/L} \approx 1.34 \% \text{ TDS}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** Although the rejection is excellent (94.3 %), the retentate is 3.83× more concentrated than the feed. This retentate (or "brine") requires proper disposal or further treatment in ZLD systems.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit error trap:** When calculating $J_w$, students often forget to convert **seconds → hours** or **kg → litres**. Always end with **L·m⁻²·h⁻¹**.
> 2. **van't Hoff factor omission:** For NaCl, always use $i = 2$; for CaCl₂, $i = 3$. Forgetting $i$ loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Rejection vs. Recovery confusion:** Rejection $R$ relates to **solute** (salt) retention; Recovery $Y$ relates to **solvent (water)** yield. Do NOT mix them up.
> 4. **Osmotic pressure units:** $R$ in L·bar·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ gives $\pi$ in **bar**; $R$ in J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹ (= 8.314) gives $\pi$ in **Pa**. Pick the right one.
> 5. **Forgetting to subtract $\Delta\pi$:** A frequent mistake is $J_w = A \cdot \Delta P$ (without the osmotic correction). Always use $A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$.
> 6. **Confusing true vs. observed rejection:** $R_{true} = 1 - C_p/C_w$ accounts for concentration polarization; $R_{obs} = 1 - C_p/C_f$ does not. In KTU theory questions, state both.
> 7. **RO vs. NF rejection mistake:** NaCl rejection in RO is >99 %, not in NF (20 – 70 %). Know the spectrum!

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4 — NF & RO):**

### Core Concepts
- NF pore size: **0.5 – 2 nm**; RO pore size: **< 0.5 nm**.
- NF MWCO: **200 – 1000 Da**; RO MWCO: **< 200 Da**.
- NF pressure: **5 – 40 bar**; RO pressure: **15 – 80 bar** (seawater up to 100 bar).
- NF rejection: monovalent ions **20 – 70 %**; divalent ions **95 – 99 %**.
- RO rejection: virtually all dissolved species **99 – 99.8 %**.

### Must-Know Equations
- **Osmotic pressure:** $\pi = i M R T$
- **Water flux:** $J_w = A(\Delta P - \Delta \pi)$
- **Salt flux:** $J_s = B(C_f - C_p)$
- **Rejection:** $R = 1 - C_p/C_f$
- **Recovery:** $Y = Q_p/Q_f \times 100$
- **Concentration polarization:** $J = k \ln[(C_w - C_p)/(C_b - C_p)]$
- **True rejection:** $R_{true} = 1 - C_p/C_w$

### Mechanisms to Memorize
- **RO mechanism:** Solution-Diffusion model (Lonsdale, 1965).
- **NF mechanism:** Size exclusion + **Donnan (charge) exclusion** for ions.
- **Concentration polarization:** Film theory governs boundary layer buildup.

### Industrial Applications Quick-Recall Table

| Industry | NF Examples | RO Examples |
|----------|------------|-------------|
| Water | Softening, dye removal | Seawater desalination, WFI |
| Dairy | Whey demineralization | Milk pre-concentration |
| Beverages | Juice clarification | Juice concentration |
| Pharma | Solvent recovery (OSN) | API concentration, WFI |
| Sugar | Decolorization, ion removal | — |
| Textile | Dye recovery | Effluent ZLD |

### Numerical Problem-Solving Template (Use for Any KTU 14-Mark Question)
1. **List all given values** with units.
2. **Identify the governing equation(s)** from the formula sheet.
3. **Check units consistency** (SI vs. CGS vs. bar·L).
4. **Apply osmotic pressure correction** if water flux is asked.
5. **Show all conversions** explicitly (kg → L, s → h).
6. **Box the final answer** with units.
7. **Add a one-line engineering interpretation** (e.g., "Hence, the recovery is 75 %, suitable for industrial brackish water treatment").

### Frequently Asked KTU Theory Points
- Why does RO require more energy than NF? → Higher pressure + lower A permeability.
- Why does NF allow monovalent ions to pass? → Loose membrane + Donnan exclusion (charge repulsion is weak for monovalents).
- What limits the maximum water flux? → Membrane compaction and concentration polarization.
- What is the "B" coefficient? → Salt permeability constant, lower B = better rejection.
- Why is polyamide preferred for RO/NF membranes? → High salt rejection, chemical stability, thin-film composite structure.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Nanofiltration (NF) & Reverse Osmosis (RO)**
